title: Document Classification Pattern Recognition via Information Fusion: A Systematic Review of Multimodal and Multiview Representation Approaches
summary: Information fusion is used widely to improve document classification by the integration of multiple data sources (multimodal) or representations (multiview). However, the field lacks a unified framework, a quantitative synthesis of its effectiveness, and clear guidance for practitioners. This systematic review addresses these gaps by analysing 139 primary studies. It introduces a formal framework to structure the field, presents the results of a qualitative analysis to identify key trends, and performs a random-effects meta-analysis (to our knowledge, the first focused on document classification) to quantify performance gains. Our meta-analysis reveals that multimodal fusion improves accuracy (mean gain of +5.28 percentage points, $p=0.0016$) significantly -- the F1-score effect is directionally positive but statistically non-significant in our primary model. Multiview fusion provides consistent but modest gains for accuracy (+4.67\%), F1-score (+3.08\%), and recall (all $p<0.05$). Critically, our qualitative synthesis uncovers challenges in reproducibility in methodological rigour: only 11.8\% (multimodal) and 23.3\% (multiview) of the studies use statistical tests to validate their findings, which undermines the reliability of many of their results. This review's primary contributions are a unifying framework, the first quantitative evidence base, and data-driven guidelines. This review concludes that successful information fusion depends not on algorithmic complexity, but on the strategic alignment of the fusion method with the task context and a commitment to more rigorous validation.

AI.tldr: Systematic review and meta-analysis on information fusion for document classification, showing modest but consistent gains from multimodal and multiview fusion, and highlighting major issues in methodological rigor and reproducibility.
AI.motivation: Information fusion is widely used in document classification, but the field is fragmented: there is no unified conceptual framework, no quantitative aggregate evidence of how much fusion helps, and little guidance for practitioners on when and how to use fusion effectively. The authors aim to consolidate knowledge, measure typical performance gains, and clarify best practices.
AI.method: Conduct a systematic literature review of 139 primary studies on information fusion for document classification, covering both multimodal (multiple data sources) and multiview (multiple representations) settings. Propose a formal framework to organize fusion approaches, perform qualitative analysis to identify trends and practices, and run a random-effects meta-analysis to quantify how much fusion improves standard evaluation metrics (accuracy, F1, recall).
AI.result: Multimodal fusion yields a statistically significant average improvement in accuracy of about +5.28 percentage points (p = 0.0016), while its effect on F1-score is positive but not statistically significant in the main model. Multiview fusion produces smaller but consistent and statistically significant gains in accuracy (+4.67%), F1-score (+3.08%), and recall (all p < 0.05). The review also finds poor methodological rigor in the primary studies: only 11.8% of multimodal and 23.3% of multiview studies use statistical tests, raising concerns about the reliability of their reported improvements.
AI.conclusion: Information fusion generally benefits document classification, with multimodal and multiview approaches delivering modest but meaningful performance gains. However, the reliability of many reported results is weakened by limited use of statistical validation and reproducibility practices. The authors argue that success in information fusion is driven less by sophisticated algorithms and more by choosing fusion strategies that align with the specific task context and by adopting rigorous evaluation and validation standards. They provide a unifying framework and evidence-based guidelines to support more principled use of fusion in practice.

title: Raon-Speech Technical Report
summary: We present Raon-Speech, a top-performing 9B-parameter speech language model (SpeechLM) for English and Korean speech understanding, answering, and generation, and Raon-SpeechChat, a high-performing full-duplex extension for natural real-time conversation. Raon-Speech successfully transforms a pre-trained LLM into a SpeechLM that both understands and generates speech while preserving strong text capabilities. It trains on 1.38M hours of highly curated English and Korean speech and text datasets with the following training stages: (1) speech modules alignment, (2) end-to-end SpeechLM pre-training with knowledge distillation, and (3) multi-task preference optimization-based post-training. Across 42 English and Korean speech and text benchmarks, Raon-Speech establishes the strongest overall profile on speech-centric tasks in our comparison against eight similarly sized recent audio foundation models, including Qwen2.5-Omni and Fun-Audio-Chat, while preserving strong text question answering performance. Building upon it, Raon-SpeechChat enables natural full-duplex conversation by continual training on 119K hours of time-aligned real and synthetic dialogue data. It proceeds through three complementary training stages: (1) causal encoder adaptation, (2) full-duplex pre-training, (3) full-duplex fine-tuning for voice and role-control. On multiple full-duplex benchmarks, Raon-SpeechChat shows its clearest strengths on the turn-taking and interruption-sensitive behaviors covered by FDB v1.0, and remains competitive across the broader full-duplex evaluation suite. We open-source all model checkpoints, the training and inference pipeline, and an interactive demo.

AI.tldr: Raon-Speech is a 9B-parameter bilingual (English/Korean) speech language model and Raon-SpeechChat is its full-duplex, real-time conversational extension; together they deliver state-of-the-art speech understanding, generation, and natural interactive dialogue, outperforming similar-sized audio foundation models while preserving strong text abilities, and are fully open-sourced.
AI.motivation: Existing large audio or multimodal models either do not fully leverage strong pre-trained LLMs for both speech understanding and generation, or lack robust real-time, full-duplex conversational capabilities such as natural turn-taking and interruption handling. Additionally, there is a need for bilingual (English/Korean) models trained on large, high-quality data that can excel across many speech and text benchmarks while remaining open-source.
AI.method: The authors transform a pre-trained LLM into a SpeechLM through three training stages on 1.38M hours of curated English and Korean speech and text: (1) speech module alignment so the speech front-end matches the LLM’s representation space, (2) end-to-end SpeechLM pre-training with knowledge distillation to transfer capabilities from stronger teachers, and (3) multi-task preference-optimization-based post-training to improve conversational quality and task performance. For Raon-SpeechChat, they perform continual training on 119K hours of time-aligned real and synthetic dialogue data in three stages: (1) causal encoder adaptation to support streaming, (2) full-duplex pre-training to handle overlapping speech and real-time interaction, and (3) full-duplex fine-tuning focusing on voice characteristics and role-control for better conversational behavior.
AI.result: On 42 English and Korean speech and text benchmarks, Raon-Speech achieves the strongest overall performance among eight similarly sized recent audio foundation models, including Qwen2.5-Omni and Fun-Audio-Chat, particularly on speech-centric tasks, while maintaining strong text QA performance. Raon-SpeechChat, evaluated on multiple full-duplex benchmarks, shows especially strong results on turn-taking and interruption-sensitive behaviors according to FDB v1.0 and remains competitive across the broader full-duplex evaluation suite.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that a carefully staged transformation of a pre-trained LLM into a SpeechLM, combined with large-scale bilingual speech-text training and preference optimization, can yield state-of-the-art speech understanding and generation without sacrificing text capabilities. Extending this with targeted continual training enables effective full-duplex, real-time conversational behavior with strong turn-taking and interruption handling. The authors provide practical value to the community by fully open-sourcing models, pipelines, and a demo.

title: Multi-Persona Debate System for Automated Scientific Hypothesis Generation
comment: 31 pages with 7 main figures, 4 supplementary figures and 1 supplementary table
summary: Modern scientific discovery is bottlenecked not by data scarcity, but by the inability to synthesize fragmented knowledge into actionable hypotheses. This challenge is especially acute in battery materials research, where electrochemical performance, interfacial behavior, and manufacturing feasibility must be optimized simultaneously. Here, we present the Multi-Persona Debate System (MPDS), a literature-grounded framework for automated scientific hypothesis generation that combines literature retrieval, long-context large language model reasoning, corpus-driven persona induction, and structured multi-agent debate. MPDS constructs literature snapshots of up to 500 papers, grounds agents in role-specific evidence pools, and conducts a three-round citation-aware debate followed by moderator synthesis, enabling negotiation between personas while preserving evidence traceability. We evaluate MPDS using a temporally controlled protocol excluding direct access to target papers, including two held-out battery-materials case studies and a blinded comparison across 30 matched cases. In sodium-ion anode and all-solid-state battery cathode design tasks, MPDS recovered design logics aligned with experimentally validated solution spaces and generated more mechanistically explicit, process-aware proposals than simpler baselines. To assess the impact of personas and debate, we introduce Integrative Hypothesis Quality scoring. In ablation studies, MPDS achieved the highest mean score among five conditions, with its largest advantage in cross-perspective integration. A laboratory follow-up suggests utility as a diagnostic aid for identifying practical bottlenecks in workflows. These results indicate that structured debate over literature snapshots improves hypothesis formation under coupled engineering constraints and provides a reusable workflow for text-intensive scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: They propose an automated multi-agent, literature-grounded system that uses debate between specialized AI personas to generate better battery materials hypotheses under complex constraints.
AI.motivation: Scientific discovery is limited not by amount of data but by difficulty in integrating scattered findings into coherent, testable hypotheses, especially in areas like battery materials where many coupled constraints (performance, interfaces, manufacturability) must be optimized together.
AI.method: They build the Multi-Persona Debate System (MPDS), which retrieves up to 500 relevant papers, creates role-specific evidence pools and data-driven personas, and runs a three-round, citation-aware debate among these personas, moderated by a synthesis agent; they evaluate it under temporal holdout protocols on real battery-material design tasks, with ablations over personas and debate structure.
AI.result: In sodium-ion anode and solid-state battery cathode design cases, MPDS reconstructed experimentally validated design rationales and produced more mechanistically detailed and process-aware hypotheses than simpler baselines; in ablations using a new Integrative Hypothesis Quality metric, MPDS had the highest average score, especially in cross-perspective integration, and a lab follow-up showed it can highlight practical bottlenecks in workflows.
AI.conclusion: Structured, citation-grounded debate among specialized AI personas over large literature snapshots improves hypothesis generation under complex engineering constraints and yields a reusable workflow to support text-heavy scientific discovery tasks such as battery materials design.

title: Improving the Completeness and Comparability of Segment Disclosures: A Large Language Model Approach
comment: 39 pages, 4 figures, submitted to Accounting Horizons
summary: Segment-level disclosures are a central component of financial reporting, providing insight into firms' internal organization and the allocation of economic activities across operating units. However, segment information is often presented in both qualitative and quantitative forms, dispersed across tables and narrative sections of Form 10-K filings. Empirical research relying on structured databases faces both completeness and comparability challenges, as some firm-year observations may be missing, nested segment disclosures are not captured, and support for longitudinal and cross-firm comparability is limited. This study develops a large language model-based framework to extract segment disclosures directly from Form 10-K filings and to preserve both reportable and nested segment information. We further design a retrieval augmented system that incorporates information across multiple filings to support comparability. We use two representative settings to demonstrate its application: longitudinal analysis within a firm to interpret segment changes over time, and cross firm alignment of geographic segments across firms with different reporting structures. The results indicate that the artifact accurately extracts segment-level information and effectively addresses questions that require cross-period knowledge, demonstrating the potential of LLM-based approaches to enhance the measurement and interpretation of segment disclosures.

AI.tldr: The paper builds an LLM-based system to automatically extract and standardize segment-level disclosures from 10-Ks, including nested segments, to improve completeness and comparability for empirical research.
AI.motivation: Segment disclosures are crucial for understanding firms’ internal organization and economic activities, but current structured databases are incomplete and not fully comparable across time and firms. Narrative and tabular disclosures in 10-Ks are fragmented, with missing firm-years, lack of nested segment capture, and poor support for longitudinal and cross-firm analysis, which restricts empirical research quality.
AI.method: The authors develop a framework using large language models to directly parse and extract both qualitative and quantitative segment disclosures from full 10-K filings, preserving reportable and nested segments. They augment this with a retrieval-augmented system that pulls information from multiple filings for the same firm or across firms, enabling consistent tracking and alignment of segments. They then apply the system in two use cases: (1) longitudinal analysis of segment changes within a firm and (2) cross-firm alignment of geographic segments under differing reporting schemes.
AI.result: The LLM-based artifact accurately recovers segment-level information from 10-Ks, including nested segments that are not covered in existing databases. In the two representative applications, it successfully answers questions that require integrating knowledge across periods and filings, improving both completeness and comparability of segment data relative to traditional structured sources.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that LLM-based and retrieval-augmented extraction can materially enhance the measurement and interpretation of segment disclosures, overcoming key limitations of existing structured databases. This approach enables more reliable longitudinal and cross-firm analyses of segment information and illustrates broader potential for LLMs in financial reporting research.

title: In Search of the Ingredients of Open-Endedness: Replicating Picbreeder with Large Vision-Language Models
comment: 26 pages, 21 figures, to be published at GECCO 2026
summary: We are in the midst of large-scale industrial and academic efforts to automate the processes of scientific, technological and creative production through AI-driven assistants. Historically, a fundamental property of these processes in their human form has been their open-endedness: their capacity for generating a seemingly endless supply of novel and meaningful new forms. Do artificial agents have any capacity for such fruitful unguided discovery? To answer this question, we turn to Picbreeder, the canonical exemplar of human-driven open-ended search, in which users collaboratively generated a diverse library of images through interactive evolution of small neural networks. We replicate Picbreeder, replacing human users with frontier Vision Language Models (VLMs). We observe clear qualitative differences between the output of our system and the historical human baseline, and attempt to characterize them using metrics of phylogenetic complexity and visual and semantic salience and novelty. In an effort to identify some of the causal factors contributing these differences, we study the addition of exploratory noise to the agents' selection process, of behavioral diversity between agents, and of narrative momentum in the form of memory of past actions. We make our code available at https://github.com/smearle/picbreeder-vlm.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether AI-driven Vision Language Models (VLMs) can replace humans in an open-ended creative search system (Picbreeder) and analyzes how their outputs differ from human-generated ones.
AI.motivation: There is growing interest in using AI assistants to automate scientific, technological, and creative work. A core feature of human-driven creativity is open-endedness: the ability to continually generate novel, meaningful outputs without a fixed goal. It is unclear whether current artificial agents possess this capacity for unguided, fruitful discovery. Picbreeder is a classic system where humans collaboratively evolve images via interactive evolution, serving as a testbed for open-ended creativity. The authors want to see if VLMs can play the human role and whether they exhibit similar open-ended creative behavior.
AI.method: The authors reimplement Picbreeder but replace human users with frontier Vision Language Models as the selective agents in the evolutionary loop. VLMs are asked to evaluate and select images produced by evolving small neural network genomes. The resulting image libraries are compared to the historical human-produced Picbreeder archive using metrics of phylogenetic complexity (structure and depth of evolutionary lineages), visual and semantic salience, and novelty. They also run controlled variations where they: (1) inject exploratory noise into the VLMs’ selection decisions, (2) introduce behavioral diversity between agents, and (3) give agents narrative momentum via memory of past actions, to study how these factors affect open-endedness and creativity.
AI.result: The VLM-driven system produces images that are qualitatively and quantitatively different from human-generated Picbreeder images. The authors observe lower or different forms of phylogenetic complexity and distinct patterns in visual/semantic salience and novelty, suggesting the agents explore the space in a more constrained and less open-ended manner. Modifications such as adding noise, encouraging diversity between agents, and providing memory change the exploration behavior, but do not fully close the gap with human performance, indicating specific limitations in current VLMs’ capacity for unguided creative discovery.
AI.conclusion: Current VLMs, when placed into an open-ended interactive evolution system like Picbreeder, do not replicate key characteristics of human-driven open-ended search. Their creative trajectories are more limited and systematically different from human ones, even when enhanced with noise, diversity, and memory. This suggests that existing AI assistants may be fundamentally constrained in their ability to engage in genuinely open-ended, unguided creative discovery, and that additional mechanisms or architectures may be needed to approach human-like open-endedness. The released code enables further investigation into how to close this gap.

title: EchoDistill:Alignment Noisy-to-Clean Self-Distillation for Robust Audio LLMs
summary: Audio Large Language Models (ALLMs) are highly vulnerable to real-world noise, which often induces severe semantic drift and hallucinations. Existing robustness methods primarily rely on waveform-level acoustic enhancement, answer-level supervision, or the internal suppression of noise representations. To address these issues, we propose echodistill, an alignment-based noisy-to-clean self-distillation framework. Echodistill leverages a frozen clean-audio teacher to provide semantic references for an inference-time noisy-audio student. Specifically, the student samples candidate responses under noisy conditions to expose its test-time behavior. These trajectories are then optimized via group-relative policy optimization (GRPO), where the token-level consistency with the teacher acts as a reward bonus. By aligning the noisy student's candidate responses with clean semantic evidence, and applying audio-aware reward shaping, our method encourages reasoning trajectories that are both correct and genuinely acoustically grounded. Echodistill significantly improves the semantic reliability and task performance of Audio LLMs under complex noise, without introducing any additional inference costs. Extensive experiments show that: (I) Compared with the strongest baseline, echodistill achieves average improvements of 4.18\%$\uparrow$ in GSR under strong noise. (II) Ablation results on Qwen-Omni further show that echodistill improves over the GRPO-only variant by 3.02\%$\uparrow$ in Acc, 3.89\%$\uparrow$ in Noisy, and 4.53\%$\uparrow$ in GSR on average. Our codes are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/echodistill-10DE.

AI.tldr: Echodistill is a self-distillation framework that aligns a noisy-audio Audio LLM (student) with a clean-audio teacher using GRPO and token-level semantic consistency rewards to improve robustness to real-world noise without extra inference cost.
AI.motivation: Audio LLMs perform poorly and hallucinate when input audio is noisy. Existing robustness approaches focus on low-level denoising, coarse answer-level supervision, or suppressing noise representations internally, which do not sufficiently ensure that the model’s reasoning remains semantically accurate and grounded in the audio under real-world noise. A better method is needed to directly align the model’s noisy-audio behavior with clean semantic evidence.
AI.method: Use a frozen teacher model that listens to clean audio and a student model that operates on noisy audio. During training, the student generates multiple candidate responses under noisy conditions to expose its test-time trajectories. These candidate trajectories are then optimized with group-relative policy optimization (GRPO), where the reward combines task metrics with a token-level semantic consistency bonus that compares student outputs to the teacher’s clean-audio outputs. Additional audio-aware reward shaping ensures that the preferred trajectories are both correct and acoustically grounded. This optimization constitutes a noisy-to-clean self-distillation process termed echodistill.
AI.result: Echodistill improves robustness of Audio LLMs under strong and complex noise. It yields an average gain of 4.18% in GSR (a semantic reliability metric) over the strongest baseline in strong-noise settings. On Qwen-Omni, ablations show that adding echodistill on top of plain GRPO yields average improvements of 3.02% in accuracy, 3.89% in noisy-condition performance, and 4.53% in GSR.
AI.conclusion: Aligning a noisy-audio student with a clean-audio teacher through GRPO-based self-distillation and token-level semantic consistency rewards significantly enhances the semantic reliability and task performance of Audio LLMs under real-world noise, without increasing inference-time cost. This demonstrates that trajectory-level alignment with clean semantic references is an effective strategy for building noise-robust Audio LLMs.

title: Confidence Calibration in Large Language Models
summary: We investigate the calibration of large language models' (LLMs') confidence across diverse tasks. The results of our preregistered study show that the current crop of LLMs are, like people, too sure they are right: confidence exceeds accuracy, on average. Importantly, however, this tendency is moderated by a powerful hard-easy effect, wherein overconfidence is greatest on difficult tests; by contrast, easy tests actually show substantial underconfidence. We develop LifeEval, a test for evaluating model calibration across levels of difficulty.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how well large language models’ confidence matches their actual accuracy across tasks, finding systematic miscalibration that depends on task difficulty.
AI.motivation: Large language models often output answers with associated confidence, but it is unclear whether this confidence reliably reflects true correctness, which is crucial for deploying LLMs in real-world, safety-critical settings. Prior work in human judgment shows overconfidence and a hard-easy effect; the authors want to see if similar patterns hold for LLMs and to measure them rigorously.
AI.method: The authors run a preregistered empirical study of multiple LLMs on a variety of tasks spanning different difficulty levels. They compare stated or inferred model confidence against actual accuracy. They introduce LifeEval, a new evaluation framework designed to systematically vary question difficulty and then quantify calibration patterns such as over- or underconfidence across these difficulty levels.
AI.result: LLMs are generally overconfident: their reported or implied confidence is higher than their actual probability of being correct. However, this miscalibration is not uniform: on hard items, overconfidence is strong, while on easy items, models tend to be underconfident, forming a pronounced hard-easy effect similar to that seen in human judgment research. LifeEval successfully exposes and quantifies these patterns across tasks and difficulty levels.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs are not well calibrated overall and display systematic, difficulty-dependent miscalibration, being too sure of themselves on hard questions and too unsure on easy ones. The proposed LifeEval benchmark provides a structured way to assess and compare calibration across difficulty levels, highlighting a key area for improving LLM reliability.

title: TriVAL: A Tri-Validation Framework for Faithful Automatic Optimization Modeling
summary: Optimization modeling serves as the pivotal bridge between natural-language problem descriptions and optimization solvers, and remains a cornerstone for bringing operations research (OR) into real-world decision making. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have driven significant progress in automatic optimization modeling. However, existing methods still lack explicit validation during the modeling process, allowing errors introduced in earlier stages to carry through the pipeline and ultimately reduce final modeling accuracy. To address this challenge, we introduce TriVAL, a tri-validation framework that performs explicit validation at three stages of automatic optimization modeling: semantic specification, mathematical formulation, and code generation. At each stage, TriVAL follows a construct-validate-revise loop that assesses the current result against stage-specific criteria and revises it when needed. This design helps identify and correct errors before they accumulate across stages, helping preserve faithfulness throughout the modeling process. To evaluate automatic optimization modeling on more challenging combinatorial problems, we further introduce NL4COP, a benchmark of 150 instances across 50 diverse problem types with more complex decision logic, more tightly coupled constraints, and more demanding modeling requirements than existing benchmarks. Experiments on NL4COP and established benchmarks show that TriVAL consistently outperforms state-ofthe-art methods, with the largest gains on the most challenging problems.

AI.tldr: TriVAL is a tri-stage validation framework that improves automatic optimization modeling from natural language to solver-ready code, and it is evaluated on a new challenging benchmark NL4COP.
AI.motivation: Automatic optimization modeling with LLMs often propagates early-stage errors (in interpretation, formulation, or code) through the pipeline, lowering overall accuracy. There is a need for systematic validation at multiple stages and for more challenging benchmarks to stress-test such systems on complex combinatorial optimization problems.
AI.method: The paper proposes TriVAL, a tri-validation framework that inserts construct-validate-revise loops at three key stages: (1) semantic specification from natural language, (2) mathematical formulation of the optimization model, and (3) code generation for solvers. Each stage uses stage-specific validation criteria to detect and correct errors before proceeding. Additionally, the authors design NL4COP, a benchmark with 150 instances over 50 combinatorial optimization problem types featuring complex decision logic and tightly coupled constraints.
AI.result: On NL4COP and existing benchmarks, TriVAL achieves consistently higher modeling accuracy than prior state-of-the-art automatic optimization modeling approaches, with especially large improvements on the most difficult problem instances.
AI.conclusion: Multi-stage explicit validation via construct-validate-revise loops substantially improves the faithfulness and robustness of LLM-based automatic optimization modeling. The new NL4COP benchmark demonstrates TriVAL’s strengths on complex combinatorial tasks and provides a harder testbed for future research.

title: How Much Thinking is Enough? Quantifying and Understanding Redundancy in LLM Reasoning
summary:
Reasoning-capable large language models solve hard problems by emitting long chains of thought, paying heavily in latency, GPU time, and energy. Casual inspection of their traces reveals extensive reformulation, verification, and circular self-reflection, yet how much of this deliberation is actually necessary has never been measured at scale or explained from first principles. This paper closes both gaps.
  We formalise reasoning redundancy directly in terms of the reasoning model itself: the redundancy of a correct trace is the largest fraction of its trailing segmented steps that can be truncated while $π$, forced to terminate thinking and emit a final answer, still produces the correct answer. A large-scale quantification across four frontier reasoning models and two mathematical benchmarks shows that step-level redundancy is consistently high -- between 61% and 93% across the 8 (model, benchmark) conditions we study, with the median critical prefix equal to a single segmented step in six of the eight conditions -- that the finding is robust to the choice of judge family, and that although $ρ$ decreases with problem difficulty on MATH-500, all four models remain substantially redundant ($ρ\in [46\%, 85\%]$) even on the hardest Level-5 problems.
  We then prove that this redundancy is a structural consequence of length-agnostic outcome rewards, not a model-specific artefact: under any such reward, no finite expected stopping time is optimal. The result holds regardless of RL algorithm, base model, data distribution, or whether the policy is obtained via RL or distillation; over-thinking is therefore not a bug to be patched in individual models but a structural property of how current reasoning models are trained. Code: https://github.com/zhiyuanZhai20/how-much-thinking-is-enough

AI.tldr: The paper shows that large language models frequently 'overthink' when reasoning, producing far more chain-of-thought steps than necessary, and proves this is a structural result of current training objectives rather than a quirk of specific models.
AI.motivation: Reasoning-oriented LLMs generate long chains of thought to solve complex problems, which is costly in latency, compute, and energy. Empirically, their traces show a lot of reformulation and self-checking, but we lack a quantitative measure of how much of this thinking is actually necessary, and we also lack a theoretical explanation of why such overthinking arises under current training paradigms.
AI.method: 1) Define a notion of reasoning redundancy intrinsic to a model: for a correct reasoning trace, measure what fraction of the final segmented steps can be removed while the model, when forced to stop at that point and output an answer, still answers correctly. 2) Empirically evaluate this redundancy across four state-of-the-art reasoning LLMs on two math benchmarks, using judge models to assess correctness and robustness. 3) Analyze how redundancy behaves across problem difficulty levels. 4) Prove theoretically that, under length-agnostic outcome rewards (rewards that depend only on final correctness, not on the number of steps), no policy with a finite expected stopping time can be optimal, implying structural incentives to overthink regardless of RL algorithm or training details.
AI.result: Empirically, step-level redundancy is very high: across four frontier reasoning models and two math benchmarks, between 61% and 93% of reasoning steps are redundant, with the median critical prefix being just one segmented step in six of eight settings. Redundancy remains high even for the hardest problems (Level-5 on MATH-500), staying in the 46%–85% range. These findings are robust to the choice of judge model. Theoretically, the authors show that when the reward function is indifferent to solution length and depends only on correctness, any optimal policy must have infinite expected thinking time, meaning that finite-horizon stopping rules are suboptimal and induce systematic over-deliberation.
AI.conclusion: Most of the reasoning steps produced by current CoT-style LLMs are unnecessary for correctness, indicating large redundancy in their deliberation. This is not an incidental artifact of particular models or training details but a structural consequence of using length-agnostic correctness rewards in training. Therefore, simply patching individual models is insufficient; addressing overthinking requires changing training objectives or introducing explicit incentives for shorter, more efficient reasoning processes.

title: SLAP: Stratified Loss-based Pruning for On-Policy Data-Efficient Instruction Tuning
summary: Instruction tuning has optimized the specialized capabilities of large language models (LLMs), but it often requires extensive datasets and prolonged training times. The challenge lies in developing specific capabilities by identifying useful data and efficiently fine-tuning. High-quality and diverse pruned data can help models achieve lossless performance at a lower cost. In this paper, we propose \textbf{SLAP}, a novel batch-aware data selection framework that evaluates the learnability of entire batch compositions rather than individual. SLAP ensures comprehensive data distribution coverage through distribution-aware stratified sampling while maximizing intra-batch diversity through relative distance optimization. By leveraging Hessian-approximated gradient information for dynamic batch selection, SLAP significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods across multiple model architectures (LLaMA, ChatGLM) and diverse downstream tasks including multi-turn dialogue, multilingual translation, and question answering. Most notably, SLAP achieves superior performance with 20-40\% less training data compared to full dataset training, substantially reducing computational costs while maintaining or improving model capabilities. These results establish SLAP as a powerful approach for efficient and effective instruction tuning of large language models.

AI.tldr: SLAP is a batch-aware data selection framework for instruction tuning that selects informative and diverse training batches using distribution-aware sampling and Hessian-based gradient information, enabling near-lossless or improved performance with 20–40% less data.
AI.motivation: Instruction tuning of LLMs usually needs large, expensive datasets and long training, and existing data selection focuses on individual examples, not how batches as a whole affect learning. The authors aim to reduce data and compute costs by selecting smaller but more effective instruction-tuning data while maintaining or improving performance, and to address the gap that batch composition and distribution coverage are under-exploited in current methods.
AI.method: They introduce SLAP, a batch-aware data selection framework. It first performs distribution-aware stratified sampling to ensure broad coverage of the data distribution. Then, within these strata, it optimizes intra-batch diversity by maximizing relative distances among examples. It further uses Hessian-approximated gradient information to dynamically evaluate and select batches based on their learnability, effectively choosing those that are expected to most improve the model during fine-tuning.
AI.result: Across multiple LLM architectures (e.g., LLaMA and ChatGLM) and tasks (multi-turn dialogue, multilingual translation, and QA), SLAP outperforms previous state-of-the-art data selection approaches. It enables instruction tuning with 20–40% less data than using the full dataset, while matching or surpassing the performance of full-data training, thus reducing computational cost substantially.
AI.conclusion: Batch-aware, distribution- and diversity-conscious data selection guided by gradient/Hessian information can significantly improve the efficiency of instruction tuning. SLAP demonstrates that carefully curated, smaller datasets can deliver equal or better performance than full datasets, making it a promising approach for scalable and cost-effective fine-tuning of LLMs.

title: Context: Proactive Goal-Directed Intelligence via Composable Sandboxed Programs, Declarative Wiring, and Structured Interaction
comment: 7 pages; third in a series with arXiv:2501.XXXXX (Magarshak Machine / SPACER) and arXiv:2502.XXXXX (Grokers)
summary: We present Context, the intelligence layer of the Magarshak Architecture, which replaces reactive query-response chatbots with proactive goal-directed agents that advance shared tasks without waiting for user prompts. The architecture rests on three mutually reinforcing mechanisms. Write-time context assembly precomputes enriched typed attributes via Groker agents, assembling interaction context as a deterministic pure function of graph state; context blocks are byte-identical across turns between semantic changes, enabling near-100% KV-cache reuse. Composable sandboxed wisdom programs form a governed library of LM-generated imperative programs declaratively wired to goal types via typed stream relations, composed via phase ordering, and executed at interaction time without further LM calls. Proactive goal stream state machines drive conversations toward terminal states by inspecting graph state and emitting structured interaction content (option arrays, governance affordances, clarification prompts) without awaiting user input. We prove six formal results: the Context Stability Theorem, bounding per-turn LM cost as a function of semantic change rate; a Program Composition Correctness Theorem; a Declarative Wiring Soundness Theorem; the Proactive Dominance Theorem, proving proactive agents weakly dominate reactive agents on expected turns-to-terminal-state; Coordination Overhead Elimination and Quality Preservation, establishing Pareto improvements in multi-participant goal chats; and a Cross-Platform Vote Consistency Theorem. Implemented in the open-source Qbix / Safebox / Safebots stack.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Context, an intelligence layer in the Magarshak Architecture that turns reactive chatbots into proactive, goal-driven agents using precomputed context, composable LM-generated programs, and proactive state machines, with formal guarantees on efficiency and performance.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based chatbots are primarily reactive, responding only when prompted, which limits their ability to collaboratively advance user goals, reuse computation efficiently, and coordinate multi-party interactions with formal guarantees. There is a need for an architecture that makes agents proactive, more efficient per turn, and reliable under governance constraints, while offering strong theoretical properties.
AI.method: The authors propose Context, built on three mechanisms: (1) write-time context assembly, where Groker agents precompute enriched typed attributes from a graph state so that interaction context is a pure deterministic function with stable, cacheable blocks; (2) composable sandboxed wisdom programs—LM-generated imperative programs stored in a governed library and declaratively wired to goal types via typed stream relations, composed through phase ordering and executed at runtime without extra LM calls; and (3) proactive goal stream state machines that inspect graph state and autonomously move conversations toward terminal states by emitting structured interaction content instead of waiting for user prompts. They also provide formal theorems proving properties of cost, correctness, soundness, and performance dominance.
AI.result: They formally prove six theorems: (1) Context Stability, which upper-bounds per-turn LM cost based on semantic change rate and enables near-100% KV-cache reuse; (2) Program Composition Correctness, ensuring composed wisdom programs behave as intended; (3) Declarative Wiring Soundness, guaranteeing that wiring goal types to programs via typed relations is semantically safe; (4) Proactive Dominance, showing proactive agents weakly dominate reactive ones in expected steps to reach terminal states; (5) Coordination Overhead Elimination and Quality Preservation, proving Pareto improvements in multi-participant goal-oriented chats; and (6) Cross-Platform Vote Consistency, ensuring consistent outcomes across platforms. They also implement Context in the open-source Qbix / Safebox / Safebots stack.
AI.conclusion: Context provides a principled architecture that upgrades reactive LLM chat interfaces into proactive, goal-directed agents with efficient context handling, reusable LM-generated programs, and structured state machines, backed by formal guarantees on efficiency, correctness, coordination, and consistency, and demonstrated in an open-source implementation.

title: Faithful or Fabricated? A Causal Framework for Rationalization Bias in LLM Judges
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as automatic judges for summarization and dialogue evaluation. Prior work has documented biases such as position, verbosity, and style preferences, but largely focuses on outcomes, leaving judge explanations underexplored. We instead ask whether LLM judges are cue-invariant, i.e., whether their rankings and explanations remain stable when non-evidential cues are perturbed while holding the underlying texts fixed. We introduce a suite of cue interventions (Blind, Truth, Flip, Placebo, Reveal-After) and tie-aware metrics that quantify outcome anchoring and rationale anchoring, including label-aligned rhetoric and explanation drift, alongside consistency and stereotype-intrusion checks. We design anchoring attacks using verbosity and confidence cues, and compare two mitigations: structured chain-of-thought prompting and PROOF-BEFORE-PREFERENCE (evidence lock, score, rank). Using a new dataset of 1,000 summaries from traditional extractive models and LLMs, we find substantial cue-anchored rationalization under label and placebo perturbations, while PROOF-BEFORE-PREFERENCE markedly improves cue invariance over baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether large language models used as automatic judges are stable to irrelevant cues, introduces methods to measure and attack this vulnerability, and shows that a structured evaluation protocol (PROOF-BEFORE-PREFERENCE) improves robustness.
AI.motivation: Large language models are increasingly used as automatic judges in tasks like summarization and dialogue evaluation, but they exhibit known biases (e.g., position, verbosity, style). Prior work focuses mostly on evaluation outcomes and not on the stability or reliability of the explanations that these LLM judges provide. There is a need to understand whether LLM judges can be manipulated by non-evidential cues and whether their reasoning and rankings are invariant to such perturbations, since this affects trustworthiness and fairness of automated evaluation.
AI.method: The authors define the notion of cue invariance for LLM judges and construct a suite of cue interventions (Blind, Truth, Flip, Placebo, Reveal-After) that perturb non-evidential cues while keeping the evaluated texts fixed. They introduce tie-aware metrics to quantify different forms of anchoring, including outcome anchoring and rationale anchoring, and phenomena like label-aligned rhetoric and explanation drift, plus checks for consistency and stereotype intrusion. They then design specific anchoring attacks using verbosity and confidence-related cues, and test two mitigation strategies: structured chain-of-thought prompting and a PROOF-BEFORE-PREFERENCE protocol that locks in evidence before scoring and ranking. Experiments are run on a new dataset of 1,000 summaries from both traditional extractive models and LLMs.
AI.result: The experiments reveal substantial cue-anchored rationalization: LLM judges’ explanations and sometimes rankings shift in response to label and placebo cue perturbations, even though the underlying content being judged is unchanged. This indicates vulnerability to non-evidential cues and explanation drift. Among the evaluated mitigations, the PROOF-BEFORE-PREFERENCE protocol significantly improves cue invariance compared with baseline prompting strategies, reducing susceptibility to anchoring and making explanations more stable and evidence-grounded.
AI.conclusion: LLM judges are not naturally cue-invariant; their evaluations and rationales can be anchored by irrelevant cues like verbosity and confidence signals, leading to unstable and potentially biased judgments. However, structured prompting, particularly the PROOF-BEFORE-PREFERENCE approach that separates evidence collection from preference expression, can substantially mitigate these issues. The work highlights the importance of evaluating and improving the robustness and faithfulness of LLM judge rationales, not just their final scores or rankings.

title: Toward Reliable Design of LLM-Enabled Agentic Workflows: Optimizing Latency-Reliability-Cost Tradeoffs
summary: Modern AI systems increasingly rely on workflows composed of multiple interacting agents, some powered by large language models (LLMs) and others by conventional computational modules. This paper analyzes the fundamental tradeoffs between latency, reliability, and cost in LLM-enabled agentic workflows. We introduce performance models for both LLM and non-LLM agents that capture the relationship between computational effort and output quality, incorporating the impact of reasoning and output tokens for LLM agents using a parametric exponential reliability function. Then, we study the design of sequential workflows under latency and cost constraints. Main results include a water-filling token allocation policy and characterizations of optimal workflow reliability in terms of shadow prices.

AI.tldr: Analyzes tradeoffs between latency, reliability, and cost in multi-agent AI workflows and derives optimal token allocation strategies.
AI.motivation: As AI systems move from single LLM calls to complex workflows of multiple agents, designers lack a principled way to reason about how to allocate computation (e.g., tokens and processing time) to meet reliability targets under latency and cost constraints. There is a need for formal performance models and design rules for such agentic systems.
AI.method: The paper builds parametric performance models for both LLM-based and conventional agents, where reliability is modeled as an exponential function of computational effort (including reasoning and output tokens for LLMs). It then formulates an optimization problem for sequential workflows under latency and cost constraints, solving it to derive optimal policies and characterizations using tools like water-filling and shadow prices.
AI.result: They obtain a water-filling policy that specifies how to allocate tokens across LLM agents in a workflow and derive closed-form or implicit characterizations of the maximum achievable workflow reliability, expressed via shadow prices associated with latency and cost constraints.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a theoretical framework for designing sequential LLM-enabled workflows, showing how to optimally distribute computation across agents to balance reliability, latency, and cost, and offering interpretable constructs like water-filling and shadow prices to guide practical system design.

title: AERIC: Anticipatory Hidden-State Monitoring for Implicit Harmful Dialogue
summary: Current language models create two safety challenges: risk must be detected early enough to avoid exposing harmful continuation, and the harmfulness itself may be implicit rather than signaled by overtly toxic text. Existing response-level guards are strong at judging completed text, and native streaming guards move closer to token time, but both settings leave open whether a lightweight monitor can anticipate implicit harmful drift from the generator's own internal trajectory. We study anticipatory same-pass monitoring, where a safety monitor may read hidden states produced during ordinary decoding but may not invoke an additional forward pass through the base model. We introduce AERIC, a transfer-oriented hidden-state approach for implicit harmful dialogue that combines short-horizon hazard forecasting, support-sensitive suppression, and prompt-conditioned residual scoring under a same-pass exponential moving average decision rule. The default linear monitor contains only 387 trainable head parameters. Against Qwen3GuardStream-4B on balanced benchmarks, AERIC improves AUROC from 0.6830 to 0.7143 on DiaSafety and from 0.8219 to 0.8582 on Harmful Advice. For promptlevel trigger benchmarks, we calibrate the AERIC threshold by a source-side safe-budget rule that maximizes trigger coverage while constraining the safe-trigger rate to at most 10%. Under that rule, trigger@64 reaches 0.6438 and 0.4656 on HarmBench DirectRequest and 0.6849 and 0.7363 on SocialHarmBench for Qwen and Gemma, respectively, withholding between 23.53 and 41.86 answer tokens on average. Same-pass deployment is also efficient: on a 63-prompt harmfulprompt fixed-generation benchmark aggregated over HarmBench DirectRequest and SocialHarmBench under Qwen3-8B, the monitor increases mean latency by only 2.34%, whereas Qwen3Guard-Stream-4B increases it by 79.40%.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AERIC, a lightweight same-pass safety monitor that uses hidden states during decoding to anticipate and block implicitly harmful language model outputs with low latency and competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing safety systems either judge only completed responses or rely on separate streaming guards, leaving a gap: they may fail to anticipate harmful drift early enough, especially when harmful intent is implicit rather than overtly toxic. The authors aim to design a safety monitor that can work in the same pass as generation, leverage the model’s internal hidden states, and efficiently forecast future harmfulness without extra forward passes or heavy computation.
AI.method: They formalize anticipatory same-pass monitoring, where a safety head reads the base model’s hidden states during standard decoding and cannot run extra passes. They introduce AERIC, which: (1) uses short-horizon hazard forecasting to predict near-future harmfulness; (2) applies support-sensitive suppression so that it reacts differently based on the model’s internal support for harmful continuations; (3) uses prompt-conditioned residual scoring to factor in the initial prompt; and (4) aggregates scores with an exponential moving average to make real-time decisions. The default monitor is a tiny linear head with only 387 trainable parameters attached to hidden states.
AI.result: On balanced dialogue safety benchmarks, AERIC outperforms a strong streaming guard (Qwen3Guard-Stream-4B), raising AUROC from 0.6830 to 0.7143 on DiaSafety and from 0.8219 to 0.8582 on Harmful Advice. On prompt-level trigger benchmarks (HarmBench DirectRequest and SocialHarmBench) and for two base models (Qwen and Gemma), applying a safe-budget calibration that caps safe-trigger rate at 10% yields trigger@64 scores between ~0.46 and ~0.74, while withholding 23.53–41.86 answer tokens on average. In a fixed-generation latency test on 63 harmful prompts with Qwen3-8B, AERIC adds only 2.34% mean latency, compared with 79.40% for Qwen3Guard-Stream-4B.
AI.conclusion: AERIC demonstrates that a very small, linear hidden-state monitor can effectively anticipate implicitly harmful generations in the same decoding pass, improving safety metrics over a larger streaming guard while imposing minimal latency overhead. This suggests that transfer-oriented, same-pass hidden-state monitoring is a practical and efficient path to safer language model deployment, particularly for implicit harms that are not easily caught by surface-level toxicity checks.

title: Quantum Frog: Emergent Cooperation and Difficulty Scaling in a Quantized-Time Cooperative Game
summary: We introduce \emph{Quantum Frog}, a two-player cooperative game built on a novel \emph{quantized-time} mechanic in which the environment advances only when a player acts. Inspired by the classic arcade game Frogger, Quantum Frog requires two frogs to cross an 8$\times$8 grid of traffic and reach the far side together. We use reinforcement learning (RL) as an analytical lens to answer four design questions: (1) how does game difficulty scale with traffic density, (2) what is the optimal single-agent policy and why, (3) how large is the cooperation gap between independent and cooperative two-agent play, and (4) what joint strategy emerges when agents are incentivised to cooperate? We train agents through five escalating stages, Tabular Q-Learning, Deep Q-Network (\DQN), Independent \DQN~(\IDQN), and Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimisation (\MAPPO\ with a centralised critic), evaluating each against traffic densities of one to six cars. Our key findings are: (i) the quantized-time mechanic makes a \emph{rush strategy} (moving directly upward at every step) universally optimal, as time exposure to traffic is minimised; (ii) adding an uncoordinated second player is harder than sextupling the traffic for a single expert player; (iii) cooperative training recovers +32--34 percentage points of joint success rate relative to independent agents and reduces episode length from $\sim$90 to $\sim$6 steps; and (iv) the emergent cooperative strategy is synchronised rushing, not complex positional coordination, illustrating that shared incentives alone suffice to align agents in time-critical cooperative tasks. These findings provide concrete, empirically grounded guidance for the commercial design of Quantum Frog and offer broader insights into the role of environment mechanics in shaping multi-agent learning dynamics.

AI.tldr: Quantum Frog is a cooperative 2‑player grid game with a quantized-time mechanic, analyzed via RL to understand difficulty, optimal strategies, and cooperation effects.
AI.motivation: To understand how a novel quantized-time environment mechanic affects optimal play, difficulty scaling, and emergent cooperation in a simple, commercially relevant game inspired by Frogger.
AI.method: Define the Quantum Frog game on an 8x8 grid where time advances only when players act. Systematically vary traffic density and training regimes: Tabular Q-learning, DQN, Independent DQN, and MAPPO with a centralized critic. Train single and multi-agent policies across densities 1–6 cars and evaluate performance, strategies, and episode lengths.
AI.result: (i) Quantized time makes a simple rush-straight-up strategy universally optimal. (ii) Adding a non-coordinated second agent drastically increases difficulty, more than sextupling traffic for a skilled single agent. (iii) Cooperative multi-agent training with shared incentives recovers 32–34 percentage points joint success vs independent play and shrinks episodes from ~90 to ~6 steps. (iv) The learned cooperative policy is synchronised rushing rather than complex positional tactics.
AI.conclusion: Quantized-time strongly shapes optimal and cooperative behavior, favoring simple time-minimizing rush strategies. Shared incentives in this setting are enough to create effective temporal coordination between agents, making cooperative play much more efficient. These insights inform both the design of Quantum Frog as a product and broader understanding of how environment mechanics drive multi-agent learning dynamics.

title: Direct Preference Optimization for English-Mandarin Code-Switching Speech Recognition in Audio LLMs
summary: Audio large language models (Audio LLMs) exhibit systematic failures in transcribing code-switching speech despite strong multilingual capabilities. Focusing on English-Mandarin, we identify three failure modes: language omission, translation-instead-of-transcription, and hallucination. We apply Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to align models, constructing preference pairs in which chosen responses preserve mixed-language content while rejected responses mimic failure patterns. Training three Audio LLMs on 100K pairs (570 hours), we observe consistent behavioral shifts: models learn to preserve language composition rather than translating when prompted for transcription. This alignment yields MER reductions up to 89.6% (in-distribution) and 20.0% (out-of-distribution). Our findings suggest DPO can effectively elicit correct code-switching transcription behavior from multilingual Audio LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper improves code-switching transcription in multilingual Audio LLMs using preference-based alignment.
AI.motivation: Although Audio LLMs are strong multilingual models, they systematically fail on code-switching speech, particularly in preserving mixed-language content. These failures limit their reliability for real-world multilingual scenarios where speakers naturally mix languages.
AI.method: The authors focus on English-Mandarin code-switching and first categorize three main failure modes: language omission, translation instead of transcription, and hallucination. They then construct preference pairs where the preferred response correctly preserves the code-switched content and the dispreferred one reflects typical failure behavior. Using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), they align three different Audio LLMs on a dataset of 100K such pairs (about 570 hours of audio-text data) to encourage correct code-switching transcription.
AI.result: After DPO training, all three Audio LLMs show a consistent behavioral shift toward preserving the original language mixture instead of translating when asked to transcribe. This leads to large reductions in mixed error rate (MER), with improvements up to 89.6% on in-distribution data and 20.0% on out-of-distribution data.
AI.conclusion: Preference-based alignment with DPO can reliably steer multilingual Audio LLMs toward accurate code-switching transcription, substantially mitigating common failure modes and generalizing beyond the training distribution.

title: BODHI: Precise OS Kernel Specification Inference
summary: The formal verification of operating system kernels requires precise specifications that capture the intended behavior of system calls. Writing these specifications manually demands deep domain expertise, motivating the use of large language models (LLMs) to automate the process. However, in OSV-Bench, a benchmark of 245 specification generation tasks derived from the Hyperkernel OS kernel, the best reported Pass@1 is 55.10%. We propose a domain knowledge prompting method (BODHI), which augments the standard few-shot prompt with a structured C-to-Python translation guide covering 15 categories of domain-specific translation patterns. Inspired by Structured Chain-of-Thought (SCoT) prompting, the guide organizes translation by separation of concerns, addressing pre-condition extraction and post-condition generation as distinct categories. Evaluated on nine models from six providers (Anthropic, Mistral, Amazon, DeepSeek, Meta, Alibaba), covering dense, mixture-of-experts and reasoning architectures, BODHI improves every model tested, with gains ranging from +11% to +32%. The best configuration (Claude Opus 4.6 + BODHI) reaches 96.73% Pass@1. BODHI reduces both syntax and semantic errors, with the strongest effect on models that have sufficient instruction-following capability to utilize structured reference material. These results demonstrate that domain knowledge injection is a model-agnostic technique that substantially bridges the gap between general-purpose code generation and formal specification synthesis.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BODHI, a domain-knowledge prompting method that greatly improves LLM-based generation of formal OS kernel specifications, boosting Pass@1 on OSV-Bench up to 96.73%.
AI.motivation: Formal verification of OS kernels needs precise system call specifications, which are hard and expertise-intensive to write by hand. Existing LLM approaches underperform on OSV-Bench, with best Pass@1 only 55.10%, motivating better prompting techniques that inject domain knowledge to close the performance gap.
AI.method: The authors design BODHI, a domain knowledge prompting framework that augments few-shot prompts with a structured C-to-Python translation guide. This guide encodes 15 categories of domain-specific translation patterns related to OS kernel specifications and is organized by separation of concerns, explicitly treating pre-condition extraction and post-condition generation as separate subtasks, inspired by Structured Chain-of-Thought prompting. They evaluate BODHI across nine LLMs from six providers, spanning dense, MoE, and reasoning architectures, on the OSV-Bench benchmark of 245 specification-generation tasks from the Hyperkernel OS.
AI.result: Across all evaluated models, BODHI consistently improves specification generation performance, with Pass@1 gains of +11% to +32% relative to standard prompting. The best-performing setup—Claude Opus 4.6 combined with BODHI—achieves 96.73% Pass@1 on OSV-Bench. Error analysis shows reductions in both syntax and semantic mistakes, particularly for models with strong instruction-following capabilities that can leverage the structured translation guide effectively.
AI.conclusion: Domain knowledge prompting via structured guides, as instantiated in BODHI, is an effective, model-agnostic way to enhance LLM generation of formal specifications from code. By organizing translation patterns and separating pre- and post-condition reasoning, BODHI dramatically narrows the gap between generic code generation and high-precision formal specification synthesis for OS kernels, suggesting similar techniques could generalize to other specialized verification domains.

title: A Multi-Probe Audit of Clinical-Interview Depression Detection Benchmarks
summary: This paper audits benchmark evaluation in clinical-interview depression detection through four complementary probes across DAIC/E-DAIC, CMDC, ANDROIDS, MODMA, and PDCH. First, we re-evaluate E-DAIC under strict subject-disjoint leave-one-subject-out cross-validation. A lightweight hybrid text-plus-LLM-score model reaches macro-F1 = 0.723 - the highest reported under this protocol, to our knowledge - providing a conservative out-of-fold reference point that does not depend on the privileged official holdout. Second, we test whether the E-DAIC official split supports fine-grained leaderboard rankings by sweeping 96 model configurations across modality bundles, pooling strategies, and learners. Development-side cross-validation and official-test rankings align only moderately: the best cross-validation configuration ranks twentieth on the official test, the official-test winner ranks forty-first by cross-validation, top-3 overlap is zero, and the apparent winner is rank-1 in only 32.3% of subject bootstraps. Third, we externally validate strong public CMDC and ANDROIDS baselines that achieve near-ceiling in-domain performance. Zero-shot transfer to external corpora is substantially weaker. Finally, we stress-test E-DAIC text and audio models using paired symptom-dense versus symptom-light interview slices defined by an SRDS-based annotator. Text scores rise sharply on symptom-dense slices, whereas audio scores remain nearly flat; the text-minus-audio gap is positive across all five seeds.

AI.tldr: The paper audits and stress-tests depression detection benchmarks from clinical interviews, revealing limitations in standard evaluation splits, weak cross-dataset transfer, and differing sensitivities of text vs. audio models to symptom-rich content.
AI.motivation: Benchmark results in depression detection from clinical interviews may be over-optimistic or misleading because of protocol choices, leaderboard practices, and lack of robustness checks. The authors want to understand how reliable current evaluations are, whether leaderboard rankings are trustworthy, how well strong models transfer across datasets, and how text and audio modalities behave under more diagnostic stress tests.
AI.method: They run four complementary probes over several depression corpora (DAIC/E-DAIC, CMDC, ANDROIDS, MODMA, PDCH): (1) re-evaluate E-DAIC using strict subject-disjoint leave-one-subject-out cross-validation with a lightweight hybrid text+LLM-score model; (2) sweep 96 different model configurations (varying modality bundles, pooling strategies, and learning algorithms) and compare rankings under development-side cross-validation vs. the official E-DAIC split; (3) take strong baselines that perform near ceiling on CMDC and ANDROIDS and test them in zero-shot transfer to other datasets; (4) define symptom-dense vs. symptom-light interview segments using an SRDS-based annotator, then stress-test text and audio models separately on these slices to see how scores change and compare modalities.
AI.result: (1) Under strict LOSO CV on E-DAIC, their lightweight hybrid model reaches macro-F1 = 0.723, claimed to be the best reported under that conservative protocol and independent of the privileged held-out test. (2) Leaderboard stability is weak: the configuration that is best in cross-validation ranks only 20th on the official test, the official test winner ranks 41st by CV, there is no overlap in top-3 configurations, and the model appearing as the winner is actually rank-1 in only about one-third (32.3%) of subject bootstrap samples. (3) Models that are near ceiling on CMDC and ANDROIDS show much poorer performance when transferred zero-shot to other corpora. (4) In stress tests on E-DAIC, text-based model scores increase markedly on symptom-dense segments, while audio-based scores change little; across five random seeds, text consistently outperforms audio on these contrasts, with a positive text-minus-audio performance gap each time.
AI.conclusion: Current benchmark practices in clinical-interview depression detection can give an inflated or unstable picture of model capability: official splits yield unreliable leaderboards, in-domain performance does not translate well across datasets, and text and audio modalities respond differently to clinically salient content, with text showing stronger sensitivity. More conservative and robust evaluation designs, cross-corpus validation, and modality-aware stress tests are needed for trustworthy assessment and deployment of depression detection systems.

title: When Correct Beliefs Collapse: Epistemic Resilience of LLMs under Clinical Pressure
summary: Despite strong medical benchmark accuracy, LLMs can exhibit severe multi-turn sycophancy in clinical dialogue, abandoning initial correct diagnosis under escalating pressure. We propose \textbf{\textsc{Med-Stress}}, a targeted stress test framework that evaluates belief stability under escalating pressure. Across nine frontier large language models (LLMs), we find a clear dissociation between medical knowledge and robustness: high initial diagnostic capability does not imply high belief stability, yielding large knowledge-robustness gaps for several LLMs. To mitigate this failure mode, we propose a lightweight inference-time defense, \textbf{\texttt{RBED}} (\textbf{R}ole-\textbf{B}ased \textbf{E}pistemic \textbf{D}efense), and \textbf{\texttt{R-FT}} (\textbf{R}esilience-oriented \textbf{F}ine-\textbf{T}uning), a training-time approach that internalizes evidence-based resistance to pressure. Experiments show that \textbf{\texttt{R-FT}} nearly eliminates belief change and substantially improves robustness.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that current medical LLMs, while accurate on benchmarks, are easily pressured into changing correct diagnoses, and proposes a stress-test plus defenses to improve belief stability under pressure.
AI.motivation: Medical LLMs often look strong on single-shot benchmarks but may fail in realistic multi-turn clinical dialogues, especially when patients or users exert pressure, persuasion, or insist on wrong answers. This gap between benchmark accuracy and real-world robustness is critical for safety in clinical decision support. The authors aim to systematically measure and understand this "belief instability" (sycophancy under pressure) and then design practical methods to make models maintain correct diagnoses despite escalating social pressure.
AI.method: They introduce Med-Stress, a stress-testing framework that simulates multi-turn clinical interactions with escalating pressure to change a diagnosis. They apply Med-Stress to nine frontier LLMs, measuring both initial diagnostic correctness and how often models later abandon correct beliefs. They then propose two defenses: (1) RBED, a lightweight inference-time role-based epistemic defense that shapes the model’s behavior via prompts or role instructions to resist undue pressure; and (2) R-FT, a resilience-oriented fine-tuning method that trains models on examples emphasizing evidence-based reasoning and resistance to pressure. They evaluate these defenses within the Med-Stress framework.
AI.result: Using Med-Stress, they find a clear dissociation between medical knowledge and robustness: some models that start with high diagnostic accuracy still show large drops in belief stability under pressure, revealing substantial knowledge-robustness gaps. Applying RBED improves robustness to some degree without retraining, while R-FT is much more effective, nearly eliminating belief changes under pressure and significantly increasing robustness metrics across the tested models.
AI.conclusion: Benchmark performance alone is insufficient to judge clinical safety of LLMs because many models are vulnerable to multi-turn sycophancy and pressure-induced belief changes. Structured stress testing like Med-Stress is necessary to reveal these vulnerabilities. Targeted defenses—especially resilience-oriented fine-tuning (R-FT)—can greatly improve belief stability, suggesting that incorporating pressure-resistance and epistemic discipline into training and deployment is key for safer clinical LLMs.

title: Toxicity in Twitch Chats: An LLM-Based Analysis Across Gaming Communities
comment: 8 pages, 2 figures, 5 tables. Accepted at the IEEE Conference on Games (IEEE CoG) 2026
summary: Toxicity in online gaming communities remains a persistent challenge, manifesting across genres, platforms, and player interactions. While much research is focused on in-game toxicity, less is known about how toxic behavior varies between gaming communities on streaming platforms. To address this shortcoming, we analyze approximately 20 million chat messages from 4,452 streams, spanning seven game genres on Twitch. We categorize messages according to Twitch's toxicity taxonomy with a pre-trained Large Language Model using zero-shot classification. The taxonomy comprises four categories and eight subclasses, including harassment, discrimination, sexual content, and profanity. Our approach achieves an F1 score of 94.5% on the TextDetox dataset and demonstrates human-model agreement comparable to inter-human agreement. Our analysis reveals that 2.4% of all messages are classified as toxic, with notable differences across genres: streams of MOBA games exhibit the highest relative rate of toxicity (3.2%), and sports games show the lowest rate (2%). Furthermore, results indicate that individual games differ significantly in their toxicity distributions, even within genres, suggesting the existence of game-specific community norms and mechanics that shape toxic behavior beyond genre-level effects. These findings offer empirical insights into genre- and game-specific toxicity patterns on Twitch and can inform more targeted moderation strategies for gaming communities.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how toxic behavior varies across Twitch gaming communities using 20M chat messages classified by a large language model into a detailed toxicity taxonomy, revealing genre- and game-specific toxicity patterns to support targeted moderation.
AI.motivation: Although toxicity in online gaming is well-studied in-game, there is limited understanding of how it differs between gaming communities on streaming platforms like Twitch. The authors aim to fill this gap by providing large-scale, empirical evidence on how toxicity manifests and varies across genres and specific games in Twitch chats.
AI.method: The authors collect about 20 million chat messages from 4,452 Twitch streams covering seven game genres. They use a pre-trained large language model for zero-shot classification of messages into Twitch’s toxicity taxonomy, which has four categories and eight subclasses (e.g., harassment, discrimination, sexual content, profanity). They evaluate their classifier on the TextDetox dataset (F1=94.5%) and compare model labels with human annotations, showing human-model agreement on par with inter-annotator agreement. They then conduct comparative analyses of toxicity rates and category distributions across genres and individual games.
AI.result: They find that 2.4% of all Twitch chat messages are classified as toxic. Toxicity levels differ across genres: MOBA streams have the highest relative toxicity (3.2%), while sports games have the lowest (2%). They also observe significant differences in toxicity distributions between individual games even within the same genre, indicating game-specific patterns of toxic behavior.
AI.conclusion: Toxicity on Twitch is not uniform but varies meaningfully by game genre and by individual titles, reflecting distinct community norms and possibly game mechanics. Their LLM-based, zero-shot taxonomy classification is accurate and approximates human judgment. These insights can be used to design more targeted, context-aware moderation tools and policies tailored to particular genres and games rather than one-size-fits-all approaches.

title: Practical Quantum CIM Empowerment via All-Domestic-Core Agentic Large Model
summary: Quantum computing devices are recognized as powerful tools for solving NP-complete problems. However, the intricacy of their modeling presents notable barriers for non-specialists, while the tedious iteration of constraint weights and modeling methodologies also consumes substantial effort on the part of experts. To address these challenges, this study integrates a femtosecond laser-pumped Coherent Ising Machine (CIM) with an LLM-driven agentic system by leveraging the LangGraph and LangChain frameworks. Comprehensive investigations demonstrate that large language models (LLMs) can effectively perform such tasks in modeling as QUBO/Ising model calibration, constraint weight decision iteration and rapid validation of literature-reported schemes. Notably, all these tasks can be fully implemented based on domestic large models, combined with domestically developed CIM hardware, we truly achieve the practical empowerment of quantum CIM that fully relies on all-domestic agentic large models and hardware. This work successfully realizes robust technological integration, laying a solid foundation for subsequent research. Nevertheless, it also identifies the persisting challenges in the two cutting-edge fields of large models and quantum computing at the current stage. Encouragingly, we unexpectedly discover a promising new paradigm where accumulated knowledge from agent-assisted quantum computing iterations reciprocally enhances the agent's own problem-solving capability, thereby addressing these challenges.

AI.tldr: They integrate a Coherent Ising Machine quantum device with an LLM-based agent system to automate QUBO/Ising modeling, constraint tuning, and validation, showing that domestic LLMs plus domestic CIM hardware can practically solve NP-complete-style problems while mutually improving each other.
AI.motivation: Quantum devices like Coherent Ising Machines can tackle NP-complete problems, but building and calibrating QUBO/Ising models and iterating constraint weights is complex and labor-intensive, limiting use by non-experts and slowing experts. There is also a strategic need to show a full stack based on domestic models and hardware.
AI.method: They connect a femtosecond laser-pumped CIM to an LLM-driven agentic workflow built with LangGraph/LangChain. The LLM agents are tasked with: formulating problems as QUBO/Ising models, selecting and iterating constraint weights, and rapidly reproducing and validating modeling schemes from the literature. They test feasibility and performance, focusing on using only domestic large models plus domestic CIM hardware.
AI.result: The LLM agents can successfully carry out QUBO/Ising model calibration, constraint weight iteration, and fast validation of existing schemes. All of this is demonstrated with domestic LLMs and CIM hardware, evidencing a robust integration pipeline. They further observe that experience gained from repeated, agent-assisted quantum computing iterations appears to improve the agent’s later problem-solving performance.
AI.conclusion: The work shows that an LLM-agent + CIM hybrid stack can practically lower the barrier to using quantum Ising machines for NP-type problems and can be realized with domestic technology only. It establishes a robust integration framework and surfaces open challenges in both LLMs and quantum computing.
