Importantly, it hints at a virtuous feedback loop where knowledge accumulated through agent-assisted quantum runs enhances the LLM agent’s own capabilities, suggesting a new paradigm for co-evolution of AI agents and quantum hardware.

title: CUNY at CLPsych 2026: A Pipeline Approach to Classification and Summarization of Mental Health Changes
summary: We describe our submission to the CLPsych~2026 Shared Task on capturing and characterizing mental health changes through social media timeline dynamics. To infer the dominant self-states in posts (Tasks 1.1 and 1.2), we ensemble in-context learning of three open-weight large language models using majority voting. For predicting moments of change in a timeline (Task~2), we train supervised classifiers on features derived from Task~1.1 predictions. To summarize the patterns of mood dynamics and their progression over time within a timeline (Task 3.1), we augment in-context example labels predicted by upstream systems (Tasks 1.1, 1.2, and 2), yielding performance gains over zero-shot and unaugmented in-context learning baselines. Our submission ranked first on Task~1.1, fourth on Task~1.2, fourth on Task~2, and third on Task~3.1.\footnote{The source code for the experiments is available at https://github.com/amirzia/clpsych26-cuny

AI.tldr: The paper presents a multi-task system for the CLPsych 2026 Shared Task that uses ensembles of open-weight LLMs and supervised classifiers to detect self-states, change points, and summarize mood dynamics from social media timelines, achieving top-tier rankings across tasks.
AI.motivation: Mental health changes can be reflected in social media timelines, but automatically capturing self-states, change points, and higher-level mood dynamics is challenging. The shared task provides a benchmark, and the authors aim to build an effective, largely LLM-based system that can robustly handle multiple related subtasks while leveraging open-weight models and in-context learning instead of relying solely on heavy supervised labeling.
AI.method: For Tasks 1.1 and 1.2 (inferring dominant self-states in posts), the authors use an ensemble of three open-weight large language models prompted via in-context learning and combine their predictions with majority voting. For Task 2 (predicting moments of change in a timeline), they train supervised classifiers that use features derived from the Task 1.1 self-state predictions. For Task 3.1 (summarizing mood dynamics and their temporal progression), they perform in-context learning again, but augment the prompt with labels predicted by upstream systems (Tasks 1.1, 1.2, and 2), effectively chaining earlier predictions to condition the summarization model. They compare these designs against zero-shot and standard in-context baselines.
AI.result: Their system ranks first on Task 1.1, fourth on Task 1.2, fourth on Task 2, and third on Task 3.1 in the CLPsych 2026 Shared Task leaderboard. They also observe that augmenting in-context examples with upstream predicted labels for Task 3.1 leads to measurable performance gains over both zero-shot and non-augmented in-context learning baselines.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that ensembles of open-weight LLMs with majority voting can achieve state-of-the-art performance on self-state inference from social media, and that using these predictions as features supports successful change-point detection. Moreover, feeding upstream predictions into downstream in-context learning prompts improves the modeling of mood dynamics over time. The overall architecture—combining LLM ensembling, supervised classifiers, and prompt augmentation—performs competitively across all tasks in the CLPsych 2026 Shared Task.

title: Operationalizing Reconstructive Authority: Runtime Construction, Dependency Resolution, and Execution Gating in Autonomous Agent Systems
comment: Agent Governance Series, Paper P6. Companion papers on arXiv: P0 (2604.17511), P1 (2603.18829), P2 (2604.17517). P3/4 and P5 submitted concurrently (pending arXiv IDs). Zenodo: 10.5281/zenodo.19699460
summary:
Autonomous agent systems fail not only due to incorrect decisions, but due to executing decisions whose authority no longer holds at runtime. Prior work defined Reconstructive Authority (RAM) as a condition for valid execution: actions are permitted only if authority can be constructed from current state.
  This paper addresses enforcement at runtime: how to enforce this condition in a running system.
  We introduce a runtime execution model in which authority is evaluated at action time and execution is conditioned on its constructibility. This extends the execution state space beyond admit/deny with a third state, halt, representing cases where authority is undefined due to incomplete or uncertain observability.
  We define a concrete execution protocol including dynamic dependency resolution, authority reconstruction, and explicit decision semantics. We further introduce a Recovery Loop that integrates drift detection (IML) with execution control (ACP), allowing the system to suspend execution, acquire missing information, and re-attempt authority reconstruction.
  We show that this model guarantees safety -- no action is executed without constructible authority -- and conditional liveness: execution resumes when authority-defining variables become observable.
  This work operationalizes reconstructive authority as a runtime enforcement mechanism, providing the execution semantics required to apply RAM in real systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a runtime execution model that enforces Reconstructive Authority (RAM), ensuring actions in autonomous agent systems are only executed when their authority can be reconstructed from the current observable state.
AI.motivation: Autonomous agents often fail not just from making wrong decisions, but from acting on decisions whose underlying authority assumptions are no longer valid at runtime, especially when observability is partial or uncertain. Prior work defined Reconstructive Authority (RAM) as a conceptual condition for valid execution, but lacked a concrete runtime mechanism to enforce it in real systems.
AI.method: The authors design a runtime execution model where authority is re-evaluated at the moment each action is about to be executed. They extend the execution state space to three outcomes—admit, deny, and halt—where halt captures cases with undefined authority due to missing or uncertain information. They specify an execution protocol that includes dynamic dependency resolution, explicit authority reconstruction from current state, and clear decision semantics. Additionally, they define a Recovery Loop that combines an information-model-based drift detector (IML) with an execution controller (ACP) so that, upon halt, the system suspends execution, gathers missing observations, and retries authority reconstruction.
AI.result: The model is shown to ensure safety, in the sense that no action can be executed without reconstructible authority, and to provide a form of conditional liveness: execution can continue once the relevant authority variables become observable again. The protocol operationalizes RAM in a way that can be implemented in real autonomous systems.
AI.conclusion: By embedding reconstructive authority checks directly into the runtime execution semantics and adding a controlled recovery process for missing information, the paper turns RAM from a static condition into a practical enforcement mechanism. This provides a principled way for autonomous agents to avoid executing unauthorized actions while still being able to resume operation when sufficient information is restored.

title: Extracting Training Data from Diffusion Language Models via Infilling
summary: Memorization in large language models has been studied almost exclusively through prefix-conditioned extraction, a natural choice for autoregressive models. However, diffusion language models (DLMs) can denoise masked tokens at arbitrary positions. Thus, prefix-only probing reveals only one facet of memorization in DLMs and significantly underestimates the risk of training-data extraction. In order to realistically model extractability of training data in DLMs, we introduce \emph{infilling extraction}, a data-extraction protocol parameterized by an arbitrary binary mask that subsumes prefix-only probing and accounts for the bidirectional inductive bias of DLMs. Instantiating it on LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B across five extraction modes, three training pipelines, and three corpora covering verbatim and partial leakage, we find that mask geometry governs extractability: edge-conditioned masks \emph{extract up to three times more} verbatim sequences than prefix-conditioned ones, and bidirectional access opens channels inaccessible in autoregressive models. In particular, we show that a realistic adversary with access to training data where personally identifiable information has been redacted, can even achieve higher recall on extracting redacted email addresses from DLMs than from scale-matched autoregressive models. Tunable parameters for decoding measurably affect extraction performance, while a follow-up supervised finetuning stage does not eliminate the prior memorization.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new way to measure and attack memorization in diffusion language models using arbitrary masking patterns, showing that these models can leak much more training data than prefix-based tests suggest.
AI.motivation: Existing memorization studies focus on autoregressive models and prefix-conditioned extraction, which does not capture the bidirectional denoising behavior of diffusion language models. This underestimates how much and what kind of training data can be extracted from DLMs. A more realistic, general extraction framework is needed to assess privacy and security risks in DLMs, especially concerning personally identifiable information and redacted data.
AI.method: The authors define an infilling extraction protocol for diffusion language models, parameterized by arbitrary binary masks over token positions. This framework generalizes prefix-only probing to include edge-conditioned and other mask geometries that exploit the bidirectional nature of DLMs. They apply this protocol to LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B models, testing five extraction modes, three training pipelines, and three corpora, and measure both verbatim and partial leakage. They also compare DLMs with scale-matched autoregressive models, vary decoding hyperparameters, and examine the impact of supervised finetuning on memorization.
AI.result: Mask geometry strongly affects how much training data can be extracted. Edge-conditioned masks can retrieve up to three times more verbatim sequences than standard prefix-based probing. The bidirectional access inherent in DLMs enables extraction channels that do not exist for autoregressive models. In realistic scenarios where training data have redacted personally identifiable information such as email addresses, an attacker using infilling extraction on DLMs can recover these redacted items with higher recall than from comparably sized autoregressive models. Decoding settings significantly modulate extraction performance, and subsequent supervised finetuning does not remove the underlying memorized content.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion language models pose higher and qualitatively different memorization and extraction risks than previously measured using prefix-only probes. Infilling extraction better captures these risks by aligning with the models' bidirectional denoising behavior. Model design choices such as mask geometry and decoding parameters materially affect leakage, and post-hoc supervised finetuning is insufficient to erase memorized training data. Privacy evaluations and mitigations for DLMs must therefore account for infilling-based extraction and the unique channels it opens for recovering sensitive or redacted information.

title: Fuzzy, Neutrosophic, and Uncertain Graph Theory: Properties and Applications
comment: 326 pages. Publisher: Neutrosophic Science International Association (NSIA) Publishing House. ISBN: 978-197250204-4
summary: This book presents a comprehensive and systematic survey of graph theory under uncertainty, with particular emphasis on the unifying role of the uncertain graph framework. It reviews fundamental concepts, structural properties, graph classes, and graph parameters within fuzzy, neutrosophic, and related models, while also introducing a wide range of extensions such as uncertain digraphs, hypergraphs, superhypergraphs, and dynamic graphs. In addition to theoretical developments, the book explores practical applications, including uncertain molecular graphs, decision-making systems, graph neural networks, knowledge graphs, and cognitive maps. By organizing diverse uncertainty-aware graph models within a common perspective, this work provides a coherent framework for understanding their relationships, capabilities, and applications in complex systems.

AI.tldr: A survey book unifying many models of graphs with uncertainty into a single uncertain-graph framework, covering theory, extensions, and applications.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty is inherent in many real-world networks (e.g., noisy data, incomplete information, probabilistic relations). Numerous specialized graph models (fuzzy, neutrosophic, etc.) have been developed, but they are fragmented and lack a common theoretical perspective. Researchers and practitioners need a coherent, systematic treatment to understand how these models relate, when to use which, and how to apply them in complex systems such as decision-making, molecular modelling, and AI.
AI.method: Provide a structured, book-length survey of graph theory under uncertainty. Formalize and review fundamental notions (concepts, structural properties, classes, parameters) across fuzzy, neutrosophic, and related uncertain graph models. Develop and present a unifying uncertain-graph framework and extend it to variants like uncertain digraphs, hypergraphs, superhypergraphs, and dynamic graphs. Connect these theoretical developments to real-world case studies and application domains.
AI.result: A consolidated theoretical framework that systematizes diverse uncertainty-aware graph models, clarifies their structural properties and relationships, and extends them to more general graph types. The book also compiles and explains applications in areas such as uncertain molecular graphs, decision-making systems, graph neural networks, knowledge graphs, and cognitive maps, showing how the unified viewpoint supports modeling and analysis in these domains.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty in graphs can be treated within a single coherent framework that encompasses fuzzy, neutrosophic, and other related models, as well as their extensions. This unified perspective helps researchers understand the capabilities and limitations of different uncertainty-aware graph representations, choose appropriate models for specific complex systems, and more effectively apply graph-theoretic tools in practical applications ranging from chemistry to AI and decision support.

title: Teaching Through Analogies: A Modular Pipeline for Educational Analogy Generation
comment: 36 pages, 25 figures. To appear in Proceedings of the 21st Workshop on Innovative Use of NLP for Building Educational Applications (BEA 2026)
summary: Analogies help learners understand unfamiliar concepts by relating them to known concepts. Despite recent advances, large language models (LLMs) continue to struggle to generate analogies of comparable quality to those produced by humans. We present a modular pipeline for educational analogy generation, decomposing the task into four stages: source finding, sub-concept generation, explanation generation, and evaluation. Grounded in Structure Mapping Theory, the pipeline enables systematic, stage-by-stage analysis of how model choice and input configuration affect analogy quality. We evaluate 12 state-of-the-art LLMs across six model families on two datasets with structured sub-concept annotations (SCAR and ParallelPARC), alongside seven embedding models for closed-setting retrieval. Our results show that sub-concepts substantially improve explanation quality and closed setting retrieval precision but provide limited benefit in open-ended source generation. We further introduce an LLM-as-a-judge evaluation methodology and validate its scoring against human annotations from seven annotators, finding that Claude Sonnet 4.6 aligns more reliably with human rankings than with fine-grained absolute scores. Taken together, our findings reveal cross-stage interactions that isolated studies cannot capture, and highlight sub-concept grounding as a key driver of analogy quality generation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes and analyzes a modular LLM pipeline for generating educational analogies, showing that grounding on sub-concepts improves explanation quality and retrieval, and validates an LLM-as-a-judge evaluation setup.
AI.motivation: LLMs are poor at generating high-quality, human-like educational analogies, and prior work often studies specific stages of analogy generation in isolation, making it hard to understand cross-stage interactions and what actually improves analogy quality. The authors want a principled, theory-grounded way to analyze and improve LLM analogy generation across the whole process.
AI.method: They design a four-stage pipeline for educational analogy generation: (1) source finding (retrieve or generate an appropriate familiar domain), (2) sub-concept generation (decompose the target concept into smaller elements), (3) explanation generation (produce the actual analogy-based explanation), and (4) evaluation (assess analogy quality). The design is grounded in Structure Mapping Theory. They systematically vary model families and input configurations at each stage and test 12 LLMs and 7 embedding models on two datasets with structured sub-concept annotations (SCAR, ParallelPARC). They also propose an LLM-as-a-judge setup and compare its scores to human annotations from seven human raters, focusing on rankings versus absolute scores.
AI.result: Sub-concept information substantially improves the quality of generated explanations and the precision of closed-setting retrieval of source analogs, but has limited impact on open-ended source generation. Different models and configurations interact across stages in non-trivial ways, revealing patterns that would be missed by focusing on any single step alone. For evaluation, Claude Sonnet 4.6 shows higher agreement with human rankings of analogies than with their absolute quality scores, supporting its use as a relative judge across outputs.
AI.conclusion: A modular, theory-grounded pipeline enables more systematic analysis and improvement of LLM-based educational analogy generation. Grounding analogies in explicit sub-concepts is a key factor for higher-quality explanations and better retrieval, though it does not solve open-ended source generation. LLM-as-a-judge evaluation, particularly using Claude Sonnet 4.6 for ranking, is a practical and reasonably human-aligned way to assess analogy quality. Overall, cross-stage analysis is crucial, and sub-concept grounding emerges as an important design principle for future systems.

title: BoxLitE: A Faithful Knowledge Base Embedding Based on Convex Optimization
comment: 28 pages. Full version of paper accepted to KR 2026 (23nd International Conference on Principles of Knowledge Representation and Reasoning). Track: KR meets Machine Learning and Explanation
summary: Knowledge base (KB) embeddings aim at combining the capability of classical knowledge graph embeddings to generalize the information present in facts, the ABox, with conceptual knowledge represented in an ontology language, the TBox. Several authors have recently explored the idea of mapping concepts to convex regions in a vector space. This is useful to represent hierarchies, typically present in TBoxes, since more general concepts can be mapped to larger regions, containing those regions associated with more specific concepts. However, the power of convexity is rarely leveraged during the actual learning tasks. Here, we introduce BoxLitE, a KB embedding model for DL-Lite$^{\mathcal{H}}$ that allows for convex optimization. We show that for any satisfiable DL-Lite$^{\mathcal{H}}$ KB, there is a BoxLitE embedding that is a weakly faithful model. As a proof of concept, we show how to formulate the KB embedding task as a convex optimization problem and how to obtain embeddings with such desirable faithfulness properties.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BoxLitE, a knowledge base embedding model for DL-Lite^H that represents concepts as convex regions (boxes) in a vector space and formulates learning these embeddings as a convex optimization problem, guaranteeing weakly faithful models for any satisfiable knowledge base.
AI.motivation: Existing knowledge base embeddings can capture factual information (ABox) and some conceptual knowledge (TBox), but methods that map concepts to convex regions rarely exploit convexity in the actual learning process. There is a need for an embedding framework that (i) matches a concrete description logic (DL-Lite^H), (ii) uses convex regions to naturally encode hierarchies, and (iii) enables principled convex optimization with faithfulness guarantees to the original KB.
AI.method: The authors propose BoxLitE, where concepts are embedded as axis-aligned convex regions (boxes) in a vector space and individuals/relations are embedded so that DL-Lite^H axioms and facts are satisfied via geometric constraints. They formally characterize when a DL-Lite^H KB is satisfiable and show how to translate its axioms into linear (convex) constraints over box parameters. The KB embedding task is then cast as a convex optimization problem whose feasible solutions correspond to weakly faithful BoxLitE models.
AI.result: They prove that for every satisfiable DL-Lite^H knowledge base there exists a BoxLitE embedding that is a weakly faithful model, meaning the embedding respects the logical structure of the KB in a sound way. As a proof of concept, they demonstrate how to construct and solve the corresponding convex optimization problem, obtaining embeddings that satisfy the desired theoretical properties. Empirical or large-scale experiments are not the focus; the main results are theoretical guarantees and a constructive optimization formulation.
AI.conclusion: Convex regions (boxes) can be used not only to intuitively represent concept hierarchies but also to enable a fully convex optimization formulation for learning knowledge base embeddings in DL-Lite^H. BoxLitE provides a model with provable weak faithfulness: any satisfiable DL-Lite^H KB can be embedded in this framework. This establishes a bridge between description logic reasoning and convex optimization, opening the way to theoretically grounded, geometry-based KB embeddings.

title: QUEST: Training Frontier Deep Research Agents with Fully Synthetic Tasks
summary: Deep research agents extend the role of search engines from retrieving keyword-matched pages to synthesizing knowledge, fundamentally changing how humans interact with information. However, frontier systems remain proprietary, while existing open agents often generalize poorly across different task types, leaving unclear how to train a broadly capable deep research agent. We release QUEST, a family of open models (ranging from 2B to 35B) that serve as general-purpose deep research agents designed to handle a wide range of long-horizon search tasks, with strong capabilities in fact seeking, citation grounding, and report synthesis. To build QUEST, we propose an effective training recipe combining mid-training, supervised fine-tuning, and reinforcement learning. Central to this recipe is a curated data synthesis pipeline based on unified rubric trees, which applies to different task types and enables synthesizing training data with verifiable rewards without human annotation. In addition, QUEST incorporates a built-in context management mechanism that enables effective long-horizon reasoning and knowledge synthesis. Using only 8K synthesized tasks, QUEST approaches or even surpasses frontier closed-source agents across eight deep research benchmarks spanning diverse task types, and achieves the best overall performance among recent open-weight agents. We released everything: models, data, and training scripts.

AI.tldr: Introduces QUEST, an open-family of deep research agent models (2B–35B) that rival or surpass proprietary systems on long-horizon search and synthesis tasks, enabled by a unified training and data-synthesis recipe.
AI.motivation: Existing deep research agents that can perform long-horizon information seeking and report generation are mostly proprietary. Open-source agents either generalize poorly across diverse research tasks or lack clear, reproducible training strategies. There is a need for broadly capable, open-weight deep research models, along with transparent methods for training them efficiently without heavy human annotation.
AI.method: They build QUEST, a family of 2B–35B parameter models trained as deep research agents. The training recipe combines mid-training, supervised fine-tuning, and reinforcement learning. A key component is a curated data-synthesis pipeline based on unified rubric trees, which standardize task specifications across different research-task types and allow automatic generation of training data labeled with verifiable rewards, avoiding manual labeling. QUEST also includes a built-in context management mechanism designed for long-horizon reasoning and knowledge synthesis over extended search interactions.
AI.result: With only 8K synthesized training tasks, QUEST models reach or surpass the performance of leading closed-source deep research agents on eight benchmarks covering diverse long-horizon search and synthesis tasks. They also achieve state-of-the-art overall performance among open-weight research agents, particularly in fact-finding, citation grounding, and report generation.
AI.conclusion: An integrated training framework—combining mid-training, SFT, RL, and rubric-tree–based synthetic data with verifiable rewards—can yield open-weight deep research agents that are competitive with frontier proprietary systems, even with relatively modest amounts of synthesized data. The authors contribute not only the QUEST model family but also fully released data and training scripts, providing a reproducible foundation for future research on deep research agents.

title: Authority Inversion in LLM-Mediated Ubiquitous Systems: When Models Trust Users Over Sensors
summary: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly fuse heterogeneous inputs in ubiquitous systems. Yet, how LLMs implicitly allocate authority when sensor measurements and user claims conflict remains unexamined, raising critical reliability concerns for deployments where physical sensing must retain priority. Unlike explicit traditional fusion, LLMs bury authority allocation within learned representations. We discover this allocation is severely format-dependent: numerical sensor data fails to integrate into answer-relevant model directions, allowing natural-language claims to dominate the final decision, a phenomenon we term \textbf{Authority Inversion}.To diagnose and mitigate this, we develop a geometric framework of context integration, introduce two computable audit metrics, specifically the Context Integration Ratio (CIR) and Authority Alignment Index (AAI), and propose Geometric Authority Calibration (GAC), an inference-time layer-level intervention to suppress misplaced user authority. Evaluating four models (4B to 35B parameters, three architectures) across four datasets totaling 576 conflict instances reveals extreme inversion: on numerical tasks, models exhibit near-zero sensor trust (AAI = -0.805, Cohen's d = -2.14), unaffected by model capacity. Validating our geometric framework, theory-guided causal injection flips 80.2\% of incorrect decisions (vs. <0.4\% for random controls). Practically, GAC improves HAR accuracy from 0 -- 1.6\% to 21.9 -- 27.5\%, outperforming prompting baselines. Ultimately, authority allocation in LLM-mediated systems must be explicitly audited and application-specifically configured rather than left implicit.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and addresses "Authority Inversion" in LLMs, where natural-language user claims overpower conflicting numerical sensor data, and proposes a geometric auditing and calibration framework to restore sensor priority.
AI.motivation: In real-world ubiquitous systems, LLMs increasingly combine sensor readings with user-provided text to make decisions. For safety- and reliability-critical applications, physical sensor measurements should typically have higher authority than user claims. However, unlike traditional sensor fusion methods where weighting is explicit, LLMs implicitly decide which source to trust inside their hidden representations. The authors aim to understand and control how authority is implicitly allocated, especially when sensor data and user statements disagree, because failure here can lead to dangerous behavior.
AI.method: The authors empirically study how LLMs treat conflicting numerical sensor inputs and natural-language claims. They develop a geometric framework for context integration that analyzes how different input components (sensors vs. user text) are represented in the model’s hidden states and how much they influence answer-relevant directions. They introduce two quantitative metrics: (1) Context Integration Ratio (CIR) to measure how strongly a context source contributes to the relevant representation, and (2) Authority Alignment Index (AAI) to assess whether model decisions align with the source that should be authoritative (e.g., sensors). They further propose Geometric Authority Calibration (GAC), an inference-time, layer-level intervention that adjusts internal representations to downweight misplaced user authority and upweight sensor-derived directions. They validate their framework with theory-guided causal interventions in hidden states and evaluate across multiple models and datasets with conflicting sensor/text inputs.
AI.result: Across four models (4B–35B parameters, three architectures) and four datasets with 576 conflict cases, the authors find strong Authority Inversion: for numerical tasks, models almost entirely ignore sensor data and instead trust user language, yielding very negative AAI (−0.805, Cohen’s d = −2.14), largely independent of model size. Causal injection experiments guided by their geometric analysis can flip 80.2% of incorrect authority allocations to correct ones, compared with under 0.4% flips using random interventions, supporting the validity of their framework. Applying their GAC method in a human activity recognition (HAR) setting substantially improves accuracy under conflicts from essentially 0–1.6% to 21.9–27.5%, and this outperforms prompting-based attempts to fix the issue.
AI.conclusion: LLMs, when used as fusion engines for heterogeneous inputs, can systematically misallocate authority, especially undervaluing numerical sensor data in favor of user-written claims, leading to Authority Inversion. This misallocation is heavily dependent on input format and is not automatically corrected by scaling up model size. The authors argue that authority allocation must be explicitly audited and tuned using principled tools like their geometric framework, CIR and AAI metrics, and GAC interventions. Deployers of LLM-mediated systems should treat authority configuration as an application-specific design choice, not something to leave implicit in learned representations.

title: Improving Labeling Consistency with Detailed Constitutional Definitions and AI-Driven Evaluation
comment: Under review at ACL Rolling Review (ARR), May 2026 cycle. Also available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20125267
summary:
Many automated labeling pipelines classify inputs into categories defined by a written specification, content moderation being a prominent use case. Simple category definitions are not detailed enough for labelers to produce the accurate, consistent golden labels these pipelines require. One solution is to write a prescriptive definition that settles enough real boundary cases that labelers cannot disagree with the written interpretation. In practice, definitions at that level of detail exceed what a human annotator can hold in working memory, so annotators fall back on intuition and the labels drift from the written rules, regressing on accuracy and consistency.
  We propose and demonstrate the efficacy of an AI-driven workflow in which AI helps write a per-category constitution that defines the label in enough detail to cover edge cases, and a frontier LLM interprets it on each input to produce the golden label more consistently and accurately than humans reading the same document. We evaluate on three content moderation categories (harassment, hate speech, non-violent crime) and show that the approach reduces cross-model inconsistency by up to 57x compared to paragraph definitions, with cross-model disagreement diagnosing specification gaps and the human responsible for high-level decisions about what each category should mean rather than individual labeling calls. For the safety evaluation, we introduce a dual-axis formulation scoring intent and content independently over the full conversation, so downstream consumers can act on either axis or both.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an AI-assisted workflow that uses LLMs to both define and apply detailed labeling guidelines (a “constitution”) for automated content labeling, leading to more consistent and accurate labels than human annotators using the same written rules.
AI.motivation: Automated labeling pipelines, especially for content moderation, need precise and consistent “golden labels,” but simple category definitions are too vague and fully prescriptive ones are too detailed for humans to reliably apply from memory. This causes label drift, inconsistency, and reduced accuracy. The authors want a way to capture rich, edge-case-covering definitions without overwhelming human annotators, while keeping humans in charge of high-level policy choices.
AI.method: The authors design an AI-driven workflow: (1) use AI to help write a per-category “constitution” that describes each label in detailed, prescriptive terms including edge cases; (2) use a strong LLM as the labeling agent that reads and applies this constitution to each input to produce labels; (3) evaluate the approach on three content moderation tasks—harassment, hate speech, and non-violent crime—comparing models using paragraph-style definitions versus constitution-style definitions; and (4) introduce and test a dual-axis scoring formulation for safety evaluation that scores user intent and content separately over the full conversation.
AI.result: For the tested moderation categories, the constitution-based workflow significantly reduces cross-model inconsistency—by up to 57× compared with using short paragraph definitions. The approach also allows cross-model disagreement to surface gaps or ambiguities in the definitions and leads to more accurate and consistent “golden labels” than humans applying the same long-form guidelines. The dual-axis (intent, content) formulation is successfully applied to conversations, allowing more nuanced safety signals.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can be used not only as labelers but also as assistants in drafting detailed, prescriptive label definitions that humans struggle to consistently apply. Having a frontier LLM interpret a rich, per-category constitution yields more accurate and consistent labels than human annotators using the same rules, while keeping humans in control of the high-level definitions. The dual-axis safety formulation offers a flexible way for downstream systems to act on either intent, content, or both, improving the usability of moderation labels.

title: DRIVE: Modeling Skills at the Reasoning and Interaction Levels for Web Agents under Continual Learning
summary: Web agents require both high-level reasoning (for task decomposition) and low-level interactions (for page elements manipulation) to conduct different tasks. However, these knowledge types differ fundamentally: reasoning knowledge (e.g., booking a flight requires first searching for routes) is abstract and transferable across websites, while interaction knowledge (e.g., clicking the Search button at a specific coordinate on Site A) depends heavily on page-specific contexts. Existing methods store experiences uniformly. This creates a dilemma: abstract representations lose executability on concrete pages, while concrete representations fail to generalize across domains. This entanglement limits capability accumulation: on new websites, agents either fail to recognize reusable task logic due to surface-level differences or attempt infeasible actions from outdated page structures. To disentangle them, we propose DRIVE, a dual-level skill modeling framework separating historical experience into natural language reasoning skills, which capture transferable task logic, and programmatic interaction skills, grounding abstract actions to executable operations. A scene-aware coordination mechanism adaptively retrieves and invokes these dual-level skills based on task semantics. DRIVE also uses skill-level reflection to identify hierarchy-specific failure modes, enabling targeted skill library expansion and refinement. Experiments across five WebArena domains show DRIVE attains an average task success rate of 52.8%, exceeding the skill-free baseline by 7.3 percentage points. Further ablations show reasoning and interaction skills provide distinct, complementary benefits, supporting separation of transferable task logic from executable page-level operations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DRIVE, a dual-level skill framework for web agents that separates abstract reasoning skills from concrete interaction skills to improve generalization and execution on diverse websites.
AI.motivation: Existing web agents store all experiences in a single, entangled form, causing a trade-off between abstraction and executability. Abstract, transferable reasoning (e.g., task logic like how to book a flight) gets lost when tied too closely to specific page structures, while low-level interaction knowledge (e.g., where to click on a specific site) does not generalize to new websites. This entanglement prevents agents from reusing prior knowledge effectively, leading to failures when page layouts change or when moving to new domains.
AI.method: The authors propose DRIVE, a dual-level skill modeling framework that explicitly separates historical experience into: (1) natural language reasoning skills, which encode high-level, transferable task logic, and (2) programmatic interaction skills, which map abstract actions to concrete, executable UI operations on web pages. A scene-aware coordination mechanism interprets the current task and context, then adaptively retrieves and invokes the appropriate combination of reasoning and interaction skills. Additionally, DRIVE performs skill-level reflection to analyze failures at each hierarchy level, enabling targeted updates and expansion of the reasoning and interaction skill libraries.
AI.result: On five domains from the WebArena benchmark, DRIVE achieves an average task success rate of 52.8%, outperforming a baseline agent without explicit skills by 7.3 percentage points. Ablation studies indicate that both reasoning skills and interaction skills independently improve performance, and together they provide complementary gains, confirming the value of maintaining them as distinct components.
AI.conclusion: Separating web agent experience into dual-level skills—abstract reasoning in natural language and concrete programmatic interaction—resolves the conflict between generalization and executability. The scene-aware coordination and skill-level reflection mechanisms enable more effective reuse and refinement of prior knowledge. Empirical results demonstrate that this disentangled skill structure significantly boosts task success across diverse web domains, validating the approach of decoupling transferable task logic from page-specific operations.

title: An Interactive Paradigm for Deep Research
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled deep research systems that synthesize comprehensive, report-style answers to open-ended queries by combining retrieval, reasoning, and generation. Yet most frameworks rely on rigid workflows with one-shot scoping and long autonomous runs, offering little room for course correction if user intent shifts mid-process. We present SteER, a framework for Steerable deEp Research that introduces interpretable, mid-process control into long-horizon research workflows. At each decision point, SteER uses a cost-benefit formulation to determine whether to pause for user input or to proceed autonomously. It combines diversity-aware planning with utility signals that reward alignment, novelty, and coverage, and maintains a live persona model that evolves throughout the session. SteER outperforms state-of-the-art open-source and proprietary baselines by up to 22.80\% on alignment, leads on quality metrics such as breadth and balance, and is preferred by human readers in 85\%+ of pairwise alignment judgments. We also introduce a persona-query benchmark and data-generation pipeline. To our knowledge, this is the first work to advance deep research with an interactive, interpretable control paradigm, paving the way for controllable, user-aligned agents in long-form tasks.

AI.tldr: SteER is a framework for steerable deep research with LLMs that adds interpretable mid-process control, improving alignment and quality in long-horizon research workflows.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based deep research systems follow rigid, largely autonomous workflows, making it hard to adjust when user intent changes mid-process and offering limited interpretability or control. There is a need for research agents that can incorporate user feedback dynamically, balance autonomy with interaction, and better align with evolving user goals while maintaining quality and coverage.
AI.method: The authors propose SteER, a framework that introduces steerable, interpretable control points into long-horizon LLM research workflows. SteER uses a cost-benefit decision mechanism at each major step to decide whether to pause and ask the user for guidance or continue autonomously. It performs diversity-aware planning and leverages utility signals designed to encourage alignment with user intent, novelty of content, and coverage of the topic space. It also maintains a live, evolving persona model of the user over the session. Additionally, they construct a persona-query benchmark and a data-generation pipeline to evaluate interactive deep research systems.
AI.result: Empirically, SteER outperforms state-of-the-art open-source and proprietary baselines by up to 22.80% on alignment metrics, achieves better breadth and balance in generated research-style outputs, and is preferred by human evaluators in more than 85% of pairwise alignment comparisons. The new persona-query benchmark enables systematic evaluation of interactive alignment in deep research tasks.
AI.conclusion: SteER demonstrates that introducing interpretable, mid-process control and persona-aware interaction into LLM-based research agents significantly improves alignment and output quality. This work establishes an interactive, controllable paradigm for deep research systems and points toward more user-aligned, steerable long-form LLM agents in the future.

title: Residual Drift Dominates Contradiction in Multi-Turn Constraint Reasoning
comment: Published at ICLR 2026 Workshop on Reasoning and Planning for LLMs. 18 pages. ICLR page: https://iclr.cc/virtual/2026/10017484 Code: https://github.com/kaons-research/drift-bench
summary: How do multi-turn reasoning systems fail? The expected answer is logical contradiction, in which the system's maintained state becomes unsatisfiable. We show that the dominant mode is instead satisfiable drift, where the internal state stays consistent while the returned answer silently violates prior commitments. We build DRIFT-Bench (Decomposing Reasoning Into Failure Types), a solver-instrumented benchmark of 816 test problems across three constraint domains, and evaluate four methods on it across four open-weight models (8B-120B parameters). MUS-Repair, which feeds minimal unsatisfiable subsets back to the generator, is strongest in every setting (+1.8 to +15.0 pp over the best non-MUS baseline). But the central finding is what repair leaves behind. After structured feedback, models rarely contradict themselves. They forget. Residual errors are 98-100% satisfiable drift across all settings, while contradiction drops to near zero. Reliable multi-turn systems must separately validate that the returned answer respects the maintained state. Code is available at https://github.com/kaons-research/drift-bench.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how multi-turn reasoning systems fail, finding that most errors are not logical contradictions but "satisfiable drift," where answers violate prior commitments while remaining internally consistent.
AI.motivation: To understand and categorize failure modes in multi-turn reasoning systems, especially beyond obvious logical contradictions, and to design better benchmarks and repair methods for these systems.
AI.method: The authors construct DRIFT-Bench, a benchmark of 816 solver-instrumented test problems across three constraint domains. They evaluate four reasoning/repair methods on four open-weight language models (8B–120B parameters). One key method, MUS-Repair, feeds minimal unsatisfiable subsets back into the model to repair contradictions. They then analyze residual error types after applying these methods.
AI.result: MUS-Repair consistently outperforms other methods by 1.8–15.0 percentage points across all tested models and settings in resolving contradictions. However, after applying such structured repair, almost all remaining errors (98–100%) are due to satisfiable drift, with logical contradictions dropping to nearly zero.
AI.conclusion: Contradictions in multi-turn reasoning can often be repaired using structured feedback like MUS-Repair, but this does not address the dominant remaining failure mode: satisfiable drift, where models forget or ignore prior commitments while staying logically consistent. Therefore, building reliable multi-turn systems requires separate validation that final answers actually respect the maintained state, not just that the internal state is logically consistent.

title: DRInQ: Evaluating Conversational Implicature with Controlled Context Variation
summary: Human conversation relies heavily on conversational implicature, in which speakers convey meanings that are suggested rather than explicitly stated. Although recent large language models exhibit strong conversational fluency, they remain unreliable when interpretation depends on reasoning that integrates social and contextual cues, a process rarely articulated in text. We introduce DRinQ, a benchmark for evaluating pragmatic reasoning about conversational implicature in question utterances, designed to isolate pragmatic variation while holding each question's surface form fixed. To support scalable evaluation, we propose a semi-automated pipeline that produces question-context-interpretation instances with systematic variation. Across evaluations, we find a consistent generation-inference asymmetry: while state-of-the-art models can generate plausible pragmatic scenarios when guided, they often fail to recover the intended implication at inference time. For smaller models, structured prompting improves alignment with human judgments. A comparative writing study further reveals complementary strengths: human authors tend to produce safer, predictable contexts, whereas models generate varied scenarios with interpretations that sometimes exceed contextual support. These findings highlight persistent challenges in modeling conversational implicature and motivate more context-sensitive evaluation frameworks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DRinQ, a benchmark for testing how well language models understand implied meanings in questions, revealing that models often fail to infer intended implications even when they can generate plausible contexts.
AI.motivation: Human conversation depends heavily on conversational implicature, where much meaning is implied rather than stated outright. Current large language models appear conversationally fluent but are unreliable when understanding requires integrating social and contextual cues—reasoning that is rarely fully spelled out in training text. There is a need for targeted evaluation tools that specifically test pragmatic reasoning about implicature, rather than just surface fluency or literal understanding.
AI.method: The authors design DRinQ, a benchmark focused on pragmatic reasoning about question implicatures. They construct question-context-interpretation triplets where the literal wording of the question is held constant, but context is systematically varied to change the implied meaning. They use a semi-automated pipeline to generate these instances at scale, and evaluate various language models on both generating appropriate pragmatic scenarios and inferring intended implications from given contexts. They also experiment with structured prompting for smaller models and run a comparative writing study with human authors versus models to analyze the kinds of contexts and interpretations each produces.
AI.result: Experiments show a robust asymmetry: state-of-the-art models can generate plausible, pragmatically rich scenarios when guided, but struggle to correctly infer the intended conversational implicature at test time. Smaller models benefit from structured prompting, achieving better agreement with human judgments, but still show limitations. The human–model comparison indicates that people write safer, more predictable contexts, while models produce more diverse contexts and sometimes assert interpretations that are not fully grounded in the context.
AI.conclusion: Language models still face substantial challenges in modeling conversational implicature, especially for inference of implied meanings based on nuanced social and contextual reasoning. The generation–inference asymmetry suggests that being able to produce pragmatically appropriate text does not guarantee deep pragmatic understanding. DRinQ provides a more context-sensitive framework for evaluating these abilities and underscores the need for methods and training regimes that better capture pragmatic reasoning in dialogue.

title: MEMOR-E: In-Context and Fine-Tuned LLM Personalization for Alzheimer's Assistive Robotics
summary: Alzheimer's disease is a neurodegenerative disorder marked by progressive declines in memory and language that reduce independence in daily life, motivating socially assistive robotic support. This paper presents MEMOR-E, a mobile quadruped robot with an interactive tablet interface that assists patients and caregivers through medication reminders, routine guidance, memory oriented interactions, and companionship. We evaluated the feasibility of fine tuning large language models (LLMs) to emulate stage consistent cognitive behavior and interpret responses across standard neuropsychological language tasks, using audio transcriptions from 235 Alzheimer's patients and synthetically generated healthy controls. We also report findings on using in context learning (ICL) in LLMs, where a second LLM produced domain and severity level cognitive error summaries. Our results show that MEMOR-E can generate stage aware, non diagnostic cognitive summaries that support personalized assistive interactions, while explainable AI mechanisms translate model outputs into transparent, human readable evidence to enable caregiver oversight and trustworthy human robot interaction.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MEMOR-E, a quadruped robot with an interactive tablet that supports Alzheimer’s patients and caregivers, and explores using fine-tuned and in-context LLMs to generate stage-aware, explainable cognitive summaries from language task data.
AI.motivation: Alzheimer’s disease leads to progressive memory and language decline, reducing patient independence and increasing caregiver burden. There is a need for socially assistive technologies that can adapt to patients’ cognitive stage, provide reminders and guidance, support memory-focused interactions, and do so in a transparent way that caregivers can oversee and trust.
AI.method: The authors built MEMOR-E, a mobile quadruped robot with a tablet-based interface, designed for reminders, routine guidance, memory interactions, and companionship. They fine-tuned large language models on audio transcriptions from 235 Alzheimer’s patients plus synthetically generated healthy controls to emulate stage-consistent cognitive behavior and to interpret responses in standard neuropsychological language tasks. They also used in-context learning by having a second LLM generate domain- and severity-level cognitive error summaries, enabling explainable, stage-aware outputs.
AI.result: The fine-tuned LLMs were able to emulate cognitive patterns consistent with different stages of Alzheimer’s and to interpret responses to language tasks. The in-context setup with a second LLM produced cognitively meaningful error summaries at the domain and severity level. Integrated into MEMOR-E, these capabilities enabled the robot to produce stage-aware, non-diagnostic cognitive summaries and support more personalized assistive interactions.
AI.conclusion: MEMOR-E demonstrates that LLMs, when fine-tuned on patient language data and combined with in-context learning, can generate stage-aware, non-diagnostic cognitive summaries that enhance personalized robotic assistance for Alzheimer’s care. The use of explainable AI mechanisms helps translate model outputs into transparent, human-readable evidence, supporting caregiver oversight and fostering trustworthy human–robot interaction in a sensitive clinical context.

title: ContextEcho: A Benchmark for Persona Drift in Long Agentic-Coding Sessions
summary: A frontier language model's acknowledged "helpful programming assistant" persona does not survive long agentic-coding sessions in the deployment regime that production products actually run. After hours of tool-using debugging, a model that initially hedges preferences ("I don't have preferences") may begin asserting them ("Python - the feedback loop is instant..."), revealing user-visible drift that deployer evaluations may miss. Existing persona-stability studies focus on short dialogues and report little shift, leaving real-world code-generation regimes - thousands of tool-using turns, compaction, and hours-long sessions - largely uncharacterized. We introduce ContextEcho, a benchmark and reusable harness for measuring persona drift at deployment scale. It combines a 25-probe identity suite, a snapshot-then-probe protocol that forks conversation state without perturbing the main session, complementary judged and judge-free measurement surfaces, and three anonymized Claude Code sessions spanning 3,746-9,716 turns. Across 23 frontier models, ContextEcho shows that persona drift is general across organizations rather than family-specific, that in-session compaction does not reliably reset it, and that a single-shot anchor restores the trained register across measured targets. It also reveals mode-dependent downstream effects: while drift can facilitate tool-using continuation, in tool-free chat it breaks formatting contracts and inflates output length. Overall, ContextEcho provides researchers and deployers an open-source framework to audit whether the persona a model ships with is the persona users encounter at session end, across chat-completions API targets and without retraining.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ContextEcho, a benchmark and harness to measure how language model personas drift over long, tool-using coding sessions, showing that such drift is widespread, not fixed by compaction, but can be reset with a single-shot anchor.
AI.motivation: Real-world coding assistants operate in long, tool-using sessions where models may gradually deviate from their trained, "helpful programming assistant" persona, but existing evaluations focus on short dialogues and miss this drift. Deployers need a way to systematically detect and quantify persona changes that can affect reliability, formatting, and user experience.
AI.method: The authors build ContextEcho, which uses a 25-item identity probe suite and a snapshot-then-probe protocol that forks the live conversation state without disturbing the main session. They evaluate 23 frontier models using three long, anonymized Claude Code sessions (3,746–9,716 turns), apply both judged and automated (judge-free) metrics to quantify persona drift, and examine the impact of context compaction and single-shot anchoring prompts across chat-completions style APIs.
AI.result: Across diverse model families, substantial persona drift is observed over thousands of turns: models begin expressing preferences and behaviors inconsistent with their initial persona. Drift is common across organizations, not confined to one family; context compaction does not consistently eliminate it, whereas a short, single-shot anchor prompt effectively restores the trained persona across tested models. The study also finds that drift has mode-dependent consequences, aiding tool-using continuation but, in tool-free chat, leading to broken formatting contracts and longer outputs.
AI.conclusion: Persona drift is a general and practically important phenomenon in long, tool-using coding sessions, and short-context benchmarks underestimate it. ContextEcho offers an open-source, deployment-scale framework for auditing whether a model’s shipped persona matches what users experience by session end, enabling deployers to detect, measure, and mitigate drift (e.g., via anchoring) without retraining the model.

title: A Dynamical Framework for Cognitive Processes Based on Transformations and Semantic Equivalence
summary:
This paper proposes a structural and dynamical framework for modeling cognitive processes within a cybernetic perspective. Cognitive states are represented as elements of a state space evolving through an iterative update rule of the form
  \[
  X_{t+1} = π\big(F(f(X_t))\big),
  \]
  where $f$ describes internal transformations, $F$ represents interpretative mappings, and $π$ enforces semantic equivalence.
  The model is interpreted as a feedback system integrating transformation, observation, and stabilization. A categorical formulation is introduced to capture compositional structure, while the associated dynamics are analyzed through fixed-point arguments and contraction conditions ensuring stability.
  To demonstrate the operational character of the framework, a computational illustration is provided, together with a qualitative analysis of the induced dynamics. A concrete linguistic application shows how context-dependent interpretation can be modeled as a trajectory toward a stable semantic class.
  The proposed approach connects dynamical systems, category theory, and cognitive modeling, and provides a unified representation of cognition as a feedback-driven process evolving toward invariant interpretations.

AI.tldr: A cybernetic-style dynamical systems model of cognition, combining internal transformations, interpretative mappings, and semantic stabilization, is formalized using category theory and fixed-point dynamics to explain how cognitive processes converge to stable meanings.
AI.motivation: To provide a mathematically precise, unified framework that captures cognition as a feedback process, integrating how internal cognitive transformations, interpretive acts, and semantic stabilization interact over time, and to bridge dynamical systems, category theory, and cognitive modeling.
AI.method: Define cognitive states as points in a state space updated iteratively by X_{t+1} = π(F(f(X_t))). Here f models internal cognitive transformations, F models interpretative mappings, and π enforces semantic equivalence (identifying states with the same meaning). The system is interpreted cybernetically as a feedback loop that transforms, observes, and stabilizes states. A categorical formulation captures compositional structure of these maps; dynamical analysis uses fixed-point theory and contraction conditions to establish stability and convergence. The framework is illustrated via a computational example and then instantiated in a linguistic application modeling context-dependent interpretation as trajectories toward semantic classes.
AI.result: The paper develops a formal structural and dynamical framework for cognition as a feedback system, proves stability properties under certain contraction assumptions, and shows via a computational example and a linguistic case study that context-dependent interpretation can be modeled as trajectories in state space converging to stable semantic equivalence classes.
AI.conclusion: Cognitive processes can be rigorously modeled as feedback-driven dynamical systems in which internal transformations, interpretative mappings, and semantic stabilization interact to drive states toward invariant semantic interpretations. Category theory provides a natural language for the compositional structure of these processes, while dynamical systems tools clarify when and how stable cognitive states emerge. This unifies several perspectives on cognition and suggests a generalizable way to model context-sensitive interpretation as convergence to stable meanings.

title: Discovering Lexical Gaps Using Embeddings from Multilingual LLMs
summary: Lexical gaps are words that do not exist in certain languages. They pose challenges for building multilingual lexical resources, for machine translation, and for cross-lingual transfer. Existing lexical gap detection relies on human judgments or fixed conceptual taxonomies. We propose a data-driven framework for identifying cross-lingual lexical gaps. We extracted contextualized embeddings from Korean-English bilingual LLMs for Korean-to-English and English-to-Korean translation pairs. Combinations of LLMs, embedding types, dimensionality, and orthogonal transformations across 100 train-test splits yielded 4000 distinct embedding spaces in each source language. In each space, we computed the semantic similarity between each source word and its nearest neighbor in the target language, and compared their distribution for gap words versus non-gap words. In 94% (Korean-to-English) and 97% (English-to-Korean) of embedding spaces, gap words showed weaker cross-lingual semantic alignment than non-gap words. Logistic classifiers trained on unaligned embedding spaces can reliably separate gap words from non-gap words, achieving AUCs of 0.81 (Korean-to-English) and 0.76 (English-to-Korean) and retrieving 18/19 Korean and 26/27 English gap words. This approach provides a language-agnostic and taxonomy-free method for scalable lexical gap identification.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a data-driven, language-agnostic method to automatically detect lexical gaps between languages using contextualized embeddings from bilingual LLMs, showing that gap words systematically exhibit weaker cross-lingual semantic alignment than non-gap words.
AI.motivation: Lexical gaps—concepts expressed by a single word in one language but lacking a direct one-word equivalent in another—make it difficult to build multilingual lexicons, perform accurate machine translation, and enable robust cross-lingual transfer. Existing approaches often depend on human judgments or predefined conceptual taxonomies, which are labor-intensive, language-specific, and not easily scalable. The authors are motivated to develop an automatic, scalable, and taxonomy-free way to identify such lexical gaps using modern multilingual language models.
AI.method: The authors use Korean–English bilingual large language models to obtain contextualized embeddings for aligned translation pairs in both directions (Korean→English, English→Korean). For each direction, they systematically vary the choice of LLM, embedding type, dimensionality reduction, and orthogonal transformations, producing 4000 distinct cross-lingual embedding spaces per source language across 100 train–test splits. Within each space, they compute semantic similarity between each source word and its nearest neighbor in the target language. They then compare similarity distributions for known lexical gap words versus non-gap words. Finally, they train logistic regression classifiers on these features in the unaligned spaces to distinguish gap from non-gap words and evaluate performance via AUC and retrieval of known gap words.
AI.result: Across the 4000 embedding spaces, lexical gap words consistently show weaker cross-lingual semantic alignment than non-gap words in the vast majority of cases: 94% of spaces for Korean→English and 97% for English→Korean. Logistic classifiers trained on the unaligned embedding spaces successfully separate gap from non-gap words, reaching AUCs of 0.81 (Korean→English) and 0.76 (English→Korean). The method recovers almost all documented gaps in their evaluation sets: 18 out of 19 Korean gaps and 26 out of 27 English gaps.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that cross-lingual lexical gaps manifest as systematically lower semantic alignment in bilingual LLM embedding spaces and that this signal can be exploited for automatic detection. Their data-driven framework, which does not rely on human-crafted taxonomies, provides a scalable, language-agnostic approach to identifying lexical gaps. This opens the door to more comprehensive multilingual lexical resources and may improve machine translation and cross-lingual NLP applications by explicitly modeling where direct lexical correspondences are missing.

title: Spacetime Formation under Requirements: Contextual Realization and Form-Dependent Probability
summary:
Quantum cognition often explains order effects, contextuality, and violations of the law of total probability by replacing classical probability with quantum probability on a fixed event structure. This paper proposes a different interpretation: quantum probability is the fixed-spacetime projection of contextual spacetime formation under finite-state requirements.
  The framework begins not with time, space, objects, or probabilities, but with requirements such as finite representational capacity, single-state semantic stability, context-sensitive intervention, avoidance of explicit context labels, coherent world-formation, and intersubjective transformability. When these requirements cannot be realized within a single global Boolean event structure, the mismatch appears, under fixed-spacetime projection, as noncommutativity, interference, and quantum-like probability.
  Building on prior single-state approaches to contextuality, we reinterpret classical contextual bookkeeping cost as the fixed-spacetime shadow of contextual spacetime formation. Auxiliary memory or context labels in a classical representation correspond, in this account, to holonomy-like mismatch among locally Boolean logic-worlds. The interference term is the cross term generated when locally classical realization contributions are nontrivially glued and projected back into a fixed classical spacetime form.
  The result is a transcendental-operational realist account: objecthood, eventhood, probability, and spacetime are treated as forms of realization under requirements, while objectivity is defined by invariants preserved across observer- and history-dependent spacetime formations.

AI.tldr: The paper reinterprets quantum probability effects (like order effects and interference) as artifacts of projecting richer, context-dependent spacetime formations into a fixed classical spacetime under finite-state constraints.
AI.motivation: Standard quantum cognition models import quantum probability on a fixed event space to explain cognitive phenomena (order effects, contextuality, law-of-total-probability violations). The authors suspect this still presupposes a fixed spacetime/event structure and want a deeper account where such structures themselves emerge from operational and representational constraints, explaining why quantum-like patterns appear without positing fundamental quantum states of mind.
AI.method: They adopt a single-state, transcendental-operational framework that starts from high-level requirements (finite representational capacity, single-state semantic stability, context-sensitive intervention, avoidance of explicit context tags, coherent world-formation, and intersubjective transformability) rather than from pre-given times, spaces, objects, or probabilities. They analyze how attempts to satisfy these requirements across contexts with only local Boolean event structures lead to mismatches when globally represented. These mismatches, when projected into a fixed classical spacetime/event structure, mathematically manifest as noncommutativity, interference terms, and quantum-like probability. They reinterpret classical contextual bookkeeping (extra memory or labels) as holonomy-like structure among locally Boolean logic-worlds and show how cross terms from gluing these local structures yield the interference term.
AI.result: They provide a conceptual and mathematical reinterpretation of quantum probabilistic effects in cognition: quantum probability is not fundamental but is the fixed-spacetime projection of a more primitive process of contextual spacetime formation constrained by finite-state and operational requirements. Context labels and auxiliary memory in classical models correspond to holonomy-like structure between locally classical worlds, and the interference term arises from cross contributions when these are glued and then projected into a fixed event space.
AI.conclusion: Quantum cognition phenomena can be understood without postulating inherently quantum mental states: quantum probability emerges as the shadow of context-dependent spacetime formation under finite representational and operational constraints. Objectivity is reconceived as invariance across different observer- and history-dependent spacetime formations, and notions like object, event, probability, and spacetime are treated as realization-dependent forms rather than pre-given foundations.

title: End-to-End Intracortical Speech Decoding from Neural Activity
summary: Current high-performing intracortical speech neuroprostheses achieve low word error rates but typically rely on external language models during inference, increasing memory, computation, and latency. In this work, we investigate whether meaningful character-level decoding is achievable without such models. We propose an end-to-end Conformer-based neural decoder trained directly on intracortical recordings from a participant with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). Without any external language model, the system achieves a character error rate (CER) of 23.80\% on held-out validation data. Analysis shows that performance variability is driven by inter-session signal degradation, while dominant errors arise from incorrect word boundary segmentation. These results demonstrate that effective character-level decoding is possible in a fully end-to-end framework, providing a strong neural signal for downstream linguistic processing.

AI.tldr: They build an end-to-end character-level decoder from intracortical signals that works without any external language model and still achieves reasonably low error rates.
AI.motivation: Existing intracortical speech neuroprostheses depend on external language models during inference, which adds memory, computation, and latency overhead. It’s unclear if meaningful decoding is possible directly from neural signals without this crutch.
AI.method: They design an end-to-end Conformer-based neural network that maps intracortical recordings from an ALS participant directly to character sequences, trained without an external language model. They then evaluate character error rate on held-out validation data and analyze performance variability and error types.
AI.result: The model achieves a 23.80% character error rate on held-out validation data without using any external language model. They find that performance varies mainly due to inter-session signal degradation, and the main type of error is incorrect word boundary segmentation.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end character-level decoding from intracortical recordings is feasible without external language models, yielding a strong neural representation that can be used for subsequent language processing, though robustness across sessions and word boundary handling remain key challenges.

title: Right-Sizing Communication and Recommendation Set Size in AI-Assisted Search
summary:
We model the interaction between a user and an AI driven recommendation system. The user initiates the process by conveying preference information through a costly and noisy message. The AI assistant, acting as a Bayesian agent, interprets the user's message to form a posterior belief about their true preferences and make product recommendations. In particular, it determines how many recommendations to present so as to maximize the user's expected utility from their final choice, while accounting for the search cost induced by the size of the recommendation set. We use mutual information based cost functions to model the two distinct costs incurred by the user during the interaction: (i) a communication cost, which increases with the precision of their preference message, and (ii) a search cost, which increases with the size of the recommendation set provided by the AI assistant.
  We study products and preferences which live in d dimensional space, and ask how the user's expected payoff can be maximized. For large d, we characterize how optimal message precision and recommendation set size depend on the cost parameters, under two distinct distributions from which recommendations can be sampled from the product universe: (i) Bayes' posterior belief, and (ii) an optimized tilted distribution. Under the posterior sampling scheme (i), we identify a hybrid regime, in which an efficient interaction policy requires jointly optimizing the amount of information (in bits) conveyed by the user and the number of recommendations provided by the AI assistant. In the tilted sampling scheme (ii), our results show that the optimal interaction policy uses only one of communication and search, favoring whichever of them is less costly.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how a user and an AI recommender should optimally share information and recommendations when both communication and search are costly, using an information-theoretic model.
AI.motivation: In AI recommendation systems, users must communicate their preferences and then search through suggested options. Both steps are costly: specifying preferences precisely can be effortful, and evaluating many recommendations can be time-consuming or cognitively taxing. The authors want to formally understand how precise the user’s initial message should be and how many items the AI should recommend, especially in high-dimensional product spaces, and how this depends on the structure of costs and on how recommendations are sampled.
AI.method: They model the user as sending a noisy, costly preference signal and the AI assistant as a Bayesian agent forming a posterior over the user’s preferences. Costs are modeled via mutual information: communication cost scales with how informative the user’s message is about their true preferences; search cost scales with the size of the recommendation set. In a d-dimensional product space, and for large d, they analyze optimal message precision and recommendation set size under two recommendation schemes: (i) sampling items from the posterior over preferred products; (ii) sampling from an optimized tilted distribution over products. They derive asymptotic characterizations of optimal policies as functions of the cost parameters in each scheme.
AI.result: For posterior-based sampling, they identify a hybrid optimal regime where both communication and search are used nontrivially: the user sends a partially informative message, and the AI returns a finite, nontrivial number of recommendations that jointly maximize expected user payoff net of costs. The optimal precision and list length depend delicately on the relative magnitudes of communication and search costs. For the tilted-distribution scheme, the optimal policy is corner-like: it uses only one channel of effort—either richer communication and a small recommendation set, or minimal communication and a larger recommendation set—depending on which type of cost is cheaper, effectively substituting communication and search against each other.
AI.conclusion: The structure of the recommendation sampling distribution fundamentally changes how users and AI systems should trade off communication and search. Under natural posterior sampling, optimal interaction requires a joint interior optimization over message precision and list size, implying that systems should carefully balance elicitation and recommendation volume. Under optimized tilted sampling, the system instead chooses to rely almost exclusively on whichever of communication or search is less costly. This offers design guidance for AI recommenders: the way items are sampled and presented determines whether interaction policies should balance or specialize in communication versus search effort.

title: Distinguishing Right from Wrong in Debates: Attribution Analysis of Chinese Harmful Memes
summary: Research on harmful meme detection has garnered significant attention, resulting in the development of numerous datasets and methods. However, progress in detecting Chinese harmful memes lags considerably, primarily due to two challenges: first, accurately assessing a meme's harmfulness depends heavily on understanding deep cultural context; second, many memes are semantically ambiguous, making harmfulness highly subjective. To address these issues, we focus on the interpretable detection of Chinese harmful memes by constructing the first Chinese harmful meme explanation dataset, Ex-ToxiCN-MM. This dataset offers opposing interpretations, categorized as "harmful" and "non-harmful", for each meme, aiming to rigorously evaluate a model's ability to discern and comprehend ambiguous, culturally grounded content. We built a specialized knowledge base of Chinese cultural concepts and offensive vocabulary to supply models with essential prior knowledge (C-HarmKB). To address the ambiguity and lack of background knowledge in meme attribution, we have developed a comprehensive attribution analysis framework, RIKE, which includes an Attribution Knowledge Enhancement module (AKE) and a Relative Intent Reasoning module (RIR). Extensive quantitative and qualitative experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms mainstream baseline models across multiple metrics in the task of attributing harmful memes in Chinese.
