The code, Ex-ToxiCN-MM dataset, and Chinese Harmful Semantic Knowledge Base (C-HarmKB) involved in this study have been open-sourced at https://github.com/wimiw123/Ex-ToxiCN-MM

AI.tldr: They build the first Chinese harmful meme explanation dataset plus a knowledge base and a reasoning framework to better, and more interpretably, detect harmful Chinese memes.
AI.motivation: Chinese harmful meme detection lags behind English because it requires deep cultural knowledge and harmfulness is often ambiguous and subjective. Existing work lacks interpretability and cannot fully handle cultural context and opposing readings of the same meme.
AI.method: 1) Construct Ex-ToxiCN-MM, the first Chinese harmful meme explanation dataset, where each meme is paired with two opposing explanations: one labeling it harmful and one non-harmful, to stress-test models on ambiguity and cultural grounding. 2) Build C-HarmKB, a specialized knowledge base of Chinese cultural concepts and offensive vocabulary to provide external priors. 3) Propose RIKE, an attribution analysis framework that includes: (a) Attribution Knowledge Enhancement (AKE) to inject and align knowledge from C-HarmKB; (b) Relative Intent Reasoning (RIR) to reason between harmful vs non-harmful interpretations and attribute intent. 4) Evaluate against mainstream baselines with both quantitative metrics and qualitative case analyses.
AI.result: The proposed RIKE framework, equipped with Ex-ToxiCN-MM and C-HarmKB, achieves better performance than mainstream baseline models on multiple metrics for harmful meme attribution in Chinese. Experiments show improved handling of ambiguous and culturally loaded memes, and more interpretable attributions compared with baselines.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating explicit cultural and offensive-knowledge resources plus relative intent reasoning substantially improves both performance and interpretability in detecting harmful Chinese memes. The released Ex-ToxiCN-MM dataset, C-HarmKB, and code provide a new benchmark and toolkit for future research on nuanced, culturally grounded harmful content detection in Chinese memes.

title: Accelerating Long-Tail Generation in Synchronous RLHF Training via Adaptive Tensor Parallelism
summary: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) has become a key post-training paradigm for improving model quality. However, the synchronous three-stage RLHF pipeline is often bottlenecked by the generation stage, where response-length skew causes the effective batch size to shrink rapidly during decoding, leaving GPUs underutilized while a few long responses remain unfinished. Mainstream frameworks employ a static tensor parallelism (TP) configuration that cannot adapt to changing batch characteristics, leaving substantial performance headroom unexplored. We propose PAT, an adaptive TP method that dynamically reconfigures TP during the generation stage of each RLHF iteration. PAT introduces two key techniques. First, a predictor-guided online reconfiguration method decides both the reconfiguration point and the target TP configuration based on offline profiling, triggering reconfiguration only when the predicted latency benefit outweighs the reconfiguration overhead. Second, a lightweight online reconfiguration mechanism updates only the states and layouts affected by TP changes: it adapts unfinished decoding states through a cost-model-based choice between KV-cache migration and recomputation, performs in-place weight resharding, and reuses cached communication groups. We implement PAT on top of SGLang and integrate it with the VeRL framework. Evaluations on LLaMA3.1-8B and Qwen3-14B using DeepScaleR show that PAT reduces generation latency by up to 34.6% and end-to-end RLHF training iteration latency by up to 27.2% compared to the original VeRL setup.

AI.tldr: PAT is an adaptive tensor-parallelism scheme that dynamically reconfigures tensor parallelism during RLHF generation to cut latency.
AI.motivation: Standard RLHF training uses a synchronous 3-stage pipeline whose generation phase is slow and GPU-inefficient because response lengths vary widely. As decoding proceeds, many sequences finish early, the effective batch size collapses, and static tensor-parallel configurations cannot exploit the changing workload, leaving GPUs underutilized and limiting throughput. There is a need for a system that can adapt tensor-parallelism to the evolving batch to improve performance.
AI.method: PAT introduces an adaptive tensor parallelism framework that dynamically alters the TP configuration during the generation stage of each RLHF iteration. It has two main techniques: (1) a predictor-guided online reconfiguration policy, built from offline profiling, that selects when to reconfigure and what TP layout to use so that expected latency savings exceed the overhead; (2) a lightweight reconfiguration mechanism that minimizes runtime cost by only updating state affected by TP changes, using a cost model to choose between migrating vs recomputing KV caches, doing in-place weight resharding, and reusing pre-built communication groups. PAT is implemented on SGLang and integrated into the VeRL RLHF framework.
AI.result: On LLaMA3.1-8B and Qwen3-14B models trained with DeepScaleR via VeRL, PAT achieves up to 34.6% reduction in generation latency and up to 27.2% reduction in overall RLHF iteration latency compared to the unmodified VeRL setup. These gains demonstrate substantial performance headroom from adaptive TP without changing model architecture or RL algorithms.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive tensor parallelism tailored to the dynamics of RLHF generation can significantly reduce latency and better utilize GPUs. By combining profiling-driven prediction of when/where to reconfigure with low-overhead state transformation mechanisms, PAT unlocks large performance gains over static TP in practical RLHF training, suggesting that fine-grained, workload-aware parallelism reconfiguration is a promising direction for large-model training systems.

title: How Much Structure Do LLMs Need? Evaluating LLMs for Bibliometric Cluster Description
summary: Large language models (LLMs) can support scientific literature synthesis, but remain prone to hallucinated references, uneven coverage, and weakly grounded thematic organization. We evaluate whether bibliometric structure improves LLM-assisted synthesis by comparing six pipelines for generating cluster descriptions under different levels of evidence and structure. Using 100 published bibliometric analyses, we reconstruct Scopus corpora, extract human-written cluster descriptions, and assess outputs by human alignment, semantic coverage, clustering quality, graph quality, and reference grounding. Results show that LLMs produce descriptions semantically close to human-written ones, but are unreliable when asked to infer bibliometric structure from scratch. Performance improves when bibliometric algorithms define the clusters and the LLM interprets them. Overall, LLM-assisted bibliometric synthesis is most promising as a hybrid workflow in which algorithms provide auditable structure and LLMs generate readable descriptions.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how adding explicit bibliometric structure affects the quality of LLM-generated descriptions of scientific literature clusters, finding that hybrid workflows where algorithms define clusters and LLMs describe them work best.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used to summarize and synthesize scientific literatures, but they suffer from hallucinated references, inconsistent topical coverage, and poorly grounded thematic organization. The authors want to know whether incorporating explicit bibliometric structure—such as citation-based clustering—can mitigate these problems and make LLM-assisted literature reviews more reliable and aligned with human-written analyses.
AI.method: The authors use 100 published bibliometric analyses as a benchmark. For each, they reconstruct the underlying Scopus document corpora, derive clusters via bibliometric methods, and collect the original human-written cluster descriptions. They then design six different LLM-based pipelines that vary in how much evidence and structure is provided to the model—from letting the LLM infer structure itself to giving it algorithmically defined clusters and metadata. They evaluate the resulting cluster descriptions along several dimensions: similarity to human descriptions, semantic coverage of the cluster content, clustering and graph quality, and how well references are grounded in the underlying data.
AI.result: LLMs can generate cluster descriptions that are semantically similar to human-written ones when given sufficient structure and evidence. However, when tasked with inferring bibliometric structure or clusters from scratch, the models perform unreliably and can misrepresent the literature. Performance substantially improves when bibliometric algorithms first define clusters and relationships, and the LLM’s role is limited to interpreting and narrating these structures. Across metrics, hybrid pipelines outperform purely LLM-driven ones.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that LLMs are not yet dependable as end-to-end tools for constructing bibliometric structures or fully autonomous literature syntheses. Their strengths lie in producing fluent, readable, and thematically coherent descriptions once robust, auditable bibliometric structure is provided. Therefore, the most effective and trustworthy use of LLMs in bibliometric synthesis is a hybrid workflow: algorithms handle clustering and graph construction, while LLMs generate human-friendly, grounded narrative summaries of the resulting structures.

title: Stop Comparing LLM Agents Without Disclosing the Harness
summary: This position paper argues that, for long-horizon tasks evaluated across models with comparable frontier capability, the agent execution harness, namely the infrastructure layer that governs context construction, tool interaction, orchestration, and verification around a language model, is often a stronger determinant of agent performance than the model it wraps. We formalize and defend the Binding Constraint Thesis: in this regime, performance variance is governed more by harness configuration than by model choice, and current evaluation protocols therefore systematically misattribute harness-level gains to model improvements. We support this thesis along three lines. First, a control-theoretic formalization treats the harness as the controller of a closed-loop dynamical system and the LLM as the stochastic policy it governs, which explains why small harness changes can produce performance shifts that exceed those obtained by substituting one model for another. Second, published benchmarks, industry deployments, and a controlled variance decomposition show that harness-induced variance can substantially exceed model-induced variance, including cases of model ranking reversal. Third, we propose a harness-aware evaluation framework with a disclosure standard and a variance decomposition protocol. Until harness specifications are disclosed, leaderboard comparisons for long-horizon agents should be treated as incomplete and potentially misleading.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that in long-horizon tasks, the design of the agent execution harness (infrastructure around an LLM) matters more for performance than the specific language model, and that current evaluations wrongly credit gains to models instead of harnesses.
AI.motivation: As LLM-based agents are increasingly used for complex, multi-step tasks, benchmarks and leaderboards typically attribute performance differences to the underlying models. However, these agents depend heavily on an execution harness that manages context, tools, and verification. The authors are motivated by the concern that current evaluation practices overlook and conflate harness effects with model quality, leading to misinterpretation of progress and misleading comparisons between systems.
AI.method: The authors (1) develop a control-theoretic formalization of LLM agents, modeling the harness as a controller in a closed-loop dynamical system and the LLM as a stochastic policy; (2) examine evidence from published benchmarks, real-world industry systems, and a controlled variance decomposition experiment to separate harness-induced from model-induced performance variance; and (3) design a harness-aware evaluation framework including a disclosure standard for reporting harness configurations and a protocol for decomposing performance variance between harness and model.
AI.result: Their analysis shows that, in the regime of long-horizon tasks with similarly strong models, small changes in the harness can drive performance gains larger than those obtained by swapping between frontier models, and can even reverse model rankings. The variance decomposition indicates that harness configuration often contributes more to outcome variability than model choice itself. They also derive a principled way to conceptualize harness effects using control theory.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that for long-horizon agent evaluations, the agent execution harness is frequently the binding constraint on performance, not the LLM. As a result, many current leaderboards and benchmark results are incomplete and potentially misleading because they fail to account for or disclose harness configurations. The authors advocate adopting harness-aware evaluation practices, mandatory disclosure of harness details, and variance decomposition methods so that future comparisons properly distinguish between model and harness contributions.

title: Structure-Aware RAG: Structured Retrieval Augmented Generation from Noisy Data for Conversational Agents
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have been widely adopted in conversational applications. However, their reliance on parametric knowledge limits reliability in real-world scenarios that require dynamic or domain-specific information. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) addresses this limitation by incorporating external knowledge during generation, but existing text-based and graph-based RAG methods often struggle with noisy or irrelevant contexts. In this work, we propose Structure-aware Retrieval Augmented Generation (SA-RAG), which uses tables as an intermediate structured representation to provide a compact and controllable interface that reduces noise while preserving essential information. We introduce a quality-aware table metadata generation framework that models metadata normalization and effectiveness, improving metadata quality and downstream performance. Furthermore, we explore both training-free and training-based table generation methods. Generation validation and direct preference optimization further improve table quality while maintaining semantic and structural consistency. Experiments on two noisy real-world datasets show that SA-RAG significantly outperforms existing RAG baselines. Our code is publicly available at a public repository.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SA-RAG, a structure-aware RAG framework that uses tables as an intermediate representation to reduce noise and improve reliability in retrieval-augmented generation.
AI.motivation: Traditional LLMs rely on static parametric knowledge and struggle with dynamic or domain-specific information. Existing RAG methods, whether text-based or graph-based, often retrieve noisy or irrelevant context, which harms answer quality in real-world, noisy settings. The authors aim to create a more compact, controllable, and robust way to inject external knowledge into LLMs.
AI.method: SA-RAG introduces tables as an intermediate structured representation between retrieved documents and the generator. The system constructs tables that distill essential information from retrieved content, using a quality-aware metadata generation framework that models both normalization and effectiveness of metadata to improve table quality. It explores training-free and training-based approaches for table construction, and applies generation validation plus direct preference optimization to refine tables while preserving semantic and structural consistency.
AI.result: On two noisy real-world datasets, SA-RAG achieves significantly better performance than standard text-based and graph-based RAG baselines, demonstrating improved robustness and answer quality under noisy retrieval conditions.
AI.conclusion: Structure-aware intermediate tables provide a compact, controllable interface for RAG that reduces contextual noise without losing key information. By improving metadata quality and table generation, SA-RAG yields more reliable LLM outputs and outperforms existing RAG approaches on noisy real-world tasks.

title: Side-by-side Comparison Amplifies Dialect Bias in Language Models
comment: In proceeding at ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency 2026
summary: Language models (LMs) can exhibit systematic biases against speakers based on variations in their dialects, even in the absence of a dialect label, a behavior known as covert dialect bias. In this work, we quantify covert dialect bias in online discourse by evaluating how LMs associate stereotypical traits (derived from social psychology research on racial bias) with intent-equivalent tweets in Standard American English (SAE) and African-American Vernacular English (AAVE). While prior work shows that LMs associate more negative stereotypes with AAVE when evaluating tweets in isolation, we are surprised to find that this bias is significantly exacerbated when SAE / AAVE tweet pairs are compared side by side, a setting that more closely reflects high-impact decision making contexts in which models are used to rank candidates. The bias only worsens when dialect labels are explicitly specified. This is striking, given the extensive efforts from commercial developers to mitigate bias in their LMs. Encouragingly, we show that counterfactual fairness finetuning can mitigate covert dialect bias for some stereotypical traits, reducing average disparities when evaluating tweets in isolation, however, these improvements do not consistently hold across traits when evaluating SAE / AAVE tweets side by side. Our findings show that existing evaluation settings for covert dialect bias may underestimate its severity, specifically in contrastive settings. Additionally, overt dialect bias remains pronounced even after safety aligned finetuning, indicating that it remains an unresolved problem, and motivates the need for more robust evaluation and mitigation frameworks.

AI.tldr: The paper uncovers and measures how language models display hidden (covert) and explicit (overt) biases against different English dialects, especially AAVE, and shows that current safety tuning and evaluation methods underestimate and insufficiently mitigate these harms.
AI.motivation: Language models are increasingly used in high‑stakes, ranking-based tasks, but there is growing evidence that they treat text written in different dialects unequally. Prior studies mainly evaluate bias on single, isolated sentences and often only implicitly, which may not reflect real deployment scenarios where models compare and rank multiple candidates. There is a need to rigorously quantify how much dialect bias worsens in more realistic, contrastive settings and to test whether existing mitigation strategies and safety alignment are actually effective.
AI.method: The authors construct pairs of tweets that are intent-equivalent but written in two dialects: Standard American English (SAE) and African-American Vernacular English (AAVE). They draw stereotypical trait dimensions from social psychology literature on racial bias and prompt language models to associate these traits with each tweet. They evaluate bias in two main settings: (1) tweets evaluated in isolation and (2) SAE/AAVE tweet pairs evaluated side by side, mimicking ranking or selection scenarios. They additionally test conditions with and without explicit dialect labels and apply counterfactual fairness finetuning to assess whether such techniques can reduce covert dialect bias across traits and evaluation modes.
AI.result: Compared to prior findings that LMs already associate more negative stereotypes with AAVE in isolation, the study finds that bias is significantly amplified when SAE/AAVE tweets are evaluated contrastively, i.e., side by side. Adding explicit dialect labels further worsens this disparity. Counterfactual fairness finetuning can partially mitigate covert dialect bias in the single-tweet setting and for some stereotype dimensions, but its effectiveness is inconsistent and largely breaks down in the side-by-side, contrastive evaluation. Safety-aligned commercial models still display strong overt dialect bias even after mitigation efforts.
AI.conclusion: Current benchmarks that measure dialect bias only on isolated inputs underestimate how severe covert dialect bias is in realistic, contrastive decision contexts. Explicit dialect information can aggravate discrimination, and existing safety-tuning or counterfactual-fairness finetuning approaches are insufficient and brittle across settings and traits. The work argues for rethinking evaluation to include contrastive and ranking-like scenarios, and for developing more robust, dialect-aware mitigation frameworks to address both covert and overt dialect bias in language models.

title: Machine Psychometrics: A Mathematical Psychology of Artificial Intelligence
summary: Artificial agents now generate behavior rich enough to invite trust, surprise, and concern, yet our evaluation tools still privilege capability scores over psychological structure. This paper argues that the philosophical impasse between two symmetrical errors (Artificial Mind Blindness, which dismisses psychological organization in non-biological systems, and Artificial Mind Projection, which infers human-like inner life from fluent behavior alone) can be circumvented not by resolving the consciousness question, but by introducing a disciplined measurement layer beneath it. Drawing on Michael Levin's continuum view of cognition as goal-directed competency across substrates, and on the methodological repertoire of mathematical psychology (Item Response Theory, Signal Detection Theory, Bayesian cognitive modeling, calibration analysis, cognitive-bias batteries), the paper develops Machine Psychometrics as a measurement science of latent behavioral, metacognitive, communicative, and self-modeling dispositions in artificial agents. Its operational core is the Machine Mindprint: a multidimensional, domain-bounded, versioned profile spanning calibration, source integrity, suggestibility resistance, context stability, expressive alignment, tool integrity, drift monitoring, and distributional grounding. A complementary Trust Protocol turns Mindprints into deployment decisions through probe batteries, perturbation testing, reliability and validity analysis, and longitudinal monitoring across high-stakes domains. The philosophical contribution is a third stance, Artificial Mind Discipline, that neither anthropomorphizes nor dismisses, neither presupposes consciousness nor forecloses it. The aim is not to humanize artificial agents, but to understand them precisely because they are not human, through measurement before judgment.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new field, Machine Psychometrics, to systematically measure and profile the psychological-like dispositions of artificial agents, enabling more disciplined trust and deployment decisions without assuming they are conscious or human-like.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation of AI systems focuses on capabilities and benchmarks, ignoring their underlying psychological-like organization and leading to two mirrored philosophical errors: dismissing any mind-like structure in machines (Artificial Mind Blindness) or over-attributing human-like minds based on fluent behavior (Artificial Mind Projection). There is a need for a principled way to understand, compare, and regulate AI behavior that does not depend on solving the hard problem of machine consciousness.
AI.method: The paper synthesizes Michael Levin’s continuum view of cognition with tools from mathematical psychology (Item Response Theory, Signal Detection Theory, Bayesian cognitive modeling, calibration analysis, cognitive-bias batteries). It formalizes Machine Psychometrics as a measurement framework for latent dispositions in artificial agents, centered on constructing and analyzing a multidimensional ‘Machine Mindprint’ via structured tests, probes, and perturbations, then linking these profiles to deployment choices through a ‘Trust Protocol’ that uses reliability/validity analysis and longitudinal monitoring.
AI.result: It defines a concrete construct, the Machine Mindprint, with dimensions such as calibration, source integrity, suggestibility resistance, context stability, expressive alignment, tool integrity, drift monitoring, and distributional grounding. It also sketches a Trust Protocol for converting measurement outcomes into domain-specific deployment and oversight strategies. Conceptually, it shows how established psychometric and cognitive-modeling techniques can be adapted to AI systems to yield stable, interpretable behavioral profiles rather than ad hoc benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Machine Psychometrics and the Machine Mindprint offer a disciplined, measurement-first way to study artificial agents’ behavioral and metacognitive dispositions without presuming or denying consciousness. This supports a new philosophical stance—Artificial Mind Discipline—that avoids both anthropomorphic overreach and mechanistic dismissal, and provides a practical basis for trust, governance, and ongoing monitoring of AI systems in high-stakes contexts.

title: SEAL: Synergistic Co-Evolution of Agents and Learning Environments
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) agents are increasingly improved through interaction, yet most self-evolution methods adapt either the policy or the learning environment in isolation. We identify this structural gap as \emph{Agent-Environment Misalignment}: the agent's capability frontier changes during training, while the environment that provides supervision remains static or only weakly coupled to the agent's revealed failures. We propose SEAL, a closed-loop co-evolution framework for interactive tool-use agents. SEAL collects on-policy trajectories under executable verification, diagnoses failed rollouts into turn-level failure labels, and uses these diagnoses as a shared signal for both environment-side adaptation and model-side policy optimization. The environment evolves its training-time learning interface by exposing clearer tool affordance cues, constraint information, and recovery-oriented feedback, while the policy is updated with diagnosis-guided advantage reweighting. Extensive experiments across in-distribution and out-of-distribution multi-turn tool-use evaluations show that SEAL improves low-resource agent learning: with only 400 training samples, it yields +8.25 to +26.25 average-point gains across three backbones and exhibits positive out-of-distribution transfer. These results demonstrate the value of jointly adapting the learner and its training-time learning substrate for robust self-improving LLM agents.

AI.tldr: SEAL is a co-evolution framework that jointly adapts LLM agents and their training environments to reduce agent-environment misalignment in multi-turn tool-use tasks, achieving strong gains with few training samples.
AI.motivation: Most existing self-evolution approaches for LLM agents update either the policy or the environment independently, causing a mismatch because the agent’s capabilities change while the supervision environment remains static or only weakly responsive to new failure modes. This agent-environment misalignment limits the effectiveness and robustness of interactive, tool-using LLM agents, especially in low-resource training regimes. The paper aims to create a principled framework where both the agent and its learning environment co-evolve based on shared diagnostic signals from real interaction failures.
AI.method: The authors introduce SEAL, a closed-loop co-evolution framework for interactive tool-use agents. SEAL runs on-policy rollouts that are checked via executable verification, then automatically diagnoses each failed trajectory into fine-grained, turn-level failure labels. These labels are used as a unified signal for two coupled processes: (1) environment-side adaptation, which updates the training-time interface by adding clearer cues about tool affordances, constraints, and recovery-oriented feedback; and (2) model-side learning, where policy optimization leverages the diagnosis through diagnosis-guided advantage reweighting to emphasize learning from informative failures and successes. The system is evaluated on multi-turn tool-use benchmarks in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings, under low-sample training conditions.
AI.result: Across multiple multi-turn tool-use evaluations, SEAL yields substantial performance improvements in low-resource settings, using only 400 training samples. It provides average gains of +8.25 to +26.25 points across three different backbone models. The framework also shows positive out-of-distribution transfer, indicating that agents trained with SEAL generalize better to unseen tool-use tasks compared to baselines that do not jointly adapt the agent and training environment.
AI.conclusion: Jointly adapting both the agent policy and its training-time environment based on shared failure diagnoses reduces agent-environment misalignment and leads to more robust, data-efficient self-improvement for LLM tool-use agents. SEAL demonstrates that a closed-loop co-evolution framework can substantially enhance performance and generalization, suggesting that future self-improving LLM systems should treat the learning substrate and the learner as a coupled system rather than optimizing them in isolation.

title: From Accuracy to Auditability: A Survey of Determinism in Financial AI Systems
summary: Deploying machine learning in regulated financial environments -- credit risk, fraud detection, and anti-money laundering -- exposes critical vulnerabilities in algorithmic reproducibility. While early financial ML addressed statistical challenges such as backtest overfitting, deep neural networks and Generative AI have introduced mechanical nondeterminism rooted in hardware and architecture. This survey provides a systems perspective on reproducibility failures across three modalities now dominant in financial AI: tabular models (post-hoc explanation variance), graph networks (stochastic sampling and temporal asynchrony), and LLM-based agentic workflows (batch-dependent divergence and trajectory drift). We supplement the literature analysis with first-party experiments on public financial datasets -- quantifying explanation rank instability in credit scoring, prediction flip rates in GNN-based fraud detection, and tensor-parallel-induced output divergence in LLM entity extraction. We propose a layered evaluation framework linking modality-specific metrics (RBO, D_cos, TDI, PSD) to audit readiness, and empirically validate the complementarity of logit-level and semantic-level determinism measures.

AI.tldr: Survey of reproducibility failures in financial ML and a proposed evaluation framework.
AI.motivation: To address critical vulnerabilities in algorithmic reproducibility when deploying modern ML (especially DNNs and Generative AI) in regulated financial settings like credit risk, fraud detection, and AML.
AI.method: Literature survey from a systems perspective across three financial AI modalities (tabular, graph, LLM agents), plus first-party experiments on public financial datasets measuring explanation rank instability, prediction flip rates, and tensor-parallel-induced divergence.
AI.result: Finds concrete reproducibility failures: unstable post-hoc explanations in credit scoring, high prediction flip rates in GNN-based fraud detection, and batch/parallelism-dependent output divergence in LLM-based entity extraction; quantifies these using specific metrics (RBO, D_cos, TDI, PSD).
AI.conclusion: Proposes a layered evaluation framework that connects modality-specific reproducibility metrics to audit readiness and shows that combining logit-level and semantic-level determinism metrics provides complementary assurance about system reproducibility.

title: Found in Conversation: LLMs Teach Themselves to Close the Multi-Turn Gap
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) interactions are typically underspecified, with users clarifying all necessary details across multiple conversational turns. Yet recent work shows that LLMs perform far worse in this multi-turn setting than in a single turn with same information being available at once, a phenomenon termed "Lost-in-Conversation." However, bridging this gap effectively remains an open problem. Here we introduce Found in Conversation (FiC), a training framework where a model teaches itself to find and recover its single-turn competence given underspecified multi-turn prompts. We develop View-Asymmetric Self-Distillation, which distills across two views of the same task information--single-turn view for the teacher, multi-turn view for the student--transferring strong single-turn behavior into weak multi-turn behavior. This requires no stronger external teacher, which is unavailable as even frontier LLMs exhibit this gap. Across model families (Llama, Qwen, Phi, and OLMo) and sizes (3B-14B), FiC recovers at least 92% of single-turn performance and reaches 100% on two Llama backbones, yielding more efficient and helpful multi-turn conversations with single-turn capabilities intact.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Found in Conversation (FiC), a self-distillation training framework that makes large language models recover their strong single-turn performance when operating in realistic multi-turn conversations.
AI.motivation: LLMs show significantly worse performance in multi-turn conversations than in single-turn prompts that contain the same total information, a phenomenon called "Lost-in-Conversation." This is problematic because real-world LLM use is predominantly interactive and multi-turn. The authors aim to close this performance gap without relying on an even stronger external teacher model, which does not exist since current frontier models also suffer from the same issue.
AI.method: They introduce Found in Conversation (FiC), built around View-Asymmetric Self-Distillation. The idea is to create two views of the same task: a single-turn view, where all information is presented at once and the model acts as a teacher, and a multi-turn view, where information is underspecified and spread across turns, and the same model acts as a student. By distilling the teacher’s strong single-turn behavior into the student’s multi-turn behavior, the model learns to "find" and recover its single-turn competence in conversational settings. This process uses only the model itself and specially constructed training data, requiring no stronger external teacher.
AI.result: Applied across several open model families (Llama, Qwen, Phi, OLMo) and sizes (3B–14B), FiC recovers at least 92% of each model’s original single-turn performance when evaluated in multi-turn conditions. For two Llama backbones, FiC closes the gap entirely, reaching 100% of single-turn performance in multi-turn dialogue. The improved models generate more efficient and helpful responses in conversations without degrading their original single-turn capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The Lost-in-Conversation gap can be substantially or fully closed via a self-training approach that does not depend on stronger external teachers. View-Asymmetric Self-Distillation effectively transfers single-turn competence into multi-turn interaction behavior, making LLMs more reliable and useful in their primary real-world setting: underspecified, evolving conversations.

title: QUIVER: A Formal Framework for Quantifying Perturbation Propagation and Bifurcation in Compound AI Systems
summary: Compound AI systems that chain multiple LLM calls into directed computation graphs are now the dominant architecture for production AI. Although these architectures leverage heterogeneous nodes with mixed-mode outputs, no existing framework quantifies how perturbations propagate through such pipelines, where nodes are stochastic and execution paths can diverge structurally. We introduce QUIVER, a formal framework for measuring perturbation propagation in graph-structured LLM pipelines. The framework defines: (1) a sensitivity matrix with type-dispatched distance metrics that classifies edges as amplifiers, absorbers, or threshold-sensitive, complemented by occurrence-lift; (2) trajectory divergence decomposing variation into value drift, structural path divergence, and iteration count divergence; (3) bifurcation thresholds identifying the smallest perturbation that causes structural execution path changes; and (4) distribution faithfulness, quantifying when per node evaluation datasets diverge from production distributions. We validate on two production enterprise pipelines and a public DSPy multihop QA pipeline, three structurally distinct architectures. Across 8,200+ instrumented traces (32,000+ pair comparisons), we demonstrate that QUIVER reveals distinct sensitivity profiles across architectures, distinguishes mechanistically different cascade patterns producing identical divergence rates, predicts nodes prone to trajectory bifurcation from observational data alone, and localizes stale evaluation artifacts to specific node-field categories that aggregate metrics cannot surface.

AI.tldr: A framework (QUIVER) to analyze how small changes propagate through complex, graph-structured LLM pipelines.
AI.motivation: Production AI increasingly relies on compound systems where multiple LLM calls and tools are chained into directed graphs. These pipelines are stochastic and can follow different execution paths, yet there is no principled way to measure how input or internal perturbations propagate through them, which nodes amplify or absorb changes, when small perturbations trigger structural path changes, or whether evaluation data still reflects real production behavior. This limits debugging, reliability assessment, and robust design of such systems.
AI.method: Define a formal framework, QUIVER, for perturbation analysis in LLM graphs. QUIVER introduces: (1) a sensitivity matrix using type-specific distance metrics on node inputs/outputs, along with occurrence-lift, to characterize edges as amplifiers, absorbers, or threshold-sensitive; (2) trajectory divergence that decomposes differences between paired executions into value drift, structural path divergence, and iteration count divergence; (3) bifurcation thresholds that estimate the minimal perturbation size needed to change the structural execution path; and (4) distribution faithfulness metrics that compare evaluation datasets to production distributions at the level of individual nodes and fields. They instrument several real pipelines, collect paired traces under perturbations, and compute these metrics.
AI.result: Applied QUIVER to two enterprise production pipelines and one public DSPy multihop QA pipeline, covering three different architectures and over 8,200 traces (32,000+ pair comparisons). The framework reveals distinct sensitivity patterns across architectures, identifies which edges/nodes amplify versus absorb perturbations, differentiates mechanisms that yield similar overall divergence but arise from different cascade structures, predicts which nodes are likely to exhibit trajectory bifurcations using only observational data, and detects specific node-field categories where evaluation data has become unrepresentative of production, which coarse-grained aggregate metrics fail to expose.
AI.conclusion: QUIVER provides a systematic way to analyze and quantify how perturbations propagate through complex, graph-structured LLM pipelines. It enables finer-grained diagnosis of sensitivity, trajectory bifurcation, and evaluation drift than existing aggregate metrics, and can guide debugging, monitoring, and design of more robust compound AI systems across diverse architectures.

title: Phonetic Modeling of Dialectal Variation in Vietnamese Speech
summary: Vietnamese exhibits substantial dialectal phonetic variation across Northern, Central, and Southern regions, where identical lexical items may be realized with markedly different pronunciations. Such variation poses challenges for automatic speech recognition (ASR) and remains difficult to model computationally due to the complex relationship between Vietnamese orthography and phonology. Existing approaches typically address dialect variability at the word level, assuming dialect-invariant mappings between spelling and pronunciation, which limits their ability to capture systematic phonetic differences. We propose a dialect-aware phonetic framework that explicitly models Vietnamese phonological structure and dialectal variation at both the vocabulary and decoding levels. The framework introduces a phonetic vocabulary that decomposes each syllable into structured phonetic components and maps them to dialect-specific IPA representations, together with a phonetic-structure decoder that jointly predicts these components. Experiments on the UIT-ViMD, a only-available dataset for multi-dialect in Vietnamese, show that the proposed approach outperforms various pre-trained baselines, \textbf{especially matches the performance of the strongest pretrained wav2ve2-base-vi-250h} across dialects while \textbf{using substantially fewer parameters and no external pretraining}. Code for experimental reproducibility will be publicly available upon the acceptance of this paper.

AI.tldr: Dialect-aware phonetic modeling framework for Vietnamese ASR that decomposes syllables into structured components and maps them to dialect-specific IPA, achieving strong performance with fewer parameters and no pretraining.
AI.motivation: Vietnamese has strong dialectal phonetic variation and a complex orthography–phonology relationship. Existing ASR methods mostly treat dialect differences at the word level and assume a fixed mapping from spelling to pronunciation, which cannot capture systematic phonetic shifts across Northern, Central, and Southern dialects. This limits recognition accuracy, especially in a multi-dialect setting with limited labeled data and resources.
AI.method: Design a dialect-aware phonetic framework that: (1) constructs a phonetic vocabulary where each Vietnamese syllable is decomposed into structured phonetic components; (2) maps these components to dialect-specific IPA representations for Northern, Central, and Southern speech; and (3) introduces a phonetic-structure decoder that jointly predicts these components during ASR decoding. The system is evaluated on UIT-ViMD, the only available multi-dialect Vietnamese dataset, and compared against several pre-trained baselines, including wav2vec2-base-vi-250h.
AI.result: On UIT-ViMD, the proposed dialect-aware phonetic framework outperforms multiple pre-trained ASR baselines. It matches the performance of the strongest baseline, wav2vec2-base-vi-250h, across dialects while requiring substantially fewer model parameters and no external pretraining data.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling Vietnamese phonological structure and dialect-specific phonetic realizations at both the vocabulary and decoding levels enables more efficient and accurate multi-dialect ASR. The proposed approach can achieve competitive performance to large pre-trained models with lower complexity and without external pretraining, making it promising for resource-constrained Vietnamese speech applications.

title: Temporal Concept Drift in Legal Judgment Prediction: Neural Baselines Across Three Epochs of Ukrainian Court Decisions
summary: Legal NLP benchmarks evaluate models on randomly split data, implicitly assuming that legal language is stationary. We test this assumption by fine-tuning four transformer encoders -- XLM-RoBERTa (base and large) and their legal-domain variants -- on Ukrainian court decisions from three temporal epochs defined by geopolitical disruptions: pre-war (2008-2013), hybrid war (2014-2021), and full-scale invasion (2022-2026). Each model is trained on one epoch and evaluated on all three, producing a 3x3 cross-temporal generalization matrix. Four findings emerge. (1) Forward degradation is severe: models trained on pre-war data lose up to 27.2 percentage points of macro-F1 when applied to full-scale invasion era decisions. (2) The degradation is asymmetric: backward transfer (full-scale to pre-war) is substantially more robust than forward transfer, consistent with the hypothesis that legal language is additive. (3) Legal-domain pretraining (Legal-XLM-R) does not improve absolute performance but reduces forward degradation magnitude and asymmetry. (4) Chronological continual learning eliminates catastrophic forgetting for general XLM-R: pre-war knowledge is fully retained (+1.8 to +6.2 pp) while full-scale performance gains +16.5 to +19.0 pp; reverse-chronological training causes severe forgetting. Cross-jurisdictional pretraining on Swiss Judgment Prediction data improves absolute performance but does not reduce temporal degradation magnitude, confirming that temporal drift is an intrinsic property of legal language evolution. The dataset (428K decisions across three epochs) is publicly available as a LEXTREME contribution.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that legal-language models trained on one time period perform much worse on later periods, revealing strong temporal drift in legal text, and explores domain pretraining and continual learning to mitigate this.
AI.motivation: Most legal NLP benchmarks assume that the statistical properties of legal language are stable over time by using random train-test splits. However, law and legal language evolve, especially around major geopolitical events. The authors want to test how strongly models trained on past legal decisions degrade on future ones, whether specialized legal pretraining or cross-jurisdictional pretraining helps, and how to train models that remain robust across temporal shifts.
AI.method: They collect 428K Ukrainian court decisions and divide them into three epochs aligned with major geopolitical phases: pre-war (2008-2013), hybrid war (2014-2021), and full-scale invasion (2022-2026). They fine-tune four transformer encoders—XLM-R base and large, and their legal-domain variants Legal-XLM-R—on one epoch at a time and evaluate them on all three, generating a 3x3 cross-temporal generalization matrix for each model. They analyze forward (older→newer) and backward (newer→older) transfer, test the impact of legal-domain pretraining and cross-jurisdiction pretraining (Swiss Judgment Prediction), and experiment with chronological vs reverse-chronological continual learning to study catastrophic forgetting and retention of earlier knowledge.
AI.result: 1) Strong forward temporal degradation: models trained on pre-war data lose up to 27.2 macro-F1 points when evaluated on full-scale invasion decisions. 2) Degradation is asymmetric: models trained on newer data transfer back to older data more robustly than the reverse, suggesting that newer legal language contains older patterns plus additions. 3) Legal-domain pretraining (Legal-XLM-R) does not significantly improve peak performance but reduces the size and asymmetry of forward degradation. 4) Chronological continual learning for general XLM-R preserves pre-war performance (+1.8 to +6.2 pp) and greatly improves full-scale era performance (+16.5 to +19.0 pp), while reverse-chronological training leads to severe forgetting of earlier periods. 5) Cross-jurisdictional pretraining on Swiss legal data boosts overall accuracy but fails to mitigate temporal degradation, indicating that time drift is inherent to evolving legal language.
AI.conclusion: Legal language in Ukrainian court decisions exhibits strong temporal drift, especially after geopolitical disruptions, invalidating the assumption that random splits are temporally stable. Models trained on past data can perform dramatically worse on future cases, with degradation being directionally asymmetric. Legal-domain pretraining and well-designed chronological continual learning can help reduce degradation and avoid catastrophic forgetting but cannot remove temporal drift entirely. Temporal robustness should thus be a core consideration in building and evaluating legal NLP systems, and the released 428K-decision dataset, contributed to LEXTREME, provides a new benchmark for studying these effects.

title: LGMT: Logic-Grounded Metamorphic Testing for Evaluating the Reasoning Reliability of LLMs
comment: Zheng Zheng is the corresponding author
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong performance on logical reasoning benchmarks, yet their reliability remains uncertain. Existing evaluations rely on static benchmarks, which fail to assess robustness under logically equivalent transformations and often overestimate reasoning capability. We propose LGMT (Logic-Grounded Metamorphic Testing), an oracle-free framework that leverages first-order logic (FOL) to evaluate LLM reasoning. By deriving metamorphic relations from formal logical equivalences, LGMT constructs semantically invariant test cases and detects reasoning defects through cross-case consistency checking. Experiments on six state-of-the-art LLMs show that LGMT exposes substantial hidden defects missed by traditional reference-based evaluations. We further find that models are particularly sensitive to symbol-level and conclusion-level variations, and that advanced prompting such as Few-shot CoT only partially mitigates these issues. These results suggest that LLM evaluation should move beyond isolated correctness toward robustness under logical invariance. LGMT provides a principled and scalable approach for diagnosing reasoning failures.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LGMT, a logic-grounded metamorphic testing framework that evaluates LLM reasoning robustness by checking consistency across logically equivalent problem variants, revealing hidden defects that standard benchmarks miss.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs perform well on logical reasoning benchmarks, their true reliability is unclear because current evaluations use static datasets and only check single-instance correctness, which can overestimate reasoning ability and ignore robustness to logically equivalent reformulations. There is a need for an oracle-free, systematic way to test whether LLMs preserve correctness under logical transformations.
AI.method: The authors propose LGMT, a framework based on first-order logic that derives metamorphic relations from formal logical equivalences (e.g., rewriting premises or conclusions without changing semantics). Using these relations, LGMT generates families of semantically invariant test cases and then evaluates LLMs by checking cross-case consistency of their answers, instead of relying solely on ground-truth labels. The framework is applied to six state-of-the-art LLMs under different prompting strategies, including Few-shot Chain-of-Thought.
AI.result: LGMT uncovers many reasoning defects that traditional reference-based evaluations fail to detect, showing that models often change their answers when problems are reformulated in logically equivalent ways. The experiments reveal particular sensitivity to low-level symbol changes and to variations in how conclusions are stated, and show that more advanced prompting like Few-shot CoT only partially reduces these inconsistencies.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that evaluating LLMs on logical reasoning should focus not only on isolated correctness but also on robustness to logically invariant transformations. LGMT offers a principled, scalable method to diagnose and quantify reasoning failures by leveraging formal logic-based metamorphic testing, highlighting substantial gaps between apparent benchmark performance and true reasoning robustness.

title: Decompose-and-Refine: Structured Legal Question Answering with Parametric Retrieval
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown strong performance in the legal domain, demonstrating notable potential in Legal Question Answering (LQA). However, unlike general QA, LQA requires answers that are not only accurate but also rigorously grounded in explicit legal authority. In statutory LQA, many questions require multi-hop reasoning across multiple legal issues, substantially increasing the risk of hallucination, thereby making accurate retrieval of supporting statutory provisions a critical prerequisite. Despite recent progress in multi-hop QA, existing approaches often rely on reasoning in natural language or retrieval without explicit query reformulation, leaving the vocabulary gap between user questions and statutory text largely unaddressed. To address this challenge, we propose Decompose-and-Refine (DaR), a statute-grounded LQA framework that tightly integrates step-wise question decomposition with parametric knowledge-based query refinement. DaR progressively decomposes a complex legal question into atomic sub-questions and generates statute-aligned parametric queries for each sub-question, enabling the selection of a single most central statutory provision corresponding to each legal issue. We evaluate DaR on KoBLEX, a Korean multi-hop LQA benchmark grounded in statutory law, using Qwen3-32B and Gemma3-27B. Experimental results demonstrate that DaR consistently improves both retrieval accuracy and final answer quality over existing approaches. Moreover, by explicitly separating sub-questions and their corresponding statutory provisions, DaR facilitates transparent, issue-level verification of complex legal reasoning processes.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Decompose-and-Refine (DaR), a framework that improves legal question answering by decomposing complex statutory questions into sub-questions and refining queries to retrieve precise supporting statutes, leading to better retrieval and answer quality.
AI.motivation: Legal Question Answering requires not only correct answers but explicit grounding in legal authority. Multi-hop statutory questions often need reasoning over several legal issues, increasing hallucination risk, and existing multi-hop QA methods do not adequately bridge the vocabulary gap between user questions and statutory text, making retrieval unreliable.
AI.method: DaR integrates step-wise question decomposition with parametric knowledge-based query refinement. It breaks complex legal questions into atomic sub-questions, then generates statute-aligned parametric queries for each, and selects a single central statutory provision per legal issue. The framework is tested in a Korean statutory LQA setting using large language models (Qwen3-32B and Gemma3-27B) on the KoBLEX benchmark.
AI.result: On the KoBLEX benchmark, DaR consistently outperforms existing approaches in both retrieval accuracy of statutory provisions and final answer quality when used with Qwen3-32B and Gemma3-27B.
AI.conclusion: Explicit decomposition of legal questions and refined, statute-aligned queries help retrieve more accurate legal bases and improve answer quality. DaR also brings transparency by explicitly mapping each sub-question to its supporting statute, enabling issue-level verification of complex legal reasoning.

title: Why We Need World Models for AGI: Where LLMs Fail and How World Models May Outperform
summary: Large language models achieve strong performance in language generation and knowledge-intensive tasks, yet remain limited in settings requiring causal reasoning, persistent state tracking, and long-horizon planning. We argue that these limitations may arise from an objective-level mismatch between sequence prediction and reasoning over latent environment dynamics. To formalize this distinction, we introduce Latent Dynamics Inference (LDI), a conceptual perspective that interprets language and multimodal observations as partial evidence of underlying transition dynamics. To empirically investigate this perspective, we introduce Flux, a sequential reasoning environment specified entirely through natural-language rules. As a proof-of-concept case study, the rules are first compiled into an explicit state-transition simulator, illustrating that structured latent transition dynamics can, in some cases, be operationally extracted from textual rule descriptions. This enables a controlled comparison between the LLMs operating purely over textual observations and reinforcement-learning agents trained directly within the extracted latent state space. Within this case study, agents operating with explicit access to the latent state space exhibit substantially more stable behavior in long-horizon gameplay, achieving an aggregate win rate of approximately 79% versus 11% for LLMs. Qualitative analysis further reveals failure modes consistent with unstable persistent state tracking, including invalid actions, state-tracking errors, and short-horizon reasoning failures. The complete implementation of the Flux environment available at https://github.com/FeisalAlaswad/FLUX-RL-Agent Within the evaluated setting, these results suggest that strong sequence prediction alone may struggle to support robust long-horizon dynamic reasoning without mechanisms for persistent state tracking and transition modeling

AI.tldr: The paper argues that large language models (LLMs) are inherently mismatched to tasks that require reasoning about latent environment dynamics, and demonstrates this using a new natural-language-defined environment where RL agents with explicit state access significantly outperform LLMs.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs excel at next-token prediction and many knowledge-intensive tasks, they perform poorly on problems demanding causal reasoning, long-horizon planning, and persistent state tracking. The authors suspect this is not just an implementation issue but a fundamental mismatch between the sequence prediction objective of LLMs and the requirements of reasoning over hidden environment dynamics. They aim to formalize this distinction and empirically test whether giving agents explicit access to latent state dynamics yields more robust performance than operating purely over text.
AI.method: The authors introduce the conceptual framework of Latent Dynamics Inference (LDI), which views language and multimodal inputs as partial observations of an underlying transition system. To study this, they build Flux, a sequential reasoning environment fully specified by natural-language rules. They then compile these rules into an explicit state-transition simulator, effectively extracting a structured latent state space from text. Using Flux, they compare two agent types: (1) LLMs that interact with the environment only through textual observations and rules, and (2) reinforcement learning agents that are trained directly on the extracted latent state representation. They evaluate both on long-horizon gameplay tasks and analyze qualitative failure modes of LLMs.
AI.result: In Flux, the RL agents with explicit access to the latent state space achieve an aggregate win rate of about 79%, while LLM-based agents operating purely on textual observations achieve only about 11%. The RL agents show more stable and reliable long-horizon behavior. The LLMs frequently exhibit unstable state tracking, choosing invalid actions, making errors about the current environment state, and failing at long-range reasoning over sequences of actions.
AI.conclusion: The study supports the view that strong sequence prediction alone is insufficient for robust long-horizon dynamic reasoning. There appears to be a structural mismatch between the standard LLM training objective and tasks requiring accurate modeling of latent transition dynamics and persistent state. The results suggest that to handle such tasks reliably, systems may need explicit mechanisms for state representation, tracking, and transition modeling, rather than relying solely on text-based next-token prediction—even when that prediction is very strong.

title: Grammatically-Guided Sparse Attention for Efficient and Interpretable Transformers
summary: The quadratic complexity of self-attention in Transformer models remains a significant bottleneck for processing long sequences and deploying large language models efficiently. For this approach, there has been significant research into Sparse Attention, and Deepseek Sparse Attention has combined various methods of creating segments of tokens to reduce the time complexity. This paper introduces a novel approach, Grammatically-Guided Sparse Attention, which constrains attention computations based on the grammatical roles of tokens. By leveraging Parts-of-Speech (POS) tags, attention masks are dynamically generated that enforce linguistically coherent connections between tokens, reducing the computational graph without sacrificing essential linguistic dependencies. Two masking strategies are proposed and evaluated: a hard mask that strictly allows only predefined grammatical interactions, and a soft mask that biases attention towards these interactions. The experiments, conducted on the SST-2 sentiment classification task using a DistilBERT-like architecture, demonstrate that Grammatically-Guided Sparse Attention maintains comparable accuracy to full attention while significantly reducing the theoretical computational overhead. Preliminary results show accuracy values of 0.8200 for hard masking and 0.8165 for soft masking, closely matching the 0.8200 of full attention, providing a path towards more efficient, interpretable, and linguistically-informed Transformer architectures.

AI.tldr: They propose Grammatically-Guided Sparse Attention, which uses POS-tag-based attention masks to reduce attention computation while keeping performance close to full attention.
AI.motivation: Transformer self-attention is quadratic in sequence length, which is a bottleneck for long sequences and efficient deployment of large language models. Existing sparse attention methods (including Deepseek Sparse Attention) reduce complexity by segmenting tokens but ignore explicit linguistic structure. The authors aim to exploit grammatical information to guide sparsity, hoping to cut computation while preserving essential linguistic dependencies and adding interpretability.
AI.method: They introduce Grammatically-Guided Sparse Attention, where attention patterns are constrained by grammatical roles derived from Part-of-Speech (POS) tags. From POS tags they dynamically build attention masks that specify which token types can attend to which others. They investigate two variants: (1) a hard mask that permits only a fixed set of POS-to-POS attention interactions, zeroing out all others; (2) a soft mask that increases attention to permissible interactions while merely down-weighting others. They integrate these masks into a DistilBERT-like Transformer and run experiments on the SST-2 sentiment classification benchmark. Complexity is analyzed theoretically via reduced attention graph density.
AI.result: On SST-2, a DistilBERT-like model with full attention attains 0.8200 accuracy. The hard-masked Grammatically-Guided Sparse Attention model also attains 0.8200, and the soft-masked variant attains 0.8165 accuracy—effectively matching full attention. Theoretical analysis shows that the grammatical masks substantially reduce the attention computation compared with dense self-attention, thus lowering the asymptotic overhead while keeping task performance almost unchanged.
AI.conclusion: Grammatically-Guided Sparse Attention can exploit POS-tag information to enforce linguistically meaningful sparsity in Transformers. It achieves comparable sentiment classification accuracy to full attention while cutting theoretical attention cost, and potentially improves interpretability through explicit grammatical constraints. The approach suggests a promising direction for building more efficient and linguistically informed Transformer architectures, though results are preliminary and shown on a single benchmark with a DistilBERT-like setup.

title: Saturating Scaling Laws for Equational Discovery: A Phenomenology of Growth Dynamics in Three Toy Substrates with Two Real-World Replications
comment: 17 pages, 5 figures, 4 tables, 2 algorithms. Code and data at https://github.com/fabio-rovai/saturating-scaling-laws (currently private; will be made public on acceptance)
summary: We investigate growth dynamics in deterministic equational discovery substrates. Across three toy domains (arithmetic, boolean, higher-order list; n=592 trajectories), short-range substrate sizes fit a power-law N(t) proportional to t^b. Within each substrate b is architecture-sensitive (cross-validated R^2 approximately 0.82); the regression does not transfer across substrates (arith+bool to list yields R^2 approximately -0.84). A heuristic mean-field closure model predicts a saturating power-law dN/dt = K N^k exp(-mu N) of which the pure power-law is the short-range approximation. Three robustness checks: bootstrap intervals on (k, mu) are tight in 4/5 toy trajectories and degenerate in 1/5; out-of-sample forecasting on toy data (fit first 100 epochs, predict next 400) is won by pure power-law 5/5, indicating the toy trajectories do not reach saturation; on two real-world growth proxies the result splits. New Mathlib/*.lean file additions per month (mathlib4, 60 months, 9701 files) support the saturating form on OOS forecasting by approximately 7x over pure power-law; Coq mathcomp monthly commits (129 months, 3083 commits) favour pure power-law on both tests with mu collapsing to zero. The dynamics are substrate-conditional at two levels: within-substrate architecture-to-b regressions do not transfer, and the preferred functional family for N(t) itself (pure vs. saturating power-law) differs by substrate. We propose "saturating power-law growth with substrate-conditional (k, mu), observable when the substrate has reached its saturation regime" as a working framing.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how the number of discovered equations grows over time in different deterministic symbol-manipulation systems, comparing pure power-law growth to a saturating power-law model, and finds that growth laws and parameters are highly dependent on the specific substrate and architecture.
AI.motivation: To understand and characterize the growth dynamics of automated equational discovery systems (e.g., theorem-proving-like or program-synthesis substrates) and to determine whether their discovery trajectories follow universal laws (like power laws) or are strongly dependent on the underlying domain and architecture. This matters for forecasting, benchmarking, and designing such systems.
AI.method: They analyze 592 growth trajectories across three synthetic toy domains (arithmetic, boolean, higher-order list) and fit short-range growth using a power-law N(t) ∝ t^b, where N(t) is the cumulative number of discoveries and b is an architecture-dependent exponent. They then propose and fit a heuristic mean-field model with saturating power-law dynamics dN/dt = K N^k exp(−μN) and compare it to the pure power-law model using cross-validated R^2 and out-of-sample forecasting on both toy and real-world data (Mathlib Lean file additions and Coq mathcomp commits). They conduct robustness checks such as bootstrap confidence intervals for parameters and OOS prediction using early-epoch fits to forecast later behavior.
AI.result: Within each substrate, the exponent b in the pure power-law fit is sensitive to architecture and provides reasonably good in-domain fits (cross-validated R^2 around 0.82), but these regressions fail to transfer across substrates (e.g., using arithmetic+boolean regression to predict list gives negative R^2). The mean-field saturating model fits well to most toy trajectories in terms of parameter stability, but OOS forecasting on toy data still favors pure power-law because those trajectories have not yet reached saturation. For real-world data, the Lean mathlib file-addition series supports the saturating model and shows substantially better OOS performance (~7x) than the pure power-law, whereas the Coq mathcomp commit series favors the pure power-law with μ ≈ 0 (no clear saturation). Overall, both the parameters (k, μ) and which functional family is preferred (pure vs. saturating) depend on the substrate.
AI.conclusion: Growth in deterministic equational discovery systems is not governed by a single universal law; instead, it is substrate-conditional in at least two ways: (1) mappings from architecture features to power-law exponents do not generalize across domains, and (2) the appropriate functional form for N(t) itself (pure vs. saturating power-law) can differ by substrate. A reasonable overall picture is "saturating power-law growth with substrate-conditional (k, μ)," where saturation becomes observable only once a given substrate has entered its saturation regime. This framing cautions against naive extrapolation and highlights the need for substrate-specific modeling and longer time horizons to detect saturation.

title: Unveil: Unified Visual-Textual Integration and Distillation for Multi-modal Document Retrieval
summary: Document retrieval in real-world scenarios faces significant challenges due to diverse document formats and modalities. Traditional text-based approaches rely on tailored parsing techniques that disregard layout information and are prone to errors, while recent parsing-free visual methods often struggle to capture fine-grained textual semantics in text-rich scenarios. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{Unveil}, a novel visual-textual embedding framework that effectively integrates textual and visual features for robust document representation. Through knowledge distillation, we transfer the semantic understanding capabilities from the visual-textual embedding model to a purely visual model, enabling efficient parsing-free retrieval while preserving semantic fidelity. Experimental results demonstrate that our visual-textual embedding method surpasses existing approaches, while knowledge distillation successfully bridges the performance gap between visual-textual and visual-only methods, improving both retrieval accuracy and efficiency.

AI.tldr: Unveil is a new document retrieval framework that jointly uses visual and textual embeddings, then distills them into a purely visual model for fast, parsing-free retrieval with strong semantics.
AI.motivation: Real-world document retrieval is hard because documents come in many formats and modalities. Traditional text-based methods need custom parsers, ignore layout, and are error-prone. New parsing-free visual methods avoid parsing but fail at fine-grained text understanding in text-heavy documents. A method is needed that combines visual and textual strengths and can still be deployed efficiently without parsing.
AI.method: The authors propose Unveil, a visual-textual embedding framework that integrates both visual and textual features to form robust document representations. They then apply knowledge distillation to transfer the semantic understanding from this visual-textual teacher model to a lighter, purely visual student model. This allows the student to perform parsing-free retrieval while approximating the teacher’s semantic capabilities.
AI.result: Experiments show that the joint visual-textual embedding outperforms existing baselines on document retrieval tasks. Furthermore, the distilled purely visual model narrows the performance gap between multimodal (visual-textual) and visual-only approaches, achieving better retrieval accuracy and efficiency than prior visual-only methods.
AI.conclusion: Integrating visual and textual information in Unveil yields stronger document embeddings, and distilling this knowledge into a purely visual model enables accurate, efficient, parsing-free document retrieval. The approach overcomes weaknesses of both traditional text-parsing methods and naïve visual-only methods in realistic, text-rich document scenarios.

title: Beyond Predefined Learning Objects: A Thinking-Learning Interaction Model for Up-to-Date Autonomous Robot Learning
summary: Autonomous robots operating in open and changing environments cannot always rely on predefined inputs, outputs, and action routines. Although existing learning methods enable robots to improve their performance through environmental interaction, the objects of learning are often fixed in advance, such as input features, recognition outputs, network structures, task goals, or action sequences. This limits their ability to adapt when new features, new categories, or more efficient task routines appear during long-term operation. To address this problem, this paper proposes a thinking-learning interaction model for autonomous robots. The core idea is that thinking guides learning by identifying potential changes, selecting useful evidence, organizing training materials, and planning verification actions, while learning promotes thinking by updating task knowledge, feature-selection experience, action strategies, and future reasoning processes. Based on this bidirectional mechanism, the robot can gradually move beyond predefined learning settings and adapt its recognition relations and action relations through continuous interaction with the environment. Specifically, the proposed model supports adaptive input feature discovery, output category expansion, learning model update, and action routine reconstruction. Experimental results show that the proposed model improves the final recognition accuracy from 0.419 to 0.845 in feature adaptation, achieves higher new-category formation accuracy and model-update success rate, and reduces the average action length from 13.0 to 4.0 in action routine reconstruction. In learning-enhanced thinking, the useful evidence selection rate increases from 0.272 to 0.965, indicating that learning results can effectively improve future evidence selection and reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a bidirectional thinking-learning interaction model that lets autonomous robots adapt their features, categories, models, and action routines beyond predefined settings, significantly improving recognition accuracy, evidence selection, and action efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing robot learning methods assume fixed inputs, outputs, models, or task structures, which limits long-term autonomy in open, changing environments where new features, categories, and more efficient routines can emerge. The authors aim to enable robots to adapt these elements themselves during operation.
AI.method: They design a thinking-learning interaction model in which ‘thinking’ modules monitor for potential changes, choose relevant evidence, organize training data, and plan verification actions, while ‘learning’ modules update task knowledge, feature-selection strategies, recognition models, and action policies. This bidirectional loop supports adaptive feature discovery, category expansion, model updating, and reconstruction of action routines, and is evaluated in experiments on recognition and action tasks.
AI.result: With the new model, recognition accuracy in feature adaptation improves from 0.419 to 0.845; new-category formation accuracy and model-update success rate increase (exact numbers not all given); average action length in routine reconstruction drops from 13.0 to 4.0; and the rate of selecting useful evidence for learning-enhanced thinking rises from 0.272 to 0.965.
AI.conclusion: Integrating thinking and learning in a closed loop allows autonomous robots to move beyond fixed learning configurations and continuously adapt recognition and action relations in dynamic environments, yielding better recognition performance, more efficient actions, and more reliable evidence selection for future reasoning and learning.

title: Generating Legal Commentaries from Case Databases via Retrieval, Clustering, and Generation
summary: We present a fully automated pipeline that transforms large collections of court decisions into legal commentaries for statutes - without providing any handcrafted doctrinal framework. Using 4.555 decisions of the German Federal Court of Justice that cite sections 242, 280, 812 and 823 of the German Civil Code (BGB), we extract paragraph-level chunks, summarize their reasoning, and derive keywords, which are embedded and clustered. For each cluster, an LLM generates headings and synthesizes citation-rich sections, which are then merged into coherent commentaries by four state-of-the-art LLMs. We evaluate along five dimensions - topical relevance, heading-match, citation faithfulness, cluster distinction and logical ordering - using both a human expert and an LLM-judge. Our results show that commentary-like argument mining from court decisions to generate reports that can be refreshed within minutes at minimal cost is feasible, yet they highlight limitations arising from restricted sources and the normativity of legal reasoning.

AI.tldr: An automated pipeline converts thousands of German Federal Court decisions into statute-centered, commentary-like texts, showing this is feasible but limited by data coverage and the nature of legal reasoning.
AI.motivation: Legal commentary is labor-intensive to produce and update, and traditional methods rely on handcrafted doctrinal structures. The authors aim to see whether large language models and automated text processing can mine court decisions at scale to create updatable, statute-focused commentaries without manual doctrinal design, thus lowering costs and speeding up legal knowledge consolidation.
AI.method: They collect 4,555 German Federal Court of Justice decisions citing four key sections of the German Civil Code (242, 280, 812, 823 BGB). The pipeline: (1) splits decisions into paragraph-level chunks; (2) summarizes each chunk’s reasoning and extracts keywords; (3) embeds chunks and clusters them based on semantic similarity; (4) for each cluster, uses an LLM to generate headings and citation-rich synthesized sections; and (5) has four state-of-the-art LLMs merge these cluster-level texts into coherent statute commentaries. They then evaluate the generated commentaries along five criteria—topical relevance, heading-match, citation faithfulness, cluster distinction, and logical ordering—using both a human legal expert and an LLM-based evaluator.
AI.result: The system successfully produces statute-centered, commentary-like texts derived from case law, generated quickly and at low marginal cost. Evaluations show that the outputs are often topically relevant and reasonably structured, with meaningful citation use and some differentiation between clusters. However, the assessments also reveal issues such as imperfect citation faithfulness, overlapping or weakly distinct clusters, and occasional problems in logical ordering and doctrinal nuance.
AI.conclusion: Automated, commentary-like argument mining from large collections of court decisions is practically achievable and can generate useful, refreshable reports for specific statutory provisions.
