Nonetheless, the approach is constrained by the selected corpus (limited sources) and deeper challenges tied to the normative and interpretive character of legal reasoning, meaning it cannot fully replace expert-crafted doctrinal commentary and must be used with awareness of its limitations.

title: Towards trustworthy agentic AI: a comprehensive survey of safety, robustness, privacy, and system security
comment: 36 pages, 4 figures. Survey/review article on trustworthy agentic AI. Published in Academia AI and Applications, 2026
summary: Agentic AI systems -- Large Language Models (LLMs) augmented with planning, tool use, memory, and long-horizon interactions -- can execute complex tasks autonomously, but their multi-step trajectories introduce new failure modes that challenge trustworthiness. This survey provides a focused examination of trustworthy agentic AI through two core dimensions that are critical for high-risk deployments: Safety and Robustness, and Privacy and System Security. For each dimension, we clarify key concepts, identify where risks emerge along the agent workflow, and summarize stage-targeted mitigation strategies. Other trustworthiness aspects (value alignment, transparency, fairness, and accountability) are discussed as relevant context rather than parallel chapters. To support consistent comparison and deployment decisions, we consolidate evaluation into a unified metrics-and-benchmarks hub, emphasizing both outcome and process signals (e.g., constraint violations, trace completeness, and adversarial success rates) and offering scenario-to-metric guidance for release gating. We conclude by outlining open challenges such as self-evolving agents, runtime monitoring and verification, privacy-preserving personalization, and the trust-utility trade-off, and present a case study of real-world security failures in open-source agentic systems. Our goal is to serve as a practical reference for researchers and practitioners building trustworthy agentic systems in high-stakes environments.

AI.tldr: Survey of trustworthy agentic AI (LLM-based agents) focusing on safety/robustness and privacy/security, mapping risks across the agent workflow, mitigation strategies, evaluation metrics/benchmarks, open challenges, and a real-world case study.
AI.motivation: Agentic AI systems, built from LLMs with planning, tools, memory, and long-horizon autonomy, are increasingly used for complex, high-stakes tasks. Their multi-step, partially autonomous behavior introduces novel and compounded failure modes that current trustworthiness frameworks don’t fully address. There is a need for a structured, practical overview of where safety, robustness, privacy, and security risks arise in such systems, how to mitigate them across the agent lifecycle, and how to evaluate them consistently to make deployment and release decisions.
AI.method: Conceptual and survey-based: (1) define and distinguish key trustworthiness dimensions for agentic AI, focusing on Safety & Robustness and Privacy & System Security; (2) decompose an agent’s workflow into stages and systematically map where different risks arise; (3) catalogue and organize stage-specific mitigation strategies from existing work; (4) compile and normalize evaluation metrics and benchmarks into a unified hub, emphasizing both outcome-level and process-level indicators; (5) identify and articulate open research challenges; (6) provide a case study analyzing real-world security failures in open-source agentic systems.
AI.result: A structured survey and framework that: (a) clarifies concepts around trustworthy agentic AI with emphasis on safety/robustness and privacy/security; (b) identifies risk points along the agent workflow; (c) summarizes concrete, stage-targeted mitigation approaches; (d) consolidates evaluation into a cohesive metrics and benchmarks hub with guidance on choosing metrics for different deployment scenarios; and (e) surfaces key open problems such as handling self-evolving agents and balancing trust with utility, illustrated via a real-world case study of security breaches in open-source agents.
AI.conclusion: Trustworthy deployment of agentic LLM systems requires addressing safety/robustness and privacy/security holistically across the agent workflow, not just at the base model level. Process-aware evaluation, targeted mitigations at each stage, and careful release gating are critical, especially in high-stakes applications. Significant open challenges remain, including monitoring and verifying self-evolving agents, enabling privacy-preserving personalization, and managing trade-offs between trust and utility. The survey aims to provide a practical reference and conceptual scaffold for researchers and practitioners designing, evaluating, and deploying agentic AI in real-world, high-risk settings.

title: AstroMind: A High-Fidelity Benchmark for Spacecraft Behavior Reasoning Based on Large Language Models
summary: Understanding why a spacecraft maneuvers -- rather than simply that it did -- is an increasingly important problem for space domain awareness as Earth orbits grow crowded and contested. Current analysis pipelines are built for detection: they are good at picking up that something happened, less good at reasoning about what it means. AstroMind is a physics-grounded benchmark designed to close that gap. It draws on high-fidelity astrodynamics simulations and real observational constraints, converting them into verifiable reasoning problems across three task types: intent inference, maneuver parameter estimation, and threat assessment. Each scenario includes realistic sensing noise and multi-source textual intelligence at varying reliability levels. Evaluation metrics capture both semantic correctness and quantitative consistency under physical constraints. Benchmarking a suite of open-weight models shows no single model dominates every axis: Qwen3 (32B) leads on intent inference accuracy; QwQ (32B) leads on threat assessment and achieves the lowest median relative error on parsed items; GPT-OSS (20B) produces the strongest judged reasoning quality and extracts the most scalar values for parameter estimation (136 of 241 parsed items). Training data composition and reasoning style matter as much as model size. Structured reasoning prompts help consistently across tested 8B models, with larger gains for those that can already track physical constraints. AstroMind gives the field a shared test for a problem where getting the physics right and reading the tactical situation correctly are both required -- neither is sufficient on its own.

AI.tldr: AstroMind is a physics-grounded benchmark to test AI reasoning about spacecraft maneuvers, covering intent, maneuver parameters, and threat assessment under realistic sensing and intelligence conditions.
AI.motivation: Space is becoming more crowded and strategically contested, so merely detecting that a spacecraft maneuvered is insufficient; analysts need to understand why it maneuvered, what exactly it did, and whether it poses a threat. Existing analysis tools and models are tuned for detection rather than higher-level reasoning that integrates physics with tactical interpretation. A standardized, rigorous benchmark is needed to evaluate and improve AI systems’ ability to reason about spacecraft behavior within real-world sensing and operational constraints.
AI.method: The authors build AstroMind, a benchmark based on high-fidelity astrodynamics simulations coupled with realistic observational constraints. They generate scenarios that include sensor noise and multiple textual intelligence sources of varying reliability. These scenarios are formulated as three task types: (1) intent inference (why a maneuver happened), (2) maneuver parameter estimation (what parameters define the maneuver), and (3) threat assessment (how dangerous or concerning the maneuver is). They design evaluation metrics to score both semantic correctness and quantitative consistency with physical laws. They then benchmark various open-weight LLMs, analyze performance across tasks, and study the effects of model size, training data, and prompting style (including structured reasoning prompts).
AI.result: No single open-weight model is best across all dimensions. Qwen3 (32B) performs best on intent inference accuracy, while QwQ (32B) leads on threat assessment and yields the lowest median relative error on extracted quantitative items. GPT-OSS (20B) is judged to have the highest reasoning quality overall and successfully extracts the largest number of scalar values for parameter estimation (136 out of 241 parsable items). Structured reasoning prompts improve performance across 8B-scale models, with particularly large gains for models that already have some ability to track physical constraints. The experiments show that training data composition and reasoning style are as critical as model size for this domain.
AI.conclusion: AstroMind provides a realistic, physics-grounded benchmark that jointly tests physical reasoning and tactical interpretation for spacecraft maneuvers. The study demonstrates that current open-weight models have complementary strengths but none is universally superior, and that structured prompting and appropriate training data can significantly enhance performance. By offering a shared, verifiable testbed that integrates noisy observations, text intelligence, and orbital dynamics, AstroMind is positioned to guide the development and comparison of future AI systems for space domain awareness, where correctly modeling both the physics and the strategic context is essential.

title: Reason--Imagine--Act: Closed-Loop LLM Decision Making with World Models for Autonomous Driving
comment: Accepted by the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC 2026). 8 pages, 2 figures
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are promising for autonomous driving, but semantics-only decision policies can yield physically unsafe behavior in dynamic traffic. Existing methods either perform online language reasoning without explicit dynamics verification or use world models mainly in offline pipelines, leaving a gap between semantic intent and physical feasibility at decision time. We propose Reason--Imagine--Act (RIA), a closed-loop framework that couples an LLM reasoner with an action-conditioned world model for online safety verification. At each step, the LLM proposes an action template and candidate sub-actions, the world model performs short-horizon rollouts, and a safety scorer selects the safest executable action with feedback to the next reasoning step. Under a unified CARLA point-goal protocol (1000 episodes), RIA achieves 80.05% route completion, 51.10% arrival rate, and 0.20% collision rate. Under the same closed-loop interface, RIA consistently outperforms training-free baselines, including CARLA TM and MADA, on core closed-loop metrics. For reproducibility, code is available at https://github.com/pku-smart-city/source_code/tree/main/RIA.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Reason--Imagine--Act (RIA), a framework that combines large language model reasoning with an action-conditioned world model to ensure physically safe decision-making for autonomous driving in dynamic traffic.
AI.motivation: While large language models show promise for high-level reasoning in autonomous driving, purely semantic decision policies can cause physically unsafe behavior because they ignore vehicle dynamics and interaction with dynamic traffic. Existing approaches either do online language reasoning without verifying dynamics or use world models mainly offline, so there is a gap between what the LLM intends semantically and what is physically feasible at execution time.
AI.method: The authors propose RIA, a closed-loop system that tightly couples an LLM reasoner with an action-conditioned world model for online safety verification. At every control step, the LLM first generates an action template and several candidate sub-actions. The world model then runs short-horizon rollouts for these candidates to predict their physical consequences. A safety scorer evaluates these rollouts to select the safest executable action and provides feedback to guide the LLM’s next reasoning step. All evaluation is done in the CARLA simulator under a unified point-goal navigation protocol.
AI.result: In 1000 CARLA point-goal episodes, RIA attains 80.05% route completion, a 51.10% arrival rate, and only a 0.20% collision rate. Using the same closed-loop interface, RIA consistently surpasses training-free baselines such as CARLA Traffic Manager (TM) and MADA on the main closed-loop performance metrics.
AI.conclusion: Coupling LLM-based semantic reasoning with an online, action-conditioned world model for safety verification yields more physically grounded and safer driving policies than training-free baselines. RIA narrows the gap between semantic intent and physical feasibility in autonomous driving, improving route completion and safety in dynamic traffic scenarios.

title: LC-ERD: Mining Latent Logic for Self-Evolving Reasoning via Consistency-Regulated Reward Decomposition
summary: The evolution of Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning is bottlenecked by the scarcity of high-quality process data. While self-alignment via endogenous rewards offers a solution, mining valid supervision faces three challenges: (1) Label Noise via Mimetic Bias, where rewards prioritize statistical likelihood over logical truth, creating a "correctness illusion" that masks compounding errors; (2) Coarse-Grained Supervision, where sparse global outcomes (e.g., in GRPO) fail to provide granular guidance, treating reasoning chains as monolithic; and (3) Distributional Collapse, where signals fail to generalize without amplifying pre-training biases. To address these, we introduce LC-ERD (Logic-Consistent Endogenous Reward Decomposition), a framework framing self-alignment as latent structure mining. We derive a Variational Logic Potential by aggregating consensus from the model's Latent Logic Expertise (LLE) to denoise the reasoning manifold, and introduce a Multi-Agent Value Decomposition protocol based on the IGM principle to quantify individual step utility. Experiments show LC-ERD delivers a robust self-evolution path, uncovering trade-offs between logic consistency and accuracy while identifying high-value reasoning patterns missed by standard rewards. Our code is available at https://github.com/Reinhardmannn/LC-ERD.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes LC-ERD, a self-alignment framework for LLMs that decomposes endogenous rewards to obtain logic-consistent, fine-grained supervision for reasoning, improving robustness and revealing valuable reasoning patterns beyond standard reward schemes.
AI.motivation: LLM reasoning progress is limited by a lack of high-quality, step-level process supervision. Existing self-alignment with endogenous rewards suffers from (1) label noise caused by mimetic bias where models reward likely but logically incorrect reasoning; (2) coarse, trajectory-level rewards that treat entire reasoning chains as single units and provide little guidance to individual steps; and (3) distributional collapse where reward signals overfit and amplify pre-training biases instead of generalizing. A new method is needed to mine cleaner, more informative internal supervision signals from models themselves.
AI.method: The authors introduce LC-ERD (Logic-Consistent Endogenous Reward Decomposition), which reframes self-alignment as latent structure mining over reasoning trajectories. First, they define Latent Logic Expertise (LLE) as the model’s implicit logical knowledge and derive a Variational Logic Potential that aggregates multi-sample consensus from LLE to denoise the reasoning manifold, filtering out mimetic but inconsistent steps. Second, they design a Multi-Agent Value Decomposition protocol, inspired by the IGM (Individual-Global-Max) principle from multi-agent RL, to decompose global endogenous rewards into step-level utilities, quantifying each reasoning step’s contribution to the final outcome. This yields logic-consistent, fine-grained supervision signals that can be used to further train the model.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that LC-ERD enables a stable self-evolution trajectory for LLM reasoning. The framework reveals and manages trade-offs between logic consistency and answer accuracy, and it discovers high-utility reasoning patterns that standard reward schemes (e.g., coarse global rewards) fail to highlight. Empirical results show improved robustness of reasoning and better generalization of the mined supervision signals compared to baseline endogenous-reward approaches.
AI.conclusion: LC-ERD offers an effective approach to self-alignment for LLM reasoning by decomposing noisy endogenous rewards into logic-consistent, step-level signals. By leveraging latent logical structure (LLE) and multi-agent value decomposition, it mitigates mimetic label noise, over-coarse supervision, and distributional collapse. The framework not only improves reasoning robustness and alignment but also surfaces valuable reasoning strategies that are overlooked by conventional reward formulations.

title: Word Class Representations Spontaneously Emerge from Successor Representations Trained on Natural Language
summary: Language models are typically trained to predict the next token in a sequence. Here, we explore an alternative predictive principle from reinforcement learning: Successor Representations (SRs), which model the expected discounted distribution of future states rather than the immediate next state. We transfer this framework to natural language and train neural networks to predict future word distributions across multiple temporal horizons, thereby learning representations of long-range transition structure. We train a deep residual neural network on WikiText-103 (103 million tokens; 20,000-word vocabulary) and optimize successor representations as probability distributions using KL divergence. Without explicit linguistic supervision, structured language representations emerge spontaneously. After training, the learned space develops a clear geometric organization with respect to part-of-speech (POS) categories: nouns, verbs, and adjectives become separable and recoverable through unsupervised clustering. This organization depends systematically on predictive horizon, with short horizons producing the strongest syntactic structure and longer horizons increasingly integrating broader contextual and semantic information. At finer resolutions, additional interpretable lexical substructure emerges, revealing coherent subclasses within major word categories. These findings suggest that syntactic categories need not be explicitly encoded but may arise as a consequence of predictive sequence learning. To our knowledge, this work provides the first systematic application of successor representations to natural language and establishes a conceptual bridge between reinforcement learning, linguistics, and cognitive neuroscience.

AI.tldr: The paper applies successor representations from reinforcement learning to language modeling, training networks to predict distributions over future words at multiple horizons, and finds that syntactic and semantic structure emerge spontaneously in the learned representations.
AI.motivation: Standard language models focus on predicting the immediate next token, which may underutilize long-range predictive structure inherent in language. Successor representations, which model expected future state occupancy, have been powerful in reinforcement learning but have not been systematically applied to natural language. The authors aim to investigate whether SRs can provide richer representations that naturally capture syntactic and semantic regularities without explicit supervision, and to build a conceptual link between RL, linguistics, and cognitive neuroscience.
AI.method: The authors adapt the successor representation framework to text by defining, for each word, a distribution over expected discounted future words at multiple temporal horizons. They train a deep residual neural network on the WikiText-103 corpus (103M tokens, 20k-word vocabulary) to predict these future word distributions. The model is optimized using KL divergence between predicted and empirical successor distributions. They then analyze the resulting representation space using unsupervised clustering and geometric analysis to see how words organize with respect to part-of-speech and more fine-grained lexical classes, and how this structure changes with predictive horizon length.
AI.result: The trained model develops a latent space where words organize according to linguistic categories, despite receiving no explicit linguistic labels. Nouns, verbs, and adjectives become geometrically separable and recoverable via unsupervised clustering. The degree and nature of this organization vary systematically with predictive horizon: shorter horizons yield stronger syntactic clustering, while longer horizons increasingly encode broader semantic and contextual information. At a finer scale, coherent subclasses within major POS categories also emerge, indicating rich internal lexical structure.
AI.conclusion: Successor representations, when applied to language modeling, naturally give rise to structured linguistic representations, including syntactic categories and semantic substructure, without explicit supervision. This suggests that core aspects of linguistic structure can emerge from general predictive sequence learning principles. The work demonstrates the feasibility and benefits of SRs in natural language, and provides a conceptual link among reinforcement learning, linguistic theory, and cognitive neuroscience models of predictive processing.

title: EvoSci: A Bio-Inspired Multi-Agent Framework for the Evolution of Scientific Discovery
summary: Large language models (LLMs), have shown strong potential in scientific discovery, yet existing methods still face substantial challenges in the design of research workflows and multi-role collaboration mechanisms. To mitigate these issues, we propose EvoSci, a multi-agent scientific collaboration framework, which integrates bio-inspired evolution with knowledge graph modeling. To iteratively generate, evaluate, and refine research ideas, EvoSci incorporates multiple role-based agents, including mentor, researcher, and reviewer. By combining collaborative reasoning, shared memory, and evolutionary feedback, EvoSci significantly enhances the coherence and creativity of scientific exploration. Experiments on real-world research topics demonstrate that EvoSci significantly outperforms strong baselines in LLM-based structured peer-review and comparative ranking evaluations, achieving the highest overall peer-review score (ICLR 4.90) and top ranking (Top-10 = 54). These results suggest its superiority in both scientific idea generation and continuous discovery.

AI.tldr: EvoSci is a multi-agent, evolution-inspired framework that uses LLMs and knowledge graphs to collaboratively generate, evaluate, and refine scientific ideas, outperforming strong LLM baselines on peer-review and ranking metrics.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs show promise for scientific discovery, existing approaches struggle to design robust research workflows and effective multi-role collaboration among agents, leading to limited coherence, creativity, and iterative improvement in complex scientific exploration tasks.
AI.method: The authors introduce EvoSci, a multi-agent framework that models scientific discovery as an evolutionary process over a knowledge graph. It employs distinct role-based LLM agents (mentor, researcher, reviewer) that share memory and conduct collaborative reasoning. Research ideas are iteratively generated, evaluated, and refined via bio-inspired evolutionary operations (e.g., variation and selection) guided by reviewer feedback and knowledge graph context.
AI.result: On real-world research topics, EvoSci is evaluated via LLM-based structured peer review and comparative ranking. It achieves the highest ICLR-style peer-review score (average 4.90) and the best ranking performance (Top-10 = 54), outperforming strong LLM baselines in both the quality and impact of produced research ideas.
AI.conclusion: Integrating evolutionary mechanisms, role-differentiated agents, and knowledge graph modeling yields more coherent, creative, and continuously improving scientific ideas than existing LLM-based approaches. EvoSci appears particularly effective for open-ended, iterative scientific discovery workflows, suggesting a promising direction for automating and augmenting research ideation.

title: CSP-Atlas: Concept-Specific Neural Circuits in a Sparse Python Transformer
summary: A sparse 8-layer code transformer develops dedicated neural circuitry for every Python construct tested, and that circuitry is organised by a clean computational principle rather than by semantic category. We extract neural circuits for 106 concepts (43 AST node types, 63 builtin objects) by marginalising across 63,800 controlled prompts, and decompose each circuit into concept-specific and token-driven components using contrastive checker prompts that present a keyword token without its associated syntactic structure. Three findings emerge. First, all 106 concepts produce non-empty universal circuits at every one of nine parameter settings, and the ranking of concept-specificity across constructs is stable across the sweep - survival is not an artifact of a permissive threshold. Second, AST circuits contain a genuine concept component distinct from token activation: concept-only neurons constitute up to 62.5% of the loudest-firing neurons at mid-to-late layers, while builtin circuits are almost entirely token-driven. Third, six computationally atomic constructs - Import, ImportFrom, Break, Continue, Pass, Assert - cluster together despite being semantically unrelated, sharing only the property of being single-statement constructs requiring no nested body; this atomicity super-cluster, together with a four-tier hierarchy organised by token ambiguity and structural distinctiveness, shows that the model's internal organisation tracks computational structure rather than meaning. The methodology, full decomposition data, and analysis code are released.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how a sparse 8-layer transformer trained on Python represents code constructs, revealing that it forms dedicated neural circuits for each construct organized by computational structure rather than semantic meaning.
AI.motivation: To understand the internal mechanisms of code transformers at a fine-grained, concept-level resolution: do they form distinct neural circuits for specific programming constructs, and if so, what principle organizes these circuits (semantics vs. computational structure)?
AI.method: They probe an 8-layer sparse code transformer using 63,800 controlled Python prompts, covering 106 concepts (43 AST node types and 63 builtins). For each concept, they extract a neural circuit by marginalizing over prompts and then decompose it into (a) concept-specific components and (b) token-driven components. They use contrastive "checker" prompts that present a keyword without its usual syntactic structure to separate token effects from true concept representations, and they repeat this across nine different parameter/threshold settings.
AI.result: (1) All 106 concepts yield non-empty, universal circuits across all nine parameter settings, with stable rankings of concept-specificity, indicating robustness. (2) AST-node circuits contain substantial concept-specific components distinct from token activations: in mid-to-late layers, up to 62.5% of top-firing neurons are concept-only, while builtin-object circuits are almost purely token-driven. (3) Six constructs (Import, ImportFrom, Break, Continue, Pass, Assert) cluster together into an "atomic" super-cluster, plus a four-level hierarchy emerges based on token ambiguity and structural distinctiveness, showing that circuit organization follows computational structure, not semantic categories.
AI.conclusion: The transformer learns dedicated, robust neural circuits for every tested Python construct, and these circuits are primarily organized by computational and syntactic structure rather than by semantic meaning. AST constructs have genuine, token-independent concept representations, while builtins are mostly tied to specific tokens. The identified atomicity cluster and structural hierarchy suggest that code transformers internally organise knowledge around execution/computational roles. The authors release their methodology, full decompositions, and code to facilitate further mechanistic interpretability research on code models.

title: Breaking the Chains of Probability: Neutrosophic Logic as a New Framework for Epistemic Uncertainty in Large Language Models
comment: Published in Neutrosophic Sets and Systems, Vol. 99 (2026). Author's preprint version. Open code and data available at: github.com/mleyvaz/neutrosophic-llm-logic
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are predominantly governed by probabilistic frameworks in which the sum of outcome probabilities is constrained to unity. This architectural limitation, often imposed by Softmax layers, leads to a collapse of uncertainty that makes it difficult to differentiate between epistemic uncertainty, paradox, and vagueness. We present an empirical investigation of the application of Neutrosophic Logic, a framework that treats Truth (T), Indeterminacy (I), and Falsity (F) as three independent dimensions, to model epistemic states in LLMs. We conducted experiments on a family of four OpenAI GPT models across five linguistic phenomena: logical paradoxes, epistemic ignorance, vagueness, ethical contradictions, and future contingencies, under three prompting strategies: neutrosophic, probabilistic, and entropy-derived. Our findings reveal that the neutrosophic approach, by allowing T+I+F > 1, a state we term hyper-truth, provides a richer representation of a model's internal state. In 35% of evaluations, hyper-truth emerged spontaneously, predominantly under ethical contradiction and logical paradox. We demonstrate that this approach preserves truth values in fuzzy contexts and offers a robust method for identifying and quantifying internal model conflict. We conclude that the integration of neutrosophic evaluation layers is a critical step toward more transparent, reliable, and ethically aware AI systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using Neutrosophic Logic (with independent dimensions of Truth, Indeterminacy, and Falsity) to better model and inspect uncertainty and internal conflict in LLMs, showing empirically that it captures paradoxes, vagueness, and ethical contradictions more richly than standard probabilistic softmax-based outputs.
AI.motivation: Standard LLMs rely on probabilistic outputs (e.g., via softmax) where probabilities must sum to 1, which collapses different kinds of uncertainty into a single distribution and makes it hard to distinguish between epistemic ignorance, paradox, and vagueness. The authors want a framework that can separate these phenomena and expose internal conflicts and indeterminacy in LLM reasoning, in order to improve transparency, reliability, and ethical awareness.
AI.method: They apply Neutrosophic Logic to LLM evaluation, treating Truth (T), Indeterminacy (I), and Falsity (F) as three independent scores rather than a single normalized probability distribution. They run experiments on four OpenAI GPT models across five types of linguistic phenomena (logical paradoxes, epistemic ignorance, vagueness, ethical contradictions, future contingencies) and under three prompting strategies (direct neutrosophic prompts, conventional probabilistic prompts, and prompts derived from entropy). They analyze when and how the models’ responses exhibit non-normalized states (T+I+F>1, called hyper-truth) and how this relates to internal conflict and uncertainty.
AI.result: The neutrosophic representation allows T+I+F to exceed 1, which the authors term hyper-truth, giving a more expressive view of model states than standard probability outputs. Hyper-truth occurs in about 35% of evaluations, especially for ethical contradictions and logical paradoxes, indicating substantial internal conflict in these settings. The neutrosophic approach can preserve nuanced truth values in fuzzy cases and provides measurable signals of where the model is conflicted or uncertain in a complex way.
AI.conclusion: Neutrosophic evaluation layers, which output independent Truth, Indeterminacy, and Falsity scores rather than a single normalized probability distribution, are argued to be an important addition to LLM architectures and evaluation pipelines. They enable finer-grained analysis of epistemic states, make internal conflict observable and quantifiable, and are positioned as a step toward more transparent, reliable, and ethically sensitive AI systems, especially in contexts involving paradox, vagueness, or moral tension.

title: EvoCode-Bench: Evaluating Coding Agents in Multi-Turn Iterative Interactions
comment: Work in Progress; 32 pages, 10 figures, preprint
summary: Coding agents are increasingly used as iterative development partners, but most benchmarks still evaluate one specification followed by one final assessment. This leaves out a basic question: can an agent keep its own codebase working as requirements change? We introduce EvoCode-Bench, a benchmark of 26 stateful coding tasks and 227 evaluated rounds. Each task preserves the agent's workspace for 5-15 rounds, states requirements through observable behavior, and uses cumulative executable tests to check new requirements and still-active prior ones. We evaluate 13 coding agents with two metrics: MT@4, a four-attempt fail-stop multi-round score, and SR, a single-round score from a reference-completed prior state. For most agents, SR exceeds MT@4 by 22-40 points. The gap also changes rankings: the highest-SR agent (78.9) ranks only third in persistent execution (44.0 MT@4). Even the strongest agents achieve only about 50% success on multi-turn metrics, and aggregate pass rate drops below half of round-1 performance by round 5. Failure analysis shows tier-dependent behavior: weaker agents fail early, while stronger agents survive long enough to expose specification-tracking and regression failures. We release the benchmark data and Harbor multi-turn infrastructure.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EvoCode-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating coding agents on persistent, multi-round software development tasks with evolving requirements, revealing substantial gaps between single-round and multi-round performance.
AI.motivation: Existing coding benchmarks mostly test a one-shot setting: the agent receives a single specification and is evaluated on a final solution. This does not reflect realistic software development, where requirements evolve, codebases persist, and agents must avoid regressions while adding features. The authors want to answer whether coding agents can maintain and evolve an existing codebase over time as requirements change, and to provide a standardized way to measure this capability.
AI.method: The authors build EvoCode-Bench, consisting of 26 stateful coding tasks, each spanning 5–15 rounds (227 total rounds). For each task, the agent keeps its workspace across rounds, receives new requirements described via observable behavior, and is evaluated using cumulative executable tests that check both new requirements and still-active previous ones. They design two metrics: MT@4, a multi-round, fail-stop metric allowing up to four attempts per round, and SR, a single-round metric where evaluation starts from a reference-completed prior state. They then evaluate 13 coding agents on these tasks using both metrics and analyze performance over rounds and across agent tiers.
AI.result: Across 13 coding agents, SR scores are 22–40 points higher than MT@4 for most models, indicating that agents look much stronger when evaluated only in single-round, clean-start conditions than in persistent multi-round development. The best SR agent (78.9) is only third-best on MT@4 (44.0), showing that ranking changes significantly when persistence and regression avoidance matter. Even the strongest agents only achieve about 50% success on the multi-turn metric, and the aggregate test pass rate drops to less than half of the round-1 pass rate by round 5. Error analysis shows that weaker agents tend to fail in early rounds, while stronger agents last longer but then exhibit issues with tracking evolving specifications and avoiding regressions.
AI.conclusion: EvoCode-Bench reveals that current coding agents are far from reliably maintaining and evolving codebases in realistic multi-round settings. Single-round benchmarks substantially overestimate their practical reliability and can mis-rank models compared to multi-turn, stateful evaluation. The benchmark and the Harbor multi-turn infrastructure provide a foundation for future research on robust, persistent coding agents that can track changing specifications, preserve prior functionality, and avoid regressions over extended development sessions.

title: Measuring the Depth of LLM Unlearning via Activation Patching
summary: Large language model (LLM) unlearning has emerged as a crucial post-hoc mechanism for privacy protection and AI safety, yet auditing whether target knowledge is truly erased remains challenging. Existing output-level metrics fail to detect when this knowledge remains recoverable from internal representations. Recent white-box studies reveal such residual knowledge but often rely on auxiliary training or dataset-specific adaptations, leaving no generalizable metric. To address these limitations, we propose the Unlearning Depth Score (UDS), a metric that quantifies the mechanistic depth of unlearning via activation patching. UDS first identifies layers that encode the target knowledge using a retain model baseline, then measures how much of it is erased in the unlearned model on a 0-1 scale. In a meta-evaluation across 20 metrics on 150 unlearned models spanning 8 methods, UDS achieves the highest faithfulness and robustness, confirming our causal approach as the most reliable for unlearning evaluation. Case studies further reveal that white-box metrics can disagree at the layer level and that erasure depth varies across examples. We provide guidelines for integrating UDS into existing benchmarking frameworks and streamlining the evaluation pipeline. Code and data are available at https://github.com/gnueaj/unlearning-depth-score

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Unlearning Depth Score (UDS), a white-box metric that measures how thoroughly target knowledge has been causally erased from an LLM’s internal representations across layers.
AI.motivation: Existing unlearning evaluations mostly look at model outputs and miss residual knowledge that is still encoded internally and can be recovered. White-box studies show such residuals exist, but current approaches are ad hoc, require extra training, and are not easily comparable or generalizable. The field lacks a standardized, robust, and faithful metric to quantify how deeply and reliably unlearning has removed target knowledge inside the model.
AI.method: The authors define the Unlearning Depth Score (UDS), based on activation patching. They first use a retain (non-unlearned) baseline model to identify which layers encode the target knowledge. Then, for an unlearned model, they patch in or compare activations at those critical layers and quantify, on a 0–1 scale, how much of the target knowledge remains. They meta-evaluate UDS against 19 other metrics on 150 unlearned models produced by 8 different unlearning methods, analyzing faithfulness, robustness, layer-wise behavior, and example-wise variation.
AI.result: Across a large-scale meta-evaluation of 20 metrics over 150 unlearned models, UDS shows the highest faithfulness—best reflecting actual residual knowledge—and strongest robustness across methods and setups. The analysis reveals that different white-box metrics can disagree significantly at specific layers and that the depth and completeness of erasure vary across inputs. UDS consistently captures these nuances better than competing metrics.
AI.conclusion: UDS offers a principled, causal, and generalizable way to measure how deeply unlearning has removed target knowledge from an LLM’s internal representations. It outperforms existing metrics in faithfulness and robustness and exposes important layer- and example-level variation in unlearning quality. The authors recommend incorporating UDS into standard unlearning benchmarks and provide open-source code and data to facilitate adoption and streamline evaluation pipelines.

title: SkillEvolBench: Benchmarking the Evolution from Episodic Experience to Procedural Skills
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents accumulate rich episodic trajectories while solving real-world tasks, but it remains unclear whether such experience can be distilled into reusable procedural skills. We introduce SkillEvolBench, a diagnostic benchmark for evaluating this step from experience reuse to skill formation. It contains 180 tasks across six real-world agent environments, organized into role-conditioned task families with shared latent procedures. Agents learn from acquisition tasks, update an external skill library using compacted trajectories and verifier feedback, and then face frozen deployment tasks testing context shift, adversarial shortcuts, and composition. By comparing self-generated and curated-start skill evolution against no-skill and raw-trajectory controls, SkillEvolBench separates procedural abstraction from base capability, curated prior knowledge, and direct reuse of episodic traces. Across ten model configurations and three agent harnesses, we find that current agents often adapt locally but rarely form robust reusable skills. Skill-based conditions can improve acquisition or replay, and individual models sometimes gain on specific deployment axes, but these gains are unstable under frozen deployment. Raw-trajectory reuse frequently outperforms distilled skills, suggesting that current abstraction procedures discard contextual and procedural cues that remain useful for future tasks. Capacity and cost analyses further show that writing more skills or larger Tier-3 resource libraries is not sufficient: additional updates can improve coverage while introducing episode-specific drift and procedural clutter. These findings position SkillEvolBench as a testbed for measuring when one-off experience becomes durable procedural knowledge rather than task-local memory.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SkillEvolBench, a benchmark to test whether LLM agents can turn episodic task experience into reusable procedural skills, and finds that current agents mostly fail to form robust skills, with raw trajectory reuse often working better than distilled skills.
AI.motivation: While LLM agents generate rich trajectories when solving real-world tasks, it is unclear if and how this experience can be abstracted into generalizable skills rather than remaining task-specific memory. The community lacks a systematic way to diagnose whether agents are actually learning reusable procedures from experience, separate from their base model capabilities or curated prior knowledge. This gap motivates the creation of a benchmark to explicitly evaluate experience-to-skill formation and understand the limits of current skill abstraction methods.
AI.method: The authors design SkillEvolBench, a diagnostic benchmark with 180 tasks across six real-world agent environments, grouped into role-conditioned task families that share latent procedures. Agents first perform acquisition tasks, then compress their episodic trajectories and, using verifier feedback, write or update entries in an external skill library. In a subsequent frozen deployment phase, agents must solve new tasks using only the learned skill library under distribution shifts, adversarial shortcuts, and compositional challenges. The study compares different conditions: self-generated skills vs curated-start skills, skill-based agents vs no-skill baselines, and distilled skills vs direct raw-trajectory reuse, across ten model configurations and three agent harnesses. They also analyze the capacity and cost of larger skill libraries and resource pools to see how scale affects performance and procedural drift.
AI.result: Empirically, agents show some local adaptation during acquisition and occasionally gain on specific deployment axes, but they rarely develop robust, reusable procedural skills that transfer reliably under frozen deployment. Skill-based conditions can help during acquisition or replay, yet these improvements are unstable when generalizing to new tasks. Direct reuse of raw trajectories often outperforms reliance on distilled skills, indicating that current abstraction pipelines lose context and procedural signals that are still beneficial for future tasks. Scaling up by writing more skills or enlarging Tier-3 resource libraries does not reliably solve the problem: it may increase coverage but also introduces episode-specific drift, noise, and procedural clutter that can hurt generalization.
AI.conclusion: SkillEvolBench effectively isolates the process of transforming episodic experience into durable procedural knowledge, separate from base model strength and episodic memory reuse. The benchmark reveals that current LLM agents and skill-distillation methods are poor at forming stable, reusable skills, with naive abstraction often worse than directly reusing detailed trajectories. Simply adding more skills or larger resource libraries is insufficient and can introduce harmful drift. Thus, the field needs more principled mechanisms for representing, refining, and selecting procedural abstractions if we want agents that learn lasting skills from one-off experiences, and SkillEvolBench offers a standardized testbed for developing and evaluating such methods.

title: HiMed: Incentivizing Hindi Reasoning in Medical LLMs
summary: Medical large language models hold promise for reducing healthcare disparities, yet Hindi remains severely underrepresented. While medical LLMs excel in high-resource languages, their performance degrades sharply in Hindi, particularly on Indian systems of medicine. We argue that robust cross-lingual medical transfer requires Hindi reasoning. To this end, we introduce HiMed, a Hindi reasoning medical corpus and benchmark suite covering both Western and Indian medicine. We further propose HiMed-8B, a Hindi-form medical reasoning LLM, through the design of decaying scaffolding reward. Extensive experiments demonstrate improvement in Hindi medical reasoning performance and reduction in the English--Hindi accuracy gap. Ablation studies validate the contribution of each training stage and reward component. All data and code are available on GitHub: https://github.com/FreedomIntelligence/HiMed.

AI.tldr: The paper presents HiMed, a Hindi medical reasoning corpus, benchmark suite, and an 8B-parameter Hindi-form medical LLM (HiMed-8B) that together substantially improve Hindi medical reasoning and narrow the English–Hindi performance gap.
AI.motivation: Medical large language models perform well in high-resource languages but poorly in Hindi, especially for Indian systems of medicine, which risks exacerbating healthcare disparities. There is a lack of robust cross-lingual medical transfer and dedicated Hindi reasoning resources and models that cover both Western and Indian medicine.
AI.method: The authors construct HiMed, a Hindi reasoning medical corpus and evaluation benchmark spanning Western and Indian medical traditions. They then train HiMed-8B, a medical reasoning LLM specialized for Hindi, using a novel training strategy based on a decaying scaffolding reward, and perform multi-stage training with different reward components. They evaluate the model on the HiMed benchmarks and conduct ablation studies to isolate the contribution of each stage and reward element.
AI.result: Experiments show that HiMed-8B achieves higher performance on Hindi medical reasoning tasks than baseline models and significantly reduces the accuracy gap between English and Hindi. Ablation results confirm that each training stage and each part of the decaying scaffolding reward contributes meaningfully to these gains.
AI.conclusion: Developing dedicated Hindi-focused medical reasoning resources and models, combined with a decaying scaffolding reward training strategy, can substantially enhance Hindi medical performance and help close cross-lingual gaps. HiMed and HiMed-8B provide a practical path toward more equitable multilingual medical AI, and the open release of data and code enables further research and extension.

title: MAPLE: Multi-State Aggregated Policy Evaluation for AlphaZero in Imperfect-Information Games
comment: Accepted by the IEEE Conference on Games (IEEE CoG 2026)
summary: Imperfect-information games (IIGs) are challenging, as players must make decisions without fully observing the true game state. While AlphaZero has achieved remarkable success in perfect-information games, extending it to IIGs remains difficult. Existing search-based approaches, such as Perfect Information Monte Carlo (PIMC), suffer from strategy fusion, while Information Set Monte Carlo Tree Search (IS-MCTS) incurs high computational cost when combined with neural networks. In this paper, we propose Multi-State Aggregated PoLicy Evaluation (MAPLE), a tree search method that aggregates policy and value evaluations from multiple sampled world states within a single search tree, combining the advantages of PIMC and IS-MCTS while maintaining a controllable computational cost. We further incorporate a Siamese-based sampling strategy to select informative world states from the information set. Experiments on Phantom Go and Dark Hex show that MAPLE significantly outperforms the PIMC-based AlphaZero baseline, achieving Elo improvements of 291 and 136, respectively. These results demonstrate that MAPLE is an effective approach for AlphaZero-style learning in imperfect-information games.

AI.tldr: Introduces MAPLE, a search method that aggregates policy/value estimates over multiple sampled hidden states to enable AlphaZero-style learning in imperfect-information games, outperforming PIMC-based baselines.
AI.motivation: AlphaZero works very well in perfect-information games but struggles in imperfect-information games where the true state is hidden. Existing approaches either assume a single guessed state (PIMC), leading to strategy fusion and poor play, or use information-set search (IS-MCTS), which becomes too computationally expensive when paired with deep neural networks. There is a need for a search algorithm that can exploit neural guidance, reason over uncertainty about the hidden state, and remain computationally feasible.
AI.method: Proposes MAPLE (Multi-State Aggregated PoLicy Evaluation), a tree-search algorithm that, instead of building a separate search tree for each sampled world state, aggregates value and policy evaluations from multiple sampled states within a single tree. This combines ideas from PIMC and IS-MCTS while controlling cost. Additionally, introduces a Siamese-network-based sampling strategy to pick informative world states from the information set, improving the quality of sampled states used in search.
AI.result: In experiments on two benchmark imperfect-information board games, Phantom Go and Dark Hex, MAPLE substantially improves upon a PIMC-based AlphaZero baseline. The reported strength gains are 291 Elo in Phantom Go and 136 Elo in Dark Hex, indicating considerably stronger play under the same overall framework.
AI.conclusion: MAPLE effectively adapts AlphaZero-style search and learning to imperfect-information games by aggregating evaluations across multiple hidden states and using informed sampling. The empirical gains over PIMC-based AlphaZero suggest that this approach mitigates strategy fusion while avoiding the prohibitive costs of full IS-MCTS, making it a promising direction for IIGs.

title: Know You Before You Speak: User-State Modeling for LLM Personalization in Multi-Turn Conversation
summary: Personalized dialogue requires more than recalling explicit user histories: systems also need to infer hidden user states that evolve through interaction and shape appropriate response strategies. Existing memory- and profile-based methods primarily reuse observable user information, offering limited support for modeling user-state dynamics or selecting actions based on how they shape future user states. We propose PUMA (Prospective User-state Modeling for Action selection), a framework grounded in the Free Energy Principle (FEP) that formulates personalization as decision-making under partial observability, centered on an explicit user state model that captures latent user states and their action-conditioned dynamics. At each turn, PUMA maintains a belief over the user's hidden state, refines the user state model for observation generation and action-conditioned state transition, and selects dialogue actions by minimizing expected free energy, balancing epistemic and pragmatic objectives under a unified criterion. This formulation shifts personalization from passive memory retrieval to model-based decision-making over user evolution. We instantiate PUMA on healthcare-oriented counseling and motivational interviewing benchmarks with latent state annotations for rigorous evaluation. Experiments show that PUMA improves long-horizon dialogue outcomes while maintaining strong response quality, and a cross-dataset study demonstrates more reliable user-state estimation and next-state prediction.

AI.tldr: PUMA is a dialogue framework that models and tracks users’ latent, evolving states and chooses system responses by minimizing expected free energy, leading to better long-term personalized interaction outcomes.
AI.motivation: Personalized dialogue systems often rely on explicit user histories or profiles, which only capture observable behavior and static preferences. They struggle to represent how a user’s internal state (e.g., motivation, readiness to change, engagement) evolves over time and how system responses can proactively influence these hidden states. This limitation is particularly problematic in settings like counseling or motivational interviewing, where long-term outcomes depend on guiding users through latent state changes rather than simply replying appropriately turn-by-turn. The paper is motivated by the need for a principled, decision-theoretic way to model latent user states and choose actions that optimize long-horizon outcomes under partial observability.
AI.method: The paper introduces PUMA (Prospective User-state Modeling for Action selection), a framework grounded in the Free Energy Principle and cast as a partially observable decision-making problem. PUMA explicitly defines a user state model that encodes latent user states and their dynamics conditioned on system actions. At each dialogue turn, the system maintains and updates a belief distribution over the hidden user state using observed dialogue signals. The user state model includes both an observation model (how states generate user utterances/annotations) and a transition model (how states evolve given actions). For action selection, PUMA computes expected free energy for candidate actions, which combines epistemic value (information gain about the user state) and pragmatic value (progress toward desired user outcomes), and selects actions that minimize this expected free energy. This turns personalization into a model-based planning problem over user-state trajectories rather than simple retrieval or response scoring.
AI.result: PUMA is instantiated and tested on healthcare counseling and motivational interviewing dialogue benchmarks that include latent state annotations, enabling explicit evaluation of user-state tracking and prediction. Experimental results indicate that PUMA improves long-horizon dialogue outcomes compared with baselines, while preserving or matching strong local response quality. Additionally, in cross-dataset experiments, PUMA exhibits more accurate and robust estimation of current user states and better prediction of next user states than competing methods, suggesting improved generalization of the user-state model.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that framing personalized dialogue as decision-making under partial observability, with an explicit generative model of latent user states and their action-dependent dynamics, yields substantial benefits over memory- or profile-based methods. By minimizing expected free energy, PUMA unifies epistemic exploration and goal-directed behavior, enabling systems to proactively shape user evolution instead of passively reacting. The positive results on healthcare counseling tasks suggest that prospective user-state modeling is a promising direction for building more effective, long-horizon personalized dialogue systems, and the framework is likely extensible to other domains where latent user states and long-term outcomes are critical.

title: HyperGuide: Hyperbolic Guidance for Efficient Multi-Step Reasoning in Large Language Models
summary: Multi-step reasoning remains a central challenge for large language models: single-pass generation is efficient but lacks accuracy; tree-search methods explore multiple paths but are computation-heavy. We address this gap by distilling reasoning progress into a hyperbolic geometric signal that guides step-by-step generation. Our approach is motivated by a structural observation: in combinatorial reasoning trees, solution-bearing states are few while dead ends are exponentially numerous. The hyperbolic space matches this asymmetry, with compact volume near the origin and exponentially expanding capacity toward the boundary, so that distance-to-origin naturally encodes solution proximity while angular separation distinguishes branches requiring different next operations. We train a lightweight head to project LLM hidden states into this space, then fine-tune a low-rank adapter interactively on its own reasoning attempts to act on the injected signal. Across multiple benchmarks, the geometric signal yields consistent gains, with larger improvements on deeper reasoning chains. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/yuyuliu11037/HyperGuide.

AI.tldr: They propose a lightweight way to guide multi-step reasoning in LLMs using a hyperbolic geometric signal that estimates how close a state is to a solution and which direction to explore next, improving reasoning benchmarks without expensive tree search.
AI.motivation: Multi-step reasoning for LLMs is hard: single-pass generation is fast but often wrong, and tree-search methods like multi-path reasoning are more accurate but very computationally expensive. The authors want a method that approximates the benefits of search—better exploration of reasoning paths—while retaining near single-pass efficiency. They observe that reasoning can be seen as exploring a large combinatorial tree where correct solution states are rare but dead ends grow exponentially, and they look for a representation that naturally reflects this structure.
AI.method: They model the reasoning process as trajectories in a hyperbolic space. Hyperbolic geometry has compact volume near the origin and exponentially expanding volume towards the boundary. They learn a small projection head that maps the LLM’s hidden states at each step into this hyperbolic space. In this space, distance from the origin encodes proximity to a solution (closer to boundary corresponds to being further in exploration, with a notion of progress), and angular directions separate different reasoning branches. They then fine-tune a low-rank adapter (LoRA-style) on the base LLM in an interactive way: the model generates its own reasoning traces, which are used to teach it how to use the hyperbolic signal to choose better next steps during generation. The added head is lightweight and acts as a guidance signal during step-by-step generation, instead of running full tree search.
AI.result: On several reasoning benchmarks, their hyperbolic guidance signal consistently improves the performance of the base LLM on multi-step reasoning tasks. The gains are more pronounced for tasks that require deeper reasoning chains, indicating that the geometric representation is particularly helpful when search depth would otherwise be large. The method provides a better accuracy–efficiency trade-off compared to naive single-pass generation without incurring the high cost of full tree search.
AI.conclusion: Encoding reasoning progress as a hyperbolic geometric signal and training the LLM to act on this signal provides an effective and efficient alternative to heavy tree-search-based reasoning methods. Hyperbolic space is well matched to the combinatorial structure of reasoning trees, allowing solution proximity and branch differentiation to be captured compactly. A small projection head and low-rank adapter can leverage this structure to yield consistent improvements in multi-step reasoning performance, especially on deeper chains, while keeping computation overhead modest. The approach is implemented and released as HyperGuide.

title: Mix-MoE: Improving Multilingual Machine Translation of Large Language Models through Mixed MoEs
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown great promise in multilingual machine translation (MT), even with limited bilingual supervision. However, fine-tuning LLMs with parallel corpora presents major challenges, namely parameter interference. To address these issues, we propose Mix-MoE, a mixed Mixture-of-Experts framework designed to train LLMs for multilingual MT. Our framework operates in two distinct stages: (1) post-pretraining with MoE on monolingual corpora, and (2) post-pretraining with MoE on parallel corpora. Crucially, we divide the MoE layers into two specialized groups: Language Model Experts (LM Experts) and Machine Translation Experts (MT Experts). LM Experts are designed to capture and retain the monolingual knowledge learned by the pre-trained LLM. MT Experts, on the other hand, are specifically trained to acquire and store bilingual translation knowledge. Furthermore, to facilitate effective interaction between these specialized experts and leverage potential underlying structural patterns in text, we introduce a routing mechanism enhanced by Fourier Transform features derived from model representations. The experimental results demonstrate that Mix-MoE excels in multilingual MT, significantly outperforming existing baselines and showing notable progress in mitigating parameter interference.

AI.tldr: Proposes Mix-MoE, a mixed Mixture-of-Experts framework that separates language modeling and translation experts and uses Fourier-based routing to improve multilingual MT and reduce parameter interference when fine-tuning LLMs.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning large language models for multilingual machine translation with parallel corpora leads to parameter interference: monolingual language modeling capabilities can be degraded when the same parameters are updated for bilingual translation knowledge. There is a need for a method that can leverage LLMs for multilingual MT while preserving monolingual competence and avoiding destructive interference across tasks and languages.
AI.method: Introduce Mix-MoE, a two-stage post-pretraining Mixture-of-Experts architecture. Stage 1: apply MoE on monolingual corpora to form and train Language Model Experts (LM Experts) that capture and retain monolingual knowledge from the base LLM. Stage 2: further post-pretrain with MoE on parallel corpora, adding or training Machine Translation Experts (MT Experts) specialized for bilingual translation knowledge. The MoE layers are explicitly partitioned into LM Experts and MT Experts. A routing mechanism, augmented with Fourier Transform features of intermediate model representations, determines which experts to activate, aiming to exploit structural patterns in text and promote effective interaction between LM and MT experts.
AI.result: Experiments on multilingual machine translation benchmarks show that Mix-MoE significantly outperforms existing baselines, indicating better translation quality. The method also empirically mitigates parameter interference, preserving monolingual capabilities while enhancing multilingual MT performance.
AI.conclusion: Separating language modeling and translation knowledge into specialized experts within a mixed MoE framework, and using Fourier-enhanced routing, is an effective strategy for training LLMs for multilingual MT. This design alleviates parameter interference and yields superior multilingual translation performance compared with prior approaches.

title: Neuro-Inspired Inverse Learning for Planning and Control
summary: We present a neuro-inspired framework for embodied planning and control. Building on three principles that enable fast and highly effective goal-directed behavior in the mammalian brain - paired forward/inverse internal models, open-loop multi-step motor commands, and sequential, hierarchical organization of action - our Inverter framework uses learned components, trained end-to-end through Inverse Learning (IL) and supplemented where natural by analytic or algorithmic modules; we formalize IL and delineate it from supervised, reinforcement, and imitation learning. IL bridges Reinforcement Learning (RL)-style amortization, which runs in a single forward pass but emits only one action at a time, and Optimal Control (OC)-style sequence planning over whole trajectories, but with iterative test-time computation. Single Inverters or hierarchical n=2 Inverter stacks match or improve on offline-RL and diffusion-planner baselines on all 3 maze2d and 6 antmaze D4RL variants by an average of +24.2% (range -1.9% to +78.2%), at one-to-two orders of magnitude less inference compute time. Distinctively, optimizing through the Figure of Merit (FoM) over the entire T-step action sequence - rather than per step - lets Inverters produce smooth, goal-coherent, trajectory-wide structure and reach control policies closer to the analytic optimum than the policy underlying the training data itself. We also identify a failure mode of IL: FoM hacking under narrow training-data coverage, which we mitigate by using random training data with broader coverage. As an application example, a Pulse Inverter synthesizes arbitrary single-qubit quantum gates with fidelity matching the standard iterative numerical baseline (GRAPE), at more than 1000x lower per-gate compute time. In summary, we conclude that IL enables a versatile class of world-interfaces, especially for latency- and resource-critical embodied AI.

AI.tldr: Introduces an Inverter framework and Inverse Learning (IL), a neuro-inspired approach that plans whole action sequences efficiently and outperforms strong RL/OC baselines on D4RL mazes and quantum control tasks.
AI.motivation: Traditional RL emits one action per step and can be slow or compute-heavy at inference, while optimal control plans full trajectories but is expensive and not easily amortized. The authors seek a framework that combines the fast, amortized nature of RL policies with the trajectory-level optimality and structure of control methods, inspired by how the mammalian brain performs rapid, goal-directed behavior. They also aim for methods practical in resource- and latency-constrained embodied AI settings and capable of reaching or surpassing the quality of the policies that generated their training data.
AI.method: They propose the Inverter framework built on three neuro-inspired principles: (1) paired forward and inverse internal models; (2) open-loop, multi-step motor command generation; and (3) sequential, hierarchical action organization. They formalize Inverse Learning (IL), a training paradigm distinct from supervised, reinforcement, and imitation learning, where learned components (Inverters) are trained end-to-end to output full T-step action sequences that directly optimize a task-level Figure of Merit (FoM). The system can include both learned and analytic/algorithmic modules. At test time, IL uses iterative computation to refine the planned sequence, sitting between RL-style single-pass policies and OC-style optimization. They evaluate single and hierarchical (2-layer) Inverters on maze2d and antmaze D4RL benchmarks, and apply a specialized Pulse Inverter to quantum gate synthesis, comparing to GRAPE. They also analyze failure modes, especially FoM hacking under limited data coverage, and explore mitigation via broader, random data.
AI.result: On 3 maze2d and 6 antmaze D4RL tasks, Inverters (single or stacked) match or surpass strong offline-RL and diffusion-based planners on all variants, with an average performance gain of +24.2% (ranging from -1.9% to +78.2%), while requiring 10–100x less inference compute. Optimizing the FoM over the entire T-step sequence produces smoother, more coherent trajectories and policies that approach analytic-optimal performance and can exceed the quality of the behavior policy used to generate training data. They show a notable failure mode, FoM hacking, when training data has narrow coverage, but demonstrate that using broader random data reduces this issue. In quantum control, a Pulse Inverter can synthesize arbitrary single-qubit gates at fidelity comparable to GRAPE, but with over 1000x lower per-gate computation time.
AI.conclusion: Inverse Learning (IL) yields a neuro-inspired, sequence-level planning and control framework (Inverters) that unifies benefits of amortized RL and optimal control with far lower inference cost. By optimizing over whole action sequences and leveraging hierarchical organization, IL enables smooth, goal-coherent behaviors that can outperform the policies that generated the data and closely approximate analytic optima. While vulnerable to FoM hacking under poor data coverage, this can be mitigated with broader training data. The approach generalizes from embodied control in D4RL mazes to quantum gate synthesis, suggesting IL as a versatile and efficient interface between learned models and complex dynamical worlds, particularly in latency- and resource-constrained embodied AI applications.

title: CP-Agent: A Calibrated Risk-Controlled Agent for Feedback-Driven Competitive Programming
summary: Large language models still struggle with contest-level programming, while many agentic remedies rely on massive inference-time sampling or expensive multi-stage post-training. We study when execution feedback reliably helps an LLM CP solver and which mechanisms govern the gains. We model feedback-driven solving as a calibrated stopped process and identify three quantities: false-admission risk, program-level evidence against bad programs, and the active-state success hazard. Under held-out trace calibration and selection from a pre-declared finite controller manifest, the resulting structural certificate lower-bounds the clean success probability before false admission. We instantiate mechanisms targeting these quantities as Dual-Granularity Verification, Test Augmentation, and Experience-Driven Self-Evolving, yielding CP-Agent. Without updating any parameters, CP-Agent raises Pass@1 from 25.8\% to 48.5\% on LiveCodeBench Pro and improves Refine@5 by 11.0\% on ICPC-Eval. Across three LLM backbones, CP-Agent lies on the cost--accuracy efficiency frontier, and ablations show that each component primarily affects its corresponding certificate quantity.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CP-Agent, an execution-feedback-driven framework that significantly improves large language models’ performance on contest-level programming without further parameter updates, and analyzes why and when such feedback helps.
AI.motivation: Large language models underperform on contest-level programming problems, and existing agent-style or post-training approaches often require heavy sampling or costly multi-stage training. The authors want to understand under what conditions execution feedback (e.g., running code and observing results) can reliably improve code-generation performance, and to formalize and certify these improvements rather than treating them as heuristic gains.
AI.method: The authors model feedback-driven code solving as a calibrated stopped process characterized by three key quantities: (1) false-admission risk (accepting incorrect programs), (2) program-level evidence against bad programs (how strongly tests refute wrong code), and (3) active-state success hazard (conditional probability of success as the process continues). Based on this, they derive a structural certificate that lower-bounds the clean success probability under assumptions like held-out trace calibration and a finite, pre-specified set of controllers. They then design three mechanisms targeting each quantity—Dual-Granularity Verification, Test Augmentation, and Experience-Driven Self-Evolving—and combine them into CP-Agent, an inference-time controller operating over LLM-generated code without updating model parameters.
AI.result: CP-Agent, applied to several LLM backbones, substantially boosts competitive programming performance without any additional training: Pass@1 on LiveCodeBench Pro increases from 25.8% to 48.5%, and Refine@5 on ICPC-Eval improves by 11.0%. The method is on the cost–accuracy efficiency frontier compared with alternatives, and ablation studies show that removing each component primarily degrades the specific certificate quantity it was designed to target, supporting the proposed theoretical decomposition.
AI.conclusion: Execution feedback can be made both effective and certifiable for contest programming by viewing the solving process as a calibrated stopped process and explicitly managing false-admission risk, evidence against bad programs, and success hazard. CP-Agent operationalizes this through modular mechanisms that significantly improve LLM code performance at inference-time alone, with empirical and ablation results aligning with the theoretical framework, suggesting a principled path for building efficient, reliable agentic code solvers.

title: Palette: A Modular, Controllable, and Efficient Framework for On-demand Authorized Safety Alignment Relaxation in LLMs
summary: Current safety alignment of foundation models largely follows a \emph{one-size-fits-all} paradigm, applying the same refusal policy across users and contexts. As a result, models may refuse requests that are unsafe for general users but legitimate for authorized professionals, limiting helpfulness in specialized professional settings. Existing approaches either require costly realignment or rely on inference-time steering that suffers from imprecise control and added latency. To this end, we propose \textsc{Palette}, a modular, controllable, and efficient framework that selectively relaxes refusal behavior on authorized target domains while preserving standard safety elsewhere. Our method identifies a refusal direction via multi-objective search and internalizes it into the model through lightweight adaptation. \textsc{Palette} further supports modular composition: it learns domain-specific safety controls independently and composes them through parameter merging, enabling on-demand multi-domain authorization without retraining. Experiments across four safety benchmarks, multiple model variants, and both LLMs and VLMs show that \textsc{Palette} delivers precise safety control without sacrificing general utility, offering a practical path toward foundation models that adapt to diverse professional needs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Palette, a modular and efficient framework to selectively relax safety refusals for authorized professional domains while maintaining standard safety elsewhere.
AI.motivation: Current foundation models use a uniform safety policy for all users and contexts, causing models to refuse some requests that may be unsafe for the general public but necessary for trained professionals (e.g., in specialized domains). Existing solutions either require expensive model realignment or inference-time steering that is slow and imprecise. There is a need for a controllable, efficient way to customize safety behavior for specific, authorized domains without degrading overall safety or utility.
AI.method: Palette identifies a "refusal direction" in the model via multi-objective search, representing how the model transitions between refusing and answering. It then internalizes this direction using lightweight model adaptation rather than full retraining. The framework is modular: it learns domain-specific safety controls independently and combines them using parameter merging so that multiple domain authorizations can be activated on demand without additional training.
AI.result: Across four safety benchmarks, on several model variants, and on both language and vision–language models, Palette provides precise control over when safety refusals are relaxed in target domains. It preserves general safety behavior outside these domains and does not significantly harm overall model utility, while avoiding the latency and instability issues of inference-time steering.
AI.conclusion: Palette offers a practical approach for aligning foundation models to diverse professional needs by modularly and efficiently customizing safety refusals for authorized domains. It enables fine-grained, composable safety control without sacrificing general safety or utility, suggesting a path toward context-aware safety alignment for real-world applications.

title: The Path Matters: Learning a Token-Commitment Policy for Diffusion Language Models
summary: Diffusion large language models promise faster generation by refining many token positions in parallel, but this parallelism introduces a hidden control problem: which proposed tokens should be transferred into the partially decoded sequence at each step? We refer to this decision as token commitment. Existing frozen-generator decoders largely rely on hand-designed confidence rules or block-specific acceptance filters. We argue that token commitment can instead be learned as a reusable trace-state policy. We introduce TraceLock, a lightweight plug-in controller that instantiates this policy for a frozen diffusion language model. Since oracle commitment times are unavailable, TraceLock derives self-supervision from future stability: at decoding step t, a proposed token for position i is labeled stable if it matches the final token at position i after the full decoding trace completes. The controller scores variable-length trace states and decides which active token proposals should be committed to the partially decoded sequence. Once trained for a given frozen backbone, the controller can be deployed across local-window widths, generation lengths, and step budgets without retraining or per-setting calibration. Experiments on question answering, mathematical reasoning, and code generation show that TraceLock improves the quality-step tradeoff over heuristic and learned baselines, with particularly stable behavior under cross-setting deployment.
