It is evaluated on language modeling (long-context), vision-language, and vision-language-action tasks, and analyzed with tools like LogitLens and CKA to understand its effect on representation depth and information flow.
AI.result: Across evaluated tasks, Lngram outperforms baseline Transformers and Engram, consistently lowering perplexity on long-context language modeling and providing effective domain-knowledge injection when added after pretraining. Joint training of Lngram with the backbone surpasses full fine-tuning of the backbone alone. In multimodal tasks (vision-language and vision-language-action), Lngram yields overall performance improvements. Representation analyses show that prediction-relevant information appears earlier in the network when using Lngram, effectively increasing the model’s usable depth with modest inference and memory overhead.
AI.conclusion: Lngram successfully decouples local retrieval from backbone computation in a tokenizer-agnostic, modality-general way by operating over learned latent symbols. It improves long-context and multimodal performance and can be used both as a post hoc add-on for pretrained models and as a component in joint training, achieving better results than full fine-tuning. The analyses indicate that Lngram makes the model more depth-efficient, enabling earlier emergence of task-relevant information with limited extra cost.

title: Adaptive Human-AI Coordination via Hierarchical Action Disentanglement
summary: Human-AI collaboration requires agents that can adapt to diverse partner behaviors and skill levels while remaining robust to unseen partners. Existing methods often collapse to a single dominant behavior or learn poorly aligned skills, limiting effective coordination. We propose Intrinsic Action Disentanglement (IAD), a deep hierarchical reinforcement learning (DHRL) framework that learns distinct, partner-aware low-level action sequences conditioned on high-level latent skills. IAD introduces an intrinsic reward that explicitly encourages disentangled action distributions of the agent's low-level policy across skills, yielding an interpretable mapping between high-level decisions and partner-specific behavioral responses. By capturing temporally extended interaction patterns, IAD enables flexible adaptation to heterogeneous partner dynamics under distributional shift. We evaluate IAD in the Overcooked-AI domain across multiple layouts and diverse partner settings, including unseen simulated partners, a human-proxy model trained on human-human gameplay, and real human partners. Results show that IAD consistently outperforms strong baselines and achieves more reliable, adaptive coordination across all settings.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Intrinsic Action Disentanglement (IAD), a hierarchical RL method that learns disentangled, partner-aware skills to improve human-AI collaboration and robustness to unseen partners.
AI.motivation: Human-AI collaboration requires agents to coordinate with partners of varying behaviors and skills, including unseen partners. Existing multi-agent and collaborative RL methods tend to either converge to a single dominant behavior or learn skills that are not well aligned with different partner types, which harms coordination and adaptability. The authors aim to create agents that flexibly adapt to heterogeneous partners while maintaining robustness under distributional shift in partner behavior.
AI.method: The authors propose Intrinsic Action Disentanglement (IAD), a deep hierarchical reinforcement learning framework. IAD uses high-level latent skills that condition a low-level policy producing action sequences. To ensure that these skills correspond to distinct, partner-aware behaviors, they introduce an intrinsic reward that encourages the low-level action distributions across different skills to be disentangled. This creates a clear mapping from high-level decisions to interpretable, partner-specific behavioral responses and captures temporally extended interaction patterns during collaboration.
AI.result: In the Overcooked-AI environment, tested across various layouts and diverse partner types (unseen simulated agents, a human-proxy model trained from human-human data, and real human partners), IAD consistently performs better than strong baseline methods. It yields higher coordination performance and more reliable adaptation to different partner behaviors in all evaluated settings.
AI.conclusion: Enforcing intrinsic action disentanglement within a hierarchical RL framework leads to partner-aware, interpretable skills that improve adaptability and robustness in human-AI collaboration. IAD provides a practical approach for building collaborative agents that coordinate effectively with heterogeneous and unseen partners, outperforming existing baselines in a challenging multi-agent domain.

title: Towards a Universal Causal Reasoner
summary: Despite the importance of causal reasoning, training LLMs to reason causally remains underexplored. Existing data efforts mostly focus on benchmarking LLMs on specific aspects of causality, making them less suitable for training generalizable causal reasoners. To address this, we propose UniCo, a data generation framework that both (1) addresses 18 causal query types across Pearl's Causal Ladder and (2) translates natively symbolic examples into code and natural language forms to simulate real-world use cases where causal terms are not explicitly specified. To ensure data quality, UniCo grounds answers with exact causal inference and filters cases with reasoning shortcuts. Upon supervised finetuning with 66.6K UniCo-generated instances, Qwen3-4B, Qwen3-8B and Olmo-3-7B-Instruct achieve an average of 22.9% improvements across all 18 in-distribution query types, and 8.1% over state-of-the-art causal data generation frameworks on 7 established causal benchmarks outside the training distribution. More importantly, in real-world medical understanding, legal decision, and tabular reasoning, UniCo-trained models consistently display more faithful reasoning traces, outperforming the base models by an average of 20.2% in faithfulness metrics. These suggest that causality-centered training not only strengthens causal reasoning, but also equips LLMs with a causal mindset in general reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces UniCo, a data generation framework to train large language models (LLMs) for generalizable causal reasoning across many query types and representations, leading to significantly better causal and general reasoning performance.
AI.motivation: Causal reasoning is crucial for trustworthy and explainable AI, but current efforts mainly build benchmarks that test only narrow aspects of causality and are not well-suited for training LLMs to become robust, general causal reasoners. There is a need for a systematic way to generate high-quality, diverse causal training data that spans Pearl’s Causal Ladder, avoids reasoning shortcuts, and reflects real-world use cases where causal structure is implicit rather than explicitly described in formal causal language.
AI.method: The authors propose UniCo, a data generation framework that (1) systematically covers 18 causal query types aligned with Pearl’s Causal Ladder (associational, interventional, and counterfactual queries), and (2) converts symbolic causal examples into both code-based and natural language formats to mimic realistic scenarios where causal concepts are embedded but not overtly named. UniCo uses exact causal inference to compute ground-truth answers and applies filtering to remove cases where models might exploit superficial shortcuts instead of performing genuine causal reasoning. The generated 66.6K examples are then used to supervisedly finetune several LLMs, including Qwen3-4B, Qwen3-8B, and Olmo-3-7B-Instruct.
AI.result: After finetuning on 66.6K UniCo-generated instances, Qwen3-4B, Qwen3-8B, and Olmo-3-7B-Instruct show on average a 22.9% improvement across the 18 in-distribution causal query types. On seven standard causal reasoning benchmarks that are out-of-distribution with respect to UniCo’s training data, UniCo-trained models achieve an 8.1% average improvement over prior state-of-the-art causal data generation frameworks. In applied domains such as medical understanding, legal decision-making, and tabular (structured data) reasoning, the UniCo-trained models exhibit more faithful step-by-step reasoning traces, with an average 20.2% gain in faithfulness metrics compared to the corresponding base models.
AI.conclusion: UniCo demonstrates that carefully designed, causality-centered training data can substantially improve LLMs’ ability to handle a wide spectrum of causal queries and to generalize that causal competence to new tasks and domains. By grounding examples in exact causal inference and eliminating shortcut-prone instances, UniCo not only enhances performance on dedicated causal benchmarks but also induces more faithful and causally structured reasoning in practical applications, suggesting that such training instills a broader “causal mindset” in LLMs.

title: Partner-Aware Hierarchical Skill Discovery for Robust Human-AI Collaboration
summary: Multi-agent collaboration, especially in human-AI teaming, requires agents that can adapt to novel partners with diverse and dynamic behaviors. Conventional Deep Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning (DHRL) methods focus on agent-centric rewards and overlook partner behavior, leading to shortcut learning, where skills exploit spurious information instead of adapting to partners' dynamic behaviors. This limitation undermines agents' ability to adapt and coordinate effectively with novel partners. We introduce Partner-Aware Skill Discovery (PASD), a DHRL framework that learns skills conditioned on partner behavior. PASD introduces a contrastive intrinsic reward to capture patterns emerging from partner interactions, aligning skill representations across similar partners while maintaining discriminability across diverse strategies. By structuring the skill space based on partner interactions, this approach mitigates shortcut learning and promotes behavioral consistency, enabling robust and adaptive coordination. We extensively evaluate PASD in the Overcooked-AI benchmark with a diverse population of partners characterized by varying skill levels and play styles. We further evaluate the approach with human proxy models trained from human-human gameplay trajectories. PASD consistently outperforms existing population-based and hierarchical baselines, demonstrating transferable skill learning that generalizes across a wide range of partner behaviors. Analysis of learned skill representations shows that PASD adapts effectively to diverse partner behaviors, highlighting its robustness in human-AI collaboration.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Partner-Aware Skill Discovery (PASD), a hierarchical RL framework that learns skills conditioned on partner behavior for better human-AI collaboration, and shows it outperforms baselines in Overcooked-AI with diverse partners and human proxies.
AI.motivation: Existing Deep Hierarchical RL methods are agent-centric and ignore the behavior of collaborators, leading to shortcut learning where skills exploit spurious patterns instead of genuinely adapting to diverse and dynamic partners. This hurts coordination and generalization to novel partners in multi-agent and human-AI teaming scenarios.
AI.method: Introduce PASD, a Partner-Aware Skill Discovery framework in hierarchical RL that conditions skills on partner behavior and uses a contrastive intrinsic reward. The contrastive objective aligns skill representations for similar partner behaviors while keeping them separable for different partner strategies, thereby structuring the skill space according to partner interaction patterns and discouraging shortcut policies.
AI.result: In the Overcooked-AI benchmark with a wide range of partner skill levels and play styles, as well as with human proxy models learned from human gameplay trajectories, PASD achieves higher coordination performance than population-based and hierarchical RL baselines, and learns skills that transfer across diverse partners.
AI.conclusion: Conditioning hierarchical skills on partner behavior and shaping them with a contrastive intrinsic reward mitigates shortcut learning, produces more consistent and partner-adaptive behaviors, and yields robust, transferable skills that generalize to novel collaborators in human-AI teaming settings.

title: DTO: a Differentiable Training Objective for Effective Counterfactual Story Rewriting
summary: Counterfactual story rewriting is a natural language processing task that requires updating an existing story to reflect a chosen alternative event, yet preserving all the unaffected storyline elements and overall coherence. While large language models have recently made remarkable progress on this task, it still remains challenging since the required modifications are typically very small in size and highly localized. As a consequence, models trained in a conventional manner with the maximum-likelihood training objective tend to overlook these nuances. At the same time, more sophisticated training approaches based on reinforcement learning are notoriously slow and difficult to set up. For these reasons, our paper proposes a novel, differentiable training objective (DTO) that directly optimizes for the requisite counterfactual improvements. In our approach, a transformer model is fine-tuned via end-to-end backpropagation against a fully differentiable loss function that jointly rewards (i) fidelity to the reference rewrite and (ii) semantic consistency with the source narrative. The empirical evaluation on the TimeTravel and ART datasets shows that the proposed DTO approach has been able to surpass a maximum-likelihood baseline and a preference-based approach, and perform competitively against two contemporary large language models in all evaluation metrics. These findings substantiate the effectiveness of task-specific differentiable objectives for nuanced, controlled text-generation tasks.

AI.tldr: They propose a new differentiable training objective for counterfactual story rewriting that improves fidelity to edits and consistency with the original story, outperforming standard maximum-likelihood and some preference-based and LLM baselines.
AI.motivation: Counterfactual story rewriting needs very small, localized edits to a story while maintaining overall coherence. Standard maximum-likelihood training often misses these subtle changes, and reinforcement-learning-based methods, though more targeted, are slow and hard to configure. There is a need for a simple, efficient training objective that explicitly optimizes for high‑quality counterfactual rewrites without RL complexity.
AI.method: They introduce a differentiable training objective (DTO) for fine‑tuning transformer models on the counterfactual rewriting task. DTO is an end‑to‑end, backpropagation‑friendly loss that combines two components: (i) a term rewarding fidelity of the generated rewrite to a gold reference rewrite, and (ii) a term rewarding semantic consistency with the original source narrative. This composite loss directly encodes the desired properties of counterfactual rewrites and replaces or augments standard maximum‑likelihood training, avoiding reinforcement learning altogether.
AI.result: On the TimeTravel and ART counterfactual rewriting datasets, transformer models trained with DTO outperform a maximum-likelihood baseline and a preference-based training approach, and are competitive with two contemporary large language models across all reported evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: Task-specific, fully differentiable training objectives like DTO can effectively guide models to perform nuanced, controlled text generation—here, counterfactual story rewriting—without resorting to complex RL setups. Directly encoding counterfactual quality criteria into the loss leads to measurable gains over generic maximum-likelihood training and some preference-based methods, while remaining efficient and easy to optimize.

title: Distilling Game Code World Model Generation into Lightweight Large Language Models
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown great ability in generating executable code from natural language, opening the possibility of automatically constructing environments for AI agents. Recent work on Code World Models (CWMs) demonstrates that LLMs can translate game rules into Python implementations compatible with solvers like Monte Carlo Tree Search. We study this problem in game settings, where generated environments must implement rules, legal actions, state transitions, observations, and rewards. We refer to these game-specific executable models as Game Code World Models (GameCWMs). However, current approaches to generating code world models rely on frontier models and inference-time refinement loops, limiting accessibility and scalability. This work investigates whether GameCWM generation capabilities can be distilled into smaller models through post-training. We introduce: (1) a curated dataset of 30 games spanning perfect and imperfect information games, (2) a verification framework that evaluates generated code against structural and semantic game properties, and (3) a post-training pipeline combining Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). We experiment with Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct and find that SFT can increase syntactic correctness, while RLVR can improve execution-level adherence to game rules, thereby improving Qwen's ability to generate valid GameCWMs in both perfect and imperfect information games. Overall, our pipeline makes Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct more capable of generating valid GameCWMs, thereby offering a scalable path toward automatic environment generation from natural language.

AI.tldr: They distill the ability to generate game environments as executable code from large models into a small 3B model using SFT plus RL with verifiable rewards, backed by a curated game dataset and verification framework.
AI.motivation: LLMs can generate executable code and thus entire environments for AI agents, but current code world model approaches depend on very large frontier models and costly refinement loops, making them hard to scale or democratize. The authors want to see if smaller models can be trained to perform this task reliably for games, where environments must implement full rule-compliant dynamics (actions, transitions, observations, rewards).
AI.method: They define Game Code World Models (GameCWMs): game environments expressed as executable code capturing rules, legal moves, state transitions, observations, and rewards. They build (1) a dataset of 30 games (covering perfect and imperfect information), (2) an automatic verification framework that checks generated code structurally and semantically against game properties, and (3) a post-training pipeline for a 3B model (Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct) that first uses Supervised Fine-Tuning on curated examples and then Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), where the verification framework supplies reward signals based on how well the generated code satisfies game specifications. They then evaluate GameCWM generation quality pre- and post-training.
AI.result: On Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, Supervised Fine-Tuning improves the syntactic correctness of generated game environment code, while the RLVR stage improves semantic and execution-level fidelity to the intended game rules. Together, these steps significantly increase the rate at which the 3B model can produce valid GameCWMs across both perfect and imperfect information games, compared with the base model and with SFT alone.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that the capability to generate valid executable game environments from natural language can be effectively distilled into a relatively small 3B model using a combination of SFT and RL with verifiable rewards, guided by an automatic verification framework. This offers a more scalable and accessible alternative to relying on large frontier models with expensive refinement loops for automatic environment generation from natural language descriptions of games.

title: When Reasoning Hurts: Source-Aware Evaluation of Frontier LLMs for Clinical SOAP Note Generation
summary: Reasoning-enabled LLMs perform strongly on medical reasoning benchmarks, but it remains unclear whether these gains transfer to structured clinical documentation; we investigate this question using SOAP note generation from clinical dialogue in a source-aware benchmark spanning OMI Health, ACI-Bench, and PriMock57. We evaluate GPT-5.4, DeepSeek-V4-Flash, and Gemma-4-E4B in a controlled 2x2 design that independently toggles provider-native reasoning and same-source retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). Outputs are assessed using seven automatic metrics alongside two reference-aware LLM judges. Both evaluation approaches agree that a non-reasoning GPT-5.4 configuration achieves the highest overall quality, while DeepSeek-V4-Flash performs best among reasoning-enabled configurations. Enabling reasoning significantly degrades GPT-5.4 performance across all three datasets, whereas same-source RAG yields smaller, model-dependent improvements. Overall, the findings indicate that stronger reasoning capability should not be assumed to improve fidelity-sensitive SOAP note generation without dedicated, task-specific evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether reasoning-enabled large language models (LLMs) actually help for generating structured clinical SOAP notes from dialogue, and finds that added reasoning can hurt performance, especially for GPT-5.4, while retrieval helps only slightly.
AI.motivation: Although reasoning-augmented LLMs do well on medical reasoning benchmarks, it is unknown whether these improvements carry over to practical, fidelity-sensitive clinical documentation tasks like SOAP note generation. The authors want to close this gap and challenge the assumption that better reasoning always helps in real clinical NLP applications.
AI.method: They create a source-aware benchmark for SOAP note generation from clinical dialogue, combining three datasets (OMI Health, ACI-Bench, PriMock57). They run a 2x2 controlled experiment toggling two factors independently: (1) whether provider-native reasoning is enabled in the LLM; and (2) whether same-source retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is used. They test three models: GPT-5.4, DeepSeek-V4-Flash, and Gemma-4-E4B. Outputs are evaluated both with seven automatic metrics and with two reference-aware LLM judges, enabling comparison across models and configurations.
AI.result: A non-reasoning configuration of GPT-5.4 yields the highest overall quality in SOAP note generation according to both automatic metrics and LLM judges. Among reasoning-enabled setups, DeepSeek-V4-Flash performs best. Turning on reasoning significantly worsens GPT-5.4 performance across all three datasets. In contrast, enabling same-source RAG leads to smaller, model-dependent gains rather than universally strong improvements.
AI.conclusion: Enhanced general reasoning capabilities in LLMs should not be presumed to improve performance on structured, fidelity-critical clinical documentation tasks such as SOAP note generation. Reasoning can even degrade performance for some models, and RAG offers only modest, model-dependent benefits. Therefore, task-specific evaluation is essential before deploying reasoning-enabled LLMs in clinical note generation workflows.

title: A governance horizon for ethical-use constraints in open-weight AI models
summary: Ethical constraints on open-weight AI models are both a reflection of societal concerns and a foundation for AI governance policy. They are expected to propagate to downstream derivatives while implemented as voluntary metadata disclosures that must be restated at each generation of reuse. We audit 2,142,823 model repositories on Hugging Face Hub to test whether this disclosure-based governance infrastructure can sustain traceability across deep model lineages. Restriction evidence decays with a half-life of 1.31 derivation steps ($R^2$=0.98), and beyond seven downstream generations at least 80% of descendant models lack sufficient public evidence for a governance determination, a depth boundary we formalize as the governance horizon. Platform-level interventions to restore missing licence metadata reveal that policy design (not enforcement alone) is the binding factor: inheritance-only designs require near-complete enforcement to move the horizon, whereas a mandatory-declaration design that explicitly resolves orphan lineage components shifts the horizon already at moderate enforcement. The structural bottleneck is lineages with no inheritable upstream intent: such orphan components remain undecidable under any inheritance-only policy regardless of enforcement rate, and unresolved upstream nodes additionally create direct downstream undecidability bottlenecks that inheritance rules alone cannot recover. Comparison with PyPI, where governance signals are carried by explicit machine-readable declarations, corroborates that the collapse is topology-specific to open-weight derivation rather than inherent to open ecosystems. These results establish that disclosure-based governance has a shallow, structurally determined reach in open-weight AI, and that achieving deep supply-chain accountability requires provenance mechanisms propagating governance signals through derivation itself.

AI.tldr: The paper audits over 2.1M Hugging Face models and shows that ethical and license metadata rapidly disappears along derivation chains, making current disclosure-based governance shallow and unreliable for deep lineages.
AI.motivation: Open-weight AI models are widely reused and modified, and governance policies assume that ethical and license constraints can be tracked through voluntary metadata disclosures. Policymakers and platforms rely on this assumption to ensure accountability and compliance in AI supply chains, but it is unclear whether such disclosure-based mechanisms actually preserve governance-relevant information as models are repeatedly derived and republished.
AI.method: The authors crawl and analyze 2,142,823 model repositories on Hugging Face Hub, reconstructing derivation lineages and checking the presence and quality of license/ethical restriction metadata across generations. They model how often governance-relevant signals are preserved or lost with each derivation step and estimate a decay curve for traceability. They then simulate or evaluate different platform-level policy designs—inheritance-only versus mandatory-declaration schemes with orphan resolution—to see how these affect the depth at which governance decisions remain possible. They compare these dynamics with those on PyPI, where governance information is encoded via explicit machine-readable declarations, to isolate whether the collapse is specific to open-weight derivation topologies.
AI.result: They find that evidence of governance restrictions decays exponentially along derivation chains, with a half-life of about 1.31 derivation steps (R^2 = 0.98). After more than seven generations, at least 80% of descendant models lack sufficient metadata to make any governance determination; this critical depth is termed the “governance horizon.” Policy simulations show that inheritance-only metadata designs can extend this horizon only if enforcement is nearly perfect, while designs that require explicit declarations and resolve “orphan” lineage components meaningfully extend the horizon even at moderate enforcement. The analysis reveals that orphan nodes—models with no inheritable upstream intent—form structural bottlenecks that make downstream governance undecidable under any inheritance-only regime. Cross-ecosystem comparison with PyPI suggests that the observed collapse is driven by the specific topology and practices of open-weight model derivation, rather than being an unavoidable feature of open software ecosystems.
AI.conclusion: Disclosure-only, inheritance-based governance mechanisms for open-weight AI have intrinsically shallow reach: governance-relevant metadata rapidly disappears along derivation chains, leading to a “governance horizon” beyond which most models are undecidable from a policy perspective. Even strong enforcement cannot overcome structural bottlenecks caused by orphan components in model lineages. To achieve robust, deep supply-chain accountability, governance information must propagate through the derivation process itself via stronger provenance and mandatory, machine-readable declarations, rather than relying primarily on optional upstream metadata inheritance.

title: Quantifying the Impact of Translation Errors on Multilingual LLM Evaluation
summary: Machine-translated benchmarks are widely used to assess the multilingual capabilities of large language models (LLMs), yet translation errors in these benchmarks remain underexplored, raising concerns about the reliability and comparability of multilingual evaluation. We address two practical gaps: (i) how well automatic MQM-style error spans from LLM judges and a span-aware QE baseline (xCOMET-XXL) match expert human span annotations on benchmark translations, and (ii) how strongly translation errors (as opposed to source-side issues in the English original) explain accuracy drops on translated benchmarks. We find that span agreement is non-trivial on naturally occurring benchmark translations, and that target-side translation errors are consistently associated with measurable, percentage-point drops in translated accuracy even after controlling for English correctness and source-side anomalies.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how translation errors in machine-translated multilingual benchmarks affect the measured performance of large language models, and how well automatic error-span detection aligns with human expert annotations.
AI.motivation: Multilingual abilities of LLMs are often evaluated on benchmarks that are created by machine-translating English datasets. However, these translations can contain errors, and their impact on evaluation reliability and cross-language comparability is poorly understood. There is also limited understanding of how well automatic methods for detecting translation errors, including LLM-based MQM-style judges and quality estimation models, match expert human annotations at the span level. This motivates a systematic analysis of translation errors and their link to performance drops on translated benchmarks.
AI.method: The authors compare automatic MQM-style error-span annotations produced by LLM judges and a span-aware quality estimation system (xCOMET-XXL) against expert human span-level annotations on real benchmark translations. They quantify span-level agreement to see how accurately automatic methods can localize translation errors. They then conduct a statistical analysis relating the presence and severity of target-side translation errors, as well as source-side issues in the original English data, to the observed accuracy of LLMs on translated benchmark items, controlling for correctness on the English versions and for source anomalies.
AI.result: They find that achieving high span-level agreement between automatic annotators (LLM judges and xCOMET-XXL) and expert humans is challenging on naturally occurring benchmark translations, indicating that automatic error localization is imperfect. Nonetheless, they show that target-side translation errors have a consistent and measurable negative effect on LLM accuracy on translated benchmarks, leading to percentage-point drops even when controlling for performance on the original English items and for source-side problems.
AI.conclusion: Automatic methods for detecting translation errors in benchmark data only partially match expert human span annotations, which limits their reliability for fine-grained analysis. However, translation errors in the target language demonstrably and systematically degrade measured performance on multilingual benchmarks, independent of English correctness and source anomalies. This implies that current machine-translated benchmarks can underestimate true multilingual capabilities of LLMs and that improving translation quality or evaluation methodology is essential for more reliable multilingual assessment.

title: Understanding and Mitigating Premature Confidence for Better LLM Reasoning
summary: Long chains of thought (CoT) from current language models frequently contain logical gaps and unjustified leaps, limiting the gains from additional test-time compute. Improving reasoning quality directly would require process reward models, but the step-level annotations needed to train them are expensive and scarce. We find such a signal in how the model's confidence evolves during reasoning: premature confidence, the tendency to commit to an answer early and use the remaining tokens to rationalize it, strongly predicts flawed reasoning across tasks and model scales. We exploit this in progressive confidence shaping, a reinforcement learning objective that trains models to update their confidence as they reason rather than commit early -- rewarding gradual confidence growth and penalizing early commitment, with no external labels or reward models. The method improves accuracy and reasoning quality from 1.5B to 8B parameters across arithmetic (Countdown), math (DAPO, AIME), and science (ScienceQA): on Countdown, accuracy improves 3.2x (+42.0pp) and flawed reasoning drops 48pp; on AIME, Pass@64 improves 6.6pp. Consistent with this mechanism, the method also improves faithfulness: on a safety benchmark, our models more transparently surface misleading content in their reasoning traces rather than concealing it. Controlled experiments reveal that the problem and its remedy scale together: premature confidence grows with model size and task difficulty, and so do the gains from addressing it.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and mitigates “premature confidence” in chain-of-thought reasoning by training models, via a new RL objective, to let confidence grow progressively instead of committing early, improving accuracy, reasoning quality, and faithfulness across several math and science benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Chain-of-thought reasoning in current language models often has logical gaps, with models committing to an answer early and then rationalizing it, which reduces the value of extra test-time computation. Training process reward models could fix this but requires expensive, scarce step-level annotations. The authors seek a cheaper, scalable signal to improve reasoning quality without manual labels.
AI.method: They study how a model’s token-level confidence evolves over a reasoning trace and discover that “premature confidence” (early high confidence followed by rationalization) is predictive of flawed reasoning. Building on this, they introduce progressive confidence shaping, a reinforcement learning objective that encourages confidence to increase gradually during reasoning. The RL setup rewards trajectories where confidence grows as more reasoning unfolds and penalizes early commitment, using only the model’s own confidence profile rather than external labels or process reward models.
AI.result: Across models from 1.5B to 8B parameters and multiple benchmarks—Countdown (arithmetic), DAPO and AIME (math), and ScienceQA (science)—progressive confidence shaping substantially improves both accuracy and reasoning quality. For Countdown, accuracy increases by a factor of 3.2 (+42 percentage points) and flawed reasoning is reduced by 48 percentage points; on AIME, Pass@64 improves by 6.6 percentage points. On a safety benchmark, the trained models more clearly expose misleading content in their reasoning traces, indicating improved faithfulness.
AI.conclusion: Premature confidence is a systematic failure mode in chain-of-thought reasoning that worsens with larger models and harder tasks, but it also provides a useful training signal. By shaping models’ confidence profiles so they update beliefs gradually rather than committing too early, the authors can improve accuracy, reasoning quality, and faithfulness without costly step-level supervision. The severity of premature confidence and the benefits of the proposed remedy both scale with model size and task difficulty.

title: Overview of the PsyDefDetect Shared Task at BioNLP 2026: Detecting Levels of Psychological Defense Mechanisms in Supportive Conversations
summary: We present an overview of PsyDefDetect, the shared task on detecting levels of psychological defense mechanisms in emotional support dialogues, co-located with BioNLP@ACL 2026. Grounded in the clinically validated Defense Mechanism Rating Scales (DMRS) framework, the task asks systems to classify a target seeker utterance, given its preceding dialogue context, into one of nine categories: seven hierarchical DMRS levels plus two auxiliary labels. Participants worked on PsyDefConv, a newly released corpus of 200 dialogues and 2336 help-seeker utterances annotated under DMRS with substantial inter-annotator agreement. The task attracted 172 participants on CodaBench who produced 563 submissions, with 21 teams officially registering their results for the final ranking. The best system achieved a macro F1-score of 0.420, surpassing the strongest fine-tuned baseline reported in the dataset paper by a notable margin, yet leaving clear headroom. Our analysis highlights (i) a persistent tendency to over-predict the majority High-Adaptive class, (ii) a widening gap between accuracy and macro-F1 that reveals class-imbalance sensitivity, and (iii) the value of theory-aware and LLM-based approaches for fine-grained defensive-function classification. We release all task materials and invite the community to continue work on this novel intersection of clinical psychology and NLP.

AI.tldr: Overview of PsyDefDetect shared task: classify psychological defense levels in emotional support dialogues using DMRS-based labels.
AI.motivation: Enable automatic, fine-grained detection of psychological defense mechanisms in help-seeker utterances, bridging clinical psychology and NLP, and providing a benchmark task and dataset.
AI.method: Organized a shared task (PsyDefDetect) using the PsyDefConv corpus, where systems classify each target utterance into one of nine DMRS-based categories given dialogue context; evaluated many submitted models with macro F1; analyzed error patterns, class imbalance, and the impact of theory-aware and LLM-based methods.
AI.result: 172 participants made 563 submissions; 21 teams in final ranking; top system achieved macro F1=0.420, clearly outperforming the best fine-tuned baseline from the dataset paper; analysis shows over-prediction of the majority High-Adaptive class and a growing divergence between accuracy and macro-F1 due to class imbalance.
AI.conclusion: The task is feasible but remains challenging, with significant headroom for improvement; models are sensitive to class imbalance and tend to over-predict the majority class; incorporating psychological theory and LLMs is promising for fine-grained defense classification; all materials are released to promote further research at the intersection of clinical psychology and NLP.

title: ConceptM$^3$oE: Concept-Guided Multimodal Mixture of Experts for Interpretable Computational Pathology
summary: Healthcare models are transitioning from unimodal prediction toward multimodal reasoning over heterogeneous diagnostic inputs. In computational pathology, for complex tumor subtypes where morphology alone can be challenging to distinguish, pathology reports and molecular measurements may provide additional diagnostic evidence alongside whole-slide images, yet existing models often fail to clarify how diverse signals assemble into recognizable diagnostic concepts. We propose ConceptM$^3$oE (Concept Multimodal MoE), which embeds concept formation directly within interaction-aware mixture-of-experts (MoE) pathways. The architecture decomposes evidence into modality-specific, redundant, and synergistic experts, which are then projected into structured concept bottlenecks mapping latent features to a hierarchy of morphology and biomarker concepts. To prevent the information loss typical of interpretable bottlenecks, we utilize residual pathways within each expert to allow task-relevant signals to flow both through the concepts and directly to the final task prediction, so that high performance is maintained alongside interpretability. Across an institutional pediatric brain tumor cohort and a public glioma cohort, the framework delivers competitive performance to unconstrained models while producing reasoning traces validated by an independent neuropathologist. In data-limited regimes, ConceptM$^3$oE improves limited-data performance, increasing macro-F1 from 56.41% to 66.70% at small training sizes compared to non-concept-informed baselines, while also showing faster training convergence consistent with the regularizing effect of concept learning. This work offers a scalable path toward high-performance medical AI that is inherently verifiable and better aligned with the complex decision-making of clinical practice.

AI.tldr: They introduce ConceptM3oE, a multimodal mixture-of-experts model that learns explicit medical concepts to interpret and improve tumor diagnosis from images, reports, and molecular data.
AI.motivation: Existing computational pathology models combine heterogeneous data (WSIs, reports, molecular data) but do not clearly show how different modalities contribute to final diagnostic concepts, limiting interpretability, clinical trust, and data efficiency, especially for complex tumor subtypes.
AI.method: They design a multimodal mixture-of-experts (MoE) architecture that separates evidence into modality-specific, redundant, and synergistic experts and passes them through structured concept bottlenecks representing a hierarchy of morphology and biomarker concepts. Residual pathways around these bottlenecks preserve task-relevant information so that predictions use both concept-level reasoning and direct latent features.
AI.result: On an institutional pediatric brain tumor cohort and a public glioma cohort, ConceptM3oE matches or competes with standard unconstrained models while providing expert-validated reasoning traces. In low-data settings it notably improves performance, e.g., macro-F1 from 56.41% to 66.70%, and converges faster during training, indicating a regularizing effect of concept learning.
AI.conclusion: ConceptM3oE offers a scalable, interpretable multimodal framework for medical AI that preserves diagnostic accuracy, enhances performance in data-limited regimes, and aligns model reasoning with clinically meaningful morphology and biomarker concepts, thereby supporting more verifiable and trustworthy clinical decision support.

title: MultiHaluDet: Multilingual Hallucination Detection via LLM Hidden State Probing
summary: Hallucinations in Large Language Models (LLMs) represent a critical barrier to their reliable deployment, a vulnerability heavily exacerbated in non-English and resource-constrained contexts. Existing detection approaches that rely on output confidence heuristics or single-layer internal representations frequently fail to capture deep, complex factual inconsistencies across diverse languages. To address this, we introduce MultiHaluDet, a novel three-stage stacking framework that detects multilingual hallucinations by probing the full hidden state trajectories of frozen LLMs without requiring language-specific fine-tuning. Our method extracts sequential features across multiple layers and processes them via a hybrid architecture using multi-scale attention and self-attention pooling. By generating out-of-fold embeddings that feed into a calibrated classical classifier ensemble, MultiHaluDet captures both fine-grained and coarse-grained patterns of factual inconsistency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework achieves state-of-the-art detection performance, reaching up to 98.55% AUROC on the English HaluEval and TriviaQA benchmarks using Mistral-7B and LLaMA2-7B architectures. Crucially, we rigorously evaluate our framework's cross-lingual generalization across high (French), medium (Bangla), and low-resource (Amharic) languages. MultiHaluDet demonstrates exceptional representational robustness, consistently outperforming baselines and successfully transferring hallucination detection capabilities across typologically diverse linguistic tiers.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MultiHaluDet, a three-stage, multilingual hallucination detector for LLMs based on full hidden-state trajectories, achieving state-of-the-art AUROC and strong cross-lingual transfer, especially in low-resource languages.
AI.motivation: Hallucinations in LLMs undermine reliability, particularly in non-English and low-resource languages where data scarcity and linguistic diversity make factual error detection more difficult. Existing methods often depend on shallow signals such as output confidence or single-layer representations, which are insufficient to detect deep factual inconsistencies across languages. There is a need for a language-agnostic, robust hallucination detector that can leverage the rich internal dynamics of LLMs and generalize across typologically diverse languages without language-specific tuning.
AI.method: The authors introduce MultiHaluDet, a three-stage stacking framework that uses frozen LLMs and probes their full hidden state trajectories across multiple layers. First, they extract sequential features layer-by-layer from the model. Second, these features are processed using a hybrid neural architecture that combines multi-scale attention (to capture patterns at different granularities) and self-attention pooling (to aggregate sequence information). Third, they produce out-of-fold embeddings from this model and feed them into a calibrated ensemble of classical classifiers. This stacked setup allows the system to capture both fine-grained and coarse-grained patterns correlated with hallucinations, while remaining language-agnostic and avoiding language-specific fine-tuning of the base LLM.
AI.result: MultiHaluDet achieves state-of-the-art hallucination detection, with AUROC up to 98.55% on English benchmarks such as HaluEval and TriviaQA when built on Mistral-7B and LLaMA2-7B. The framework is extensively evaluated in multilingual settings, including French (high-resource), Bangla (medium-resource), and Amharic (low-resource). Across these typologically diverse languages, MultiHaluDet consistently outperforms baseline detectors and maintains strong performance, showing that its learned representations and classifier ensemble are robust and transferable across languages and resource levels.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that probing full hidden-state trajectories with a stacked, hybrid attention architecture is an effective strategy for detecting hallucinations in LLMs, overcoming the limitations of confidence-based or single-layer methods. MultiHaluDet not only sets new performance levels on English datasets but also offers strong cross-lingual generalization, making it a practical, language-agnostic solution for hallucination detection, especially valuable in low-resource language contexts.

title: Advancing Graph Few-Shot Learning via In-Context Learning
summary: Graph few-shot learning, which aims to classify nodes from novel classes with only a few labeled examples, is a widely studied problem in graph learning. However, existing methods often face two key limitations. First, the predominant graph few-shot learning paradigm relies on supervised tasks, failing to leverage the vast number of unlabeled nodes in the graph. Second, many approaches require complex task adaptation or fine-tuning during inference, limiting their efficiency and applicability. Inspired by the powerful in-context learning capabilities of large language models, we propose a novel model named VISION for adVancIng graph few-Shot learning via In-cOntext LearNing to address these challenges. Our model reframes graph few-shot learning as a fine-tuning-free sequence reasoning problem. At its core is a context-aware network that initializes nodes with role embeddings and employs a dual-context fusion module to synergistically integrate local topological structures and global task-level dependencies. This allows our model to dynamically generate class-aware representations for the query set conditioned on the support set context in a single forward pass. To effectively train our model, we introduce an unsupervised task generator that creates structure-adaptive features and constructs diverse pseudo-tasks from abundant unlabeled data. Our method unifies unsupervised meta-learning with graph in-context learning, achieving efficient inference. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate the superiority of our model. Our public code can be found

AI.tldr: VISION is a graph few-shot learning model that performs fine-tuning-free, in-context classification of nodes using both local and global graph context, trained via unsupervised pseudo-task generation from unlabeled data.
AI.motivation: Existing graph few-shot learning methods rely mainly on supervised tasks and require complex adaptation or fine-tuning at inference time. This wastes abundant unlabeled nodes and limits efficiency and scalability. The authors seek a framework that can exploit unlabeled data and support efficient, fine-tuning-free inference, inspired by in-context learning in large language models.
AI.method: They reframe graph few-shot learning as a sequence reasoning problem and propose VISION, whose core is a context-aware network. Nodes are initialized with role embeddings (e.g., support/query, class roles) and passed through a dual-context fusion module that jointly models local topological structure and global task-level dependencies. This yields class-aware query representations conditioned on the support set in a single forward pass. For training, they design an unsupervised task generator that derives structure-adaptive features and constructs diverse pseudo-tasks from abundant unlabeled data, enabling unsupervised meta-learning on graphs.
AI.result: On multiple standard graph few-shot learning benchmarks, VISION outperforms prior methods, demonstrating better accuracy and efficiency (fine-tuning-free, single-pass inference).
AI.conclusion: By unifying unsupervised meta-learning with graph in-context learning, VISION enables efficient, fine-tuning-free graph few-shot learning that effectively exploits unlabeled nodes and better captures both local and global context. The approach generalizes well across datasets and sets a new performance bar for graph few-shot classification.

title: H$^{2}$MT: Semantic Hierarchy-Aware Hierarchical Memory Transformer
summary: Transformer-based LLMs achieve strong results on many language tasks; however, long inputs remain challenging because context windows are finite, and prefill latency and memory grow rapidly with prompt length. Flat token-stream processing and chunk-based retrieval can therefore spend substantial computation and context budget on text unrelated to the query. Offline-indexed RAG additionally introduces external storage and index management overhead, and typically appends retrieved evidence as raw text, increasing prefill cost and latency. H^{2}MT makes long-context inference structure-aware: it builds a semantic hierarchy offline, computes a memory embedding for each node via bottom-up post-order aggregation, and routes queries coarse-to-fine at inference to prune irrelevant branches early. On LongBench QA (NarrativeQA, HotpotQA, QASPER) and two structured technical-document settings, H MT achieves favorable quality efficiency trade-offs, delivering competitive ROUGE-L and F1 (where applicable) with lower peak GPU memory and time-to-first-token (TTFT) than prompt compression, memory-token methods, and retrieval-augmented generation baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes H^{2}MT, a hierarchical, structure-aware method to make long-context inference in LLMs more efficient, improving latency and memory while maintaining QA quality.
AI.motivation: Transformer LLMs struggle with long inputs because context windows are finite and both prefill latency and memory usage scale poorly with prompt length. Existing approaches like flat token processing, chunk-based retrieval, and RAG often waste computation and context on irrelevant text and add overhead from external indexes and raw-text evidence concatenation. The authors seek a way to use document structure to avoid processing irrelevant content while preserving answer quality.
AI.method: The authors introduce H^{2}MT, which constructs an offline semantic hierarchy over documents and computes a memory embedding for each node via bottom-up post-order aggregation. At inference, queries are routed in a coarse-to-fine fashion down this hierarchy, allowing the system to prune irrelevant branches early. This structure-aware routing reduces the amount of text and states the LLM must process compared to flat prompts, prompt compression, memory-token schemes, or standard RAG that appends raw retrieved chunks.
AI.result: On LongBench QA benchmarks (NarrativeQA, HotpotQA, QASPER) and two structured technical-document tasks, H^{2}MT attains competitive or comparable ROUGE-L and F1 scores to strong baselines while using less peak GPU memory and achieving lower time-to-first-token. This demonstrates better quality-efficiency trade-offs than prompt compression, memory-token methods, and RAG baselines.
AI.conclusion: Making long-context inference structure-aware via a hierarchical semantic index and coarse-to-fine query routing can significantly cut latency and memory without sacrificing QA performance. H^{2}MT shows that exploiting document hierarchy is an effective alternative or complement to conventional RAG and prompt-engineering approaches for long-context LLM applications.

title: The Model Is Not the Product: A Dual-Pillar Architecture for Local-First Psychological Coaching
summary: Existing language model applications struggle to meet the demand for emotionally oriented support, primarily due to their inability to maintain deep, persistent context across sessions. This report introduces Psych LM, an iOS application that validates the thesis that, for such applications, the surrounding architecture is paramount. Psych LM runs a local, on-device language model within a purpose-built, local-first runtime designed for behavioral and life-coaching applications. The system achieves the practical effect of a near-infinite context window through an automated, user-inspectable memory corpus that converts conversations into structured memory cards, including facts, goals, and events, and dynamically injects them into the prompt via semantic and vector search. As such, the system can be defined as an active-learning, retrieval-augmented generative, on-device architecture. This architecture delivers four primary contributions: a local-first design where privacy is a core property; a detailed description of the memory corpus for persistent context of key user information; a deterministic orchestration layer that provides a stable behavioral spine independent of the model's internal state; and a benchmark framework focused on evaluating the integrated system's reliability under realistic operating conditions. The R and D process confirms that complex, context-aware interaction can be reliably achieved under the strict constraints of a mobile environment by prioritizing architectural control and resource management over simple model size.

AI.tldr: Psych LM is an on-device, privacy-preserving behavioral coaching app that uses a structured memory system to simulate a near-infinite context window and is evaluated as a full integrated system.
AI.motivation: Conventional language model apps cannot reliably provide emotionally oriented, long-term support because they lose context across sessions and depend too much on raw model size rather than architecture. There is a need for systems that maintain deep, persistent, and private user context on resource-constrained devices like phones.
AI.method: Design and implementation of Psych LM, an iOS application running a local language model inside a custom, local-first runtime. The system automatically converts conversations into structured memory cards (facts, goals, events), stores them in an inspectable memory corpus, and uses semantic and vector search to retrieve and inject relevant memories back into prompts. It adds a deterministic orchestration layer that governs interaction flow independently of the model state and defines a benchmark framework to test reliability under realistic conditions on mobile hardware.
AI.result: The system effectively emulates a near-infinite context window on-device by using retrieval from the structured memory corpus, maintains user privacy by running locally, and supports complex, context-aware behavioral and life-coaching interactions within mobile resource limits. The deterministic orchestration and memory design provide stable, reproducible behavior, and the benchmark framework demonstrates the practicality and reliability of the integrated architecture.
AI.conclusion: Architectural choices—local-first design, structured persistent memory, deterministic orchestration, and system-level evaluation—are more critical than simply scaling model size for emotionally oriented, context-intensive applications on mobile. With the right architecture, deep, reliable, privacy-preserving, context-aware interactions are achievable even under strict device constraints.

title: NITP: Next Implicit Token Prediction for LLM Pre-training
summary: Standard next-token prediction (NTP) supervises language models solely through discrete labels in the output logit space. We argue that this sparse one-hot supervision leaves the latent representation space under-constrained, allowing hidden states to drift into degenerate and anisotropic configurations that can limit generalization. To address this issue, we propose Next Implicit Token Prediction (NITP), which augments discrete prediction with dense continuous supervision directly in the representation space. NITP trains the model to predict the implicit semantic content of the next token, using shallow-layer representations from the same model as stable self-supervised targets. We provide theoretical analysis showing that NITP regularizes the optimization landscape by mitigating under-constrained degrees of freedom and encouraging a compact, structured representation geometry. Empirically, across dense and MoE models ranging from 0.5B to 9B parameters, NITP consistently improves downstream performance with negligible computational overhead. On a 9B MoE model, NITP achieves a 5.7% absolute improvement on MMLU-Pro, along with gains of 6.4% on C3 and 4.3% on CommonsenseQA, with approximately 2% additional training FLOPs and no additional inference cost. Our implementation is available at https://github.com/aHapBean/NITP.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Next Implicit Token Prediction (NITP), a training objective that adds dense representation-level supervision to standard next-token prediction, leading to better-structured hidden states and improved downstream performance with minimal extra compute.
AI.motivation: Standard next-token prediction uses only sparse one-hot labels in logit space, leaving internal representations under-constrained. This can cause degenerate, anisotropic latent spaces that hurt generalization. The authors want to better regularize and structure the representation space during training without incurring large computational or inference costs.
AI.method: They introduce Next Implicit Token Prediction (NITP), which augments NTP by asking the model to predict the implicit semantic content of the next token directly in representation space. Concretely, they use shallow-layer representations from the same model as self-supervised targets for deeper-layer states, adding a continuous loss that aligns these internal vectors. They provide theoretical analysis showing that this reduces under-constrained degrees of freedom and encourages a compact, well-structured geometry in the latent space. The method is applied to both dense and mixture-of-experts (MoE) transformer models with small additional FLOPs and no inference-time overhead.
AI.result: Across models from 0.5B to 9B parameters (dense and MoE), NITP consistently improves downstream task performance. On a 9B MoE model, NITP yields a 5.7% absolute improvement on MMLU-Pro, 6.4% on C3, and 4.3% on CommonsenseQA, while adding only about 2% extra training FLOPs and zero extra inference cost.
AI.conclusion: Adding dense, representation-level supervision via NITP regularizes the optimization landscape and shapes the model’s internal geometry in a beneficial way, leading to better generalization and task performance with negligible computational overhead. NITP is thus a practical and effective complement to standard next-token prediction for training language models.

title: JT-SAFE-V2: Safety-by-Design Foundation Model with World-Context Data
summary: We introduce JT-Safe-V2, a large language model designed to advance the safety and trustworthiness of foundation models, extending our previous JT-Safe model toward a more comprehensive safety-by-design paradigm. JT-Safe-V2 emphasizes the joint optimization of general intelligence and safety-by-design through several key innovations: enriching pre-training data with contextual world knowledge, high-certainty pre-training procedures, and safety strengthening post-training mechanisms for enterprise-oriented agentic capabilities. Building on these safety-enhanced foundation models, we propose Safe-MoMA (Safe Mixture of Models and Agents), a framework that enables traceable and efficient inference through the orchestrated deployment of multiple models and agents. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that JT-Safe-V2 achieves state-of-the-art performance across both general intelligence and safety benchmarks. Moreover, Safe-MoMA reduces inference costs by more than 30\% compared to using the largest standalone model baseline while maintaining comparable performance. To facilitate future research on safety-by-design foundation models, we publicly release the post-trained JT-Safe-V2-35B model checkpoint.

AI.tldr: JT-Safe-V2 is a safer, more capable large language model and Safe-MoMA is a cost-efficient framework that coordinates multiple safe models/agents to deliver strong performance with lower inference cost.
AI.motivation: Existing large language models struggle to simultaneously maximize general intelligence, safety, and deployment efficiency, especially for enterprise and agentic use cases. There is a need for foundation models that are safety-by-design rather than safety-as-an-afterthought, along with inference frameworks that can orchestrate multiple such models in a traceable and cost-effective way.
AI.method: The authors extend their previous JT-Safe model into JT-Safe-V2 by: (1) enriching pre-training data with contextual world knowledge, (2) using high-certainty pre-training procedures, and (3) applying safety-strengthening post-training tailored for enterprise and agentic capabilities. On top of these models, they design Safe-MoMA (Safe Mixture of Models and Agents), an orchestration framework that routes tasks among multiple models and agents to achieve traceable, efficient inference.
AI.result: JT-Safe-V2 attains state-of-the-art performance on both general intelligence and safety benchmarks. The Safe-MoMA framework cuts inference costs by over 30% relative to using the single largest model alone, while maintaining similar performance. They also release a post-trained 35B-parameter JT-Safe-V2 checkpoint for public use.
AI.conclusion: Jointly optimizing safety-by-design and general intelligence in pre-training and post-training can yield foundation models that are both safer and more capable. When combined with a mixture-of-models-and-agents inference framework like Safe-MoMA, these models can be deployed in a traceable and significantly more cost-efficient way without substantial performance loss, supporting safer enterprise and agentic applications.

title: SEP-Attack: A Simple and Effective Paradigm for Transfer-Based Textual Adversarial Attack
summary: Despite the strong performance of deep neural networks in modern Web and language applications, they remain vulnerable to adversarial attacks, especially transferable attacks that generate adversarial examples using surrogate models without accessing the victim model. Transferable attacks in the text domain are still under-explored, with only a few studies addressing this challenging issue, often with suboptimal results due to equal treatment of submodels or inaccurate estimation of importance scores. To address these challenges, we propose a simple yet effective paradigm for transfer-based textual adversarial attack, named SEP-Attack. Specifically, we employ the Determinantal Point Process (DPP) to generate diverse surrogate ensemble weights, representing the transferability of submodels. Using these weights, we introduce a new metric to evaluate prediction confidence scores, which in turn are used to calculate word importance scores and generate adversarial candidates. Finally, we quantify the transferability score for each candidate and select the top ones as the final transferable adversarial examples. Experiments conducted on four datasets and two real-world APIs validate the efficacy of SEP-Attack, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SEP-Attack, a new, more effective method for generating transferable adversarial text examples using surrogate model ensembles and diversity-aware weighting.
AI.motivation: Deep neural networks used in web and language applications are vulnerable to adversarial examples. Attacks that transfer across models, especially in text, are hard to design and existing methods perform poorly because they treat all surrogate models equally or estimate word importance inaccurately. The work aims to build a stronger, more principled transfer-based textual adversarial attack method.
AI.method: They introduce SEP-Attack, a transfer-based textual adversarial attack framework. It uses Determinantal Point Processes (DPP) to sample diverse weightings over an ensemble of surrogate models, capturing each submodel’s contribution to transferability. These DPP-based weights define a new confidence metric over predictions, which is then used to compute word importance scores and generate candidate adversarial texts. For each candidate, they compute a transferability score based on the weighted surrogate ensemble and select the highest-scoring candidates as final adversarial examples.
AI.result: On four benchmark datasets and two commercial, real-world API models, SEP-Attack achieves significantly higher attack success rates than existing state-of-the-art transfer-based text attack baselines, demonstrating better transferability of adversarial examples.
AI.conclusion: Properly modeling surrogate model diversity and non-uniform importance via DPP-based ensemble weighting allows more accurate confidence and word-importance estimation, leading to substantially stronger transferable textual attacks. SEP-Attack offers a simple yet powerful paradigm for improving transfer-based adversarial attacks in NLP settings.

title: Benchmarking the Limits of In-Context Reinforcement Learning for Ad-Hoc Teamwork
summary: In-Context Reinforcement Learning (ICRL) has enabled foundation agents to adapt instantaneously to novel tasks, yet its efficacy in Ad-Hoc Teamwork (AHT)-where coordination with unknown partners is required-remains unexplored. To rigorously evaluate this, we introduce a large-scale benchmark ICRL4AHT, built upon a high-throughput JAX implementation of Overcooked-V2. Our benchmark includes a large, diverse teammate suite spanning both RL and heuristic policies, enabling controlled train-test shifts, and provides a reproducible end-to-end pipeline for teammate generation, learning-history collection, dataset construction, and online multi-episode evaluation. We evaluate representative history-conditioned ICRL algorithms, including Algorithm Distillation (AD) and Decision-Pretrained Transformer (DPT), across millions of transitions. Results reveal notable limitations: contrary to their success in single-agent domains, these baselines fail to exhibit robust test-time adaptation in multi-agent settings. Specifically, these methods frequently underperform random baselines across both unseen teammate and unseen layout tracks, with no clear in-context improvement over long horizons. These findings highlight the challenges of strategic inference under partial observability within the OvercookedV2 AHT protocol, establishing our benchmark as a critical testbed for next-generation coordination algorithms.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ICRL4AHT, a benchmark to test in-context reinforcement learning methods in ad-hoc teamwork and shows that current ICRL approaches fail to robustly adapt to unknown teammates in multi-agent Overcooked-V2.
AI.motivation: In-context RL has shown strong performance in single-agent settings, but its effectiveness for ad-hoc teamwork—where agents must coordinate with unknown partners on the fly—was not well understood due to a lack of rigorous, large-scale, and reproducible benchmarks for multi-agent coordination.
AI.method: The authors build ICRL4AHT, a large-scale benchmark on a JAX implementation of Overcooked-V2, including a diverse suite of RL and heuristic teammates and tools for teammate generation, history collection, dataset construction, and multi-episode evaluation. They then train and evaluate history-conditioned ICRL methods (Algorithm Distillation and Decision-Pretrained Transformer) over millions of transitions under controlled train-test shifts in teammates and layouts.
AI.result: Across both unseen teammate and unseen layout settings, Algorithm Distillation and DPT struggle to achieve robust test-time adaptation: they often underperform even random baselines and do not show clear in-context improvement over long horizons in the ad-hoc teamwork tasks.
AI.conclusion: Strategic inference for coordination under partial observability in multi-agent Overcooked-V2 is substantially harder than standard single-agent ICRL tasks, and current history-conditioned ICRL methods are inadequate; ICRL4AHT therefore serves as a challenging and important benchmark for developing next-generation algorithms for ad-hoc teamwork and multi-agent coordination.

title: Investigating the Interplay between Contextual and Parametric Chain-of-Thought Faithfulness under Optimization
comment: The first two authors contributed equally and share first-authorship
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) faithfulness, i.e., whether CoTs genuinely reflect large language models' (LLM) underlying behavior, is typically evaluated under two disjoint paradigms: contextual faithfulness, measured by perturbing the input or CoT trace, and parametric faithfulness, assessed by intervening on a model's parametric knowledge. Yet prior work compares them only descriptively. We fill this gap by proposing FaithMate, a unified preference-alignment interface for optimizing models towards either faithfulness paradigm. It enables us to investigate the interplay between the two paradigms, examining whether and to what extent faithfulness gains generalize within and across paradigms. Across three models, two datasets, and six faithfulness metrics, we find that the two paradigms are positively coupled, yet asymmetric: optimizing towards parametric faithfulness yields consistent gains across both paradigms, whereas the contextual counterpart delivers more variable gains. Within the contextual paradigm, faithfulness gains on one metric do not consistently transfer to others, implying that existing contextual metrics capture disjoint facets of faithfulness and exposing inherent trade-offs. These findings imply that CoT faithfulness is not a monolithic objective and therefore requires multifaceted optimization and evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how well chain-of-thought (CoT) explanations actually reflect LLMs’ internal reasoning, unifying and comparing two main evaluation paradigms—contextual and parametric faithfulness—and analyzing how improvements in one transfer to the other.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of CoT faithfulness rely on two separate paradigms: contextual faithfulness (via input/trace perturbations) and parametric faithfulness (via interventions on model knowledge). Prior work treats them separately and only descriptively compares them, leaving open how they interact, whether improvements in one paradigm generalize to the other, and whether existing metrics capture a single coherent notion of faithfulness or multiple facets.
AI.method: The authors propose FaithMate, a unified preference-alignment framework that can optimize LLMs toward higher faithfulness under either contextual or parametric paradigms. Using FaithMate, they run systematic experiments on three LLMs, two datasets, and six faithfulness metrics, training or fine-tuning models with preference signals tailored to each paradigm and then evaluating how gains transfer within and across paradigms and metrics.
AI.result: They find that contextual and parametric faithfulness are positively correlated but asymmetrical. Optimizing for parametric faithfulness tends to improve both parametric and contextual metrics consistently, whereas optimizing for contextual faithfulness yields less stable, more variable effects across paradigms. Within the contextual paradigm, improvements on one metric do not reliably transfer to others, suggesting that different contextual metrics measure different aspects of faithfulness and can conflict with each other.
AI.conclusion: CoT faithfulness is not a single, unified property but a multifaceted objective. Effective development and evaluation of faithful CoT reasoning requires multi-metric, multi-paradigm optimization rather than relying on a single contextual metric or paradigm. Parametric-oriented training appears to provide more robust cross-paradigm faithfulness gains, while contextual metrics expose important but partially disjoint facets that must be jointly balanced.

title: SAM: State-Adaptive Memory for Long-Horizon Reasoning Agent
summary: Long-horizon agentic reasoning requires large language models to act over long interaction histories containing thoughts, tool calls, observations, and partial conclusions. The challenge is not merely that these histories grow long, but that information needed for the current decision may be scattered across distant steps and only become relevant later. Existing approaches address this difficulty by truncating the interaction history, compressing it into shorter surrogates, or retrieving selected parts of it for reuse, but they do not explicitly model how access to past interaction should adapt to the agent's evolving state. We instead cast long-horizon reasoning as a problem of state-adaptive memory. To this end, we propose State-Adaptive Memory~(SAM), a standalone framework that consolidates ongoing interaction into compact memory cues while preserving raw trajectory pages for intent-driven recall. These cues are not treated as replacements for history; rather, they serve as lightweight handles that allow the agent to reconstruct temporally distant information according to its current needs, without retraining the underlying backbone. We further optimize the memory module through expert-guided supervision and reinforcement learning, aligning it with trajectory-level utility. Across BrowseComp, BrowseComp-ZH, WideSearch, and HLE, SAM consistently outperforms strong baselines over diverse agent backbones. Our results suggest that explicit memory modeling provides a simple and effective foundation for long-horizon agentic reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces State-Adaptive Memory (SAM), a framework that helps language-model agents perform long-horizon reasoning by constructing compact, state-dependent memory cues that enable effective recall of past interaction history without retraining the base model.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon language agents must reason over long histories of thoughts, tool calls, and observations. Simply truncating, compressing, or retrieving pieces of history fails to account for how the relevance of past information changes with the agent’s evolving state. There is a need for an explicit memory mechanism that allows agents to flexibly and efficiently access temporally distant but contextually important information for current decisions.
AI.method: The authors formulate long-horizon reasoning as a state-adaptive memory problem and propose State-Adaptive Memory (SAM). SAM consolidates ongoing interactions into compact memory cues while preserving the full raw trajectory as "pages." The cues act as lightweight indices or handles that help the agent selectively reconstruct relevant past information on demand, based on its current state, without modifying the backbone LLM. The memory module is further trained and refined using expert-guided supervision and reinforcement learning, optimizing it for trajectory-level utility in downstream tasks.
AI.result: On multiple benchmarks—BrowseComp, BrowseComp-ZH, WideSearch, and HLE—SAM consistently outperforms strong baseline approaches across various agent backbones. This demonstrates that SAM improves the effectiveness of long-horizon reasoning in realistic agentic settings and is robust across languages and tasks.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, state-adaptive memory modeling via SAM offers a simple yet powerful foundation for long-horizon agentic reasoning. By using compact cues as intent-driven handles into preserved raw trajectories, SAM enables better use of long interaction histories without retraining the underlying language model, leading to consistent performance gains across diverse benchmarks and backbones.

title: Large Language Model Selection with Limited Annotations
summary: Choosing a Large Language Model (LLM) for a given task requires comparing many strong candidates, yet standard evaluation relies on costly annotations over fixed evaluation sets. To address this challenge, we develop SELECT-LLM, the first framework for active model selection of LLMs. SELECT-LLM aims to find a small set of queries whose annotations are most informative for identifying the best LLM for a given task. To this end, we introduce a query selection rule based on expected information gain, computed from pairwise similarities between candidate model outputs. Because this rule only uses generated model responses, SELECT-LLM can be applied across candidate models without assumptions about their architecture or access to model weights. This makes it suitable for both open-weight and black-box LLMs. We evaluate SELECT-LLM across 23 datasets, 156 evaluated models, diverse task families, and multiple text evaluation metrics. Across all experiments, SELECT-LLM improves over the strongest baseline in every setting, with annotation cost reductions up to 81.8% for best model selection and up to 84.78% for near-best model selection.
