AI.tldr: SELECT-LLM is an active model selection framework that finds the best LLM for a task using far fewer labeled examples by choosing the most informative queries to annotate.
AI.motivation: Standard LLM evaluation and model selection require many human annotations on fixed benchmarks, which is expensive and inefficient when comparing many strong LLMs. There is a need for a method that can decide which model is best (or near-best) for a given task while minimizing annotation cost, and that works even when models are black-box APIs without access to weights.
AI.method: The authors propose SELECT-LLM, an active selection framework that iteratively chooses a small set of input queries to label. The key idea is a query selection rule based on expected information gain: for each potential query, they estimate how informative its annotation would be for discriminating between candidate models. This is computed using pairwise similarities between model outputs on that query, requiring only generated responses and no access to model internals. The framework is model-agnostic and applies to both open-weight and black-box LLMs and across tasks and metrics.
AI.result: Across 23 datasets and 156 LLMs covering diverse task families and evaluation metrics, SELECT-LLM outperforms the strongest existing baselines for active evaluation/model selection in every tested setting. It achieves substantial reductions in human annotation cost, up to 81.8% for identifying the best model and up to 84.78% for selecting a near-best model, while preserving selection quality.
AI.conclusion: Active, information-gain–based query selection can dramatically reduce the number of human-labeled examples needed to choose the best or near-best LLM for a task. By relying only on model outputs and pairwise similarities, SELECT-LLM offers a practical, architecture-agnostic solution for efficient model selection among many competing LLMs, applicable even to black-box APIs.

title: SPACE: Unifying Symmetric and Asymmetric Routing Problems for Generalist Neural Solver
summary: Generalist neural routing solvers have shown great potential in solving diverse vehicle routing problems (VRPs) with a unified model. However, existing solvers are typically limited to symmetric settings or degrade in performance when switching to asymmetric settings due to input inconsistencies or inherent structural differences, substantially limiting their practicality in real-world scenarios that encompass both scenarios. To address this limitation, we define the spatial position of each node based on the relative distances to a specific set of pivots and further propose a Spatial Pivot-Aligned Coordinate-free Embedding (SPACE) framework that unifies node representation and solution generation across symmetric and asymmetric VRPs. Specifically, we construct a bidirectional Frechet representation using a novel furthest pivot sampling strategy to enable invariant node representations across distinct problem settings. Furthermore, we introduce a weight-decomposed adaptive decoding mechanism that decouples geometric perception from problem representations, mitigating the overfitting of constraint decisions to a specific geometry setting. Extensive experiments on 110 VRP variants, comprising 55 symmetric problems and their asymmetric counterparts, demonstrate that SPACE achieves promising zero-shot generalization in both symmetric and asymmetric VRPs.

AI.tldr: They propose SPACE, a unified neural framework that represents nodes via pivot-based coordinate-free embeddings and decodes routes with geometry-aware but problem-agnostic mechanisms, enabling one generalist model to handle both symmetric and asymmetric VRPs with strong zero-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: Existing generalist neural solvers for vehicle routing work well mainly on symmetric VRPs and either fail or degrade substantially on asymmetric VRPs because (1) inputs are inconsistent between settings and (2) symmetric and asymmetric problems have different structural/geometric properties. This limits practical deployment, since many real-world logistics tasks mix both types. A unified approach that can seamlessly generalize across symmetric and asymmetric VRPs is needed.
AI.method: They introduce SPACE (Spatial Pivot-Aligned Coordinate-free Embedding), which builds a common representation for nodes across symmetric and asymmetric VRPs. First, they define each node’s spatial position via relative distances to a chosen set of pivot nodes, rather than explicit coordinates or direct pairwise distances. They design a novel furthest-pivot sampling strategy and construct a bidirectional Fréchet representation so that node embeddings are invariant to the underlying VRP symmetry/asymmetry. On top of this, they propose a weight-decomposed adaptive decoder that separates geometric perception (captured via the SPACE embeddings) from problem-specific constraint reasoning, reducing overfitting to any particular geometric structure. This architecture is trained as a generalist neural routing solver.
AI.result: Evaluated on a large benchmark of 110 VRP variants (55 symmetric VRPs and their 55 asymmetric counterparts), SPACE shows strong performance without retraining, i.e., promising zero-shot generalization across both symmetric and asymmetric settings. Quantitatively, it maintains high solution quality and robustness when moving between these problem types, improving over prior generalist routing solvers, especially on asymmetric cases.
AI.conclusion: By replacing coordinate- or matrix-based inputs with pivot-based coordinate-free spatial embeddings and decoupling geometry from constraint reasoning in the decoder, SPACE offers a unified neural architecture for vehicle routing that handles both symmetric and asymmetric VRPs within a single model. This resolves key input and structural inconsistencies that previously hindered generalization, enabling practical, general-purpose neural routing solvers applicable to broad real-world routing scenarios.

title: Exploring Profiles of Cognitive Distortions Associated with Mental Health Disorders
summary: Cognitive distortions, distorted patterns of thinking, have been increasingly studied in computational mental health research. Although they are related to many, if not all, mental health disorders, most existing studies focus primarily on depression. In this work, we explore distortion profiles across multiple mental health conditions. We analyzed a large Reddit-based dataset containing posts from nine self-reported mental health groups as well as a control group using both an n-gram-based method and a fine-tuned transformer model for detecting cognitive distortions. Mental health groups, both when pooled together and when examined individually, showed higher prevalence of cognitive distortions compared to the control group, with the effect sizes ranging from small to moderate. When comparing distortion profiles across conditions, we observed largely similar patterns, although some groups exhibited overall higher levels of distortions than others. These findings suggest that relatively simple lexical approaches can be useful for exploratory analyses of group-level trends in large-scale mental health text data.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how common cognitive distortions are across different mental health conditions using Reddit posts, comparing them with a control group, and shows that distortions are more prevalent in mental health groups and can be detected with relatively simple text methods.
AI.motivation: Cognitive distortions play a central role in many mental health disorders, but computational work has mostly focused on depression. The authors want to understand whether and how cognitive distortions differ across multiple mental health conditions, and whether simple lexical and machine-learning methods can characterize these patterns at scale using social media data.
AI.method: The authors collect a large Reddit dataset with posts from nine self-identified mental health communities and a control group. They apply two approaches to detect cognitive distortions in text: an n-gram-based lexical method and a fine-tuned transformer model. They then compare the prevalence and profiles of distortions between each mental health group and the control group, as well as among the different conditions, using effect sizes to quantify differences.
AI.result: All mental health groups, whether aggregated or analyzed separately, show higher levels of cognitive distortions than the control group, with small to moderate effect sizes. Across different mental health conditions, the overall distortion profiles are broadly similar, though some groups display generally higher distortion levels than others.
AI.conclusion: Cognitive distortions are more prevalent in online posts from people with self-reported mental health conditions than in control users, and the patterns are largely shared across conditions. Furthermore, relatively simple lexical approaches, supplemented by transformer-based models, are sufficient for exploratory, group-level analysis of cognitive distortions in large-scale mental health text data.

title: AgentFugue: Agent Scaling for Long-Horizon Tasks through Collective Reasoning
summary: Recent progress on long-horizon agentic tasks has been driven largely by scaling up individual agents through stronger models, better tools, and more effective scaffolding. In contrast, much less is understood about scaling out: whether multiple peer agents, all targeting the same task, can become an additional source of capability without relying on explicit role specialization or workflow orchestration. We study this question and propose AgentFugue, a collective reasoning framework built around a shared reasoning hub. As peer agents explore the same task in parallel, the hub records concise notes on what each agent has established, attempted, or ruled out, and enables each agent to selectively access what other agents have discovered in a form useful for its current search. This design turns otherwise isolated trajectories into a connected ecology of reusable intermediate reasoning without requiring centralized planning. We instantiate the hub as a plug-in communication layer, trained with supervised fine-tuning and end-to-end reinforcement learning. Across the challenging long-horizon settings we study, AgentFugue improves over strong baselines. Our results suggest that collective reasoning can turn scaling out peer agent systems into a distinct source of capability gains, rather than merely a way of spending more compute.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AgentFugue, a framework where multiple peer AI agents collaboratively solve long-horizon tasks by sharing intermediate reasoning through a central hub, yielding better performance than strong single- or multi-agent baselines.
AI.motivation: Most advances in long-horizon AI tasks come from making single agents more powerful (“scaling up”) via larger models, better tools, or sophisticated scaffolding. However, it is unclear whether simply running multiple peer agents on the same task (“scaling out”) can provide genuine capability gains, rather than just extra compute, especially without complex role specialization or orchestrated workflows. The paper aims to understand whether and how collective reasoning among peer agents can turn scaling out into an independent source of improvements.
AI.method: The authors propose AgentFugue, a collective reasoning framework centered on a shared reasoning hub. Multiple peer agents tackle the same task in parallel; as they reason, the hub logs concise notes about their progress: what has been established, attempted, or ruled out. Each agent can then selectively query this shared memory to retrieve information most relevant to its current reasoning trajectory. This converts otherwise independent trajectories into an interconnected system of reusable partial reasoning without a central planner. The hub is implemented as a plug-in communication layer, trained via supervised fine-tuning and end-to-end reinforcement learning to manage, summarize, and expose useful information between agents.
AI.result: In several challenging long-horizon environments, AgentFugue outperforms strong baselines, including powerful single agents and other multi-agent setups without this structured collective reasoning hub. The empirical results show higher success rates and more efficient search, indicating that the shared hub meaningfully improves coordination and reuse of intermediate reasoning steps.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that structured collective reasoning, implemented through a shared hub for intermediate reasoning, can transform “scaling out” peer agents into a real source of capability gains for long-horizon tasks. Rather than merely spending more compute on multiple isolated agents, AgentFugue enables them to build on each other’s partial discoveries, suggesting a promising direction for future multi-agent systems and collaborative AI architectures.

title: Better, Faster: Harnessing Self-Improvement in Large Reasoning Models
summary: Self-improvement training enables the large reasoning models (LRMs) to improve themselves by self-generating reasoning trajectories as training data without external supervision. However, we find that this method often falls short in complex reasoning tasks and even leads to model collapse. Through a series of preliminary analyses, we reveal two problems: (1) data imbalance, where most training samples are simple, but the challenging yet crucial samples are scarce; (2) overthinking, where many undesired samples with redundant reasoning steps are used for self-training. To this end, we propose HSIR, which effectively Harnesses Self-Improvement in large Reasoning models via two simple-yet-effective approaches. Specifically, HSIR introduces a verify-then-exit sampling strategy to mitigate data imbalance by efficiently collecting more accurate solutions for difficult queries, and designs an Intrinsic Diversity score to quantify overthinking and filter out the undesired solutions. We apply HSIR to various post-training paradigms, among which we further propose H-GRPO, an enhanced GRPO algorithm that leverages the intrinsic diversity as an external reward to encourage concise and diverse reasoning via reinforcement learning. Extensive results show that HSIR not only effectively enhances the reasoning performance, i.e., bringing up to +10.9% average performance gains, but also significantly improves the reasoning efficiency by reducing up to 42.4% relative inference overhead.

AI.tldr: HSIR improves self-improvement training for large reasoning models by fixing data imbalance and overthinking, leading to better accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Self-improvement training lets large reasoning models generate their own reasoning traces as training data, but in complex reasoning tasks this often fails and can cause model collapse. The authors observe two key issues: most self-generated data is too easy (data imbalance), and many traces include unnecessary, overly long reasoning (overthinking). They aim to make self-improvement both more effective and efficient by addressing these problems.
AI.method: They propose HSIR, a framework with two main components: (1) a verify-then-exit sampling strategy that emphasizes collecting more accurate solutions for difficult queries, thus improving the distribution of training data; and (2) an Intrinsic Diversity (ID) score that measures redundancy and overthinking in reasoning traces, allowing the system to filter out low-quality, overly long, or repetitive solutions. HSIR is integrated into different post-training paradigms, including a new algorithm H-GRPO—an enhanced version of GRPO—that uses the Intrinsic Diversity score as an external reward in reinforcement learning to encourage concise and diverse reasoning trajectories.
AI.result: Across multiple benchmarks and post-training settings, HSIR yields substantial gains in reasoning performance, with up to +10.9% average improvement, and boosts efficiency by reducing inference overhead by as much as 42.4%. This shows that the curated and balanced self-generated data, combined with diversity-aware filtering and RL-based optimization, produces models that reason both more accurately and with fewer steps.
AI.conclusion: Addressing data imbalance and overthinking is critical for successful self-improvement of large reasoning models. HSIR demonstrates that simple mechanisms—verify-then-exit sampling and an Intrinsic Diversity-based filtering/reward signal—can significantly enhance both the effectiveness and efficiency of self-improvement training. The framework generalizes across post-training paradigms and provides a practical path to more robust, concise, and capable reasoning models.

title: TIGER: Text-Informed Generalized Enzyme-Reaction Retrieval
summary: Enzyme-reaction retrieval is a fundamental problem in computational biology, underpinning enzyme characterization, reaction mechanism elucidation, and the rational design of metabolic pathways and biocatalysts. As a bidirectional task, it entails both enzyme-to-reaction and reaction-to-enzyme mapping. However, existing approaches suffer from poor generalization across tasks and distributions, with performance highly sensitive to dataset splits and substantial asymmetry between retrieval directions. To address these challenges, we present TIGER, a Text-Informed Generalized Enzyme-Reaction Retrieval framework that leverages protein-to-text generation models to distill textual semantic knowledge from enzyme sequences, providing a generalized representation that bridges enzymes and biochemical reactions. To ensure the quality and reliability of textual semantics, we design a Dynamic Gating Network that adaptively fuses text-derived knowledge with sequence features, enabling more consistent and informative enzyme representations, while a Structure-Shared Feature Projector aligns enzyme and reaction representations within a unified latent space. Extensive experiments demonstrate that, under bidirectional retrieval supervision, TIGER significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across diverse distributions and exhibits strong robustness and transferability across tasks.

AI.tldr: They propose TIGER, a unified framework for mapping between enzymes and biochemical reactions in both directions, using text-informed representations to improve generalization, robustness, and performance over prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing enzyme-reaction retrieval methods perform inconsistently across different dataset splits and show strong asymmetry between enzyme-to-reaction and reaction-to-enzyme tasks. There is a need for a more generalizable, bidirectional system that can robustly connect enzyme sequences with their catalyzed reactions, which is crucial for enzyme characterization, mechanism study, and pathway/biocatalyst design.
AI.method: TIGER uses protein-to-text generation models to extract semantic textual knowledge from enzyme sequences, forming enriched enzyme representations. A Dynamic Gating Network adaptively fuses these text-derived semantics with raw sequence features to ensure reliable and informative representations. Then, a Structure-Shared Feature Projector maps both enzyme and reaction representations into a shared latent space so that bidirectional retrieval (enzyme→reaction and reaction→enzyme) can be trained jointly.
AI.result: In extensive experiments with bidirectional retrieval training, TIGER consistently and significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on multiple data distributions. It shows improved robustness, better generalization to different splits, and strong transferability across enzyme-reaction retrieval tasks, reducing the performance gap between the two retrieval directions.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating text-informed semantics via protein-to-text models, combined with adaptive gating and a shared latent projection, yields more generalizable and symmetric enzyme-reaction retrieval. TIGER offers a robust, bidirectional framework that advances the state of the art for linking enzymes to biochemical reactions across diverse conditions.

title: Language Bias in LVLMs: From In-Depth Analysis to Simple and Effective Mitigation
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) extend large language models with visual understanding, but remain vulnerable to hallucination, where outputs are fluent yet inconsistent with images. Recent studies link this issue to language bias-the tendency of LVLMs to over-rely on text while neglecting visual inputs. Yet most analyses remain empirical without uncovering its underlying cause. In this paper, we provide a systematic study of language bias and identify its root in modality misalignment during training. Our analysis shows that both Visual Instruction Tuning (VIT) and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) often prioritize textual improvements, which may cause LVLMs to overly lean toward language modeling rather than balanced multimodal understanding. To address this, we propose two simple yet effective methods: Language Bias Regularization (LBR) which mitigates language bias through regularization during instruction tuning, and Language Bias Penalty (LBP), which penalizes language bias in the DPO training process. Extensive experiments across diverse models and benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. LBR consistently improves performance on over ten general benchmarks, while LBP significantly reduces hallucination and improves trustworthiness. Together, these methods not only mitigate language bias but also advance the overall alignment of LVLMs, all without introducing any additional data or auxiliary models. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/lab-klc/LVLM-Language-Bias.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and mitigates language bias in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs), proposing two simple training-time methods that reduce hallucination and improve multimodal alignment without extra data.
AI.motivation: LVLMs, despite strong performance, often hallucinate—producing text that is fluent but inconsistent with the input image. Prior work attributes this partly to language bias (over-reliance on text vs. vision), but evidence has been mostly empirical and lacks a clear explanation of the underlying training causes. The authors aim to systematically analyze where this bias comes from in current training pipelines and to design lightweight methods to reduce it while improving reliability and alignment.
AI.method: The authors analyze the training dynamics of LVLMs, focusing on Visual Instruction Tuning (VIT) and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). They argue these stages over-emphasize textual quality and preferences, causing modality misalignment and language bias. To address this, they introduce: (1) Language Bias Regularization (LBR), a regularization term added during instruction tuning to discourage over-reliance on language-only signals and encourage better use of visual features; and (2) Language Bias Penalty (LBP), a modification to DPO that explicitly penalizes language-biased behavior during preference optimization. Both methods are designed to be simple, data- and model-agnostic add-ons to existing training pipelines, requiring no additional datasets or auxiliary models.
AI.result: Across multiple LVLM architectures and a variety of benchmarks, LBR yields consistent gains on more than ten general evaluation tasks, indicating better multimodal capabilities. LBP leads to marked reductions in hallucination rates and improves trustworthiness metrics in preference-based evaluation setups. The improvements are achieved without adding new data sources or external models, showing that the gains come from better training objectives rather than scale or supervision increases.
AI.conclusion: Language bias in LVLMs stems from modality misalignment introduced by common training practices, particularly VIT and DPO, which favor textual optimization over balanced multimodal learning. By adjusting the objectives—through LBR in instruction tuning and LBP in preference optimization—the authors effectively mitigate language bias, reduce hallucinations, and enhance overall alignment and performance of LVLMs. Their work provides both a conceptual explanation of the problem and practical, lightweight fixes that can be easily integrated into existing LVLM training pipelines.

title: Market Regime Council for Dynamic Credit Assignment in Multi-Agent LLM Decision Systems
summary: Multi-agent LLM decision systems for portfolio management still lack a principled way to assign credit across specialist agents, remain vulnerable to cold-start dominance under regime shifts, and offer limited transparency into how final allocations are formed. We propose Market Regime Council (MRC), a cooperative multi-agent decision system that computes exact Shapley credits across all single, pairwise, and Grand-coalition outputs for online agent weighting. Instantiated with N=3 specialist agents, at each trading period, MRC recomputes coalition-based Shapley weights from exponentially weighted performance histories, uses a Bayesian adaptive mixture to stabilize early periods, applies regime-dependent multipliers to adjust agent authority, and records each rebalance through a five-layer causal trace. Over 1,037 trading days across 13 crypto assets and five seeds, MRC achieves a Sharpe ratio of 1.51 and a cumulative return of 440.1%, ranking first on CR, SR, and IR among active baselines and attaining the lowest MDD among active methods. Ablation results show that the gains come from Shapley-weighted integration across coalition outputs rather than from any single stage in isolation. Code and demo data are included in the supplementary material.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent LLM-driven portfolio system that uses Shapley-value credit assignment across agent coalitions to dynamically weight specialists, improving crypto trading performance and transparency.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based multi-agent portfolio managers lack principled credit assignment to specialist agents, suffer from cold-start dominance when regimes change, and provide little transparency into how final allocations are produced. There is a need for a framework that can fairly and adaptively allocate influence among agents while giving interpretable traces of the decision process.
AI.method: The authors propose Market Regime Council (MRC), a cooperative multi-agent decision system with N=3 specialist agents. For each trading period, MRC: (1) computes exact Shapley credits across all single agents, pairwise coalitions, and the grand coalition using exponentially weighted performance histories; (2) uses these credits to form online agent weights; (3) employs a Bayesian adaptive mixture to stabilize decisions in early (data-poor) periods; (4) applies regime-dependent multipliers to modulate each agent’s authority based on market regime; and (5) stores each rebalance decision in a five-layer causal trace for transparency and analysis.
AI.result: On 1,037 trading days across 13 crypto assets and five random seeds, MRC achieves a Sharpe ratio of 1.51 and a cumulative return of 440.1%. It ranks first among active baselines on cumulative return (CR), Sharpe ratio (SR), and information ratio (IR), while also achieving the lowest maximum drawdown (MDD) among active methods. Ablation studies show that performance improvements arise from the Shapley-weighted integration of coalition outputs rather than from any individual pipeline component alone.
AI.conclusion: MRC provides a principled, Shapley-value-based mechanism for credit assignment and weighting in multi-agent LLM portfolio systems, mitigating cold-start dominance and enhancing transparency via causal traces. The empirical results on crypto markets demonstrate state-of-the-art risk-adjusted returns and robustness, and ablations indicate that the key benefit comes from coalition-aware Shapley integration rather than from isolated design choices. Code and demo data are available for reproducibility.

title: TRACE: A taxonomy-grounded synthetic dataset for teaching-program generation and session interpretation in Applied Behavior Analysis
summary: Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA) is a clinical discipline whose documentation, teaching programs and multi-session behavioral logs, is formulaic and high-volume, yet real session data is HIPAA-protected and bound by professional confidentiality rules, blocking the release of a training corpus. We present TRACE (Taxonomy-Referenced ABA Clinical Examples), a 2,999-example synthetic instruction-tuning dataset covering two ABA tasks: teaching-program generation across Discrete Trial Training, Natural Environment Teaching, and Task Analysis; and multi-session behavioral interpretation across twelve trajectory patterns and thirteen target behaviors. Every example is produced by a deterministic taxonomy-driven generator grounded in the canonical ABA literature, and every example carries complete sampling provenance, the exact taxonomy cells that produced it. The dataset is released under CC BY-NC 4.0 for data and MIT for code, with stratified train (2,549), validation (149), test (281), and sanity (20) splits. TRACE is a research artifact and has not been clinically validated.

AI.tldr: They introduce TRACE, a fully synthetic, taxonomy-driven dataset (2,999 examples) for instruction-tuning models on ABA tasks while avoiding HIPAA constraints.
AI.motivation: Real ABA clinical records are highly structured and voluminous, but privacy and professional confidentiality (HIPAA) prevent releasing them as an open training corpus. The authors want a way to train and evaluate models on ABA tasks without using real patient data.
AI.method: They design a deterministic, taxonomy-based data generator grounded in standard ABA literature. Using this generator, they synthesize 2,999 instruction-style examples for two task families: (1) teaching-program generation across three ABA pedagogical formats (Discrete Trial Training, Natural Environment Teaching, Task Analysis); and (2) multi-session behavioral interpretation, systematically spanning 12 trajectory patterns and 13 target behaviors. Each example includes full provenance: which taxonomy cells and sampling decisions produced it. They split the data into stratified train/validation/test/sanity subsets and release both data and code under open licenses.
AI.result: The result is TRACE, a structured, reproducible, synthetic dataset with 2,999 fully documented ABA clinical-style examples, organized into public splits suitable for training and evaluating instruction-tuned models on ABA-related tasks, all without exposing real clinical data.
AI.conclusion: TRACE demonstrates that a deterministic, literature-grounded taxonomy can be used to generate a large, well-documented synthetic corpus for a privacy-sensitive clinical domain like ABA. While it is explicitly not clinically validated, it provides a research resource—both dataset and generator—for experimenting with and benchmarking models on ABA instruction-following tasks under permissive research licenses.

title: Reasoning as an Attack Surface: Adaptive Evolutionary CoT Jailbreaks for LLMs
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in reasoning and generation tasks and are increasingly deployed in real-world applications. However, their explicit chain-of-thought (CoT) mechanism introduces new security risks, making them particularly vulnerable to jailbreak attacks. Existing approaches often rely on static CoT templates to elicit harmful outputs, but such fixed designs suffer from limited diversity, adaptability, and effectiveness. To overcome these limitations, we propose an adaptive evolutionary CoT jailbreak framework, called AE-CoT. Specifically, the method first rewrites harmful goals into mild prompts with teacher role-play and decomposes them into semantically coherent reasoning fragments to construct a pool of CoT jailbreak candidates. Then, within a structured representation space, we perform multi-generation evolutionary search, where candidate diversity is expanded through fragment-level crossover and a mutation strategy with an adaptive mutation-rate control mechanism. An independent scoring model provides graded harmfulness evaluations, and high-scoring candidates are further enhanced with a harmful CoT template to induce more destructive generations. Extensive experiments across multiple models and datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed AE-CoT, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art jailbreak methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AE-CoT, an adaptive evolutionary chain-of-thought jailbreak framework that more effectively bypasses safety in large reasoning models than prior static-template methods.
AI.motivation: Large Reasoning Models use explicit chain-of-thought reasoning, which improves performance but exposes intermediate reasoning steps that adversaries can exploit for jailbreak attacks. Existing jailbreak methods largely depend on hand-crafted, static CoT templates that lack diversity and adaptability, limiting their success and robustness across models and scenarios. The paper aims to design a more flexible, automated, and powerful jailbreak method that can evolve prompts and reasoning structures to reliably induce harmful behavior in LRMs.
AI.method: AE-CoT begins by transforming an attacker’s harmful goal into a milder, teacher-style role-play prompt and decomposing it into semantically coherent reasoning fragments. These fragments form a pool of candidate CoT jailbreak prompts represented in a structured space. The framework then performs a multi-generation evolutionary search: candidate prompts are recombined via fragment-level crossover, and diversified through a mutation strategy whose mutation rate is adaptively controlled based on search dynamics. An independent scoring model assigns graded harmfulness scores to model outputs, which guide selection in the evolutionary loop. High-scoring prompts are finally augmented with a specialized harmful CoT template to maximize the likelihood and severity of harmful generations.
AI.result: Across multiple LRM architectures and benchmark datasets, AE-CoT consistently achieves higher jailbreak success rates and generates more severe harmful content than existing state-of-the-art jailbreak approaches. The experiments show that its adaptive evolutionary strategy yields more diverse and transferable jailbreak prompts, and that the harmfulness-guided selection notably improves attack effectiveness over non-adaptive or static-template baselines.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that explicit CoT reasoning in LRMs significantly broadens the surface for adversarial exploitation, and that adaptive, evolution-based prompt construction like AE-CoT can substantially outperform fixed-template jailbreak attacks. It highlights the need for stronger safety mechanisms that are robust not only to static prompts but also to adaptive, optimization-driven jailbreak strategies, and suggests that defense research must anticipate and counter such evolving attack methodologies.

title: Faithfulness Metrics Don't Measure Faithfulness: A Meta-Evaluation with Ground Truth
summary: Chains of thought (CoTs) have become central in interpreting and auditing behaviors of large language models. Yet growing evidence suggests that these traces often fail to faithfully represent the computations behind a model's predictions. Several faithfulness metrics have been proposed, but whether they indeed measure faithfulness remains unknown. Answering this requires ground-truth labels, which are hard to obtain since internal computations are not directly observable. Consequently, most works proposing metrics report only absolute scores or comparisons to prior metrics, and the few existing benchmarks rely on proxies like plausibility or importance, properties orthogonal to faithfulness that can mislead about whether a CoT can be trusted. We address this challenge by constructing tasks whose outputs reveal which intermediate computations must have produced them, and developing an automated labeling pipeline that yields ground-truth faithfulness labels at both the step and CoT level. Building on this methodology, we present BonaFide, a benchmark of 3,066 labeled CoTs across 13 tasks and 10 models, and use it to conduct the first systematic evaluation of prominent faithfulness metrics. Our experiments show that most metrics perform near chance, exhibit strong prediction biases and degrade on longer CoTs. The best metric reaches only 0.70 AUROC at the CoT level while another reaches 0.59 at the step level, with neither transferring across settings, while entailing prohibitively high computational cost. Our results expose fundamental gaps in current faithfulness evaluation and call for the development of more reliable and efficient metrics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BonaFide, a benchmark with ground-truth labels to evaluate how faithfully chain-of-thought (CoT) explanations reflect model reasoning, and finds that existing faithfulness metrics are weak and unreliable.
AI.motivation: Chains of thought are widely used to interpret and audit large language models, but growing evidence shows that these explanations are often unfaithful to the model’s actual internal computations. Existing metrics for CoT faithfulness are themselves unevaluated, because ground-truth about a model’s true intermediate reasoning is hard to obtain. Current benchmarks rely on plausibility or importance, which are not the same as faithfulness and can give a false sense of trust. The authors aim to rigorously test whether faithfulness metrics actually measure what they claim.
AI.method: The authors design special tasks whose final outputs logically constrain which intermediate computations must have occurred, making it possible to infer ground-truth about which steps are faithful. They build an automated labeling pipeline that assigns faithfulness labels at both the individual reasoning-step level and the whole-CoT level. Using this pipeline, they construct the BonaFide benchmark: 3,066 labeled CoTs spanning 13 tasks and 10 different models. They then systematically evaluate several prominent CoT faithfulness metrics on this benchmark, examining performance, biases, length sensitivity, cross-setting transfer, and computational cost.
AI.result: On the BonaFide benchmark, most existing faithfulness metrics perform around chance, show strong prediction biases, and their performance worsens as CoTs get longer. The best-performing metric achieves only 0.70 AUROC at the CoT level, while another reaches just 0.59 AUROC at the step level. These better metrics do not generalize well across different settings and are also very computationally expensive to run.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that current metrics for evaluating the faithfulness of chain-of-thought explanations are fundamentally inadequate: they are only weakly predictive, biased, fragile to longer reasoning traces, non-transferable, and costly. This reveals a major gap in how the community assesses CoT faithfulness and underscores the need for developing new, more reliable and efficient faithfulness metrics, supported by benchmarks like BonaFide that use ground-truth labels rather than plausibility proxies.

title: Hypothesis Generation and Inductive Inference in Children and Language Models
summary: Real world decision-making requires constructing mental models under uncertainty over evidence, over the underlying causal rules, and over the state of the world itself. Which computational principles underpin human inference under such conditions, and do LLM-based agents exhibit similar behavior given matching constraints? We address these questions using an inductive inference Box Task in which participants, human children and LLM-based agents, infer a latent cause through sequential interaction with an uncertain environment. We formalize this task as program induction with Bayesian particle-based inference, admitting two complementary interpretations: (1) as a constraint satisfaction process over hypotheses, and (2) as a program synthesis problem in which hypotheses are executable programs evaluated against evidence. Using the constraint-based formulation, we show that children's behavior is best explained by a combination of subjective evidence reliability and online hypothesis generation, accounting for both their evidence-seeking patterns and their dissociation between task completion and rule generalization. Using the program synthesis formulation, we treat LLM-based agents as model organisms: controllable systems that allow systematic manipulation of task conditions. Across backends, LLM-based agents replicate children's responses to changes in evidence reliability and observability, including discounting unreliable evidence, seeking to resolve partial information, and dissociating between task completion and causal generalization. At the same time, LLM-based agents tend to over-observe and over-comply with instructions relative to children. These results suggest that while children and LLM-based agents adapt similarly to environmental structure, their information-seeking behavior exhibits distinct underlying costs and inductive biases.

AI.tldr: The paper compares how human children and LLM-based agents infer hidden causes in an uncertain environment, modeling both with Bayesian program-induction and showing overlapping adaptations but distinct information-seeking biases.
AI.motivation: To understand the computational principles behind human inductive inference under multiple kinds of uncertainty, and to test whether large language model (LLM) agents show similar inference and information-seeking patterns when placed in equivalent tasks and constraints.
AI.method: They use an inductive "Box Task" where agents (children and LLMs) interact sequentially with an uncertain environment to infer a latent causal rule. They formalize the task as program induction with Bayesian particle-based inference, providing two views: (1) constraint satisfaction over hypotheses and (2) program synthesis where hypotheses are executable programs. With the constraint-satisfaction model, they fit children's behavior by manipulating subjective evidence reliability and online hypothesis generation. With the program-synthesis view, they run controlled experiments on LLM-based agents across backends, systematically varying evidence reliability and observability, and compare their behaviors to children.
AI.result: The best-fitting model of children's performance combines subjective evidence reliability with online hypothesis generation, explaining both how they seek evidence and why they can solve the immediate task without fully generalizing the underlying rule. LLM-based agents, across different backends, reproduce key qualitative patterns: they discount unreliable evidence, resolve partial information, and also show a dissociation between task completion and deeper causal generalization. However, LLM agents systematically differ by over-observing (seeking more information than children) and over-complying with instructions.
AI.conclusion: Children and LLM-based agents both adapt their inference strategies to environmental structure in the Box Task, but their information-seeking behaviors arise from different underlying costs and inductive biases. Bayesian program-induction provides a unifying framework to characterize these similarities and differences, suggesting that LLMs can serve as useful "model organisms" for studying structured inference, while still differing meaningfully from human learners.

title: Evidence-Linked Radiology Reporting: A Human-Supervised Reference Architecture for Structured Imaging Intelligence
comment: Technical report, 27 pages, 2 figures, 12 tables, 1 listing; reference architecture paper; does not report clinical outcomes or validated diagnostic performance
summary: Radiology reports remain the primary mechanism by which imaging findings are communicated to clinical teams. However, much of the structured information behind these reports, including measurements, image evidence, prior comparisons, lesion identity, uncertainty, and terminology, often remains trapped in free text or fragmented across picture archiving and communication systems, radiology information systems, reporting workstations, worksheets, advanced visualization tools, and electronic health records. This paper proposes a human-supervised, evidence-linked reference architecture for structured radiology reporting. The framework combines exam-specific templates, speech-to-structure processing, measurement and segmentation capture, controlled AI-assisted drafting, and standards-based interoperability using DICOM, DICOM Structured Reporting, DICOM Segmentation, HL7 FHIR, RadLex, SNOMED CT, LOINC, and UCUM. The system is positioned not as an autonomous report generator, but as a structured intelligence layer for enterprise imaging that supports reviewed reporting, longitudinal comparison, clinical data reuse, governance, and integration with PACS, RIS, EHR, analytics, and registry workflows. The paper also discusses modality-specific deployment considerations, clinical safety risks, validation requirements, cybersecurity, privacy, quality management, and regulatory boundaries for AI-assisted radiology reporting systems.

AI.tldr: Proposes a human-supervised, interoperable architecture to turn radiology reports from free text into structured, evidence-linked data integrated across hospital systems.
AI.motivation: Current radiology reports are mostly free text, leaving key structured elements—measurements, lesion IDs, uncertainty, priors, terminology—buried or scattered across PACS, RIS, workstations, and EHR, which limits reuse, consistency, and integration with downstream clinical and analytic workflows.
AI.method: Design of a reference architecture for structured radiology reporting that integrates exam-specific templates, speech-to-structured input, automated capture of measurements and segmentations, controlled AI-assisted drafting, and standards-based data exchange using DICOM/SR/Seg, HL7 FHIR, and standard vocabularies such as RadLex, SNOMED CT, LOINC, and UCUM. The system is explicitly human-supervised and tightly integrated with existing imaging and health IT infrastructure.
AI.result: The architecture is positioned as an intelligence and structuring layer for enterprise imaging that supports reviewed reporting, longitudinal comparison, data reuse, and bidirectional integration with PACS, RIS, EHR, analytics platforms, and registries. The paper also surfaces practical considerations around modality-specific deployment, clinical safety, validation, cybersecurity, privacy, quality management, and regulatory boundaries.
AI.conclusion: A human-in-the-loop, standards-based structured reporting framework can transform radiology reports from isolated free text into interoperable, evidence-linked data assets, while still respecting safety, governance, and regulatory constraints for AI-assisted clinical systems.

title: DemoEvolve: Overcoming Sparse Feedback in Agentic Harness Evolution with Demonstrations
summary: Agent harness evolution improves frozen language-model agents by modifying the executable structures around them. We study this paradigm as a form of sample-efficient fast adaptation: instead of updating model weights, an agent can acquire task-specific competence by changing its external harness, while leaving the base model's general capabilities intact. Prior work shows that self-generated rollouts can support harness search, suggesting that agents may acquire new task competence through practice. Yet in long-horizon stochastic environments, self-practice becomes fragile: rewards are sparse, outcomes are high-variance, and failures are hard to attribute to concrete harness mechanisms. We introduce DemoEvolve, a demonstration-bootstrapped approach to harness evolution. When reward-only search is too broad and noisy, competent human trajectories serve as expert reference experience for the coding proposer, guiding harness-level diagnosis and editing. Experiments on Liar's Dice show that self-rollout evolution can work when episodes are short and failures are attributable. In contrast, Balatro exposes a harder long-horizon stochastic regime, where self-rollout evolution is misled by sparse feedback and candidate-selection noise, while tutorial-like textual knowledge alone does not yield stable improvement. Under the same limited budget, DemoEvolve produces more effective and auditable harness edits and achieves better performance. Overall, demonstrations make sparse-feedback harness evolution more diagnosable, localizable, and stable.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes DemoEvolve, a method that improves frozen language-model agents by evolving the surrounding agent harness using human demonstrations, enabling more stable and effective adaptation in sparse, long-horizon environments.
AI.motivation: Updating large model weights for every new task is expensive and slow. Harness evolution—modifying the code and structures around a frozen language model—offers a more sample- and compute-efficient way to gain task-specific competence. However, prior harness evolution methods that rely only on self-generated rollouts struggle in long-horizon, stochastic settings with sparse rewards and high variance, where failures are hard to attribute. The authors aim to make this adaptation more robust and diagnosable in such environments.
AI.method: The authors introduce DemoEvolve, a demonstration-bootstrapped harness evolution framework. Instead of relying solely on reward signals from self-play or exploration, DemoEvolve uses competent human trajectories as expert references. A coding proposer (likely a code-generating component powered by the language model) uses these demonstrations to diagnose where the harness fails and suggests targeted harness edits. The process focuses on making edits that are auditable and localized, improving stability and interpretability while maintaining the base model weights frozen.
AI.result: In experiments, the authors evaluate on two games: Liar's Dice and Balatro. For Liar's Dice, which has relatively short episodes and more easily attributable failures, self-rollout-based harness evolution is sufficient and works well. However, for Balatro, a more challenging long-horizon stochastic environment, self-rollout evolution performs poorly due to sparse, noisy feedback and candidate-selection noise; tutorial-style textual knowledge alone also fails to yield robust improvements. Under the same limited interaction or compute budget, DemoEvolve achieves better performance than self-rollout evolution and produces more effective, auditable harness edits.
AI.conclusion: DemoEvolve demonstrates that incorporating human demonstrations into harness evolution helps agents adapt more effectively in sparse-reward, long-horizon, stochastic tasks without modifying base model weights. Demonstrations make harness evolution more diagnosable, localizable, and stable than reward-only self-practice, highlighting a promising direction for fast, sample-efficient adaptation of frozen language-model agents via structured modifications to their surrounding agent code.

title: LLM Agent Based Renewable Energy Forecasting Using Edge and IoT Data A Review of Solar Wind Weather and Grid Aware Decision Support
summary: Reliable forecasting of renewable energy generation is a foundational requirement for grid stability energy trading battery scheduling and carbon aware operational planning Solar and wind resources are inherently intermittent their output fluctuates with cloud cover wind speed atmospheric turbulence seasonal patterns and local terrain The proliferation of IoT and edge devices spanning smart meters inverters anemometers pyranometers weather stations and grid interface sensors has created an unprecedented volume of real time operational data that conventional forecasting pipelines are ill equipped to exploit fully This review investigates how large language model LLM agents can enhance renewable energy forecasting by integrating heterogeneous sensor streams weather API data historical generation records grid constraints and contextual reasoning into unified decision support workflows We survey classical forecasting methods statistical time series models deep learning architectures physics hybrid approaches and emerging LLM agent frameworks for explanation uncertainty communication and operator guidance A six layer taxonomy is proposed covering data acquisition preprocessing feature engineering model inference uncertainty estimation and natural language reporting The review identifies twelve open challenges spanning real time deployment model drift under distribution shift uncertainty quantification hallucination control in LLM agents interoperability of edge hardware and integration with energy management systems The paper concludes by recommending a research agenda centred on open benchmarks physics informed LLM grounding and federated forecasting architectures

AI.tldr: Review on using large language model agents to improve renewable energy forecasting via integrated data workflows and decision support.
AI.motivation: Renewable energy sources like solar and wind are intermittent, making accurate forecasting essential for grid stability, energy trading, battery scheduling, and carbon-aware operations. The explosion of IoT and edge sensing data has outpaced the capabilities of conventional forecasting pipelines, motivating exploration of LLM agents to better leverage heterogeneous, real-time data.
AI.method: Survey and synthesize existing forecasting approaches including classical statistical time-series models, deep learning architectures, physics-based and hybrid models, and emerging LLM-agent frameworks. Propose a six-layer taxonomy for forecasting workflows: data acquisition, preprocessing, feature engineering, model inference, uncertainty estimation, and natural language reporting. Analyze and categorize open challenges in deploying such systems.
AI.result: Provides a structured overview of methods and introduces a six-layer taxonomy for LLM-enhanced renewable forecasting workflows. Identifies twelve key open challenges related to real-time deployment, distribution shift and model drift, uncertainty quantification, hallucination control in LLM agents, interoperability of edge hardware, and integration with existing energy management systems.
AI.conclusion: LLM agents have strong potential to enhance renewable energy forecasting by unifying heterogeneous data sources and providing contextual, explainable decision support, but significant technical and operational challenges remain. The paper advocates a research agenda prioritizing open benchmarks, physics-informed grounding of LLMs, and federated forecasting architectures to move from concept to practical deployment.

title: Emission-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Sustainable Electric Vehicle Charging and Carbon Dioxide Reduction Under Varying Renewable Penetration
comment: Submitted the Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence Journal (Elsevier)
summary: The rapid growth of Electric Vehicle (EV) adoption challenges power distribution networks through peak load spikes, voltage instability, and transformer overloads from uncoordinated charging. While Model Predictive Control (MPC) and standard Reinforcement Learning (RL) methods have addressed these issues, existing approaches rarely treat real-time carbon intensity or fluctuating renewable energy (RE) availability as primary scheduling objectives, leaving substantial decarbonisation potential unrealised. This paper proposes an emission-aware RL strategy based on the Soft Actor Critic (SAC) algorithm, with a multi-objective reward that penalises carbon emissions, curtailed on-site renewables, and unmet user demand. The agent is trained within a unified benchmarking framework on the EV2Gym platform, incorporating behind-the-meter solar and wind profiles, time-varying EirGrid carbon intensity data, and realistic workplace EV behaviour across 25 Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment (EVSE) units. Nine control strategies, including heuristics, emission-aware MPC variants, and the proposed RL agent, are compared under five renewable penetration scenarios (0%-50%) over ten independent runs each. The RL agent achieves a carbon intensity as low as 23.96 grams of carbon dioxide per kilowatt-hour under 50% wind penetration, representing up to 87% emission reduction versus the uncontrolled baseline, and outperforms the external graph-based Power Distribution Network (PDN) benchmark. Transformer overload remains below 7 kWh across scenarios, against up to 1093 kWh for the As Fast As Possible (AFAP) heuristic, and renewable self-consumption reaches 52% under combined wind and solar supply. Embedding carbon intensity forecasts into the RL state and reward aligns charging with low-emission periods while preserving grid compliance and user satisfaction.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an emission-aware EV charging control strategy using Soft Actor Critic RL, jointly optimising carbon emissions, grid constraints, and user needs, and shows it outperforms heuristic and MPC baselines under various renewable penetration scenarios.
AI.motivation: Uncoordinated EV charging causes peak loads, voltage issues, and transformer overloads. Existing MPC and RL methods largely optimise cost or grid constraints but do not primarily target real-time carbon intensity or renewable utilisation, missing substantial decarbonisation potential. There is a need for control strategies that explicitly align EV charging with low-carbon, high-renewable periods while respecting grid and user constraints.
AI.method: The authors design a multi-objective, emission-aware RL controller based on the Soft Actor Critic (SAC) algorithm. The reward penalises carbon emissions, curtailment of on-site renewables, and unmet charging demand. The agent is trained and evaluated in the EV2Gym simulation environment, which models behind-the-meter solar and wind, time-varying EirGrid carbon intensity, and realistic workplace EV charging behaviour across 25 EVSEs. The approach is benchmarked against nine alternative control strategies, including heuristics and emission-aware MPC, over five renewable penetration levels (0–50%) with ten stochastic runs each.
AI.result: The SAC-based RL agent reduces carbon intensity to 23.96 gCO2/kWh under 50% wind penetration, up to an 87% reduction relative to an uncontrolled baseline and better than a graph-based PDN benchmark. It maintains transformer overload below 7 kWh in all scenarios, compared to up to 1093 kWh for the AFAP heuristic. The strategy also achieves up to 52% renewable self-consumption under combined wind and solar, while still meeting user energy needs and grid constraints.
AI.conclusion: Embedding carbon intensity forecasts into both the RL state and reward enables the controller to shift EV charging to low-emission periods, significantly cutting operational emissions while maintaining grid compliance and user satisfaction. The emission-aware SAC-based RL strategy is an effective and robust approach for co-optimising decarbonisation, renewable utilisation, and network security in EV charging networks, outperforming heuristic and MPC-based baselines across diverse renewable penetration scenarios.

title: STREAM: A Data-Centric Framework for Mining High-Value Task-Oriented Dialogues from Streaming Media
summary: Large language models for vertical domains are bottlenecked by the scarcity of complex, domain-specific task-oriented dialogues. Existing data acquisition pipelines face a persistent trilemma: expert annotation is expensive, real-world service conversations are constrained by privacy and commercial restrictions, and static corpora quickly become temporally stale. We propose Stream, a data-centric framework that leverages publicly available streaming media (live streams and short videos) to synthesize high-value service dialogues at scale. Stream mines authentic interaction signals from noisy streams and synthesizes conversations by integrating role-grounded persona construction with Conversational Blueprint construction; it further adopts retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to support knowledge-aware responses. Based on Stream, we release StreamDial, a large-scale multi-domain dataset covering Automotive, Restaurant, and Hotel. StreamDial contains 87,498 dialogue sessions and 1,497,320 turns in total, with an average of 17.11 turns per session and a comparable scale across domains. Each session is organized as a structured quadruplet $\langle P_u, P_a, B, H \rangle$ that pairs dialogue history with explicit user/agent personas and a Conversational Blueprint, capturing realistic service behaviors such as requirement mining, constraint conflicts, negotiation, and recovery. Evaluations with automatic judges and downstream tasks show that StreamDial improves intrinsic dialogue quality over strong baselines, and models trained with StreamDial improve Dialogue State Tracking across backbones; we further report a completed human-evaluation set and encouraging multilingual transfer on Qwen3-8B under a controlled training budget. The data is released in https://github.com/hitxueliang/DialogDataSetBySTREAM.

AI.tldr: Introduces Stream, a framework that converts public streaming media into large-scale, high-quality, domain-specific service dialogues, and releases the StreamDial dataset, which improves dialogue quality and dialogue state tracking.
AI.motivation: Vertical-domain LLMs lack large, complex, domain-specific task-oriented dialogue data. Existing sources are limited: expert labels are costly, real service logs are locked by privacy/commercial issues, and pre-built corpora rapidly become outdated. The paper aims to create a scalable, up-to-date way to obtain realistic, knowledge-intensive service dialogues without relying on proprietary logs or massive expert annotation.
AI.method: They propose Stream, a data-centric pipeline that: (1) harvests public live streams and short videos as a noisy but rich signal of real interactions; (2) mines authentic interaction cues from these streams; (3) constructs role-grounded user and agent personas; (4) builds a Conversational Blueprint that specifies goals, constraints, and dialogue flow; and (5) synthesizes dialogues conditioned on personas and blueprints. The framework uses retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) so responses can reference up-to-date external knowledge. The resulting conversations are stored as structured quadruplets ⟨Pu, Pa, B, H⟩: user persona, agent persona, blueprint, and dialogue history.
AI.result: Using Stream, they build StreamDial, a multi-domain task-oriented dialogue dataset in Automotive, Restaurant, and Hotel. It has 87,498 sessions and 1,497,320 turns (≈17.11 turns/session), with balanced domain coverage. Each session includes explicit personas and a blueprint, leading to realistic behaviors such as requirement elicitation, negotiation, handling conflicts, and error recovery. Automatic evaluation and downstream experiments show that StreamDial outperforms strong baseline datasets on intrinsic dialogue metrics and that models trained on it yield better Dialogue State Tracking performance across different LLM backbones. They also provide a human-evaluation subset and demonstrate positive multilingual transfer on Qwen3-8B within a limited training budget.
AI.conclusion: Public streaming media can be systematically converted into high-value, domain-specific service dialogues at scale via the Stream framework. The resulting StreamDial corpus is large, structurally rich, multi-domain, and empirically useful: it enhances dialogue quality and improves Dialogue State Tracking, with evidence of cross-lingual benefits. The approach offers a practical, updatable alternative to costly expert annotation and restricted proprietary logs for building vertical-domain conversational agents.

title: Beyond Control-Flow: Integrating the Resource Perspective into Multi-Collaborative Process Modeling from Text
summary: Process modeling is a sub-domain of Business Process Management (BPM) focused on the translation of process artifacts into formal models. This task traditionally requires extensive human input and domain expertise in both BPM notations and the specific business context. While Large Language Models (LLMs) can now automate much of this manual work, current text-to-model approaches focus predominantly on the control-flow perspective-ordering activities without considering the collaborative aspect of the processes. In this paper, we introduce a resource-aware generation pipeline that produces formal BPMN 2.0 collaboration diagrams from natural-language descriptions. Rather than solely prompting an LLM for raw XML, we describe a compact, executable intermediate language with mandatory resource details defining both the organization (pool) and the role (lane). Cross-organization dependencies are materialized using the standard formal notation for such interactions-message events-while an orthogonal layout routine automatically handles the spatial arrangement of elements within pools and lanes. Experiments on ten business processes with nine LLMs show strong resource discovery while preserving control-flow quality and adding only marginal runtime overhead. This approach moves generative modeling toward a more comprehensive, multi-collaborative representation of business operations.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a resource-aware LLM-based pipeline that generates BPMN 2.0 collaboration diagrams from natural-language process descriptions, capturing both control-flow and organizational/resources aspects.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based text-to-model methods in BPM mostly focus on control-flow (activity ordering) and ignore the collaborative/resource perspective (who does what, across which organizations and roles). Manual creation of such detailed BPMN collaboration diagrams is labor-intensive and requires expertise in BPMN and the business domain. There is a need for automated, comprehensive process model generation that includes resources and cross-organizational interactions, not just activity sequences.
AI.method: The authors design a resource-aware generation pipeline where natural-language process descriptions are first translated by an LLM into a compact, executable intermediate language. This language explicitly encodes resource information, including organizations as pools and roles as lanes. From this intermediate representation, they automatically generate BPMN 2.0 collaboration diagrams. Cross-organization dependencies are expressed using standard BPMN message events. An orthogonal layout routine is used to automatically arrange the spatial layout of elements within pools and lanes. They evaluate the pipeline on ten business processes using nine different LLMs.
AI.result: Across the ten case processes and nine LLMs, the pipeline exhibits strong capability in discovering and representing resources (organizations and roles) while maintaining a quality control-flow comparable to existing methods. The inclusion of resource-awareness adds only marginal runtime overhead relative to simpler, control-flow-only text-to-model approaches.
AI.conclusion: A resource-aware, intermediate-language-based generation pipeline enables LLMs to produce BPMN 2.0 collaboration diagrams that capture both control-flow and collaborative/resource perspectives. This moves generative process modeling toward richer, multi-party representations of business operations, demonstrating that resource information can be effectively inferred from natural language with minimal additional computational cost.

title: Re-defining Humor Data Objects for AI Humor Research
summary: In most existing AI humor research, humor was treated as either "present" or "not present." We explore the concept of humor as a social interaction with context and explanations. During this project, we defined a humor reasoning data object and developed a way to prompt LLMs to generate an explanation of humor effective for general population. We iterated from an earlier prompt to an improved prompt, found that the later version reduced important errors, and then scaled generation to a large number of data objects which have the potential to enable data synthesis and data augmentation for AI humor research. Our main takeaway is that better prompting of an LLM improves humor explanation quality, especially by handling missing context, multi-modality, and transcript issues more carefully. These results establish a strong foundation for future work on AI understanding of humor as social behavior.

AI.tldr: The paper treats humor as a contextual social interaction and shows that carefully designed prompts for large language models can substantially improve the quality of humor explanations, enabling large-scale data creation for AI humor research.
AI.motivation: Most prior AI humor work reduces humor to a binary label (present/not present), ignoring the rich social, contextual, and explanatory aspects of why something is funny. This limits AI’s ability to truly understand humor or to support research that needs nuanced reasoning about jokes. The authors want a structured way to capture and generate explanations of humor that reflect real-world social interaction and can be used as data for more advanced models.
AI.method: They define a "humor reasoning data object"—a structured representation for humor explanations—and design prompt templates that instruct an LLM to generate explanations of why content is funny for a general audience. They iteratively refine the prompting strategy, comparing an initial prompt to an improved version with better handling of missing context, multi‑modality (e.g., text plus image/audio), and transcript imperfections. After validating that the improved prompt reduces important errors, they scale up and use it to generate a large collection of humor reasoning data objects.
AI.result: The improved prompt produces higher-quality humor explanations than the initial version, particularly in scenarios with incomplete context, multiple modalities, or noisy transcripts. Using this refined prompting method, they successfully generate a large number of structured humor reasoning objects suitable for downstream uses such as data synthesis and augmentation in humor-related AI tasks.
AI.conclusion: Prompt design has a major impact on an LLM’s ability to explain humor as a social, contextual phenomenon rather than a simple binary label. Their refined prompting method yields more accurate and robust explanations, building a sizable resource of humor reasoning data. This work lays groundwork for future research into AI systems that more deeply understand humor as social behavior, beyond simple joke detection.

title: PALoRA: Projection-Adaptive LoRA for Preserving Reasoning in Large Language Models
summary: Efficiently updating Large Language Models (LLMs) with new or evolving factual knowledge remains a central challenge, as even parameter-efficient adaptation can erode previously acquired reasoning abilities. This tension reflects a plasticity-stability dilemma: models must incorporate new knowledge while preserving skill-critical representations. In this work, we study this trade-off through the spectral structure of multilayer perceptron weight matrices. We show, both theoretically and empirically, that information essential for reasoning is not localized only in dominant singular directions, but is instead distributed across the singular spectrum. Motivated by this observation, we introduce PALoRA, a two-stage framework for knowledge injection with reduced interference. PALoRA first trains a Singular Value Fine-Tuning (SVF) expert on a reasoning dataset and uses its learned singular scaling vector as a frozen geometric probe to identify components that are critical for the target skill. It then performs factual knowledge injection with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) under a structural orthogonality constraint, ensuring that updates avoid the identified skill-relevant subspace. Across Llama 3.1 8B and Mistral 7B, and across mathematical, coding, and scientific reasoning benchmarks, PALoRA preserves on average 95% of the SVF expert's reasoning performance while maintaining competitive factual recall. It consistently improves skill retention over prior spectral Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods while adding less than 0.006% parameter overhead.

AI.tldr: PALoRA is a PEFT framework that injects new factual knowledge into LLMs while preserving reasoning skills by constraining LoRA updates to be orthogonal to singular directions deemed important for reasoning.
AI.motivation: Updating LLMs with new or changing factual knowledge often harms previously learned reasoning abilities, reflecting a plasticity-stability dilemma. Existing parameter-efficient fine-tuning approaches do not adequately protect skill-critical representations, especially because important reasoning information is spread across the whole singular spectrum of MLP weights, not only in top singular directions. The paper aims to preserve reasoning while still enabling effective factual knowledge injection.
AI.method: 1) Analyze the spectral structure (SVD) of MLP weight matrices and show that reasoning-critical information is distributed across singular values, not just dominant ones. 2) Propose PALoRA, a two-stage method: (a) train a Singular Value Fine-Tuning (SVF) expert on a reasoning dataset that learns a singular scaling vector; freeze this vector as a geometric probe to indicate skill-relevant directions; (b) inject factual knowledge using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) with a structural orthogonality constraint such that LoRA updates avoid the subspace marked as reasoning-critical by the SVF probe. This yields PEFT-style updates with minimal interference to reasoning representations.
AI.result: On Llama 3.1 8B and Mistral 7B, across mathematical, coding, and scientific reasoning benchmarks, PALoRA preserves about 95% of the SVF expert’s reasoning performance while achieving competitive factual recall. It outperforms previous spectral PEFT methods in skill retention and does so with very low additional parameter overhead (<0.006%). Empirical findings support the theoretical claim that reasoning information is distributed along the singular spectrum.
AI.conclusion: Spectral analysis reveals that reasoning-relevant information in LLM MLPs is broadly distributed across singular directions, so naively updating dominant modes risks harming reasoning skills. By first identifying a reasoning-critical subspace via SVF and then constraining LoRA-based factual updates to be orthogonal to it, PALoRA effectively mitigates the plasticity-stability trade-off, preserving most reasoning performance while enabling factual knowledge updates with negligible parameter cost and reduced interference compared to prior spectral PEFT techniques.

title: Locality Matters for Training-Free Audio Token Compression in Audio-Language Models
comment: Preprint. 8 pages main text, 10 pages total
summary: Audio-language models (ALMs) are increasingly used for audio captioning, question answering, and open-ended audio understanding, but their inference cost remains high when audio inputs are represented as long prefix-token sequences. These audio prefixes consume context budget, increase memory usage, and make deployment harder in resource-constrained or latency-sensitive settings. Existing training-free audio-token reduction methods mainly rely on fixed pooling or score-based pruning. Fixed pooling is content-agnostic, while score-based pruning can preserve isolated salient tokens but discard nearby acoustic context. We propose Local Temporal Bipartite Merging (LTBM), a training-free encoder-space compression method that merges similar nearby audio tokens under an explicit temporal window constraint. Beyond introducing LTBM, we use a controlled Global Merge variant to isolate whether temporal locality itself is a useful inductive bias for audio-token compression. Experiments on AudioCaps, Clotho, and MMAU with Qwen2-Audio show evidence of a task-dependent locality effect: locality-aware merging is more favorable for captioning at several compression settings, especially under stronger compression, while global matching is more competitive for multiple-choice audio understanding. A cross-backbone validation on Audio Flamingo 3 further supports the captioning-side advantage of locality-aware merging under moderate and aggressive compression.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Local Temporal Bipartite Merging (LTBM), a training-free method to compress audio token sequences for audio-language models by merging similar nearby tokens within a temporal window, reducing inference cost while maintaining performance, with varying benefits across tasks.
AI.motivation: Audio-language models require long sequences of audio prefix tokens, which consume context budget, increase memory, and complicate deployment in resource- or latency-constrained environments. Existing training-free compression methods either pool tokens in a content-agnostic way or prune based on saliency scores, often losing important local acoustic context. There is a need for a compression approach that is both training-free and content- and locality-aware, and to understand whether temporal locality is an effective inductive bias for compressing audio tokens.
AI.method: The authors propose Local Temporal Bipartite Merging (LTBM), a training-free compression method operating in encoder space that merges similar audio tokens that are close in time, enforced by an explicit temporal window constraint. They also design a controlled Global Merge variant, which removes the temporal window and allows globally matching similar tokens, to disentangle the effect of locality as an inductive bias. The methods are evaluated by inserting them into the inference pipeline of Qwen2-Audio and testing performance under different compression ratios and settings.
AI.result: On AudioCaps, Clotho, and MMAU using Qwen2-Audio, results show that locality-aware merging via LTBM tends to be better suited for captioning tasks, particularly when using stronger compression levels, whereas the global matching variant is more effective for multiple-choice audio understanding tasks. Additional experiments with Audio Flamingo 3 on AudioCaps further validate that, for captioning, locality-aware merging maintains better performance than global merging under both moderate and aggressive compression regimes.
AI.conclusion: LTBM offers a training-free, locality-aware strategy to compress audio token sequences for audio-language models, effectively reducing inference cost while preserving task performance. Temporal locality emerges as a useful inductive bias for captioning tasks, while global token matching can be preferable for discriminative, multiple-choice audio understanding. These findings highlight that optimal compression strategies for ALMs are task-dependent and that locality-aware merging is particularly advantageous for generative audio captioning under substantial compression.

title: Jailbreak to Protect: Buffering and Reinforcing via Temporary Jailbreaking for Safe Fine-Tuning in Large Language Models
