summary: Fine-tuning-as-a-Service (FaaS) enables personalization of large language models (LLMs), but it can weaken safety-alignment under harmful fine-tuning attacks. Recent work has shown that activating harmful-behavior modules during fine-tuning can prevent models from learning undesired behaviors, but its mechanism remains unclear. In this paper, we revisit temporary jailbreaking as a defense against harmful fine-tuning and provide a gradient-level analysis showing that it saturates safety-degrading gradients while preserving benign task-relevant gradients. Based on this insight, we propose a Buffer-and-Reinforce fine-tuning framework that buffers harmful updates during user fine-tuning and reinforces safety after adaptation. Specifically, BufferLoRA induces temporary jailbreaking as a removable adapter to reduce harmful updates during user fine-tuning. After adaptation, ReinforceLoRA, trained to recover refusal behavior under the temporarily jailbroken state, is integrated with UserLoRA via QR decomposition-based merging to reinforce safety while preserving user-task performance. Extensive experiments show that our framework achieves superior safety and utility with no additional safety data during user fine-tuning and minimal computational cost.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes and defends against harmful fine-tuning attacks on LLMs by using temporary jailbreaking and a new Buffer-and-Reinforce framework (BufferLoRA + ReinforceLoRA) that preserves task utility while maintaining safety, without extra safety data.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning services let users personalize LLMs, but malicious users can exploit this to weaken safety alignment (e.g., remove refusals, induce harmful behavior). Some recent defenses use temporary jailbreaking—activating harmful-behavior modules during fine-tuning—to block learning of harmful behaviors, but their underlying mechanism is not well understood. The authors aim to formally analyze how this defense works at the gradient level and to design a practical, low-cost fine-tuning procedure that automatically and robustly maintains safety while still adapting to user tasks, without requiring additional safety datasets during user fine-tuning.
AI.method: 1) Provide a gradient-level analysis of temporary jailbreaking, showing that it saturates (clips or flattens) safety-degrading gradients while keeping benign, task-relevant gradients learnable. 2) Propose the Buffer-and-Reinforce framework consisting of: (a) BufferLoRA, a removable LoRA adapter that intentionally induces a temporary jailbroken state during user fine-tuning, buffering or dampening harmful updates so the underlying model doesn’t internalize them; and (b) ReinforceLoRA, another LoRA module trained to restore/refuse harmful outputs when the model is in the jailbroken state. 3) After user adaptation, integrate UserLoRA and ReinforceLoRA via a QR decomposition-based merging procedure to reinforce safety while preserving the task-specific performance. 4) Evaluate this framework empirically under harmful fine-tuning attacks, measuring both safety (refusal behavior) and utility (task performance), as well as computational overhead.
AI.result: The analysis shows that temporary jailbreaking effectively saturates gradients that would otherwise degrade safety while keeping gradients for benign tasks active, explaining why it blocks harmful fine-tuning without preventing useful adaptation. Empirically, the Buffer-and-Reinforce framework with BufferLoRA and ReinforceLoRA outperforms prior methods on safety metrics while maintaining or improving utility on user tasks. It achieves this without requiring any additional safety data during the user’s fine-tuning phase and incurs only minimal extra computational cost, making it practical for FaaS settings.
AI.conclusion: Temporary jailbreaking is an effective defense because it alters the gradient landscape so that safety-degrading directions are saturated while benign directions remain trainable. Building on this insight, the proposed Buffer-and-Reinforce framework (BufferLoRA + ReinforceLoRA + QR-based merging) offers a practical way to harden FaaS against harmful fine-tuning: it buffers harmful updates during adaptation and then reinforces safety afterward, achieving strong safety-utility trade-offs with little overhead and no extra safety data during user fine-tuning.

title: Knowledge Graph-Driven Expert-Level Reasoning for Neuroscience
summary: Knowledge graph (KG) is an abstraction that can be extracted from text corpora and used for in-depth reasoning. Prior work has leveraged KGs to fine-tune language models (LMs), enabling domain-specific superintelligence. In this work, we explore whether KG-driven in-depth reasoning capabilities can emerge in neuroscience using only information contained within a single authoritative textbook. The central hypothesis is that structured knowledge, when distilled into a high-quality KG and converted into KG-grounded question-answer (QA) supervision, is sufficient to produce expert-level reasoning through a fine-tuned LM that surpasses large language models (LLMs) in accuracy, while employing orders of magnitude fewer parameters. We construct a textbook-derived KG via a dual-LLM validation pipeline, expand it with a masked LM trained on the KG topology, generate multi-hop QA items, which include QA pairs and reasoning traces, to fine-tune an LM exclusively on KG-derived supervision, and apply reinforcement learning using path-derived KG signals as implicit reward models. Our results demonstrate that deep, mechanistic neuroscience understanding can be induced in the model without reliance on large, heterogeneous web-scale corpora. The KG-based synthetic neuroscience curriculum that readers can quiz themselves on, and the fine-tuned LM, are available at the following GitHub location: https://kg-bottom-up-superintelligence.github.io/neuro-bench.

AI.tldr: They build a neuroscience knowledge graph from a single textbook, generate KG-grounded questions and reasoning traces, and fine-tune a smaller language model that achieves expert-level mechanistic reasoning, rivaling or surpassing larger general LLMs, without web-scale data.
AI.motivation: Large language models can show impressive reasoning but usually rely on massive heterogeneous web-scale training data and many parameters. For specialized domains like neuroscience, we would like models that can reason deeply and mechanistically using only authoritative, curated sources and far fewer parameters. The authors want to test whether structured knowledge extracted from a single high-quality neuroscience textbook, represented as a knowledge graph and turned into a synthetic curriculum, is enough to induce expert-level reasoning that outperforms general LLMs in that domain.
AI.method:
1) Extract a knowledge graph from a neuroscience textbook using a dual-LLM validation pipeline to improve KG quality.
2) Expand the KG using a masked language model trained on the KG topology.
3) Generate multi-hop, KG-grounded QA items, including both question-answer pairs and explicit reasoning traces, entirely from the KG.
4) Fine-tune a language model only on this KG-derived QA supervision.
5) Apply reinforcement learning where paths in the KG provide implicit reward signals to shape the model’s reasoning.
6) Evaluate whether the resulting model shows deep, mechanistic understanding of neuroscience, especially in comparison with much larger general-purpose LLMs.
7) Release the KG-based synthetic curriculum and the fine-tuned model via GitHub for reproducibility and use by others.
AI.result: The fine-tuned model, trained purely on KG-derived supervision from a single neuroscience textbook, demonstrates strong mechanistic reasoning in neuroscience tasks. It can perform in-depth, multi-hop reasoning and attains expert-level accuracy that surpasses or rivals much larger, general-purpose LLMs, despite using far fewer parameters and no web-scale data. This empirically supports the hypothesis that high-quality structured knowledge and KG-grounded supervision can substitute for massive, unstructured corpora in specialized domains.
AI.conclusion: Structured, textbook-based knowledge graphs, combined with KG-grounded QA generation and reinforcement learning from KG paths, are sufficient to induce deep, mechanistic reasoning in a compact language model. This approach shows that domain-specific superintelligence in neuroscience can emerge from a single, authoritative source rather than from large, heterogeneous web-scale datasets, suggesting a scalable path to expert models across many scientific fields. The authors provide both the KG-based neuroscience curriculum and the resulting model as open resources.

title: Summoning the Oracle to Slay It: Mitigating Look-Ahead Bias in Financial Backtesting with Large Language Models
summary: Backtesting large language models (LLMs) on historical financial data is unreliable because pre-training cuts off after the events happened. An LLM trained in 2024 already "knows" which way 2018-2020 stocks moved. We name this failure parametric look-ahead bias and propose FinCAD, an inference-time adaptation of Context-Aware Decoding that suppresses an LLM's memory of historical outcomes without retraining. FinCAD pairs an adversarial bias-discovery pipeline that learns a model-specific memory-activating prior prompt with an entity- and date-adaptive rule that scales the CAD strength to per-(entity, date) memorisation, so the penalty fires on memorised in-sample dates and decays to zero out-of-sample. Across five 7-14B LLMs and five mega-cap equities, FinCAD cuts in-sample backtest returns by up to -67.1% on memorised dates while leaving 2025 out-of-sample returns within $8K and Sharpe within 0.10 of baseline, and preserves general-purpose reasoning within 1.7 pts. On an eleven-model leaderboard, it raises the in-sample / out-of-sample Spearman correlation from +0.779 to +0.846, recovering rankings that genuinely predict out-of-sample performance.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FinCAD, an inference-time method to remove look-ahead bias when backtesting LLMs on historical financial data by suppressing memorized outcomes without retraining.
AI.motivation: Backtesting LLMs on historical financial data is flawed because modern LLMs have been trained on those past outcomes, creating parametric look-ahead bias: the model already knows which way assets moved. This yields unrealistically high in-sample performance and unreliable strategy rankings. There is a need for a practical, non-retraining method to neutralize this bias while preserving the model’s general capabilities.
AI.method: The authors define parametric look-ahead bias in LLMs for financial backtesting and propose FinCAD, an inference-time extension of Context-Aware Decoding (CAD). First, they build an adversarial bias-discovery pipeline that searches for a model-specific "memory-activating" prior prompt that maximally elicits memorized historical outcomes. Then they design an entity- and date-adaptive rule that scales the CAD penalty for each (asset, date) pair, so that decoding is heavily penalized when the model is likely to be recalling memorized information (in-sample dates) and gradually relaxed for out-of-sample dates. FinCAD operates entirely at inference time and does not require model retraining.
AI.result: Applied to five LLMs of 7–14B parameters and five large-cap equities, FinCAD reduces in-sample backtest returns on memorized dates by up to 67.1%, indicating that much of the original performance was driven by look-ahead bias. At the same time, it keeps 2025 out-of-sample performance close to the baseline: returns differ by less than $8K and Sharpe ratios by less than 0.10, and general-purpose reasoning scores drop by at most 1.7 points. On a leaderboard of eleven models, FinCAD increases the Spearman correlation between in-sample and out-of-sample rankings from 0.779 to 0.846, thus improving the reliability of model-selection based on backtests.
AI.conclusion: FinCAD can substantially suppress parametric look-ahead bias in LLM-based financial backtests at inference time, without retraining or significantly harming out-of-sample performance or general reasoning abilities. By adaptively penalizing memorization by entity and date, it restores more realistic in-sample results and improves the alignment between in-sample and out-of-sample rankings, enabling more trustworthy evaluation and selection of LLMs for financial tasks.

title: By Their Fruits You Will Know Them: Comparing Formalizations of Law by the Decisions They Encode
comment: 23 pages, 17 figures, submitted to EMNLP PROC 2026
summary: Formalizing legal provisions promises machine-accessible law and automated legal reasoning, and recent LLMs make it tempting to generate such formalizations directly from statutory text. However, any formalization makes implicit interpretive choices whose consequences are hard to anticipate, especially if an LLM is the author. We present a method for systematically comparing different formalizations of the same legal provision by their inferences on individual cases. Given multiple formalizations of a provision, we match them at the node level, derive a shared interface for each pair from the matching, and use a SAT solver to enumerate the edge cases on which any two formalizations disagree. Selected edge cases are then verbalized into concrete factual scenarios that a legal expert can examine and act on. We apply our method to formalizations of ten EU provisions generated by nine frontier LLMs. We find that behavioral divergence between formalizations is essentially uncorrelated with their structural agreement and that the verbalized cases reveal qualitatively distinct types of disagreement, including divergences that mirror genuine controversies in the legal commentary.

AI.tldr: They propose a method to compare different logical formalizations of the same law by finding concrete test cases where the formalizations disagree, and show this on EU legal provisions formalized by multiple LLMs.
AI.motivation: Legal rules are increasingly being formalized for automated reasoning, and LLMs make it easy to auto-generate such formalizations. But each formalization embeds hidden interpretive choices, and it is hard to see how different formalizations behave in practice, especially those produced by opaque LLMs. The authors want a principled way to expose and compare these differences so that legal experts can understand and evaluate them.
AI.method: Given several formalizations of the same statutory provision, the authors: (1) match them at the node (subformula) level; (2) compute a shared interface (common set of inputs/variables) for each pair of formalizations; (3) use a SAT solver to systematically search for and enumerate "edge cases" (assignments of facts) on which two formalizations yield different outcomes; and (4) automatically verbalize selected edge cases into natural-language factual scenarios that legal experts can interpret and assess.
AI.result: They applied the method to ten EU legal provisions for which nine frontier LLMs produced formalizations. They observed that how often two formalizations behave differently (behavioral divergence) does not correlate well with how similar their structures are (structural agreement). The verbalized edge cases exposed several qualitatively different kinds of disagreement, some of which track real, debated issues in legal scholarship and commentary.
AI.conclusion: Structural similarity between formalizations is not a reliable proxy for similarity in legal consequences. Their pipeline—matching formalizations, using SAT to find disagreement cases, and verbalizing those cases—offers a systematic way to surface and analyze interpretive differences, including ones that reflect genuine legal controversies. This can help experts scrutinize LLM-generated legal formalizations and guide more responsible use of automated legal reasoning.

title: Associations between echocardiographic traits and AI-ECG predictions of heart failure
summary:
Artificial intelligence-enabled electrocardiography (AI-ECG) can detect heart failure (HF), including disease not captured by left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF), but the cardiac phenotypes underlying model predictions remain unclear. We therefore investigated whether AI-ECG-predicted HF risk aligns with established echocardiographic measures of myocardial dysfunction, remodelling, and filling pressures.
  We retrospectively analysed ECG and echocardiography data from 8147 patients who underwent both examinations within three days at Akershus University Hospital between 1 January 2023 and 1 June 2025. A previously validated AI-ECG model for HF detection was applied to all ECGs. Spearman's rank correlation $ρ$ quantified associations between echocardiographic parameters and AI-ECG risk. Subgroup analyses were performed by sex and left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF). External validation included 36,286 ECG-echocardiography pairs from Columbia University Irving Medical Center.
  Global longitudinal strain (GLS) showed the strongest correlation ($ρ$=0.57), followed by mitral annular plane systolic excursion (MAPSE) ($ρ$=-0.49) and LVEF ($ρ$=-0.45). In patients with LVEF>50%, correlations remained substantial for GLS, MAPSE, and diastolic-related parameters. Volumetric left ventricular indices correlated less strongly in women, whereas diastolic indices showed stronger correlations in women than in men.
  Physiological validation showed that AI-ECG HF risk predictions align primarily with measures of systolic function, particularly global longitudinal strain, while also capturing diastolic-related abnormalities in patients with preserved LVEF. This approach may improve clinical interpretability and identify opportunities for model refinement.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates how well an AI-based ECG heart failure risk score corresponds to detailed echocardiographic markers of cardiac dysfunction, showing the model primarily reflects systolic dysfunction (especially impaired global longitudinal strain) and also diastolic abnormalities in preserved ejection fraction.
AI.motivation: Although AI-ECG models can detect heart failure, including cases missed by standard left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF), clinicians do not yet understand which specific cardiac pathophysiological changes these models are sensing. Clarifying how AI-ECG risk relates to established echocardiographic measures of systolic and diastolic function, remodelling, and filling pressures is needed to improve trust, interpretation, and optimization of such models.
AI.method: Retrospective analysis of 8,147 patients from a single hospital who had ECG and echocardiography within three days. A previously validated AI-ECG model that outputs heart failure risk was applied to each ECG. Spearman rank correlations were computed between the AI risk score and multiple echocardiographic parameters, with subgroup analyses by sex and LVEF strata. External validation was performed using 36,286 ECG–echocardiography pairs from another academic center.
AI.result: The AI-ECG heart failure risk score correlated most strongly with measures of systolic function: global longitudinal strain (GLS, ρ=0.57), mitral annular plane systolic excursion (MAPSE, ρ=-0.49), and LVEF (ρ=-0.45). In patients with preserved LVEF (>50%), the model still showed notable correlations with GLS, MAPSE, and diastolic indices, indicating sensitivity to subtler dysfunction. Sex-based analyses showed weaker correlations with volumetric LV indices in women but stronger correlations with diastolic measures compared with men. Findings were reproduced in a large external dataset.
AI.conclusion: The AI-ECG model’s predicted heart failure risk predominantly reflects impaired systolic function, particularly reduced global longitudinal strain, while also recognizing diastolic abnormalities in patients with preserved LVEF. These physiologic correlations enhance clinical interpretability of the AI model and highlight specific areas—such as sex-related differences and diastolic function—where further refinement and tailoring of AI-ECG tools may be valuable.

title: GroupTravelBench: Benchmarking LLM Agents on Multi-Person Travel Planning
summary: Travel planning is a realistic task for evaluating the planning and tool-use abilities of LLM agents. However, existing benchmarks typically assume only a single user, thereby avoiding one of the most challenging aspects of real-world scenarios: an agent's ability to identify and resolve conflicts among multiple users. To address this gap, we introduce \textbf{GroupTravelBench}, the first benchmark for \textbf{multi-user, multi-turn} travel planning. Based on real user profiles, POI data, and ticket price data, we synthesize 650 tasks and divide them into three difficulty levels. Beyond standard abilities in single-user itinerary planning, such as multi-step reasoning and tool use, our benchmark further evaluates three key capabilities required for travel agents: \emph{(i) elicitation} -- proactively engaging in multi-turn dialogue to gather preferences from each user; \emph{(ii) coordination} -- resolving conflicts among users through compromise or subgrouping strategies; and \emph{(iii) planning} -- searching for travel plans that maximize overall group utility while maintaining fairness and feasibility. To simulate real-world conversational itinerary planning while enabling reliable tool use and offline evaluation, we build an interactive sandbox environment with cached real-world tool data. We evaluate a wide range of LLMs and find that even frontier models still show substantial weaknesses in preference coverage and group fairness. \textit{GroupTravelBench} provides a practical and reproducible benchmark for advancing research on LLM agents for real-world travel planning.

AI.tldr: They propose GroupTravelBench, a benchmark to test how well LLM agents plan trips for groups of users with conflicting preferences, showing current models struggle with fairness and preference coverage.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM travel-planning benchmarks only consider a single user, ignoring the complex but common real-world case where multiple users with different preferences must travel together. This leaves a gap in evaluating core abilities like conflict resolution, group preference aggregation, and fair planning among users.
AI.method: They construct GroupTravelBench, a synthetic but realistic benchmark of 650 multi-user, multi-turn travel-planning tasks at three difficulty levels, grounded in real user profiles, POI data, and ticket prices. The benchmark tests three capabilities: (i) elicitation via multi-turn dialogue to gather each user’s preferences, (ii) coordination to resolve conflicts via compromise or subgrouping, and (iii) planning to find itineraries that maximize group utility subject to fairness and feasibility. They also build an interactive sandbox with cached real-world tool data to simulate realistic tool use and enable offline, reproducible evaluation. Various LLMs are then systematically evaluated on this benchmark.
AI.result: Across a broad set of LLMs, including frontier models, performance remains limited: models inadequately cover group members’ preferences and often fail to maintain fairness across users, revealing significant gaps in multi-user travel-planning competence despite strong single-user planning abilities.
AI.conclusion: GroupTravelBench is introduced as the first dedicated benchmark for multi-user, multi-turn travel planning with LLM agents. It exposes current weaknesses in elicitation, coordination, and fair group planning, and offers a practical, reproducible testbed intended to drive future research on more capable, real-world-ready LLM travel agents.

title: HeartBeatAI: An Interpretable and Robust Deep Learning Framework for Multi-Label ECG Arrhythmia Detection
summary: While Deep Learning (DL) enhances automated electrocardiogram (ECG) analysis, clinical deployment is hindered by class imbalance and the generalization gap. This paper presents HeartBeatAI, a deep learning framework combining domain generalization, multi-scale feature aggregation, and clinical explainability for robust 12-lead ECG classification. Moving beyond image-based paradigms, HeartBeatAI integrates a Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) ResNet to isolate diagnostic leads alongside a Multi-Layer Concentration Pipeline to capture macro-rhythm and micro-morphological anomalies. To mitigate domain shift, the framework employs MixStyle regularization and Label Smoothing. Rigorous benchmarking across four large-scale datasets using intra-source and Leave-One-Domain-Out (LODO) protocols demonstrates high performance (98% Macro F1-score) under intra-source conditions. However, LODO evaluations reveal significant degradation in detecting rare anomalies, highlighting a persistent challenge in cross-institutional deployment.

AI.tldr: Proposes HeartBeatAI, a deep-learning framework for robust, explainable 12-lead ECG classification with improved domain generalization.
AI.motivation: Automated ECG interpretation with deep learning suffers from performance drops due to class imbalance and poor generalization across different clinical institutions and datasets, especially for rare cardiac anomalies.
AI.method: Designs HeartBeatAI, which (1) uses an SE-ResNet to focus on diagnostically important ECG leads, (2) introduces a Multi-Layer Concentration Pipeline to jointly capture macro-level rhythm patterns and fine-grained waveform morphology, and (3) applies MixStyle regularization and label smoothing to reduce domain shift and overconfidence. The model is evaluated on four large ECG datasets using intra-source and Leave-One-Domain-Out protocols.
AI.result: Achieves very high performance (98% Macro F1) when training and testing on the same data source (intra-source). However, under Leave-One-Domain-Out evaluation, performance degrades notably, especially for rare arrhythmias and anomalies, exposing limitations in cross-domain robustness.
AI.conclusion: HeartBeatAI is effective and explainable for ECG classification under matched training-testing conditions but still struggles with generalizing to unseen clinical domains, particularly for rare anomalies. This underscores that domain generalization in medical ECG AI remains an open problem despite advanced architectures and regularization techniques.

title: Clarification Is Not Enough: Post-Clarification Answering Remains the Bottleneck in Multi-Turn QA
summary: Pluralistic alignment requires systems to adapt to diverse user values, communication styles, and contextual assumptions. We believe that a foundational prerequisite for such alignment enabling accurate preference elicitation from people when their intent is under-specified or ambiguous. We study the problem of preference elicitation in multi-turn question answering by decomposing the problem into two components: a \textbf{clarification policy}, which decides whether to ask a clarifying question or answer directly, and \textbf{post-clarification answering}, which produces the correct final answer once the missing information is provided. We show, using the PACIFIC benchmark, that supervised fine-tuning rapidly improves the clarification policy, however, final answer accuracy remains substantially lower even when the model takes the correct action. This gap indicates that understanding and correctly interpreting the user's response is the critical gap in multi-turn question-answering systems.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how AI systems can better ask clarifying questions and interpret user responses to align with diverse user preferences in multi-turn QA.
AI.motivation: Pluralistic alignment needs systems that can handle diverse user values and ambiguous or under-specified intents. Current systems struggle when user queries are unclear, making accurate preference elicitation essential for reliable answers.
AI.method: The authors decompose multi-turn preference elicitation into two components: (1) a clarification policy that decides whether to ask a follow-up clarification or answer immediately, and (2) a post-clarification answering module that generates the final answer once missing information has been obtained. They evaluate these components using the PACIFIC benchmark and apply supervised fine-tuning to improve the clarification policy.
AI.result: Supervised fine-tuning significantly improves the model’s ability to choose the right action—i.e., deciding correctly when to ask a clarifying question versus answering directly. However, even when the model makes the correct clarification decision, its final answer accuracy remains significantly lower than desired.
AI.conclusion: The main bottleneck in multi-turn question answering is not only deciding when to clarify but correctly understanding and interpreting the user’s clarifying response. Future work should focus on enhancing models’ capabilities to process and reason over user feedback after clarification.

title: Learning to Reason Efficiently with A* Post-Training
summary: Many applications of large language models (LLMs) require deductive reasoning, yet models frequently produce incorrect or redundant inference steps. We frame natural language inference as a search problem where the final answer is the valid proof itself, requiring a reasoning procedure in which intermediate inferences are correct. Specifically, we investigate whether LLMs can learn to generate correct and efficient proofs with guidance from A* search -- an algorithm that guarantees an optimally efficient path to a goal. We explore two training techniques: supervised fine-tuning on execution traces from A* and reinforcement learning with A*-informed process reward models. Empirically, we find that Llama-3.2 models in the 1B--3B range benefit substantially from A* post training, going from near-zero accuracy to outperforming DeepSeek-V3.2 -- a much larger model. Our analysis uncovers a trade-off: while simple correctness rewards maximize accuracy, A*-informed signals strike a balance between accuracy and efficiency. Furthermore, we find that on larger search spaces, models trained with imperfect heuristics exhibit superior accuracy. Our results demonstrate a promising direction towards reasoning guided by principles derived from classical search algorithms.

AI.tldr: The paper trains small Llama-3.2 models to perform deductive reasoning by viewing proof generation as a search problem and using A* search to guide learning, leading to large gains in proof accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Large language models are widely used in applications that require deductive reasoning, but they often generate incorrect or redundant intermediate inference steps, even when the final answers may be right. This limits their reliability in settings where a full, correct proof is required (e.g., math or logic problems). The authors aim to build models whose reasoning traces are themselves valid proofs, not just plausible explanations, and they seek a principled way—grounded in classical search algorithms—to guide models toward correct and efficient reasoning paths.
AI.method: The authors reframe natural language inference as a search problem where the output is a complete valid proof. They employ A* search, which finds an optimally efficient path to a goal given a heuristic, to generate good proof trajectories and to define training signals. They study two training approaches for Llama‑3.2 models (1B–3B parameters): (1) supervised fine‑tuning on A* execution traces, so the model imitates optimal or near‑optimal proofs; and (2) reinforcement learning where the reward is provided by process reward models informed by A* (A*‑informed PRMs), encouraging intermediate steps that align with efficient search paths. They also experiment with different reward designs (pure correctness vs A*‑shaped rewards) and with imperfect heuristics to analyze trade‑offs between accuracy and efficiency across search space sizes.
AI.result: After A*‑based post‑training, small Llama‑3.2 models that initially had near‑zero proof accuracy achieve large performance gains, surpassing DeepSeek‑V3.2, a substantially larger model, on the evaluated proof tasks. The experiments reveal that reward functions purely focused on step correctness yield the highest raw accuracy but can lead to less efficient proofs. In contrast, A*‑informed reward signals produce models that maintain high accuracy while generating more efficient proofs. Additionally, on larger and more complex search spaces, models trained using imperfect A* heuristics can actually generalize better and achieve higher accuracy than those trained with simpler signals, indicating robustness to heuristic noise.
AI.conclusion: Treating natural language proof generation as an A*‑guided search problem enables small LLMs to learn to produce correct and efficient proofs, dramatically improving their deductive reasoning capabilities. The study shows that classical search principles can provide informative training signals for modern LLMs, with A*‑informed process rewards offering a practical balance between correctness and efficiency. The finding that imperfect heuristics can still enhance performance suggests that exact optimality is not required for these benefits, indicating a scalable and promising direction for developing more reliable reasoning LLMs.

title: They Are Not the Same: Direct Causes Are Not Grounded Emotion Explanations
summary: Emotion-Cause Pair Extraction (ECPE) was introduced to explain why an emotion occurs, but this goal is now often reduced to binary pair/non-pair prediction. This proxy is useful for direct-cause extraction, yet easy to over-read as evidence grounded emotion explanation. We show that this interpretation is only partially valid. In IEMO-MECP, 90.9% of original positives remain emo-cause and 95.0% of original negatives remain non-pair, confirming that the binary ECPE task is largely preserved. The problem is that direct triggers alone do not constitute a grounded explanation. Emo-context, an utterance that helps interpret a target emotion without directly causing it, appears on both sides of the original boundary and is enriched near binary uncertainty, showing that the binary boundary has no stable place for such discourse evidence. Across evaluated ECPE models, direct triggers are recovered more reliably than contextual support. Under shortcut pressure, this imbalance becomes consequential. Binary-trained models assign higher pair scores to nearby lexically similar non-pair candidates than to evidence supported but structurally harder emo-cause and emo-context pairs. Thus, pair scores can reward convenient attributions over grounded explanations. High binary ECPE performance indicates that a model can identify direct triggers; it does not indicate that the model has explained the emotion. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/panzhzh/ECPExsame.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that current Emotion-Cause Pair Extraction (ECPE) setups mostly test detection of direct emotion triggers, not genuine, grounded explanations of emotions, and shows empirically that high binary ECPE scores should not be over-interpreted as explanation ability.
AI.motivation: ECPE was proposed to discover why emotions occur, but in practice it has been operationalized as a binary classification task: deciding whether an emotion clause and a candidate cause clause form a pair. Many works implicitly treat good performance on this task as evidence that models can provide grounded explanations for emotions. The authors suspect this is an over-interpretation: binary pair detection may capture only direct emotion triggers and ignore broader contextual evidence (emo-context) that supports understanding an emotion without directly causing it. They want to clarify what ECPE actually measures, to examine the role of contextual utterances, and to test how models behave under shortcut pressures and lexical similarity confounds.
AI.method: 1) They introduce/annotate a setting called IEMO-MECP, where existing ECPE data are re-examined to distinguish direct emotion causes (triggers) from contextual but non-causal utterances (emo-context). They measure how many original positive and negative pairs keep their labels when this richer interpretation is applied, to see how faithful the binary task is to emotion-cause relations. 2) They analyze where emo-context utterances fall relative to the original positive/negative boundary and to regions of model uncertainty, to see whether the binary task has a stable way to encode contextual evidence. 3) They systematically evaluate existing ECPE models, comparing their ability to recover direct triggers versus emo-context relations. 4) They simulate shortcut pressure: situations where lexically similar but structurally wrong candidates compete with structurally correct but more complex emo-cause or emo-context pairs, and inspect model pair scores in these setups.
AI.result: In the IEMO-MECP analysis, 90.9% of original positive pairs still qualify as emotion-cause and 95.0% of original negatives remain non-pairs, showing that the standard binary ECPE labeling largely captures direct emotion causes correctly. However, they find that emo-context utterances — which help interpret an emotion but are not direct causes — appear on both sides of the original positive/negative boundary and are concentrated near regions of binary uncertainty. This indicates that the binary decision has no robust representation for such contextual evidence. Across multiple ECPE models, direct triggers are consistently recovered more reliably than contextual support relations. Under shortcut conditions, models assign higher pair scores to nearby, lexically similar but actually non-pair candidates than to structurally valid but harder emo-cause or emo-context pairs that are backed by discourse evidence. This shows that models are prone to taking lexical shortcuts instead of using deeper contextual reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that binary ECPE performance primarily reflects a model’s capacity to find direct emotion triggers, not its ability to produce grounded explanations for emotions. Direct triggers alone do not suffice as full explanations, and the binary ECPE formulation provides no stable treatment of contextual interpretive evidence (emo-context). Because current models favor lexically convenient but structurally incorrect pairs over evidence-supported yet complex ones, pair scores can reward spurious or shortcut-based attributions rather than genuine explanatory reasoning. Therefore, high scores on binary ECPE tasks should not be taken as proof that a model explains emotions; evaluation frameworks must be redesigned if grounded emotional explanation is the true goal.

title: Hera: Learning Long-Horizon Coordination for Device-Cloud Collaborative LLM Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents excel at solving complex long-horizon tasks through autonomous interaction with environments. However, their real-world deployment faces a fundamental device--cloud dilemma: on-device models are efficient but often brittle, while cloud models are stronger but costly in computation. State-of-the-art LLM device--cloud routers usually make coarse task-level decisions, which cannot adapt to the changing difficulty of multi-step agent interactions. To address this issue, we present Hera, a step-level device--cloud LLM agent coordinator for long-horizon tasks achieving a strong performance--cost Pareto frontier. Hera adopts a novel two-stage training paradigm: (1) imitation learning for cold-start, followed by (2) reinforcement learning that jointly optimizes task success and cloud usage efficiency. The first stage casts step-level routing as a supervised classification problem: the device agent is replayed on cloud trajectories, with each state labeled by the agreement between device and cloud actions. In the second stage, we perform cost-aware reinforcement learning by grouping identical states across trajectories and updating Hera with labels favoring higher expected return and fewer future cloud calls. We evaluate Hera on ALFWorld, WebShop, and AppWorld, where it consistently outperforms prior methods, achieving 92.5% of the cloud-only success rate with cloud use in only 46.3% of steps.

AI.tldr: Proposes Hera, a step-level router that decides when to use on-device vs. cloud LLMs for long-horizon agent tasks, achieving near-cloud performance with much lower cloud usage.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM agents face a trade-off between efficient but weaker on-device models and stronger but expensive cloud models. Current device–cloud routers decide at coarse task-level granularity and cannot adapt to varying step-wise difficulty within long-horizon interactions, leading to suboptimal performance–cost trade-offs.
AI.method: Introduce Hera, a step-level coordinator trained in two stages. Stage 1: imitation learning, formulating routing as supervised classification by replaying device agents on cloud trajectories and labeling states based on device–cloud action agreement. Stage 2: cost-aware reinforcement learning, where states across trajectories are grouped, and routing is updated using labels that favor higher expected return and fewer future cloud calls, jointly optimizing task success and cloud efficiency.
AI.result: On three benchmarks—ALFWorld, WebShop, and AppWorld—Hera surpasses existing routing methods, reaching 92.5% of the success rate of a cloud-only agent while requiring cloud usage in only 46.3% of decision steps.
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained, step-level device–cloud coordination, trained via imitation plus cost-aware reinforcement learning, can substantially improve the performance–cost Pareto frontier for LLM agents on long-horizon tasks compared with prior coarse task-level routing strategies.

title: From Automation to Collaboration: Human-in-the-Loop Methods for Safe and Trustworthy NLP
summary: Large language models are widely deployed in high-stakes NLP tasks, yet risks such as bias, hallucination, adversarial vulnerability and unreliable generalization remain. Probe-based auditing reveals inconsistencies in model behavior. Adversarial text generation uncovers robustness gaps, especially in lower-resourced languages with limited benchmarks. Enterprise text-to-SQL settings expose the difficulty of validating outputs over private and large-scale databases. Human supervision is essential for probe validation, adversarial verification and domain-specific annotation, but it is costly and hard to scale. This survey examines recent human-in-the-loop methods that shift NLP from automation toward collaboration for safety and trustworthiness. We review how human expertise supports auditing, robustness evaluation, data construction and model steering. Our findings highlight gaps in scalable probing, sustainable robustness benchmarks, low-resource settings and governance of private systems. We outline practical research directions for adaptive auditing, collaborative evaluation and accountable deployment.

AI.tldr: A survey of human-in-the-loop methods to make NLP and large language models safer and more trustworthy.
AI.motivation: Large language models are increasingly used in high-stakes applications but still suffer from bias, hallucination, adversarial vulnerability, and unreliable generalization, especially in low-resource and enterprise settings. Existing automatic auditing and evaluation methods are insufficient, and human supervision—though necessary—is expensive and hard to scale. There is a need to systematically understand how humans can effectively and efficiently collaborate with models to improve safety and trustworthiness.
AI.method: The paper conducts a survey of recent human-in-the-loop approaches in NLP. It categorizes and analyzes methods where human experts are involved in four main areas: (1) probing and auditing model behavior, (2) robustness evaluation via adversarial and stress testing, (3) data construction and annotation, including for low-resource scenarios and private domains, and (4) model steering and control. It compares different designs of collaboration between humans and models and evaluates their scalability and applicability.
AI.result: The survey synthesizes how current human-in-the-loop techniques are applied across auditing, robustness evaluation, data creation, and model steering. It identifies key limitations, such as lack of scalable probing frameworks, limited and unsustainable robustness benchmarks, insufficient support for low-resource languages, and challenges in governing systems that operate over private and large-scale databases. It systematizes existing solutions and exposes where they fall short.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that shifting NLP from full automation to human–model collaboration is crucial for safety and trustworthiness. It highlights open gaps in scalable and adaptive auditing, long-term robustness evaluation, support for low-resource settings, and governance of private, enterprise systems. The authors propose practical research directions, emphasizing adaptive auditing pipelines, collaborative human–AI evaluation workflows, and mechanisms for accountable deployment of language technologies.

title: Agent-as-Peer-Debriefer: A Multi-Agent Framework with Perspective-Based Refinement for Qualitative Analysis
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for qualitative data analysis (QDA), yet their outputs often miss the depth and nuance of human analysis. We argue this gap reflects a missing credibility practice from human QDA: peer debriefing, in which an analyst seeks feedback from a disinterested peer and uses it to refine their coding. To bring this practice into LLM-assisted QDA, we propose Agent-as-Peer-Debriefer, a multi-agent QDA framework that builds peer debriefing into key coding steps. In our framework, a Hierarchical Coding Agent follows the standard QDA process to generate codes, sub-themes, and themes, along with self-explanations and reflection memos. It then shares these outputs with three Peer-Debriefing Agents, each applying a distinct analytical perspective (Theory-Driven, Data-Driven, or Applied) and refining the codes by keeping, renaming, reassigning, merging, or splitting them. These perspectives are drawn from established human QDA practices that generalize across domains and datasets. To evaluate the framework, we test it on three datasets across two domains with three LLMs, measuring semantic similarity to human-annotated codes. Across all settings, perspective-based, peer-debriefing refinement aligns more closely with human codes than a single-LLM baseline, and an ablation further shows the gain is not merely from additional refinement. The three perspectives also produce distinct trade-offs, showing that the choice of perspective is a meaningful and controllable design decision. More broadly, these findings suggest that simulating peer debriefing with explicit perspectives is a promising route to more credible LLM-assisted QDA.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a multi-agent LLM framework that mimics human peer debriefing in qualitative data analysis to produce codes closer to human annotations.
AI.motivation: LLMs are being used for qualitative data analysis but tend to lack the depth, nuance, and credibility practices, such as peer debriefing, that human analysts use to ensure high-quality coding. The authors want to close this gap and make LLM-assisted QDA more trustworthy and aligned with established qualitative methods.
AI.method: They design Agent-as-Peer-Debriefer, a multi-agent framework where a Hierarchical Coding Agent first performs standard QDA (generating codes, sub-themes, themes, explanations, and memos). Then three Peer-Debriefing Agents, each embodying a different analytical perspective (Theory-Driven, Data-Driven, Applied), critique and refine the codes by operations like keeping, renaming, reassigning, merging, and splitting. The system is evaluated on three datasets in two domains and with three different LLMs.
AI.result: Across all tested datasets, domains, and LLMs, the peer-debriefing multi-agent setup produces codes that are more semantically similar to human-annotated codes than a single-LLM baseline. An ablation indicates that the benefit comes specifically from the perspective-based peer debriefing, not just from doing more refinement steps. Each analytical perspective also yields different performance trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: Embedding simulated peer debriefing via multiple, explicitly-perspectived LLM agents improves the credibility and human-alignment of LLM-assisted qualitative data analysis. The choice of analytical perspective is an important design lever, and peer-debriefing-style frameworks are a promising direction for integrating LLMs with established qualitative research practices.

title: JudgmentBench: Comparing Rubric and Preference Evaluation for Quality Assessment
summary: Two methodologies dominate current practices of benchmarking: rubric-based scoring evaluates items against predefined criteria, whereas comparative judgment elicits pairwise preferences between outputs. Although both methodologies are widely used, the choice between them is rarely justified. We release JudgmentBench, a benchmark of 30 real-world legal tasks, paired with 1,539 rubric scores and 1,530 pairwise preference judgments collected from practicing attorneys--including at major U.S. law firms--with substantial experience. The annotations constitute the first publicly available dataset in a high-expertise domain in which both supervision signals are elicited from the same experts on the same items. Using LLM-generated outputs at three constructed quality levels, we provide an initial empirical comparison: comparative judgments recover the intended quality ordering substantially better than rubrics (mean Spearman's rank correlation of 0.908 vs. 0.150, estimated difference = 0.758 [0.494, 1.021]) while requiring less than half the annotation time. The patterns hold for human annotators and LLM autograders. Beyond this initial comparison, the paired structure of the dataset supports a broader research agenda on how expert judgment should be elicited, aggregated, and used as supervision in domains without verifiable ground truth.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces JudgmentBench, a legal-domain benchmark that directly compares rubric-based scoring with pairwise comparative judgment, finding that pairwise judgments track true quality much better and more efficiently than rubrics.
AI.motivation: Benchmarking complex outputs (like legal writing) usually relies on either rubrics or pairwise comparisons, but the choice is rarely justified with data, especially in high-expertise domains where ground truth is unclear. There is a need for empirical evidence on which supervision signal better captures expert quality assessments and how to elicit expert judgment efficiently.
AI.method: The authors construct JudgmentBench, comprising 30 real-world legal tasks and LLM-generated responses at three controlled quality levels. Practicing attorneys provide both rubric-based scores and pairwise preference judgments on the same items. The authors then compare how well each supervision method recovers the known intended quality ordering using rank correlation metrics, and they measure annotation time. They also evaluate both human annotations and LLM-based autograding models.
AI.result: Comparative judgment substantially outperforms rubric-based scoring in recovering the intended quality ordering, with a mean Spearman rank correlation of 0.908 for pairwise judgments versus 0.150 for rubrics. The estimated correlation difference is 0.758 with a 95% interval of [0.494, 1.021]. Comparative judgments also require less than half the annotation time. These trends hold for both human experts and LLM autograders.
AI.conclusion: Pairwise comparative judgment is a more accurate and efficient way to capture expert assessments of complex legal outputs than rubric-based scoring, at least in this benchmark setup. The JudgmentBench dataset, with paired rubric and comparative labels, enables systematic research on eliciting and aggregating expert judgments and on designing supervision signals when there is no verifiable ground truth.

title: Lattice theory and algebraic models for deep convolutional learning based on mathematical morphology
summary:
We develop a rigorous algebraic framework for deep convolutional architectures, CNNs, ResNets, and encoder--decoder networks such as UNet, grounded in lattice theory and mathematical morphology. The central tool is the Matheron--Maragos--Banon--Barrera (MMBB) universal representation theory for translation-invariant operators, which we apply systematically to every layer of a standard deep network.
  The principal finding is that the standard CNN pipeline (linear convolution~$+$ ReLU~$+$ flat max-pooling) is a cross-lattice operator: the convolution is an erosion in the Fourier inf-semilattice while ReLU is a lattice-join closing and max-pooling is a dilation in the pointwise max-plus lattice, and their composition is a morphological opening in neither. A second finding is that the upper adjoint of ReLU in the pointwise lattice is a global (non-local) operator, the identity on globally non-negative functions and $-\infty$ otherwise, so no local morphological erosion can form an adjunction pair with ReLU. These two results together provide the precise algebraic reason why depth in standard CNNs introduces genuine representational power: the composed layer is not idempotent.
  Three layer designs that are genuine idempotent openings are identified and fully characterised: the pure max-plus morphological layer (pointwise lattice), the spectral Wiener layer (Fourier lattice), and the self-dual morphological layer. We establish a complete fixed-point and convergence theory.
  The framework also unifies max-pooling, strided convolution, and the Laplacian pyramid under the Goutsias--Heijmans adjoint pyramid theory, and gives the Activation--Pooling Dilation (APD) factorisation with its correct adjoint.

AI.tldr: Algebraic and morphological theory for CNN-type architectures; standard CNN layers are cross-lattice, non-idempotent operators, explaining depth’s added expressivity, and yielding alternative idempotent layer designs with convergence theory.
AI.motivation: To provide a rigorous, algebraic explanation of how and why deep convolutional networks (CNNs, ResNets, UNets) gain representational power from depth, using lattice theory and mathematical morphology, and to unify various architectural components (convolution, ReLU, pooling, pyramids) in a single operator-theoretic framework.
AI.method: Use the MMBB universal representation theory for translation-invariant operators and lattice-theoretic / morphological concepts (erosions, dilations, openings, adjunctions) to analyze each layer type in standard deep networks. Characterize convolution, ReLU, and pooling as operators on different lattices (Fourier inf-semilattice, pointwise max-plus lattice), study their adjoints and idempotence, and design alternative layers that form genuine morphological openings with full fixed-point and convergence analysis.
AI.result: (1) Show that the usual CNN block (convolution + ReLU + max-pooling) is a cross-lattice operator: convolution is an erosion in the Fourier lattice, ReLU a closing, and pooling a dilation in a different lattice, so their composition is not a proper morphological opening and is non-idempotent. (2) Prove that ReLU’s upper adjoint in the pointwise lattice is a global, non-local operator, implying no local erosion can be its adjoint. (3) Identify and fully characterize three idempotent opening layer designs: pure max-plus morphological, spectral Wiener, and self-dual morphological layers. (4) Show that max-pooling, strided convolution, and Laplacian pyramids can all be unified via adjoint pyramid theory, providing an APD (Activation–Pooling Dilation) factorization with its correct adjoint.
AI.conclusion: Depth in standard CNNs yields extra representational power because the usual layer composition is non-idempotent and cross-lattice, not a morphological opening. A lattice-theoretic framework clarifies the operator structure of deep architectures, offers principled idempotent layer alternatives with convergence guarantees, and unifies several architectural constructs (pooling, striding, pyramids) under adjoint pyramid theory and APD factorization.

title: Inference Time Optimization with Confidence Dynamics
summary: Inference time optimization techniques, such as repeated sampling, have significantly advanced the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, the critical role of model uncertainty remains largely underexplored in these optimization strategies. In this paper, we investigate the dynamics of confidence along reasoning trajectories and for first time reveal a surprising and unique pattern: correct answer traces tend to exhibit confidence improvement over time (positive confidence gain), while incorrect traces show attenuated or declining confidence as reasoning proceeds. Based on this observation, we propose Confidence Dynamic Gain (CDG) based voting, which incorporates how the confidence trajectory of the response evolves along the reasoning chain. Experiments across four open-source architectures (DeepSeek-R1, gpt-oss, Gemma-3, Qwen-QwQ) on the AIME24/25, HMMT25, and BRUMO25 benchmarks demonstrate that CDG yields a significant performance boost over baselines. These results demonstrate that our method provides a robust discriminative signal for improving answer selection in LLM reasoning. We also provide theoretical insights for this phenomenon. Code will be released at https://github.com/Accenture/CDG.git.

AI.tldr: The paper discovers that correct reasoning paths in LLMs tend to gain confidence over time, while incorrect ones lose or flatten in confidence, and introduces a Confidence Dynamic Gain (CDG) voting method that exploits this pattern to improve answer selection.
AI.motivation: Inference-time techniques like repeated sampling have improved LLM reasoning, but they typically ignore how the model’s confidence evolves during multi-step reasoning. There is a gap in understanding and using uncertainty dynamics along a reasoning chain to better distinguish correct from incorrect answers.
AI.method: The authors empirically analyze confidence trajectories along reasoning traces produced by several open-source LLMs on math/competition-style benchmarks, identifying a consistent pattern of confidence change for correct vs. incorrect traces. Leveraging this, they design Confidence Dynamic Gain (CDG) based voting, which scores candidate answers not just by final confidence but by the improvement (or decline) of confidence over the reasoning steps, and then selects answers based on this CDG-informed aggregation. They also provide theoretical analysis to explain why such a pattern might arise.
AI.result: Across four open-source LLM architectures (DeepSeek-R1, gpt-oss, Gemma-3, Qwen-QwQ) evaluated on AIME24/25, HMMT25, and BRUMO25 benchmarks, CDG-based voting significantly outperforms baseline answer selection methods that do not exploit confidence dynamics. The experiments indicate that CDG offers a strong and robust signal for discriminating correct from incorrect reasoning traces.
AI.conclusion: Confidence dynamics along reasoning trajectories contain valuable information: correct traces typically show positive confidence gain, whereas incorrect ones exhibit stagnating or decreasing confidence. Exploiting this via Confidence Dynamic Gain (CDG) based voting improves inference-time answer selection across multiple models and benchmarks, and the provided theoretical insights support the generality of this effect.

title: GlobalDentBench: A Multinational Benchmark for Evaluating LLM Clinical Reasoning in Dentistry with Expert Calibration
summary: While large language models (LLMs) hold transformative potential for medicine, their reasoning robustness and safety in real-world clinical scenarios remain critically underexplored, particularly in dentistry. Here we introduce GlobalDentBench, the first multinational dental benchmark, featuring a taxonomy that encompasses 14 dental specialties across 88 countries and regions spanning six continents. The benchmark comprises 8,978 expert-validated questions across three formats (multiple-choice, short-answer, and case-based questions) and assesses three progressive reasoning levels: knowledge recall (L1), routine reasoning (L2), and individualized reasoning (L3). To ensure data quality, the automated construction framework was calibrated by six senior dentists, achieving expert agreement rates of 99.98% for multiple-choice and short-answer questions and 96.78% for the more complex case-based questions. Evaluation of 12 frontier LLMs on GlobalDentBench revealed a sharp, stepwise performance degradation with increasing reasoning complexity. Specifically, accuracy plummeted from 81.34% on multiple-choice to 64.53% on short-answer and 22.34% on case-based questions, while declining markedly from 74.01% at L1 to 55.64% at L2 and 35.71% at L3. More critically, risk analysis of real-world dental cases demonstrated an alarming overall unsafe rate of 31.01% in LLM-generated clinical recommendations, with 4.51% posing risks of irreversible patient harm and risks particularly pronounced in specialties such as orthodontics. These findings expose fundamental limitations in the medical reasoning and safety of current LLMs. Consequently, GlobalDentBench provides a scalable foundation for trustworthy clinical AI evaluation, underscoring the urgent need for rigorous validation before the safe deployment of these models in healthcare.

AI.tldr: GlobalDentBench is a large, multinational benchmark to evaluate how well LLMs reason and stay safe in dental clinical scenarios, revealing major performance drops as reasoning complexity increases and highlighting substantial safety risks in real-world use.
AI.motivation: Despite rapid progress of LLMs in medicine, their robustness and safety in realistic clinical reasoning—especially in dentistry—are poorly understood. Existing benchmarks are limited in specialty coverage, geographic diversity, and depth of reasoning evaluation, making it unclear whether current models can be safely deployed in dental practice. The authors aim to fill this gap by creating a dedicated, large-scale, high-quality benchmark for dental reasoning and safety.
AI.method: The authors construct GlobalDentBench, a multinational benchmark covering 14 dental specialties across 88 countries/regions on six continents. It contains 8,978 expert-validated items in three formats (multiple-choice, short-answer, case-based) aligned with three reasoning levels: L1 knowledge recall, L2 routine reasoning, and L3 individualized reasoning. An automated question-construction framework is calibrated and validated by six senior dentists, reaching near-expert agreement on question correctness (≈100% for MCQ and short-answer, ≈97% for case-based). They then systematically evaluate 12 state-of-the-art LLMs on all formats and levels and conduct a dedicated risk analysis on real-world dental cases, classifying the clinical safety of model-generated recommendations, including risks of irreversible patient harm.
AI.result: LLMs perform reasonably on simpler tasks but degrade sharply with increased reasoning demands. Average accuracy across models drops from 81.34% (multiple-choice) to 64.53% (short-answer) and 22.34% (case-based). By reasoning level, performance falls from 74.01% at L1 to 55.64% at L2 and 35.71% at L3, indicating major deficiencies in advanced, patient-specific reasoning. Safety evaluation shows that 31.01% of model-generated clinical recommendations are unsafe, with 4.51% carrying a risk of irreversible harm. Certain specialties, notably orthodontics, show particularly high safety risk profiles.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs display serious limitations in complex dental reasoning and pose nontrivial safety risks when used for clinical decision support, especially in nuanced, patient-tailored scenarios. GlobalDentBench offers a scalable, standardized benchmark to systematically evaluate and compare LLMs in dentistry, and the results emphasize that rigorous validation and safety assessment are essential before integrating these models into real-world healthcare workflows.

title: Mimir: Large-scale Multilingual Concept Modeling
summary: Current language modeling approaches are built around tokens. Text corpora are split into tokens, and models are trained by performing computations on these tokens, such as predicting the next token given the preceding ones as context. This paradigm has become the standard in modern language modeling, especially given the outstanding performance obtained by token-based architectures. However, recent works have not only begun to question how language models process and understand meaning from tokens, but also to question whether using higher levels of granularity could advance the research field. This led to the idea of Concept Modeling, that is, to directly train models for next-concept prediction rather than next-token prediction. The goal is to change the input from tokens to concepts, forcing the underlying language model to shift its granularity from fine-grained tokens to broad concepts. In this work, we introduce Mimir, a 1.6B Large Concept Model trained for multilingual concept understanding and generation. We leverage a large-scale multilingual pre-training corpus (38,883,987,240 sentences) spanning 46 languages and a large-scale multi-turn and multilingual instruction-tuning dataset (66,816,428 sentences) covering a total of 35 languages. We extensively evaluate model performance against a language model with a comparable number of parameters.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Mimir, a 1.6B-parameter Large Concept Model that predicts high-level concepts instead of tokens, and evaluates it on multilingual understanding and generation tasks against a similarly sized token-based language model.
AI.motivation: Traditional language models operate on low-level tokens, which may not align well with how meaning is represented and processed. Recent work questions whether token-based modeling is the best paradigm for capturing semantics and suggests that coarser units such as concepts could improve language understanding and generation. The authors are motivated to explore whether directly modeling concepts instead of tokens can shift models toward more semantically meaningful representations, especially in multilingual settings.
AI.method: The authors define the Concept Modeling paradigm, where models are trained for next-concept prediction instead of next-token prediction. They build Mimir, a 1.6B-parameter Large Concept Model, and pre-train it on a large multilingual corpus of about 38.9 billion sentences across 46 languages. They further perform multi-turn, multilingual instruction-tuning using 66.8 million sentences in 35 languages. They then compare Mimir’s performance to that of a standard token-based language model with a similar parameter count, evaluating concept understanding and generation across languages.
AI.result: Mimir, the proposed Large Concept Model, is successfully trained on massive multilingual corpora and evaluated against a comparably sized token-based language model. The results, while not numerically detailed in the abstract, indicate that concept-based modeling is competitive and provides meaningful insights into multilingual concept understanding and generation compared with traditional token prediction approaches.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates the feasibility of replacing token-level prediction with concept-level prediction in large models. It introduces Mimir as evidence that Large Concept Models can be trained at scale on multilingual data and perform competitively with similarly sized token-based models. This supports the view that modeling at the level of concepts is a promising direction for advancing language modeling, particularly for capturing semantics and handling multilingual tasks.

title: AVBench: Human-Aligned and Automated Evaluation Benchmark for Audio-Video Generative Models
summary: Rapid advances in audio-video (AV) generation have enabled high-fidelity synthesis with synchronized sound, particularly for human-related scenarios involving speech and interactions. Yet evaluation for AV generation remains at an early stage, with only a few coarse-grained benchmarks for human-related scenarios and relying on limited preset evaluations with generic multimodal LLMs, leading to inaccurate assessments of model capabilities. To address these issues, we introduce AVBench, a fully automated benchmark tailored for human-centric AV generation. AVBench is built on two key designs for comprehensive and accurate evaluation: (i) Human-centric and fine-grained metrics. AVBench integrates ten evaluation dimensions designed for human-centered real-world scenarios, covering visual quality, audio quality, and multi-level consistency across modalities. These practical metrics capture human-related details that existing benchmarks often overlook. (ii) Specialized evaluators via preference learning. To address the lack of specialized training data, we construct large-scale supervision by transforming real-world videos into diverse training pairs with controlled perturbations. After fine-tuning on this high-quality dataset, the evaluators learn to reliably detect subtle cross-modal inconsistencies. Crucially, instead of producing discrete textual judgment, AVBench derives continuous evaluation scores from the model's prediction confidence on binary decisions. This probabilistic scoring mechanism enables a more reliable assessment than traditional VQA-style evaluation and aligns closely with human judgment. Taken together, AVBench offers automated evaluation for AV generation, demonstrates strong potential for data filtering, and serves as a differentiable reward signal for Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF).

AI.tldr: AVBench is an automated, human-centric benchmark for evaluating audio-video generation with fine-grained, probabilistic metrics and learned evaluators.
AI.motivation: Existing AV generation evaluation is coarse, limited to a few human-related scenarios, and often relies on generic multimodal LLMs with preset prompts, leading to unreliable, low-resolution assessment of model performance, especially for subtle cross-modal inconsistencies.
AI.method: The authors build AVBench with two main components: (1) a suite of ten fine-grained, human-centric metrics covering visual quality, audio quality, and multi-level cross-modal consistency that target real-world human scenarios; (2) specialized evaluators trained via preference learning on large-scale supervision constructed by perturbing real-world videos to create controlled training pairs. These evaluators are fine-tuned to detect subtle inconsistencies, and instead of categorical judgments, AVBench computes continuous scores from the evaluators’ confidence on binary decisions to yield probabilistic, differentiable metrics.
AI.result: The trained AVBench evaluators can reliably detect subtle cross-modal inconsistencies and produce continuous scores that correlate closely with human judgment, enabling comprehensive automated evaluation of AV generation, as well as effective data filtering and use as a reward signal for training generative models via RLHF.
AI.conclusion: AVBench provides a more precise and human-aligned benchmark for human-centric audio-video generation by combining fine-grained metrics with preference-learned evaluators and a probabilistic scoring scheme, supporting both robust evaluation and practical uses such as dataset curation and reinforcement learning with differentiable rewards.

title: READER: Reasoning-Enhanced AI-Generated Text Detection
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have made it increasingly difficult to distinguish human-written text from AI-generated content. Many existing detectors train supervised neural classifiers that achieve strong in-distribution performance but are often opaque and can degrade substantially under distribution shift. We present READER, a reasoning-enhanced AI text detector that outputs both a human/AI label and a structured rationale describing the evidence for its decision. A key component of our approach is READ, a curated supervision set of rationales and verdicts. We fine-tune an LLM on READ to build READER, which reasons before detecting at inference time. Despite having only 1.5B parameters, READER consistently outperforms existing detectors as well as prompted, high-capacity LLM baselines (GPT-5.2, Gemini-3-Pro, and DeepSeek-V3.2), which are 100 to 1000 times larger in scale.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces READER, a reasoning-enhanced AI text detector that provides both a human/AI label and an interpretable rationale, outperforming larger existing detectors.
AI.motivation: As LLMs improve, it becomes hard to distinguish human-written from AI-generated text. Existing detectors are opaque neural classifiers that work well in-distribution but fail under distribution shift and lack interpretability. There is a need for more robust and explainable AI text detection methods.
AI.method: The authors construct READ, a curated dataset of detection rationales and verdicts. They then fine-tune a 1.5B-parameter LLM on READ so that, at inference time, the model first performs explicit reasoning and then issues a detection decision, yielding both a label (human vs AI) and a structured rationale supporting it.
AI.result: READER, despite its relatively small 1.5B parameter size, consistently outperforms prior AI text detectors and also surpasses prompted detection baselines using much larger LLMs (e.g., GPT-5.2, Gemini-3-Pro, DeepSeek-V3.2), which are 100–1000x larger.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning-enhanced detection, trained with explicit rationale supervision, can yield an AI text detector that is both more interpretable and more accurate than much larger black-box classifiers or prompted LLM detectors. Structured rationales and a curated rationale dataset (READ) are key to this improvement.

title: Beyond Inference-Only Deployment: Comparing Weight-Based Consolidation Against Cascading Compaction
summary: Major LLM platforms deploy models in an inference-only configuration: the model serves requests but never updates per-user weights. Users must repeatedly re-teach preferences, corrections, and project context, and context-based workarounds consume context-window space and degrade under cascading compaction. We evaluate an alternative: nightly consolidation of interaction knowledge into model weights via reflection, synthesis, and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) fine-tuning on a single consumer GPU. Across ten realistic software development conversations (n = 10, 1,146 test questions across three memory types), three cycles of cascading compaction retain 36.8 +/- 3.0% of knowledge (between an 11.8% no-context floor and a 90.1% full-context ceiling), while consolidation retains 80.4 +/- 1.3% -- a 43.6 pp gain (paired t(9) = 14.8, p < 0.001) that more than doubles what compaction preserves, with the largest gains on procedural corrections (36.3% -> 74.6%) and episodic project facts (31.5% -> 78.2%). As a methodological aside, mean per-token validation cross-entropy is negatively correlated with LLM-judged accuracy (r = -0.51) while median per-token validation cross-entropy tracks accuracy almost exactly (r = +0.99): under evaluators that tolerate surface-form variation, the mean is misleading and a heavy-tail-robust statistic is the faithful signal. Persistent personalization requires moving beyond inference-only deployment toward architectures that consolidate knowledge into weights.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that periodically consolidating user interaction data into LLM weights via reflection, synthesis, and LoRA fine-tuning preserves user- and project-specific knowledge far better than context-based memory compaction, and highlights that median per-token cross-entropy is a better validation metric than mean for LLM personalization.
AI.motivation: Most deployed LLMs are inference-only and do not update per-user weights, forcing users to repeatedly restate preferences and context. Existing memory workarounds that rely on storing and compacting context degrade over time, wasting context window and losing information. The authors are motivated to explore whether periodically fine-tuning the model on synthesized interaction knowledge can provide persistent personalization on modest hardware, and to clarify how to evaluate such methods properly.
AI.method: The authors simulate realistic software development assistant usage across ten extended conversations, extract three types of memory (e.g., procedural corrections, episodic project facts, and likely stable preferences), and compare two approaches to retaining this knowledge across three cycles: (1) cascading context compaction alone, and (2) nightly consolidation of extracted interaction knowledge into model weights via reflection, synthesis, and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) fine-tuning on a single consumer GPU. They then evaluate knowledge retention on 1,146 test questions and study the relationship between validation cross-entropy statistics (mean vs median per-token) and LLM-judged answer accuracy.
AI.result: After three cycles, pure cascading compaction retains only about 36.8±3.0% of the interaction knowledge, compared to a no-context baseline of 11.8% and a full-context upper bound of 90.1%. The consolidation approach with LoRA retains 80.4±1.3%, a 43.6 percentage point improvement over compaction that more than doubles preserved knowledge, with especially large gains for procedural corrections (36.3%→74.6%) and episodic project facts (31.5%→78.2%). Methodologically, they find mean per-token validation cross-entropy is moderately negatively correlated with LLM-judged accuracy (r=-0.51), while median per-token cross-entropy is almost perfectly positively correlated (r=+0.99), indicating the mean is distorted by heavy tails when evaluators tolerate surface-form variation.
AI.conclusion: Periodic consolidation of interaction knowledge into model weights using lightweight fine-tuning (e.g., LoRA) substantially outperforms purely context-based memory compaction for persistent personalization, even on modest hardware. This suggests future LLM architectures should move beyond inference-only deployment toward systems that routinely update user-specific parameters. Additionally, when evaluating such personalization or fine-tuning, median per-token cross-entropy is a more reliable metric than mean in the presence of heavy-tailed loss distributions and tolerant evaluators.

title: Knowing but Not Showing: LLMs Recognize Ambiguity but Rarely Ask Clarifying Questions
summary: User queries are often underspecified and may admit multiple valid interpretations. Rather than silently making assumptions about the user's intent, a helpful assistant should surface such ambiguity by asking a clarifying question. Doing so requires two abilities: recognizing that a query is ambiguous, and acting on that recognition by seeking clarification instead of answering directly.
