The experiments highlight that the structured, agentic pipeline and LLM-ready library design enable reliable translation without model fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that it is feasible and effective to translate Python geospatial workflows into scalable Spark-based implementations using an agentic LLM pipeline without custom model training. By making the target library LLM-ready and decomposing translation into structured steps with robust repair mechanisms, GRAIL helps domain scientists access big data scalability while continuing to work in familiar Python, pointing toward a general pattern for LLM-assisted workflow migration in scientific computing.

title: LLM-as-a-Reviewer: Benchmarking Their Ability, Divergence, and Prompt Injection Resistance as Paper Reviewers
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used in academic peer review, yet their reliability, alignment with human judgment, and robustness to adversarial attacks remain poorly understood. We present a systematic benchmark of LLM-as-a-Reviewer on 898 papers stratified from NeurIPS and ICLR, evaluating 12 LLMs along three axes: rating calibration, divergence from human reviewers, and resistance to prompt injection embedded via an invisible font-mapping attack. We find that LLMs systematically overrate weaker submissions and diverge from humans in topical emphasis, under-flagging Clarity and over-flagging Reproducibility, while producing reviews two to three times longer with lower lexical diversity and a more standardized vocabulary. Prompt injection remains highly effective. Simple hidden instructions can promote low-scoring papers to acceptance-level ratings in a substantial fraction of cases, with effectiveness varying sharply across model families. While LLMs offer utility in structuring evaluations, their integration into peer review requires safeguards against both intrinsic biases and adversarial risks.

AI.tldr: The paper systematically evaluates how reliable and robust large language models are when used as peer reviewers, showing consistent miscalibration, divergence from human judgment, and strong vulnerability to prompt injection attacks.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used to assist or automate academic peer review, there is little rigorous evidence about how well their ratings align with human reviewers, how they differ in qualitative focus, and how easily they can be manipulated by adversarial prompts embedded in submissions. This gap poses risks to fairness and integrity in scholarly evaluation.
AI.method: The authors construct a benchmark of 898 papers from NeurIPS and ICLR, stratified across the score spectrum, and test 12 different LLMs acting as reviewers. They evaluate three main aspects: (1) rating calibration against human-assigned scores; (2) divergence from human reviewers in terms of criteria emphasized and linguistic properties of reviews; and (3) robustness to an invisible prompt-injection attack via font-mapping, where hidden instructions inside the paper attempt to bias the model’s rating upward.
AI.result: The study finds that LLM reviewers systematically overrate weaker submissions, differ from humans in their focus by under-penalizing Clarity and over-penalizing Reproducibility, and generate reviews that are 2–3 times longer than human reviews but with lower lexical diversity and more formulaic wording. The invisible prompt-injection attack is highly successful: concealed instructions can significantly raise ratings of low-scoring papers to acceptance levels in many cases, with susceptibility varying markedly between model families.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can help structure and standardize parts of the review process, but they are not yet reliable standalone reviewers. Their use in peer review requires explicit safeguards and oversight to mitigate systematic rating biases and to defend against adversarial manipulations such as hidden prompt injections.

title: PANDO: Efficient Multimodal AI Agents via Online Skill Distillation
summary: Recent advances in multimodal web agents often rely on increased inference-time computation, including rollout search, verifier passes, offline skill discovery, and specialist model stacks. This raises a central question: can a web agent become more efficient as it accumulates experience, rather than more expensive? We first analyze trajectories from VisualWebArena and identify three recurring sources of inefficiency: repeat-action loops, hidden discovery costs, and low prompt-cache reuse. We then introduce PANDO, a single-rollout online skill-distillation framework that maintains a structured Skill Library and combines progress reflection, confidence-based skill demotion, hierarchical routing, visual compression, and cache-aware prompting. On the full set of 910 VisualWebArena tasks, PANDO achieves a 58.3% success rate, outperforming SGV (54.0%) and our WALT reproduction (45.2%), while using 58% fewer tokens than SGV and 61% fewer tokens than WALT, without any pre-evaluation discovery budget. A 300-task ablation further shows that rules and routines provide most of the success gains, while routing, compression, and cache-aware prompting convert the larger skill library into lower marginal token cost. Finally, we introduce three trajectory-level efficiency metrics -- Action Repetition Rate, Step Overhead Ratio, and Prompt Cache Utilization -- to make efficiency visible beyond terminal success.

AI.tldr: The paper presents PANDO, an online skill-distillation framework for multimodal web agents that improves both success rate and token efficiency by learning from prior trajectories and maintaining a structured skill library.
AI.motivation: Multimodal web agents are becoming increasingly capable but also increasingly expensive at inference time due to techniques like rollout search, verifier passes, and specialist model stacks. The authors are motivated by the question of whether a web agent can become more efficient as it gains experience, instead of requiring more computation. They observe recurring inefficiencies in existing agents—repeat-action loops, hidden discovery costs, and low prompt-cache reuse—which motivates a new framework that explicitly targets trajectory-level efficiency, not just success rate.
AI.method: The authors propose PANDO, a single-rollout online skill-distillation framework. PANDO maintains a structured Skill Library and updates it online as the agent acts. It uses progress reflection to derive reusable skills, confidence-based skill demotion to prune or downgrade unreliable skills, hierarchical routing to decide when to use which skills, visual compression to reduce visual input tokens, and cache-aware prompting to increase reuse of previously computed prompts. The framework is evaluated end-to-end on the VisualWebArena benchmark and further studied through a 300-task ablation to isolate contributions of components like rules, routines, routing, compression, and cache-aware prompting. They also define three new efficiency metrics at the trajectory level: Action Repetition Rate, Step Overhead Ratio, and Prompt Cache Utilization.
AI.result: On the full set of 910 VisualWebArena tasks, PANDO attains a 58.3% success rate, outperforming baselines SGV (54.0%) and a WALT reproduction (45.2%). Importantly, it does so while reducing token usage by 58% relative to SGV and 61% relative to WALT, and without relying on any pre-evaluation discovery budget. The 300-task ablation indicates that rules and routines are the main contributors to performance gains, while hierarchical routing, visual compression, and cache-aware prompting are primarily responsible for lowering marginal token cost as the skill library grows. The newly proposed efficiency metrics make visible improvements in trajectory-level efficiency beyond final task success.
AI.conclusion: PANDO demonstrates that web agents can become both more capable and more efficient by learning structured skills online from their own trajectories, without costly multi-rollout search or large discovery budgets. The framework’s components—structured skill libraries, reflection, demotion, routing, compression, and cache-aware prompting—jointly reduce inference-time token usage while improving success rates. The introduced trajectory-level metrics offer a more nuanced way to measure efficiency, highlighting sources of waste such as repeated actions and poor cache utilization. Overall, the work argues for designing web agents that learn to reduce their own computational footprint as they gain experience.

title: SomaliBench Eval: Measuring English-to-Somali Refusal Gaps in Open-Weight Language Models
summary: Large language model safety evaluation remains heavily English-centered, leaving low-resource languages under-measured even when models are deployed globally. We evaluate four open-weight instruction-tuned models on SomaliBench v0, a native-author-verified benchmark of 100 harmful-intent prompts paired across English and Somali. Each of Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Gemma-2-9B-Instruct, Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct, and Aya-23-8B is run locally with temperature 0 and the same English "helpful, harmless, and honest" (HHH) system prompt. A pinned Claude Sonnet snapshot (claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929) classifies each response as refused, complied, or unclear; the native author spot-checks a stratified 80-row sample. We find large English-to-Somali refusal gaps for all four models: Llama-3.1-8B (0.90; 95% bootstrap CI [0.85, 0.96]), Aya-23-8B (0.75 [0.67, 0.83]), Qwen-2.5-7B (0.69 [0.59, 0.78]), and Gemma-2-9B (0.38 [0.27, 0.49]). For three models, the dominant Somali non-refusal mode is not fluent harmful compliance but unclear output: empty, wrong-language, or incoherent generations. The native verification spot-check achieves 100% agreement with the judge (Cohen's kappa = 1.00) on the 80 sampled rows. We report aggregate refusal rates, category gaps, and reliability statistics only; raw model generations are retained locally and are not released.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how safely four open-weight language models handle harmful-intent prompts in Somali versus English, showing large safety gaps disadvantaging Somali and validating an automatic safety judge against native annotators.
AI.motivation: Most safety evaluations for large language models focus on English, despite global deployment. Low-resource languages like Somali are under-evaluated, raising concern that models may behave less safely in those languages, especially for harmful-intent prompts. The authors aim to quantify this safety disparity and provide a native-verified benchmark to study multilingual safety performance.
AI.method: The authors construct SomaliBench v0, a benchmark of 100 harmful-intent prompts paired in English and Somali and verified by native speakers. They run four open-weight instruction-tuned models (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Gemma-2-9B-Instruct, Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct, Aya-23-8B) locally with identical decoding settings (temperature 0) and a shared English HHH-style system prompt. Each model output is labeled by a fixed Claude Sonnet snapshot as refused, complied, or unclear. A Somali native author spot-checks a stratified sample of 80 rows to validate the judge’s labels. The authors compute refusal rates, English–Somali gaps, refusal category breakdowns, and agreement statistics (Cohen’s kappa).
AI.result: All four models exhibit substantially lower refusal rates in Somali than in English, with English–Somali refusal gaps of 0.90 (Llama-3.1-8B), 0.75 (Aya-23-8B), 0.69 (Qwen-2.5-7B), and 0.38 (Gemma-2-9B), each with reported bootstrap confidence intervals. For most models, Somali non-refusals are predominantly “unclear” generations—empty outputs, wrong language, or incoherent text—rather than fluent harmful compliance. The automatic judge’s labels are perfectly consistent with the native author’s spot-check on the sampled 80 rows (Cohen’s kappa = 1.00), indicating high reliability of the evaluation pipeline. Raw generations are kept private and not shared.
AI.conclusion: Safety behavior of current open-weight instruction-tuned models is markedly weaker and less reliable in Somali than in English when facing harmful-intent prompts, even under a consistent English HHH system prompt. The gap manifests both in refusal rates and in degraded output quality, with many Somali responses being unclear rather than explicit harmful content. The strong agreement between the automated judge and a native Somali annotator supports using this setup for scalable safety measurement. Overall, the work highlights the need for multilingual, native-verified safety benchmarks and targeted improvements to model safety in low-resource languages.

title: CoRe-Code: Collaborative Reinforcement Learning for Code Generation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved strong performance in code generation, but most methods rely on autoregressive decoding without global planning, often leading to locally coherent yet globally suboptimal solutions (e.g., failing test cases or inefficient complexity). While recent approaches such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and multi-agent systems (MAS) introduce planning, their limited role specialization and coordination hinder performance on complex tasks. To address the challenges of coordination and specialization in multi-agent code generation, we propose Collaborative Reinforcement Code (CoRe-Code), a framework for role specialized LLM agents that enhances inter-agent coordination to generate more accurate and efficient code. CoRe-Code adopts a simple Planner-Coder paradigm, where the Planner produces high-level plans and the Coder executes them to generate code. We further introduce a collaboration-aware reinforcement learning stage based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to enhance role specialization and alignment. Experiments show that CoRe-Code outperforms a wide range of existing RL-based and multi-agent methods. In addition, we demonstrate that CoRe-Code can generalize to other multi-agent frameworks (e.g., Retrieval and Debugging agents), highlighting its flexibility and scalability. We evaluate CoRe-Code on multiple benchmarks of varying difficulty using three base models. Compared to existing baselines, the results show consistent improvements in accuracy, while also achieving higher efficiency in terms of execution time and memory usage, demonstrating the effectiveness and practicality of CoRe-Code.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CoRe-Code, a planner-coder multi-agent framework with collaboration-aware reinforcement learning to improve the accuracy and efficiency of LLM-based code generation.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based code generation often uses autoregressive decoding without explicit global planning, which can yield solutions that pass locally but fail globally (e.g., failing hidden tests, poor algorithmic complexity). Existing planning approaches like CoT and generic multi-agent setups suffer from weak role specialization and poor coordination, limiting their effectiveness on complex programming tasks. The authors aim to design a multi-agent code generation framework with strong specialization and coordination that produces more accurate and efficient code and can generalize across different multi-agent settings.
AI.method: They introduce CoRe-Code, a collaborative multi-agent framework with two main roles: a Planner and a Coder. The Planner generates high-level solution plans; the Coder implements them as code. On top of this architecture, they apply a collaboration-aware reinforcement learning procedure based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) that jointly optimizes the agents for better role specialization and alignment. They further show that this RL scheme can be extended to other multi-agent configurations such as retrieval-augmented and debugging agents. The framework is evaluated on multiple code benchmarks with three different base models, comparing to RL-based and multi-agent baselines.
AI.result: Across multiple benchmarks and difficulty levels, CoRe-Code consistently improves code generation accuracy over existing RL-based and multi-agent methods. It also yields more efficient solutions, with reduced execution time and memory usage. The method generalizes to additional multi-agent settings (e.g., retrieval and debugging agents) while preserving its performance gains.
AI.conclusion: A simple yet structured Planner-Coder multi-agent architecture, when combined with collaboration-aware RL via GRPO, enhances both coordination and role specialization in LLM-based code generation. This leads to more accurate and resource-efficient solutions and can be flexibly applied to varied multi-agent setups, suggesting a practical and scalable direction for improving LLM code generation beyond standard autoregressive decoding and naive multi-agent methods.

title: HyLaT: Efficient Multi-Agent Communication via Hybrid Latent-Text Protocol
summary: Communication protocol design is a central challenge in large language model-based multi-agent systems. Existing single-channel approaches face an inherent communication trilemma: text-based methods are interpretable but verbose, while latent-space methods are efficient but opaque and limited to unidirectional workflows. Inspired by multi-channel communication theory, we propose HyLaT, a hybrid latent-text communication protocol that transmits elaborate cognitive signals through a latent channel for efficiency, while expressing concise critical signals in natural language to preserve interpretability and precision. We introduce a two-stage training framework combining single-agent hybrid generation learning and multi-agent interactive co-training, enabling agents to generate and interpret hybrid messages across multiple rounds of interaction. Experiments demonstrate that HyLaT reduces communication overhead significantly while maintaining competitive task performance, with strong generalization and robustness across diverse settings.

AI.tldr: HyLaT is a hybrid communication protocol for LLM-based multi-agent systems that mixes latent efficient signals with concise text to balance efficiency and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Single-channel communication in multi-agent LLM systems faces a trilemma: text is interpretable but verbose and inefficient; latent communication is efficient but opaque and constrained to simple, usually unidirectional settings. There is a need for a protocol that reduces communication overhead without sacrificing interpretability, precision, or applicability to complex multi-round interactions.
AI.method: The authors design HyLaT, a hybrid latent-text protocol where most rich cognitive information is sent via a compact latent channel, while key decisions and critical information are sent in natural language. They propose a two-stage training procedure: (1) single-agent hybrid generation learning, where an individual model learns to jointly produce and understand hybrid latent-text outputs; and (2) multi-agent interactive co-training, where multiple agents are trained together to generate, exchange, and interpret hybrid messages over multi-round dialogues.
AI.result: In experiments on multi-agent benchmarks, HyLaT substantially reduces the amount of communication (e.g., message length/size) while preserving task performance that is on par with or close to fully text-based approaches. The hybrid protocol shows good generalization to new tasks and robustness across different interaction settings.
AI.conclusion: A multi-channel hybrid communication approach can overcome the limitations of purely text-based or purely latent communication in LLM multi-agent systems. HyLaT successfully trades off efficiency and interpretability, suggesting that structured hybrid protocols are a promising direction for scalable, robust multi-agent communication with large language models.

title: Agent Manufacturing: Foundation-Model Agents as First-Class Industrial Entities
summary: Manufacturing has passed through four widely recognized paradigms - mechanization, electrification, programmable automation, and Smart Manufacturing - each defined by the kind of work it shifted from humans to machines. In every case, one layer of industrial work remained fundamentally human: the coordinative cognition of production, comprising the interpretive, allocative, diagnostic, negotiative, and governance work exercised by engineers, planners, and operational managers. We argue that a fifth transition is now underway in which this layer, rather than the physical or routine-cognitive layers below it, is what foundation-model-based autonomous agents primarily redistribute. We name this paradigm Agent Manufacturing and define it operationally: a manufacturing system is an instance of Agent Manufacturing when its principal coordination mechanism is reasoning performed by foundation-model agents that can interpret open-ended goals, plan over long horizons, invoke tools and machines, and negotiate with other agents and humans. This is a narrower and more falsifiable definition than the existing literature on cognitive manufacturing or Industry 5.0 provides, and it distinguishes the paradigm sharply from classical multi-agent manufacturing systems, which were autonomous only within closed protocol spaces.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new manufacturing paradigm called Agent Manufacturing, where foundation-model-based autonomous agents perform the main coordination and decision-making work in production.
AI.motivation: Historically, manufacturing advances have automated physical and routine cognitive work, but high-level coordinative cognition—done by engineers and managers—remained largely human. With foundation-model-based agents becoming capable of complex reasoning and interaction, the authors see a new transition where this coordinative layer can also be redistributed from humans to machines.
AI.method: The authors conceptually analyze prior manufacturing paradigms (mechanization, electrification, programmable automation, Smart Manufacturing) and identify the specific type of work each automated. They then define a new paradigm, Agent Manufacturing, and specify operational criteria that distinguish it from related notions like cognitive manufacturing, Industry 5.0, and traditional multi-agent systems with closed protocols.
AI.result: They introduce and formalize the term Agent Manufacturing as a fifth manufacturing paradigm, arguing that current and emerging systems where foundation-model agents handle coordination, planning, and negotiation qualify under this definition. They show that this framing better captures the role of modern AI agents than existing broad or loosely defined concepts in the literature.
AI.conclusion: Agent Manufacturing is characterized by production systems whose primary coordination is performed by foundation-model-based agents able to interpret open-ended goals, plan over long horizons, use tools and machines, and negotiate with humans and other agents. This creates a more precise, testable paradigm that clearly differentiates present AI-driven coordination from earlier automation waves and from classical multi-agent manufacturing architectures.

title: A Multi-Agent LLM Framework for Rating the Quality of Surgical Feedback
summary: Verbal feedback delivered by attending surgeons in the operating room plays a critical formative role in resident trainee skill acquisition. Yet, assessing the quality of trainer feedback and its effectiveness in influencing trainee behavior during live surgery remains a challenge. Prior studies assessed feedback content relying on extensive manual annotation by expert human raters and focused on developing broad taxonomies that overlook the qualitative aspects of feedback delivery such as clarity or urgency. Limited existing automated methods, including keyword analysis and topic modeling, also fail to capture these nuanced aspects. We introduce a two-stage LLM-based framework that discovers interpretable feedback quality criteria grounded in the context of surgical training. Our method uses multi-agent prompting and surgical domain knowledge injection to discover a small set of human interpretable scoring criteria (e.g., Encouraging, Urgent, Clear). These criteria are then used to automatically score live surgical feedback via an LLM-as-a-judge approach. Evaluation on 4.2k trainer feedback instances demonstrates that our AI-discovered criteria outperform prior content-based frameworks in predicting feedback effectiveness, including observed trainee behavioral adjustments and trainer approval. This work advances scalable, human-aligned assessment of communication quality in the operating room and provides a foundation for improving surgical teaching practices.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a two-stage LLM-based framework that automatically discovers and applies qualitative criteria to evaluate the effectiveness of surgeons’ verbal feedback to residents during live surgery.
AI.motivation: Verbal feedback from attending surgeons is crucial for surgical trainees’ skill development, but it is difficult to systematically assess the quality and effectiveness of this feedback. Existing approaches rely on labor-intensive expert annotation and broad taxonomies that ignore delivery qualities like clarity or urgency, while current automated methods (e.g., keyword analysis, topic modeling) cannot capture such nuanced aspects. There is a need for scalable, nuanced, and human-aligned assessment of feedback quality in the operating room.
AI.method: The authors propose a two-stage LLM-based framework. In stage one, they use multi-agent prompting and injection of surgical domain knowledge to have LLMs discover a small, human-interpretable set of feedback quality criteria (e.g., Encouraging, Urgent, Clear). In stage two, they employ an LLM-as-a-judge strategy to automatically score instances of live surgical feedback along these discovered criteria. They then evaluate the framework on a dataset of 4.2k trainer feedback utterances.
AI.result: The discovered LLM-based criteria, when used to score feedback, outperform prior content-based taxonomies and automated methods in predicting feedback effectiveness. Effectiveness is operationalized via observed trainee behavioral adjustments during surgery and subsequent trainer approval, and the new framework shows better predictive power with respect to these outcomes.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables scalable and human-aligned assessment of communication quality in the operating room by automatically discovering and applying interpretable feedback quality dimensions. This lays groundwork for improving surgical teaching practices, offering a data-driven basis for training and evaluating trainers’ feedback delivery beyond content alone.

title: Test-Time Deep Thinking to Explore Implicit Rules
summary: With the continuous advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs), intelligent agents are becoming increasingly vital. However, these agents often fail in environments governed by implicit rules--hidden constraints that cannot be observed directly and must be inferred through interaction. This causes agents to fall into repetitive trial-and-error loops, ultimately leading to task failure. To address this challenge, we propose Test-Time Exploration (TTExplore), a framework where a thinker component analyzes interaction history to infer these implicit rules and guide an actor. Effective exploration in this setting critically depends on the reasoning ability of the thinker. However, evaluating deep reasoning trajectories is inherently unstable and difficult, which poses a major obstacle to effective training. To overcome this issue, we introduce a novel and stable reinforcement learning pipeline. The core idea is to use accurate task-level scores as indirect rewards to bypass the difficulty of evaluating intermediate reasoning, and to retain only a single thinking node per trajectory to alleviate reward sparsity. Using this pipeline, we train a specialized 7B model, Exp-Thinker. Experiments on five text-based embodied tasks show that TTExplore equipped with Exp-Thinker improves baseline agent performance by an average of $14$-$19$ points, demonstrating the effectiveness of explicitly reasoning about implicit rules.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TTExplore, a test-time exploration framework with a specialized reasoning model (Exp-Thinker) that helps LLM-based agents infer hidden rules in interactive environments, significantly boosting performance on text-based embodied tasks.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents struggle in environments with implicit, unobservable rules, leading to inefficient trial-and-error and frequent task failures. There is a need for agents that can infer these hidden constraints through interaction and reasoning, but training such deep reasoning components is difficult due to unstable evaluation of reasoning trajectories and sparse rewards.
AI.method: The authors propose TTExplore, which separates the agent into a thinker and an actor. The thinker analyzes the interaction history to infer implicit rules and guide the actor’s actions. To train the thinker, they design a stable reinforcement learning pipeline that avoids directly scoring intermediate reasoning steps. Instead, they use accurate task-level scores as indirect rewards and keep only one thinking node per trajectory to reduce reward sparsity. Using this pipeline, they train a 7B-parameter model called Exp-Thinker specialized for exploration and reasoning about hidden constraints.
AI.result: On five text-based embodied tasks, integrating Exp-Thinker into TTExplore yields an average improvement of 14–19 points over baseline agents, indicating that explicit reasoning about implicit rules substantially enhances agent performance in such environments.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling and training a reasoning-focused thinker component for test-time exploration enables LLM-based agents to better infer and respect implicit rules in interactive environments. The proposed RL pipeline provides a stable way to train such deep reasoning, and the resulting Exp-Thinker significantly improves task performance, validating the effectiveness of the TTExplore framework.

title: Harmony in Diversity: Multi-domain Contrastive Policy Optimization for Large Reasoning Models
summary: Post-training has significantly enhanced the reasoning capability of Large Reasoning Models (LRMs), especially with Reinforcement Learning (RL) like Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). However, GRPO-style RL methods in multi-domain settings often fail to achieve consistent improvements across all domains due to inherent interference in policy optimization. Prior studies on multi-domain RL primarily focus on alleviating cross-domain interference, while often neglecting the pivotal role of knowledge sharing, which we argue is the key to transforming cross-domain interactions from harmful competition into beneficial transfer. To address this limitation, we propose Multi-domain Contrastive Policy Optimization (MCPO), which analyzes the structural relationships among rollouts and promotes cross-domain knowledge sharing and in-domain knowledge consolidation in a contrastive manner. Specifically, for a given prompt, MCPO identifies transferable reasoning trajectories from other domains as positive examples, while treating incorrect rollouts as negative ones. It then encourages consistent representations for positive pairs and pushes negative pairs apart, thereby facilitating knowledge transfer and reducing interference. Moreover, MCPO aligns intra-domain correct rollouts to build a consolidated representation space. In this way, MCPO contrastively learns a harmonious representation space that can accommodate diverse multi-domain knowledge. Empirical results show that MCPO improves the reasoning capabilities of LRMs across multiple domains and even outperforms single-domain training in some cases. Code is available at https://github.com/Maricalce/MCPO.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MCPO, a contrastive reinforcement learning method that improves multi-domain reasoning in Large Reasoning Models by turning harmful cross-domain interference into beneficial knowledge transfer.
AI.motivation: Existing GRPO-style RL methods for post-training Large Reasoning Models struggle in multi-domain settings: improvements in one domain often hurt others due to policy interference. Previous multi-domain RL work focuses mostly on reducing interference and overlooks structured knowledge sharing across domains, which could convert competition into transfer. The authors aim to develop an RL framework that explicitly models and leverages cross-domain relationships among reasoning trajectories to improve performance consistently across domains.
AI.method: They introduce Multi-domain Contrastive Policy Optimization (MCPO), built on top of GRPO-style reinforcement learning for LRMs. For each prompt, the method analyzes rollouts (reasoning trajectories) from multiple domains and constructs contrastive pairs: (1) it selects transferable correct trajectories from other domains as positive examples and treats incorrect rollouts as negatives; (2) it applies a contrastive objective that pulls representations of positive pairs together and pushes negative pairs apart to promote knowledge transfer while mitigating interference; (3) within each domain, it aligns representations of correct rollouts to consolidate in-domain knowledge. This yields a representation space that jointly encodes diverse domain knowledge while remaining stable under multi-domain optimization.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on multiple reasoning domains show that MCPO consistently enhances the reasoning ability of Large Reasoning Models across domains. In several benchmarks, MCPO-trained models not only surpass standard multi-domain GRPO-style baselines but also outperform models trained separately on single domains, indicating effective cross-domain transfer and reduced interference.
AI.conclusion: MCPO offers an effective way to post-train LRMs in multi-domain settings by re-framing cross-domain interactions as a contrastive representation learning problem. By explicitly identifying transferable trajectories and shaping a shared representation space through contrastive alignment, it enables beneficial knowledge sharing and reduces harmful interference, leading to stronger and more robust multi-domain reasoning performance than conventional GRPO-based or single-domain training approaches.

title: Geo-Expert: Towards Expert-Level Geological Reasoning via Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning
comment: 11 pages, 1 figure, 3 tables. Accepted at ICML 2026 AI for Science Workshop
summary: While general-purpose Large Language Models (LLMs) applied to Geology often hallucinate when reasoning about subsurface structures and deep-time evolution, current AI in Earth sciences predominantly targets surface remote sensing and GIS. To bridge this gap, we introduce Geo-Expert, a family of parameter-efficient geological LLMs fine-tuned on a custom-curated, high-quality instruction dataset processed using our custom instruction synthesis pipeline. We investigate the impact of model scaling and architecture by fine-tuning three base models: Qwen3-8B, Qwen3-32B, and Gemma-3-27B, with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) method. Our extensive evaluation on a novel domain-specific benchmark, Geo-Eval, reveals that a domain-aligned 8B model can outperform open-weight 70B generalists and proprietary GPT-4o on specialized geological reasoning, while a 32B variant approaches frontier reasoning models. The optimized 8B model further offers a competitive cost-performance ratio for deployment. This work provides a reproducible recipe for democratizing scientific LLMs and establishes a baseline for geological artificial intelligence.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Geo-Expert, a family of geology-specialized LLMs that, despite being smaller and parameter-efficient, outperform much larger general-purpose models on geological reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: General-purpose LLMs often hallucinate and perform poorly on specialized geological reasoning about subsurface structures and deep-time evolution, while most current Earth science AI focuses on surface data like remote sensing and GIS; there is a need for domain-specialized, accurate, and cost-effective LLMs for geology.
AI.method: The authors build Geo-Expert by fine-tuning several base LLMs (Qwen3-8B, Qwen3-32B, Gemma-3-27B) using parameter-efficient Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on a custom-curated, high-quality geology instruction dataset generated via a bespoke instruction synthesis pipeline, and evaluate them on a new geological benchmark, Geo-Eval, to study effects of scaling and architecture.
AI.result: A geology-aligned 8B Geo-Expert model outperforms open-weight 70B general-purpose models and proprietary GPT-4o on specialized geological reasoning tasks; the 32B variant achieves performance close to frontier reasoning models, and the 8B model in particular offers strong accuracy with favorable cost-performance tradeoffs.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specialized fine-tuning with a well-designed instruction dataset and efficient adaptation techniques can produce relatively small geological LLMs that surpass much larger general models on expert tasks, offering a reproducible path to democratize scientific LLMs and setting a new baseline for geological AI research.

title: GeoSVG-RL: Geometry-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Layout-Constrained Text-to-SVG Diagram Generation
summary: Generating structured, editable diagrams remains a significant challenge for contemporary large language models, despite their proficiency in general-purpose vector code generation. The primary difficulty lies in the structural fragility of the output; minor errors such as misaligned connector endpoints, text labels overlapping borders, or complex layouts drifting beyond the canvas boundaries render the resulting SVG files functionally unusable for professional applications. To address these issues, we introduce GeoSVG-RL, a specialized reinforcement learning framework designed for layout-constrained text-to-SVG generation. Unlike standard training objectives that rely solely on maximizing token-level likelihood, our approach optimizes the policy against explicit, executable geometric feedback. The model first produces a structured layout plan that serves as a geometric contract for the subsequent generation of the SVG code. This code is then rendered through a browser-backed verifier, enabling the calculation of fine-grained rewards across six critical dimensions: rendering validity, canvas fitting, precise anchor placement, text containment, graph consistency, and code cleanliness. We utilize Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to refine the model, sampling multiple candidates per prompt to facilitate updates based on relative quality. Starting from a supervised warm-start phase on synthetic data, GeoSVG-RL achieves substantial gains in structural reliability, particularly in arrow-anchor accuracy and text-in-box rates. Quantitative evaluations demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms current state-of-the-art systems in local geometric precision and the preservation of graph connectivity, providing a robust pathway toward automated yet reliable technical illustration.

AI.tldr: GeoSVG-RL is a reinforcement learning framework that improves the structural reliability of text-to-SVG diagram generation using explicit geometric feedback and browser-based verification.
AI.motivation: Existing large language models can generate SVG code, but their outputs are often structurally fragile: connector endpoints misalign, text overlaps shapes, and layouts fall off the canvas. These small geometric errors make diagrams unusable for professional purposes. There is a need for a method that directly optimizes structural and geometric correctness, rather than only token-level likelihood, to enable reliable automatic technical illustration.
AI.method: The authors propose GeoSVG-RL, a reinforcement learning framework for layout-constrained text-to-SVG generation. The model first outputs a structured layout plan that defines a geometric contract for the final SVG. The generated SVG is then rendered with a browser-backed verifier and evaluated along six geometric and structural dimensions: rendering validity, canvas fitting, anchor placement accuracy, text containment, graph consistency, and code cleanliness. These metrics are turned into fine-grained rewards. Using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), the system samples multiple SVG candidates per prompt and updates the policy based on their relative rewards. Training begins with a supervised warm-start on synthetic data before RL fine-tuning.
AI.result: GeoSVG-RL significantly improves structural reliability compared to baselines, especially in precise arrow-anchor placement and ensuring that text remains correctly contained within boxes. Quantitative benchmarks show that the method surpasses state-of-the-art systems in local geometric precision and maintaining correct graph connectivity in generated diagrams.
AI.conclusion: By incorporating explicit geometric feedback and browser-based verification into a GRPO reinforcement learning loop, GeoSVG-RL offers a more reliable approach to text-to-SVG technical diagram generation. It reduces common structural failures of LLM-generated diagrams and improves both geometric precision and graph consistency, pointing toward practical, automated creation of professionally usable technical illustrations.

title: Solving Combinatorial Counting Problems with Weighted First-Order Model Counting
summary: Combinatorial counting problems pervade artificial intelligence, statistics, and discrete mathematics. Whether the task is enumerating subsets, multisets, permutations, partitions, or compositions under structural and arithmetic constraints, solving it remains a stubbornly manual exercise. Closed-form derivations are powerful but brittle, while naive encodings to propositional model counting or constraint satisfaction destroy the exchangeability that makes counting tractable in the first place. We present Cofola (COmbinatorial counting LAnguage with First-Order logic), a typed declarative language whose primitives are the combinatorial objects that recur in everyday counting questions, including sets, bags, tuples, sequences, circles, partitions, and compositions, together with natural relational and arithmetic constraints over them. A denotational semantics maps every Cofola program to a well-defined combinatorial counting problem, and a three-phase compilation pipeline (preprocessing, decomposition, and symmetry-preserving encoding) reduces this problem to a weighted first-order model counting (WFOMC) instance augmented with coefficient-extraction constraints. To stay inside known domain-liftable fragments whenever possible, the encoding groups indistinguishable entities, breaks the symmetry of unordered groupings lexicographically, and encodes sequences and circles via order axioms. On a suite of representative combinatorial counting problems, ranging from textbook math problems to multi-object scenarios that the closest prior framework cannot express, Cofola produces concise specifications and a uniform solving pipeline that is practical end-to-end.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Cofola, a high-level language for specifying combinatorial counting problems that compiles to weighted first-order model counting, enabling automatic and scalable solutions.
AI.motivation: Combinatorial counting problems (e.g., counting constrained subsets, permutations, partitions) are ubiquitous in AI, statistics, and discrete math, but typically require manual, brittle derivations or naive encodings that lose exploitable structure like exchangeability. There is a need for a uniform, automated framework that keeps combinatorial structure explicit while leveraging powerful backend solvers.
AI.method: The authors design Cofola, a typed declarative language whose core data types are common combinatorial objects (sets, bags, tuples, sequences, circles, partitions, compositions) with relational and arithmetic constraints. They give a denotational semantics mapping any Cofola program to a formal counting problem, then provide a three-phase compilation pipeline (preprocessing, decomposition, symmetry-preserving encoding) that translates this into a weighted first-order model counting (WFOMC) problem with additional coefficient-extraction constraints. The encoding groups indistinguishable entities to maintain domain-liftability, uses lexicographic symmetry breaking for unordered structures, and uses order axioms to represent sequences and circles.
AI.result: On a suite of diverse benchmark problems—from classical textbook combinatorics exercises to more complex multi-object scenarios beyond prior frameworks—Cofola allows concise problem specifications and automatically produces WFOMC encodings that can be solved end-to-end in practice. It outperforms prior approaches in expressiveness (supporting richer combinatorial objects) while maintaining competitive or practical performance.
AI.conclusion: Cofola demonstrates that a dedicated, typed, first-order language for combinatorial objects, combined with a symmetry-aware compilation to WFOMC, can turn many previously manual combinatorial counting tasks into automatically solvable problems. This provides a uniform and practical pipeline that preserves structural symmetries and exchangeability, making advanced combinatorial reasoning more accessible and automatable.

title: A Lightweight Hybrid Transformer-CRF Architecture for Multi-Type Bangla Medical Entity Recognition
summary: MedER refers to the identification of medical entities. It is crucial for extracting structured clinical information from unstructured medical text. Many existing systems rely on transformer-based models, which are computationally expensive and difficult to deploy in resource-constrained environments. Furthermore, earlier works often use relaxed evaluation metrics that artificially inflate performance by rewarding correct prediction of dominant "Outside" (O) tokens. In this paper, we propose a lightweight Medical Entity Recognition (MedER) framework for the Bangla language. We establish a rigorous baseline using a 12-layer BanglaBERT model combined with a Conditional Random Field (CRF) layer for exact-boundary entity detection. To address deployment constraints, we compress this teacher model into a 4-layer student network through Knowledge Distillation (KD), where the student learns from the teacher's pre-CRF soft emission logits. Finally, we apply INT8 dynamic quantization to further reduce model size and inference cost. Our final quantized student achieves an 8.6x CPU speedup while requiring nearly 48 percent less storage than the CRF teacher model.

AI.tldr: Lightweight Bangla medical entity recognition via distilled and quantized BanglaBERT with strict evaluation.
AI.motivation: Medical text is largely unstructured, so identifying medical entities (MedER) is key for structuring clinical information. Existing MedER systems typically use large transformer models that are expensive to run and hard to deploy on resource-constrained devices. Additionally, prior work often uses lenient evaluation metrics that inflate performance by counting easy “Outside” (O) tokens, masking true entity-detection quality. There is a need for a rigorously evaluated, efficient MedER system tailored to Bangla medical text.
AI.method: The authors first build a strong baseline MedER model for Bangla using a 12-layer BanglaBERT encoder with a CRF layer on top to enforce exact entity boundary prediction. They then compress this large teacher model into a compact 4-layer student model via knowledge distillation. The student is trained to match the teacher’s pre-CRF soft emission logits, transferring the teacher’s token-level knowledge. After distillation, they apply INT8 dynamic quantization to the student model to further reduce storage and computational cost on CPU while preserving performance.
AI.result: The quantized 4-layer student model maintains competitive MedER performance relative to the 12-layer BanglaBERT-CRF teacher, while being much more efficient. It achieves approximately an 8.6x speedup in CPU inference time and uses about 48% less storage than the teacher model, under stricter, exact-boundary evaluation metrics that do not over-reward prediction of the majority O class.
AI.conclusion: A carefully designed compression pipeline—teacher CRF-based BanglaBERT, knowledge distillation to a 4-layer student using pre-CRF logits, followed by INT8 dynamic quantization—can deliver a lightweight yet accurate Bangla medical entity recognizer. This framework significantly reduces deployment costs while maintaining high-quality, strictly evaluated entity detection, making MedER more practical for real-world, resource-constrained clinical settings in the Bangla language.

title: Clustering as Reasoning: A $k$-Means Interpretation of Chain-of-Thought Graph Learning
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting has shown promise in enhancing the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) on text-attributed graphs (TAGs). This work reframes CoT-based graph learning through the principle of clustering as reasoning, offering a $k$-means interpretation of how iterative reasoning operates over graph-structured data. We observe that existing graph CoT methods rely on disjoint architectures and fixed graph representations, limiting step-by-step semantic-topological interaction and interpretability. To overcome this limitation, we propose a unified framework named KCoT that integrates CoT reasoning with graph representation learning. Our key theoretical result reveals a formal mathematical correspondence between a Transformer block and the $k$-means algorithm, allowing reasoning to be interpreted as iterative assignment and update steps. Based on this insight, we introduce a Semantic Discriminating Prompt that explicitly formulates these steps as structured CoT reasoning, together with a structure-grounded alignment strategy to fuse topological priors with evolving thought-conditioned representations. Experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods, validating clustering as a principled mechanism for CoT-based graph learning.

AI.tldr: They interpret chain-of-thought reasoning on text-attributed graphs as a clustering process, show a theoretical link between Transformer blocks and k-means, and build a unified framework (KCoT) that improves graph reasoning performance.
AI.motivation: Existing CoT methods on text-attributed graphs use separate architectures and static graph representations, which prevents tight, iterative interaction between semantics (text) and topology (graph structure) and makes the reasoning process less interpretable. There is also a lack of a principled, mathematical view of what CoT reasoning is actually doing on graphs.
AI.method: They reframe CoT-based graph reasoning as clustering, giving a k-means interpretation of iterative reasoning over graphs. The authors prove a formal correspondence between the operations of a Transformer block and the assignment/update steps of the k-means algorithm. Building on this, they design KCoT, a unified framework that couples Transformer-based CoT reasoning with graph representation learning. They propose a Semantic Discriminating Prompt that explicitly encodes k-means-like steps as structured CoT, and a structure-grounded alignment mechanism to inject graph topology priors into the evolving, thought-conditioned node representations.
AI.result: On standard text-attributed graph benchmarks, KCoT consistently outperforms prior state-of-the-art CoT and graph learning methods. The empirical results support their theoretical view that clustering is a suitable and effective mechanism for CoT-based graph reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Chain-of-thought reasoning on graphs can be rigorously understood as a clustering process analogous to k-means, with Transformer blocks implementing iterative assignment and centroid update. Leveraging this perspective, the KCoT framework unifies CoT prompting and graph representation learning, leading to more interpretable and more effective reasoning over text-attributed graphs.

title: TypedCSIP: Typed Counterfactual Pretraining for Chinese Legislative Conflict Classification
summary:
TypedCSIP is a typed counterfactual pretraining method for the conflict-classification task of the LCR-CN benchmark (Zhao et al.,
  2026): given a (superior, subordinate) provision pair, predict whether the pair conflicts and which of four legal-doctrine types
  (Responsibility, Condition, Sanction, Definition) describes the inconsistency. We exploit LCR-CN's expert-written minimal revisions
  as training-time counterfactual supervision; at test time the classifier reads only the original pair.
  Stage 1 pretrains a shared encoder with a typed Counterfactual Selective Intervention Pretraining objective on (superior,
  subordinate, expert-revised) triplets, treating the expert revision as a counterfactual that the typed factor head must classify as
  carrying no conflict evidence. Stage 2 transfers the encoder to a five-way classification head.
  The confirmatory test was registered on the Open Science Framework before observing v6 measurements: 18 seeds, locked rule requiring
  mean per-seed difference at least 0.8 pp with both seed-bootstrap and Student-t 95% lower bounds above zero. On the 696-record test
  split, the v2 variant improves macro-F1 over the strongest single-model baseline by +0.916 pp on chinese-roberta-wwm-ext and +1.288
  pp on the SAILER cross-backbone replication; both cells pass the rule. A cold-start stratified result on the 244 Unseen-gB records
  keeps the gain positive on both backbones.
  A cross-task diagnostic shows the Stage-2 encoder is classification-specialized and does not transfer to LCR-CN's superior-law
  retrieval task, so we scope the contribution to conflict classification. We release code, 72 pre-registered prediction files,
  matched-seed and MLM-control auxiliaries, and the OSF pre-registration record.

AI.tldr: TypedCSIP is a two-stage typed counterfactual pretraining method that improves legal conflict-classification performance on the LCR-CN benchmark using expert-written minimal revisions as counterfactual supervision.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to improve automatic detection and typing of conflicts between legal provisions in Chinese law, a challenging task in the LCR-CN benchmark. Existing models struggle with fine-grained legal-doctrine types and may not effectively exploit available expert annotations such as minimal revisions. The authors want a method that not only predicts whether two provisions conflict but also which doctrinal type of inconsistency is present, while leveraging high-quality expert-written counterfactuals in a statistically rigorous, pre-registered evaluation framework.
AI.method: They propose TypedCSIP, a two-stage approach. Stage 1 pretrains a shared encoder using a typed Counterfactual Selective Intervention Pretraining objective on triplets of (superior provision, subordinate provision, expert-revised subordinate). The expert revision is treated as a counterfactual example that should contain no conflict evidence, and a typed factor head learns to recognize this by predicting the absence of conflict evidence. Stage 1 thus shapes the encoder to be sensitive to typed conflict cues. In Stage 2, the pretrained encoder is transferred to a five-way classifier (four conflict types plus no-conflict) that, at test time, reads only the original (superior, subordinate) pair without the counterfactual. The training and evaluation are run under a pre-registered confirmatory-testing protocol, with specific rules for statistical significance and multiple seeds, and include replications across different backbone models (chinese-roberta-wwm-ext and SAILER) and a cold-start Unseen-gB subset.
AI.result: On the 696-record main test split of LCR-CN, the v2 TypedCSIP variant improves macro-F1 over the strongest single-model baseline by +0.916 percentage points with the chinese-roberta-wwm-ext backbone and by +1.288 percentage points with the SAILER backbone. Both improvements satisfy the pre-registered confirmatory rule: the mean per-seed difference is at least 0.8 percentage points, and both seed-bootstrap and Student-t 95% lower bounds are above zero. On a 244-record Unseen-gB subset designed as a cold-start stratified test, the method maintains positive macro-F1 gains on both backbones. A cross-task diagnostic indicates that the Stage-2 encoder is specialized for classification and does not transfer well to LCR-CN's superior-law retrieval task.
AI.conclusion: TypedCSIP effectively uses typed counterfactual supervision from expert-written minimal revisions to improve legal conflict-classification performance on LCR-CN, with statistically pre-registered, replicable gains across backbones and subsets. However, the learned encoder is task-specialized and does not generalize to related retrieval tasks, so the contribution is scoped specifically to conflict classification. The authors support transparency and reproducibility by releasing code, prediction files, auxiliary models, and the pre-registration record.

title: Inverting the Shield: Systematically Generating Safety Tests from Policy Specifications
comment: Accepted to the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2026)
summary: The widespread integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) necessitates rigorous and systematic safety evaluation. Existing paradigms either rely on constructed benchmarks to assess safety from predefined perspectives, or employ dynamic red-teaming to probe potential vulnerabilities. While effective, these approaches face challenges, as they depend heavily on expert domain knowledge, offer limited systematic guarantees, and are vulnerable to rapid obsolescence. To address these limitations, we introduce a novel framework POLARIS that brings the rigor of specification-based software testing to AI safety. POLARIS first compiles unstructured natural-language policies into First-Order Logic (FOL) representations, establishing a traceable link between high-level rules and concrete test cases. This formalization enables the construction of a Semantic Policy Graph, where complex policy violation scenarios are encoded as traversable paths. By systematically exploring this graph, POLARIS uncovers compositional violation patterns, which are then instantiated into executable natural-language test queries, enabling coverage-driven and reproducible safety testing. Experiments demonstrate that POLARIS achieves higher policy coverage and attack success counts compared to established baselines. Crucially, by bridging formal methods and AI safety, POLARIS provides a principled, automated approach to ensuring LLMs adhere to safety-critical policies with verifiable traceability. We release our code at https://github.com/huac-lxy/POLARIS.

AI.tldr: POLARIS is a framework that converts natural-language safety policies into formal logic, builds a semantic policy graph, and systematically generates test cases to evaluate LLM safety coverage and violations.
AI.motivation: Current LLM safety evaluation methods rely on static benchmarks or human/LLM red-teaming, which depend on expert knowledge, lack systematic guarantees, and become quickly outdated. The authors want a principled, systematic, and scalable way to test whether LLMs comply with complex safety policies, with formal traceability from policy to test cases.
AI.method: They translate natural-language policies into First-Order Logic (FOL) to get a precise, machine-readable policy specification. From these FOL rules, they construct a Semantic Policy Graph where nodes and edges represent atomic policy elements and their relations, and violation scenarios correspond to paths through the graph. They then systematically explore this graph to identify compositional violation patterns and automatically instantiate them into concrete natural-language prompts that serve as safety test cases. This enables coverage-driven, reproducible testing based on how much of the policy space has been exercised.
AI.result: In experiments on LLM safety evaluation, POLARIS generates tests that cover more of the formalized policy space and triggers more safety violations (attacks) than existing baselines such as standard benchmarks or traditional red-teaming methods, indicating more thorough and effective safety probing.
AI.conclusion: Formalizing safety policies into FOL and using a semantic policy graph enables principled, coverage-driven, and traceable safety testing for LLMs. POLARIS improves policy coverage and vulnerability discovery over prior approaches and demonstrates how formal methods can be integrated into AI safety evaluation. The framework and code are released for the community to use and extend.

title: IndexMem: Learned KV-Cache Eviction with Latent Memory for Long-Context LLM Inference
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly expected to operate over long contexts, yet standard softmax attention incurs a KV cache that grows linearly with sequence length, quickly becoming the bottleneck for long context inference. A practical remedy is to evict less important KV entries; however, existing eviction policies are largely heuristic and struggle to capture the rich, input-dependent distribution of token importance. In this work, we introduce a learnable indexer that predicts KV importance, enabling more accurate retention of critical tokens. Meanwhile, naively evicting tokens permanently discards their information, leading to irreversible forgetting and degraded retrieval over long ranges. To address this, we propose a lightweight latent memory module that compresses evicted tokens into a compact, online-updated state and provides residual readouts to compensate for the attention contributions lost through KV eviction. Collectively, our method enables accurate long-context inference under a bounded KV budget, delivering consistent improvements on RULER (4K/16K) across Qwen, Mistral, and Llama models (up to 25 points under aggressive eviction), markedly more stable Needle-in-a-Haystack retrieval, and superior LongBench scores and compression curves compared to existing eviction policies.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a learnable key-value (KV) importance indexer and a latent memory module to enable accurate long-context inference in LLMs under a fixed KV cache budget, outperforming heuristic eviction policies on several long-context benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Standard softmax attention in LLMs requires storing a KV cache that grows linearly with sequence length, making long-context inference memory- and latency-bottlenecked. Existing approaches that evict KV entries to stay within a budget use heuristic importance measures, which fail to capture input-dependent token importance and cause performance degradation, especially for very long contexts. Additionally, once tokens are evicted, their information is permanently lost, causing irreversible forgetting and poor long-range retrieval.
AI.method: The paper introduces two main components. First, a learnable indexer predicts the importance of each KV entry, enabling data-driven, input-dependent KV eviction decisions instead of hand-crafted heuristics. Second, a lightweight latent memory module maintains a compact, online-updated state that stores compressed information from evicted tokens. When tokens are evicted from the KV cache, their information is written into this latent memory, which later provides residual readouts that approximate the attention contributions those tokens would have had, thus compensating for eviction-induced information loss. The method is applied to existing LLMs (e.g., Qwen, Mistral, Llama) under a bounded KV budget during long-context inference.
AI.result: Under constrained KV budgets and aggressive eviction, the proposed method achieves consistent and sometimes large performance gains on standard long-context benchmarks compared to existing eviction policies. On RULER at 4K and 16K contexts, it improves performance across Qwen, Mistral, and Llama models by up to 25 points under strong eviction. It also yields more stable Needle-in-a-Haystack retrieval performance over long ranges, as well as higher scores and better compression-performance tradeoffs on LongBench relative to prior heuristic eviction approaches.
AI.conclusion: Learned KV importance prediction combined with a latent memory for compressed evicted-token information enables LLMs to perform accurate long-context inference under strict KV cache budgets. This approach mitigates the bottlenecks of linear-growth KV caches, avoids irreversible forgetting from naive eviction, and consistently surpasses heuristic policies on multiple long-context benchmarks, suggesting a practical path toward scalable long-context LLM deployment.

title: TaBIIC2: Interactive Building of Ontological Taxonomies using Weighted Self-Organizing Maps
summary: Ontologies represent the conceptual knowledge of a domain. At the core of an ontology is the taxonomy of concepts and subconcepts that represent specific entities, which can be complex to build. In many cases, information is available in the form of records describing the characteristics of relevant entities, i.e., tabular data. Identifying patterns and similarities in such data can serve as a basis for identifying concepts and organizing them. However, doing so manually can be challenging, and purely automatic approaches, such as agglomerative clustering or relying on a large language model to analyze the data, can leave the user with overwhelming results and little control. In this paper, we describe a tool that enables the progressive and interactive construction of a taxonomy of concepts by identifying clusters as well as their intentional definitions. To do so, we rely on weighted self-organizing maps as a clustering method because they enable the creation of an arbitrary number of clusters that are distinct with respect to the distributions of values of specific characteristics of the clustered entities. We show that, by integrating this mechanism and others for rapidly creating concepts that group together instances from tabular data, this tool represents a middle ground between purely manual analysis and automatic methods for building ontological taxonomies.

AI.tldr: They propose an interactive tool that uses weighted self-organizing maps to help users progressively build and refine an ontology’s concept taxonomy from tabular data.
AI.motivation: Building ontological taxonomies is difficult: manual construction is labor-intensive and requires expert knowledge, while fully automatic clustering or LLM-based analysis can produce many opaque, uncontrollable clusters that overwhelm users. Since many domains already have rich tabular data describing entities, there is an opportunity to semi-automatically discover concepts and structure them, but this needs user control and clarity over how clusters are defined.
AI.method: They design a tool that takes tabular data of entities and their attributes, then applies weighted self-organizing maps (SOMs) to cluster entities. The SOM weighting lets users emphasize particular attributes, resulting in clusters that differ in terms of specific value distributions. The interface supports progressive, interactive refinement: users can inspect clusters, understand their intentional (definitional) characteristics, adjust weights or parameters, and create/merge/split concept nodes to build a taxonomy step by step.
AI.result: Using the weighted SOMs and related mechanisms, the tool can generate an arbitrary number of well-differentiated clusters from tabular data and map them to concept candidates. The interactive workflow lets users quickly group instances into meaningful concepts and organize them, avoiding both the rigidity of manual modeling and the opacity and overload of fully automatic clustering/LLM-only methods.
AI.conclusion: The tool provides a practical middle ground between manual and automatic ontology construction. By combining weighted self-organizing map–based clustering with interactive, progressive taxonomy editing, it helps users derive clear, intentionally defined concepts from tabular data and assemble them into an ontological taxonomy with better control and comprehensibility than fully automated approaches.

title: Retrieval as Reasoning: Self-Evolving Agent-Native Retrieval via LLM-Wiki
summary: LLM agents require retrieval to behave less like one-shot context fetching and more like reasoning: searching, reading, traversing, and deciding when evidence is sufficient. However, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) typically organizes external knowledge as flat chunks retrieved by embedding similarity, exposing a retrieval-as-lookup interface that is poorly aligned with tool-using agents. We propose LLM-Wiki, an agent-native retrieval system that operationalizes the Retrieval-as-Reasoning paradigm by treating external knowledge as a compilable, composable, and self-evolving structure rather than a static retrieval index. LLM-Wiki compiles documents into structured Wiki pages with bidirectional links, exposes search, read, and link-following operations through standard tool-calling interfaces, and introduces an Error Book for persistent structural and semantic self-correction. On HotpotQA, MuSiQue, and 2WikiMultiHopQA, LLM-Wiki outperforms seven baselines, including HippoRAG 2, LightRAG, and GraphRAG, with gains of 2.0-8.1 F1 points over the strongest graph-based baseline and larger gains over Dense RAG. On AuthTrace, LLM-Wiki achieves the best overall accuracy, with especially strong gains on multi-document structured queries, showing that compilation-based knowledge organization generalizes beyond chain-style multi-hop reasoning.

AI.tldr: Proposes LLM-Wiki, an agent-native retrieval system that turns documents into a structured, link-rich wiki so LLM agents can retrieve via reasoning rather than flat similarity search, achieving strong gains on multi-hop and structured QA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation treats retrieval as simple similarity-based lookup over flat chunks, which does not match how tool-using LLM agents naturally operate: they need to search, read, traverse links, and decide when they have enough evidence. Existing graph and dense RAG methods remain limited in structure, adaptivity, and self-correction. There is a need for a retrieval system whose interface and knowledge organization are natively aligned with agentic, multi-step reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce LLM-Wiki, which compiles raw documents into structured wiki-style pages containing entities, concepts, and bidirectional links. It exposes this knowledge through tool-calling APIs that support operations such as search, reading specific pages, and following links, enabling iterative reasoning-driven retrieval. Additionally, it maintains an Error Book that logs and corrects structural and semantic mistakes in the knowledge base over time, making the system self-evolving. The approach is evaluated by plugging LLM-Wiki into LLM agents and comparing against dense and graph-based RAG baselines on multi-hop and structured QA tasks.
AI.result: On multi-hop QA benchmarks HotpotQA, MuSiQue, and 2WikiMultiHopQA, LLM-Wiki surpasses seven baselines, including advanced graph-based systems like HippoRAG 2, LightRAG, and GraphRAG. It yields improvements of roughly 2.0–8.1 F1 points over the strongest graph-based competitor and larger gains relative to standard dense RAG. On the AuthTrace benchmark for structured, often multi-document queries, LLM-Wiki attains the highest overall accuracy, with particularly notable improvements on multi-document structured questions.
AI.conclusion: Treating external knowledge as a compilable, linked wiki-like structure and exposing it via agent-friendly tools enables retrieval-as-reasoning rather than retrieval-as-lookup. LLM-Wiki’s structured, self-correcting knowledge organization better supports multi-hop and structured reasoning than existing dense or graph-based RAG, leading to consistent accuracy gains and suggesting a promising direction for agent-native retrieval systems.

title: ProActor: Timing-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Proactive Task Scheduling Agents
summary:
Proactive task-oriented agents must autonomously anticipate user needs, identify actionable opportunities, and trigger software actions at appropriate moments - fundamentally shifting from reactive systems that await explicit instructions. However, existing approaches lack generalizable end-to-end solutions for measuring and optimizing such anticipatory behaviors.
  This paper introduces ProActor, a unified framework for conversational task scheduling that integrates: (1) a domain-agnostic automated annotation methodology that enables scalable proactiveness reinforcement learning (RL) by generating full opportunity time windows instead of rigid point labels, (2) systematic proactiveness metrics capturing both timing quality and reference action alignment, and (3) RL optimization using GRPO with various reward designs. Our insight is that RULER-based rewards with proactiveness rubrics are crucial for improving timing quality, and that proactiveness optimization enabled by stage-aware composite rewards is key to balancing timing quality and reference action alignment.
  Timing-aware RL requires extensive exploration, demanding efficient infrastructure. We develop ART-F, an adaptive framework combining request-adaptive inference clusters with DDP-based training on single-node multi-GPU systems, enabling LoRA training of 4-bit Qwen2.5-14B-ProActor-Q4 with 4-8x speedups. Experiments on two newly auto-annotated datasets demonstrate significant improvements in proactive timing while maintaining action consistency comparable to state-of-the-art (SOTA) baselines. Ablations validate the effectiveness of distinct composite reward variations.

AI.tldr: The paper presents ProActor, a framework for training and evaluating proactive conversational task agents, with new annotation, metrics, RL rewards, and efficient infrastructure to improve timing of proactive actions.
AI.motivation: Existing conversational agents are mostly reactive and current proactive approaches lack a general, end-to-end way to measure and optimize when and how an agent should autonomously trigger actions. There is also a need for scalable annotation and training methods specialized for timing-aware reinforcement learning in proactive settings.
AI.method: They propose ProActor, which consists of: (1) an automated, domain-agnostic annotation method that creates full time windows during which proactive actions would be appropriate; (2) new metrics that evaluate both timing quality and how well actions align with reference actions; (3) reinforcement learning using GRPO with different reward designs, emphasizing RULER-based rewards with proactiveness rubrics and stage-aware composite rewards; and (4) an efficient infrastructure, ART-F, combining request-adaptive inference clusters and DDP-based multi-GPU training for LoRA-tuned, quantized Qwen2.5-14B models.
AI.result: On two newly auto-annotated datasets, ProActor-trained models show large gains in proactive timing quality while keeping action choice consistency on par with SOTA baselines. Ablation studies confirm that different composite reward designs contribute meaningfully to performance gains.
AI.conclusion: An end-to-end proactive task scheduling framework with domain-agnostic annotation, specialized metrics, carefully designed RL rewards, and efficient infrastructure can substantially improve when agents act proactively without sacrificing action correctness. Reward design—especially RULER-based rubrics and stage-aware composite rewards—is central to balancing timing and alignment with reference behaviors.

title: A Controlled Synthetic Benchmark for Educational Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis
summary: Educational aspect-based sentiment analysis (ABSA) can support course improvement, but public aspect-labeled student feedback remains scarce because educational reviews are private, institution-specific, and expensive to annotate. This study introduces a controlled synthetic benchmark for educational ABSA built from 10,000 synthetic course reviews with explicit train-validation-test splits and a 20-aspect pedagogical schema spanning instructional quality, assessment and course management, learning demand, learning environment, and engagement. The corpus is generated with sampled target labels, sampled nuance attributes, and a realism-tuned prompt refined through a three-cycle judge-editor procedure. On the resulting benchmark, local baselines with TF-IDF, two-step transformers, and joint encoders show that the task is nontrivial; the strongest untuned model, BERT, reaches a held-out detection micro-F1 of 0.2760, while a modest lower-rate BERT schedule improves this to 0.2930. Full-test GPT-based inference with gpt-5.2 reaches 0.2519 micro-F1 in zero-shot mode and 0.2501 with retrieval-based few-shot prompting, placing batch inference above the classical baseline and close to the compact joint encoders. A conservative external evaluation on 2,829 mapped student-feedback reviews from Herath et al. yields a micro-F1 of 0.4593 for BERT on a 9-aspect overlap, indicating partial synthetic-to-real transfer. Realism and faithfulness analyses are reported as generator diagnostics that clarify how the benchmark was stabilized and where label noise remains. The study therefore contributes a synthetic educational ABSA corpus, a documented generation procedure, and a reproducible benchmark setting for a domain in which public labeled data remain difficult to obtain.

AI.tldr: They create a large synthetic benchmark dataset for educational aspect-based sentiment analysis (ABSA), show baseline model performance on it and on a real dataset, and analyze how realistic and reliable the synthetic data are.
AI.motivation: Educational institutions want to use ABSA on student feedback to improve courses, but aspect-annotated educational reviews are rarely public because the data are private, institution-specific, and costly to label. This lack of shared data blocks method development and comparison in this domain. The paper addresses the need for a controlled, shareable benchmark that approximates real educational reviews and provides structured pedagogical aspects.
AI.method: They generate 10,000 synthetic course reviews using a controlled procedure: sampling target aspect labels and nuance attributes, then prompting a large language model with a realism-tuned prompt. The prompt is refined via a three-cycle judge-editor process to improve realism and label faithfulness. They define a 20-aspect pedagogical schema across five broad categories. They then benchmark several models—TF-IDF baselines, two-step transformers, and joint encoders (including BERT)—using fixed train/validation/test splits. They also test GPT-based zero-shot and retrieval-augmented few-shot inference. Finally, they conduct realism and faithfulness diagnostics and test transfer to a real dataset of 2,829 student reviews mapped from Herath et al. on overlapping aspects.
