AI.result: On the synthetic benchmark, traditional and transformer-based models show that the task is challenging. The best untuned model, BERT, achieves a micro-F1 of 0.2760 for aspect detection; using a lower learning-rate schedule raises this to 0.2930. GPT-based full-test inference (gpt-5.2) achieves micro-F1 of 0.2519 in zero-shot and 0.2501 in retrieval-augmented few-shot mode—better than classical baselines and comparable to compact joint encoders. On real student feedback (2,829 mapped reviews, 9 shared aspects), BERT reaches a micro-F1 of 0.4593, showing some transfer from synthetic to real data. Realism and faithfulness analyses highlight improvements from the prompt refinement and locate remaining label noise and limitations in the synthetic corpus.
AI.conclusion: The paper delivers a new synthetic corpus for educational ABSA, a transparent generation and validation pipeline, and a reproducible benchmark with multiple baselines. The results show that the benchmark captures a nontrivial version of educational ABSA and that models trained on it can partially transfer to real-world student feedback. At the same time, remaining label noise and realism gaps are documented, framing the corpus as a useful but imperfect resource for method development in a domain where public labeled data are difficult to obtain.

title: Noise-Robust Financial Numerical Entity Attribute Tagging
summary: Financial Numerical Entity (FNE) understanding aims to recover the meaning of numerical mentions in financial reports. Existing studies primarily focus on concept name prediction and face two important limitations. First, labels derived from inline XBRL may contain errors because filings are usually prepared manually. Second, other important FNE attributes, such as reporting-time relation, measurement scale, and accounting sign, are less emphasized. We propose \textbf{NO}ise-\textbf{R}obust Tagging for Rich Financial Numerical Entity \textbf{A}ttributes (\textsc{NORA}) to address these gaps. NORA uses task-aware instance-specific weighting to attenuate the influence of noisy labels during training, and we further propose the Neighborhood Prior-adjusted KNN (NPK) filtering method for more reliable evaluation on real-world noisy test sets. In addition, we construct a large-scale benchmark containing 6.6 million instances with multi-attribute labels and filing metadata. Experiments show that \textsc{NORA} performs strongly compared with state-of-the-art noisy-label baselines, including Co-teaching, Mixup, SSR, and SelfMix. Moreover, NORA is robust under both unfiltered and noise-filtered test settings. It achieves the best Accuracy, Macro F1, and Weighted F1 for concept name and time-relation prediction, while remaining competitive on scale and sign prediction. These results demonstrate the value of jointly modeling rich FNE attributes while accounting for label noise in real-world financial filings.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NORA, a noise-robust tagging framework to better understand financial numerical entities by predicting multiple attributes while handling noisy labels in real-world filings.
AI.motivation: Financial reports contain many numerical mentions whose meanings (financial numerical entities, FNEs) must be precisely understood, e.g., what concept they refer to, their time context, scale, and accounting sign. Existing work mostly focuses only on predicting the concept name and relies on labels derived from inline XBRL, which are often noisy because filings are manually prepared. This leads to unreliable models and ignores other crucial attributes that are important for downstream financial analysis and decision-making.
AI.method: The authors propose NORA (Noise-Robust Tagging for Rich Financial Numerical Entity Attributes), a tagging framework that jointly predicts multiple FNE attributes (concept name, reporting-time relation, measurement scale, accounting sign). NORA uses task-aware instance-specific weighting to reduce the impact of noisy labels during training. For evaluation on noisy test sets from real filings, they introduce Neighborhood Prior-adjusted KNN (NPK) filtering, which leverages local neighborhood structure to identify and mitigate label noise during testing. They also build a large-scale benchmark dataset with 6.6M instances, each annotated with multi-attribute labels and metadata from financial filings.
AI.result: Across several noisy-label learning baselines (Co-teaching, Mixup, SSR, SelfMix), NORA achieves superior performance. It attains the best Accuracy, Macro F1, and Weighted F1 on concept name and time-relation prediction tasks, and remains competitive for measurement scale and accounting sign prediction. The model is robust under both unfiltered (raw noisy) and noise-filtered test settings, showing that its noise-robust design works in realistic conditions.
AI.conclusion: Jointly modeling multiple attributes of financial numerical entities while explicitly accounting for label noise leads to more accurate and robust FNE understanding. NORA, with instance-specific weighting and NPK-based test-time filtering, provides state-of-the-art performance on a large real-world benchmark and demonstrates that practical financial text analysis must handle noisy supervision rather than assuming clean labels.

title: The Age of Curiosity Meets the Age of AI: Benchmarking Child Safety in Large Language Models
summary: Children increasingly have access to Large Language Models (LLMs), which may expose them to responses that are developmentally inappropriate or require age-sensitive safety, guidance, and boundaries. Existing LLM safety evaluations largely focus on harmful-content avoidance and do not explicitly target child-facing safety. We introduce KIDBench, a benchmark for evaluating child-facing LLM safety for ages 7--11 using a developmental-psychology-grounded LLM-as-a-Judge rubric. KIDBench contains realistic child queries across ten categories, with single-turn prompts and multi-turn child-actor simulations. We compare no-cues prompts with no child context, implicit-cues prompts that suggest a child speaker, and explicit age instructions. Implicit-cues improve scores by 9--47% across models, while explicit age adds a further 10--30% gain. Cross-lingual and cultural evaluations show uneven safety behavior across languages and country contexts. Multi-turn simulations show that child-facing response quality can degrade by 6--24% from the first to worst turn. Beyond evaluation, we introduce KIDGuardLlama, a child-safety evaluator, and KIDLlama, a child-oriented response model, showing how KIDBench supports safer child-facing AI

AI.tldr: The paper presents KIDBench, a benchmark and tools to evaluate and improve how safely large language models interact with children aged 7–11.
AI.motivation: Children are increasingly using LLMs, but current safety evaluations mainly focus on avoiding overtly harmful content and do not address developmentally appropriate, child-specific safety, guidance, and boundaries. There is a need for systematic, psychology-grounded assessment of how models respond to children across scenarios and cultures.
AI.method: The authors build KIDBench, a benchmark grounded in developmental psychology, using an LLM-as-a-judge rubric to score child-facing safety for ages 7–11. The benchmark includes realistic child queries in ten categories, with both single-turn prompts and multi-turn child-actor simulations. They test multiple prompting regimes: no child cues, implicit cues suggesting a child speaker, and explicit age instructions. They also run cross-lingual and cross-country evaluations, and analyze safety degradation over multi-turn conversations. In addition, they train or configure two systems: KIDGuardLlama (a child-safety evaluator) and KIDLlama (a child-oriented response model).
AI.result: Implicit child cues improve safety scores by 9–47% across models, and adding explicit age instructions yields an additional 10–30% improvement. Safety behavior varies across languages and cultural contexts, indicating uneven protection for children globally. Multi-turn conversations reveal that child-facing response quality can degrade by 6–24% from the best to the worst turn. KIDBench, KIDGuardLlama, and KIDLlama are demonstrated as practical tools to evaluate and enhance child-facing safety.
AI.conclusion: KIDBench provides a psychology-informed, systematic way to evaluate and compare LLM safety for child users aged 7–11, revealing substantial gains achievable just by specifying child context and age, as well as important cross-lingual and conversational vulnerabilities. The accompanying KIDGuardLlama and KIDLlama show that the benchmark can guide the development of safer, child-focused AI systems, but also highlight the need for continued attention to multi-turn robustness and global equity in safety behavior.

title: Energy Shields for Fairness
summary:
Runtime fairness is not a one-time constraint but a dynamic property evaluated over a sequence of decisions.
  To ensure fairness at runtime, it is necessary to account for past decisions, information neglected by conventional, static classifiers.
  Traditional fairness shields enforce runtime fairness abruptly,
  by intervening \emph{deterministically} whenever a sequence of decisions violates the target for a running fairness measure. This motivates our \emph{main conceptual contribution: \textbf{energy shields}.}
  An energy shield is a novel, lightweight, adaptive controller that monitors a sequence of decisions and intervenes \emph{probabilistically} to ensure runtime fairness smoothly, by utilizing physics-inspired energy functions to nudge the sequence toward fairness:
  the more unfair the decisions, the stronger the nudging force becomes. This makes energy shields the \emph{\textbf{first}} fairness shields to provide both \emph{short-term safety and long-term liveness guarantees}.
  Safety ensures that the running fairness measure stays within a running target interval with high probability,
  and liveness ensures that the limit of the fairness measure lies within the limit target interval.
  Intuitively, the short-term specifies the tolerated fairness values and the long-term specifies the desired fairness values.
  We also provide a synthesis procedure for constructing the least intrusive energy shield for a given target specification, and demonstrate its efficiency experimentally.
  We evaluate our energy shields against existing fairness shields through the lens of short- and long-term fairness.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces energy shields, probabilistic runtime controllers that enforce fairness over sequences of decisions using energy functions, yielding both short-term safety and long-term fairness guarantees.
AI.motivation: Existing runtime fairness mechanisms (fairness shields) are static and deterministic, reacting abruptly once fairness constraints are violated and ignoring the dynamics and history of decisions. They lack a way to smoothly manage fairness over time and to jointly guarantee short-term bounds and long-term convergence of fairness metrics. The authors aim to design a more adaptive, probabilistic, and theoretically grounded mechanism that can account for past decisions and provide both safety and liveness guarantees for fairness at runtime.
AI.method: The authors define a new construct called an energy shield, modeled as a lightweight, adaptive controller that monitors the evolving sequence of decisions from an underlying classifier or decision system. They design physics-inspired energy functions to measure how far the running fairness measure is from its target interval. Based on this “energy,” the shield intervenes probabilistically: the higher the unfairness (energy), the higher the intervention probability, effectively nudging the system back toward fairness. They formalize safety (staying within a running fairness interval with high probability) and liveness (convergence to a limit fairness interval), then devise a synthesis procedure that constructs the least intrusive energy shield satisfying a given fairness specification. Finally, they implement and experimentally evaluate these shields, comparing them to existing deterministic fairness shields under both short- and long-term criteria.
AI.result: The paper shows that energy shields can be synthesized automatically for given fairness targets and that the resulting shields intervene minimally while still enforcing the specified fairness properties. Experiments demonstrate that compared to conventional deterministic fairness shields, energy shields better manage the trade-off between intervention rate and fairness, achieving smoother behavior and satisfying both short-term safety and long-term liveness specifications more effectively and efficiently.
AI.conclusion: Energy shields provide a new, principled approach to runtime fairness enforcement by moving from deterministic, reactive interventions to probabilistic, energy-based control. This yields the first fairness shields that simultaneously guarantee short-term safety and long-term liveness of fairness measures, while being minimally intrusive. The approach is practical, supported by an effective synthesis procedure and empirical evaluation, and offers a flexible framework to reason about and enforce dynamic, history-dependent fairness requirements in decision-making systems.

title: CRPO: Character-centric Group Relative Policy Optimization for Role-aware Reasoning in Role-playing Agents
summary: Recent advancements in Reinforcement Learning (RL), particularly Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), have significantly enhanced the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models. However, applying these problem-centric optimization methods to role-playing agents often leads to a loss of character fidelity and style collapse, as they prioritize context-specific utility over persona alignment. To address this, we propose Character-Centric Group Relative Policy Optimization (CRPO), a framework designed to realign RL objectives with the role-playing task. CRPO improves character distinctiveness through three mechanisms: decoupling task logic from stylistic rewards to resolve gradient conflicts, dynamically adapting optimization constraints based on character complexity, and utilizing generic responses as negative baselines to prevent the model from reverting to a common distribution. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CRPO outperforms existing methods in consistency, emotion and others.

AI.tldr: Introduces CRPO, a reinforcement learning framework tailored for role-playing language agents, preserving character fidelity while improving reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing RL methods like GRPO improve reasoning but harm persona consistency in role-playing agents, causing style collapse and loss of character fidelity. There is a need for RL objectives that respect both task performance and stable, distinctive character behavior.
AI.method: Proposes Character-Centric Group Relative Policy Optimization (CRPO), which (1) separates task-logic rewards from stylistic rewards to avoid conflicting gradients, (2) adapts optimization constraints dynamically based on character complexity, and (3) uses generic responses as negative baselines so the model is penalized for collapsing to common, non-distinctive outputs.
AI.result: Experiments show that CRPO leads to better character distinctiveness and alignment than existing RL approaches, with improvements in metrics such as consistency and emotion expression.
AI.conclusion: CRPO successfully reorients RL training toward character-centric objectives, mitigating style collapse and improving persona fidelity in role-playing agents while maintaining strong task reasoning abilities.

title: Towards Multi-Turn Dialog Systems for Industrial Asset Operations and Maintenance
summary: Industrial asset operations and maintenance question answering is inherently multi-turn, iterative, and highly dependent on external tool invocation. However, the conventional plan-execute single-agent architecture exhibits clear limitations in maintaining cross-turn context, and reusing intermediate results. In this paper, we present a multi-turn dialog system designed for industrial scenarios based on a supervisor-specialist multi-agent architecture. To alleviate tool invocation bottlenecks, the system incorporates structured artifact reuse, dynamic replanning, and parallel tool execution. Evaluation results show that our system achieves better response quality compared with the baseline, with planning effectiveness increasing by 54.5% and task completion improving by 37.8%. System profiling further shows that cross-turn artifact reuse effectively reduces redundant tool invocation, decreasing the tool-time share from 47.3% to 26.3% and making turns 2-5 approximately 4.2x faster than the first turn.

AI.tldr: They propose a multi-agent, multi-turn industrial QA dialog system that reuses intermediate artifacts and runs tools in parallel to improve quality and speed over a single-agent baseline.
AI.motivation: Industrial asset operations and maintenance involve complex, multi-turn question answering that relies heavily on external tools. Traditional single-agent plan-execute architectures struggle to preserve context across turns and cannot effectively reuse intermediate computation or results, leading to inefficiencies and lower answer quality.
AI.method: They design a supervisor-specialist multi-agent dialog architecture tailored to industrial scenarios. The system supports structured artifact reuse across turns, dynamic replanning as the dialog evolves, and parallel execution of external tools to reduce latency and avoid bottlenecks in tool calls.
AI.result: Compared to a single-agent baseline, the proposed system improves response quality, boosts planning effectiveness by 54.5%, and increases task completion by 37.8%. Profiling shows cross-turn artifact reuse cuts the proportion of time spent in tools from 47.3% to 26.3% and speeds up dialog turns 2–5 by roughly 4.2x relative to the first turn.
AI.conclusion: A supervisor-specialist multi-agent architecture with artifact reuse, dynamic replanning, and parallel tool calls is more effective for industrial multi-turn QA than conventional single-agent plans. It yields better answers, higher task completion, and substantially lower tool latency, especially in later turns, demonstrating the value of cross-turn artifact reuse in tool-heavy dialog systems.

title: Is Inference Mediated by Distinct Semantic Structures in LLMs? A Mechanistic Interpretation
summary: Predicting a label correctly does not necessarily require representing the operation that produces it. Transformer representations are known to carry label-level information, but whether they encode semantic operations producing those labels is unclear. We investigate this in Natural Language Inference using controlled premise-hypothesis pairs that differ by a single semantic transformation. Using layer-wise activations, we estimate operation-level subspaces via SVD and test their causal relevance through activation steering in four open-weight decoder models. Transformation effects are decodable with $84.8$-$99\%$ accuracy and occupy partially distinct but overlapping subspaces, exceeding random-subspace baselines. Steering experiments show that these directions causally influence predictions, though steerability varies across models; cross-operation steering further reveals structured interference and a dissociation between subspace selectivity and cross-operation independence. These findings indicate that the models encode not only that a hypothesis relates to a premise but also, in part, how it does so, implying that mechanistic analysis and control should operate at the level of semantic operations rather than predicted labels alone.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether transformer language models encode not just NLI labels (entailment/contradiction/etc.) but the underlying semantic operations that relate a hypothesis to a premise, using operation-level subspaces and activation steering.
AI.motivation: Prior work shows transformer representations contain label-level information for tasks like Natural Language Inference (NLI), but it is unclear if models internally represent the semantic operations (e.g., negation, strengthening, changing quantifiers) that turn a premise into a hypothesis. Understanding this matters for mechanistic interpretability and for controlling model behavior beyond surface predictions.
AI.method: The authors construct controlled NLI premise–hypothesis pairs that differ by a single, known semantic transformation. For several open-weight decoder-only transformers, they extract layer-wise activations, estimate low-dimensional subspaces corresponding to each semantic operation using singular value decomposition (SVD), and test how well these subspaces encode the operations via linear decoding. They then perform activation steering: adding or subtracting these operation-direction vectors during inference to see whether predictions causally change in ways consistent with those semantic operations. They also test cross-operation steering (using one operation’s subspace while another is present) to study interference and independence between operations.
AI.result: Semantic transformation effects are linearly decodable from model activations with high accuracy (84.8–99%), and each operation occupies a partially distinct but overlapping subspace that is more structured than random baselines. Activation steering along these subspaces reliably shifts NLI predictions in expected directions, demonstrating causal relevance, though the strength and robustness of steering differ across models. Cross-operation steering reveals systematic interference patterns and shows that an operation’s subspace can be highly selective without being independent from other operations.
AI.conclusion: Transformer models do not only encode that a hypothesis is entailed, contradicted, or neutral with respect to a premise; they also partly encode the specific semantic operation relating them. These operation-level representations are organized in meaningful subspaces that can be causally manipulated. Therefore, mechanistic interpretability and intervention in language models should focus on semantic operations and their subspaces, rather than solely on final predicted labels.

title: Mitigating Object Hallucinations in Vision-Language Models through Region-Aware Attention Recalibration
summary: The generation of factually incorrect objects, commonly known as object hallucination, remains a persistent challenge in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs). Current approaches to address this issue - ranging from expensive data-driven fine-tuning and high-latency contrastive decoding to rigid attention head truncation - frequently compromise either computational efficiency or the continuity of the model's feature space. To overcome these limitations, we introduce a novel, training-free inference strategy that operates as a region-aware adaptive weighting mechanism to dynamically correct semantic drift without relying on abrupt heuristic truncations. By computing an outlier-resistant statistical midpoint across various attention heads, we establish a stable anchor for reliable visual representations. We then utilize the inter-head disagreement mapped across regions to dynamically determine intervention budgets, gently suppressing hallucination-inducing attention paths through a continuous penalty modulation. This recalibration process effectively rectifies visual-semantic misalignments while fully preserving generative fluency and language priors. Comprehensive evaluations on standard multimodal benchmarks, including CHAIR, POPE, and MME, reveal that our strategy substantially curtails both instance- and sentence-level hallucinations. The results demonstrate state-of-the-art performance against contemporary baselines, confirming our method's efficiency and algorithmic robustness. Our code will be public.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a training-free, region-aware attention reweighting method to reduce object hallucinations in large vision-language models, achieving state-of-the-art hallucination mitigation on multiple benchmarks while preserving fluency and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Object hallucination—factually incorrect objects described in model outputs—remains a key weakness of LVLMs. Existing fixes rely on costly retraining, slow decoding schemes, or crude pruning of attention heads, all of which either hurt efficiency or disrupt the model’s learned feature space. There is a need for an efficient, training-free method that can reliably reduce hallucinations without degrading generative quality.
AI.method: The authors introduce a training-free inference algorithm that adaptively reweights attention based on region-wise inter-head agreement. They first compute an outlier-robust statistical midpoint across multiple attention heads to serve as a stable anchor for visual features. Then, they measure disagreement among heads over image regions to identify potentially hallucination-inducing attention paths. A continuous penalty is applied to these paths according to a dynamically allocated intervention budget, softly suppressing harmful attention without hard truncation, thus maintaining continuity of the model’s representation space.
AI.result: On standard multimodal hallucination benchmarks such as CHAIR, POPE, and MME, the proposed method substantially reduces both instance-level and sentence-level hallucinations. Experimental comparisons show that it achieves state-of-the-art performance relative to contemporary baseline approaches while remaining computationally efficient and robust.
AI.conclusion: A region-aware, adaptive attention reweighting scheme can effectively mitigate object hallucinations in LVLMs without any training or discrete pruning. By anchoring visual representations via an outlier-resistant midpoint and gently penalizing high-disagreement attention paths, the method corrects semantic drift while preserving language fluency and priors, achieving superior and robust performance on hallucination benchmarks. The authors plan to release their code.

title: BC Protocol: Structured Dual-Expert Dialogue for Eliciting High-Quality Chain-of-Thought Post-Training Data
summary:
High-quality expert chain-of-thought (CoT) data is one of the core bottlenecks in large language model (LLM) post-training. Existing data production methods each have structural limitations: crowdsourced annotation lacks deep reasoning paths; expert solo writing is constrained by the "expert blind spot" -- experts structurally skip reasoning steps they consider obvious; RLHF only produces preference signals rather than reasoning chains.
  This paper proposes the BC Protocol -- a structured dual-expert elicitation method for LLM post-training data production. The method carefully pairs a domain expert (crystallized intelligence) with a knowledge engineer (fluid intelligence), systematically externalizing the expert's implicit judgments as natural language reasoning chains. We introduce the Participant Aptitude Model, which defines six participant characteristic dimensions that affect elicitation quality. "Calibrated Ignorance" is an original concept proposed in this paper. We further propose "Selection-over-Prescription" as a methodological principle: for implicit knowledge elicitation tasks, investing quality-control resources in personnel selection yields a higher return than investing the same resources in process design.
  In a controlled experiment in the narrative fiction domain, we directly compared CoT produced by BC Protocol dual dialogue (Group A, (n=20)) against CoT written independently by the same domain expert (Group B, (n=20)). Three cross-vendor judge models -- GPT-4o, Claude Opus 4.5, and Gemini 2.5 Pro -- conducted blind evaluation across five dimensions (600 ratings total). Results show that the BC Protocol achieves an overwhelming advantage in "naturalness of reasoning process" (Group A mean 4.80 vs. Group B mean 1.30, (p=2.4\times10^{-8}), Cliff's (δ=1.0)).

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the BC Protocol, a dual-expert method to elicit high-quality chain-of-thought data for LLM training, showing it significantly improves the naturalness of reasoning compared to solo expert writing.
AI.motivation: High-quality chain-of-thought (CoT) data is critical yet hard to obtain for LLM post-training. Existing approaches have key weaknesses: crowdsourcing lacks deep reasoning, experts writing alone skip implicit steps due to the expert blind spot, and RLHF yields preference labels rather than explicit reasoning chains. The authors aim to design a data production protocol that overcomes these structural limitations and reliably externalizes experts’ tacit reasoning in natural language form.
AI.method: The authors propose the BC Protocol, a structured dual-expert elicitation method that pairs a domain expert (crystallized intelligence) with a knowledge engineer (fluid intelligence). Through guided dialogue, the knowledge engineer prompts and structures the expert’s implicit judgments into explicit reasoning chains. They formalize the Participant Aptitude Model with six characteristic dimensions affecting elicitation quality, introduce the concept of “Calibrated Ignorance,” and argue for the methodological principle of “Selection-over-Prescription,” prioritizing careful participant selection over rigid process design. They then run a controlled experiment in narrative fiction: the same expert produces CoT in two conditions—(A) via BC Protocol dual dialogue and (B) independently writing CoT—yielding 20 samples per group. Three independent LLM judge models (GPT-4o, Claude Opus 4.5, Gemini 2.5 Pro) provide blind ratings on five dimensions, totaling 600 evaluations.
AI.result: In the narrative fiction experiment, CoT produced using the BC Protocol (Group A, n=20) was compared directly to solo expert CoT (Group B, n=20). Across three cross-vendor judge models and five evaluation dimensions, the BC Protocol showed a decisive advantage in at least one key dimension: “naturalness of reasoning process.” Group A achieved a mean score of 4.80 versus Group B’s 1.30, with very strong statistical significance (p = 2.4×10^-8) and a maximal effect size (Cliff’s δ = 1.0).
AI.conclusion: The BC Protocol is an effective method for eliciting high-quality, natural chain-of-thought data for LLM post-training by pairing domain experts with knowledge engineers. Carefully selected participants, characterized by the Participant Aptitude Model and guided by the principle of “Selection-over-Prescription,” can overcome expert blind spots and expose implicit reasoning more fully than solo expert writing. The experimental results in narrative fiction support the protocol’s superiority at least in terms of perceived naturalness of reasoning, suggesting broader applicability to other domains where tacit expert knowledge must be converted into explicit reasoning chains.

title: NeurIPS: Neuro-anatomical Inductive Priors for Sphere-based Brain Decoding
summary: Current fMRI decoders face a performance-fidelity trade-off where efficient ID encoders outperform geometrically faithful surface-based models. We argue this is partly driven by inefficient surface tokenization and the failure to use anatomy as a predictive signal. We present NeurIPS, a framework that improves surface-based decoding by reframing anatomical variation from a nuisance to a powerful inductive prior. NeurIPS unites two innovations: a Selective ROI Spherical Tokenizer (SRST) for efficient geometric encoding, and a Structure-Guided Mixture of Experts (SG-MoE) that explicitly models individual anatomy using cortical features. On the Natural Scenes Dataset, NeurIPS establishes a new state-of-the-art for surface decoders and achieves performance comparable to strong 1D baselines. This is achieved with unprecedented efficiency, as the model converges dramatically faster (10 vs. 600 epochs). This efficiency enables rapid adaptation to new subjects using only 20% of data and ensures robust scalability as the training cohort is expanded. Ablations provide causal evidence that these gains are driven by the model's use of cortical features, not by memorizing subject IDs. By leveraging anatomical priors, NeurIPS provides a principled and scalable path toward robust, generalizable brain decoding.

AI.tldr: NeurIPS is a new surface-based fMRI decoding framework that uses better cortical surface tokenization and anatomical features to achieve state-of-the-art accuracy with far higher training efficiency and better subject generalization.
AI.motivation: Existing fMRI decoders must trade off between performance and geometric fidelity: efficient 1D ID-based encoders perform well but ignore cortical geometry, while surface-based models respect cortical geometry but are inefficient and underperform. Moreover, most approaches treat inter-subject anatomical differences as noise instead of exploiting them as informative structure. The paper aims to overcome this by designing a surface-based decoder that is both geometrically faithful and computationally efficient, while explicitly leveraging anatomy as a predictive prior.
AI.method: The authors introduce NeurIPS, which combines (1) a Selective ROI Spherical Tokenizer (SRST) that converts cortical surface data into a compact and geometrically meaningful token representation focused on regions of interest, and (2) a Structure-Guided Mixture of Experts (SG-MoE) that uses cortical anatomical features to condition or route between experts, thereby explicitly modeling individual anatomical variability rather than treating it as a nuisance. They train and evaluate this architecture on the Natural Scenes Dataset and perform ablation studies to dissect the contribution of cortical features and subject-specific information.
AI.result: On the Natural Scenes Dataset, NeurIPS achieves state-of-the-art performance among surface-based decoders and reaches accuracy comparable to strong 1D baseline models. It does so with dramatically higher data and compute efficiency, converging in about 10 epochs versus roughly 600 for competing methods. The model can adapt to new subjects using only 20% of their data and scales robustly as the size of the training cohort increases. Ablation experiments indicate that performance improvements are driven by the explicit use of cortical features rather than simple memorization of subject IDs.
AI.conclusion: NeurIPS demonstrates that treating anatomical variation as a useful inductive prior instead of noise leads to large gains in both performance and efficiency for surface-based fMRI decoding. The combination of efficient spherical tokenization and anatomy-informed mixture-of-experts yields a scalable, geometrically faithful decoder that generalizes well across subjects and can be rapidly adapted to new individuals, offering a principled direction for future brain decoding models that leverage structural information.

title: PennySynth: RAG-Driven Data Synthesis for Automated Quantum Code Generation
summary: The growing complexity of quantum programming frameworks has exposed a critical limitation in existing large language model (LLM)-based code assistants: general-purpose models hallucinate PennyLane-specific gate names, misplace device configurations, and produce structurally invalid circuits when faced with specialized quantum coding challenges. We present PennySynth, a retrieval-augmented generation framework that addresses this gap by conditioning LLM inference on a curated knowledge base of 13,389 PennyLane instruction-code pairs, built via a three-stage extraction, verification, and deduplication pipeline over official PennyLane repositories, community GitHub sources, and QHack competition archives. PennySynth introduces a code-aware embedding strategy using st-codesearch-distilroberta-base, trained for natural-language-to-code retrieval, increasing average retrieval cosine similarity from 0.45 to 0.726 compared to a general-purpose baseline. Evaluated across 74 challenges spanning three years of the QHack competition (2022, 2023, 2024), PennySynth achieves 64%, 68%, and 52% pass@5 on QHack 2022, 2023, and 2024, respectively, improving over Claude Sonnet 4.6 without retrieval by +28, +25, and +28 percentage points. We further introduce a quantum-adapted CodeBLEU metric that upweights qml.* token patterns and show that structural code similarity and functional correctness capture distinct aspects of quantum code quality. Controlled ablations reveal that code-aware embeddings are the primary driver of retrieval performance, while dataset expansion and source composition provide additional gains when retrieval quality is sufficiently precise.

AI.tldr: PennySynth is a retrieval-augmented framework that grounds LLMs in a curated PennyLane code corpus to generate more accurate quantum programs, substantially outperforming a strong general-purpose LLM baseline on QHack challenges.
AI.motivation: General-purpose LLM code assistants perform poorly on specialized quantum programming tasks in PennyLane, hallucinating gate names, misconfiguring devices, and producing invalid circuits. There is a need for a domain-adapted system that can reliably interpret natural language quantum tasks and synthesize correct PennyLane code by leveraging authoritative examples.
AI.method: They build PennySynth, a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system. First, they construct a knowledge base of 13,389 high-quality PennyLane instruction–code pairs from official PennyLane repositories, community GitHub projects, and QHack archives using a three-stage pipeline: extraction, verification, and deduplication. For retrieval, they adopt a code-aware embedding model, st-codesearch-distilroberta-base, trained for natural-language-to-code mapping, replacing generic embeddings. During inference, the user query is embedded, semantically similar PennyLane examples are retrieved, and these are provided as context to an LLM to condition code generation. They also define a quantum-adapted CodeBLEU metric that emphasizes qml.* token patterns to better capture structural similarity of PennyLane programs. Ablation studies vary embeddings, dataset size, and source mixture to isolate which factors drive performance.
AI.result: Using code-aware embeddings increases average retrieval cosine similarity from 0.45 to 0.726 over a general-purpose baseline. On 74 QHack challenges from 2022–2024, PennySynth attains pass@5 of 64%, 68%, and 52% on the 2022, 2023, and 2024 sets, respectively. This represents gains of +28, +25, and +28 percentage points over Claude Sonnet 4.6 without retrieval. The quantum-adapted CodeBLEU metric demonstrates that structural similarity (per CodeBLEU) and functional correctness (pass@k) do not perfectly correlate, indicating they capture different dimensions of solution quality. Ablations show code-aware embeddings are the dominant factor for high-quality retrieval, while increasing dataset size and diversifying sources yield additional improvements once retrieval precision is high enough.
AI.conclusion: PennySynth shows that carefully designed retrieval-augmented generation, grounded in a large curated corpus of PennyLane code and powered by code-aware embeddings, substantially improves LLM performance on specialized quantum programming tasks. Quantum-specific evaluation, like the adapted CodeBLEU, is necessary to complement functional metrics. The main performance lever is embedding quality, while corpus scale and composition provide secondary benefits when retrieval is already accurate. This approach suggests a viable path to building reliable, framework-specific coding assistants for other niche programming domains.

title: Privacy-Preserving Local Language Models for Longitudinal Data Retrieval in Chronic Dermatologic Disease: Implementation in Pemphigus Patients
summary: Chronic dermatologic diseases such as pemphigus require long-term follow-up, generating extensive longitudinal clinical documentation that is difficult to review comprehensively during routine visits and increasing clinician workload as well as the risk of missing critical historical information. We evaluated whether a locally deployed, privacy-preserving small language model (SLM) could retrieve structured clinical features and generate longitudinal summaries from long-term dermatology follow-up records. In this retrospective case series, thirty pemphigus patients contributed 541 visit notes that were aggregated into full longitudinal records (89,336 words); 56 clinically relevant features were annotated by two expert dermatologists. The locally deployed SLM (Qwen3 4B Thinking 2507) was queried with each complete record to retrieve 56 features and generate one final report summaries. Across 1,680 feature retrieval tasks, mean accuracy was 82.25%. Dermatologists' ratings of AI-generated summaries were high for overall quality (8.23-8.47), clinical accuracy (7.93-8.20), and usefulness (8.47-8.50), with no significant inter-evaluator differences and an overall preference for AI summaries in 53.3% of evaluations. These findings suggest that privacy-preserving, locally deployed SLMs can outperform medical experts and reliably generate clinically meaningful longitudinal summaries. SLMs may support clinical decision-making when integrated with appropriate oversight.

AI.tldr: The paper tests a local small language model on long-term dermatology notes for pemphigus and finds it can accurately extract key features and generate high-quality longitudinal summaries that clinicians often prefer.
AI.motivation: Chronic dermatologic diseases like pemphigus produce large volumes of longitudinal notes that are hard for clinicians to review quickly, increasing workload and the risk of overlooking important historical information. There is a need for tools that can efficiently summarize and structure this information while preserving patient privacy by running locally rather than in the cloud.
AI.method: The authors performed a retrospective case series of 30 pemphigus patients, aggregating 541 visit notes into complete longitudinal records totaling 89,336 words. Two expert dermatologists annotated 56 clinically relevant features for each case as a reference standard. A locally deployed small language model (Qwen3 4B Thinking 2507) was prompted with each full record to retrieve those 56 features and to generate a final longitudinal summary report for each patient. Model performance on feature retrieval was compared against the expert annotations, and dermatologists rated the AI-generated summaries for overall quality, clinical accuracy, and usefulness, and expressed preferences between AI and standard documentation.
AI.result: Across 1,680 feature retrieval tasks, the small language model achieved a mean accuracy of 82.25% in identifying the 56 annotated features. Dermatologists rated the AI-generated summaries highly, with scores around 8–8.5 out of 10 for overall quality, clinical accuracy, and usefulness, with no significant differences between evaluators. In 53.3% of evaluations, clinicians preferred the AI-generated summaries over existing documentation approaches, indicating that the model can at least match and sometimes exceed expert performance in synthesizing longitudinal records.
AI.conclusion: Privacy-preserving, locally deployed small language models can reliably extract structured clinical information and produce clinically meaningful longitudinal summaries from extensive dermatology follow-up records. Their performance can match or surpass that of medical experts in summarization tasks, suggesting they could support clinical decision-making and reduce documentation burden when integrated into workflows with appropriate human oversight and safety measures.

title: Multilingual Phonological Feature Recognition with Self-Supervised Speech Models
summary: Phonological features provide a language-general and linguistically grounded representation of speech. We present PhonoQ-2.0, a multilingual frame-level phonological feature recognizer built on self-supervised speech models. The system directly predicts a structured 22-dimensional feature vector per frame encoding manner, vowel quality, place, and voicing, instead of deriving features from phoneme outputs. To ensure phonologically coherent predictions, we introduce a manner-conditioned gating mechanism that activates valid feature groups. Evaluated across multiple languages and corpora, PhonoQ-2.0 achieves an average macro-F1 of 91.3% in-domain and 88.9% out-of-domain. Compared to a strong CTC phoneme baseline, it delivers consistent gains of +8.8 F1 in-domain and +8.6 out-of-domain on average. In unseen-language evaluation, PhonoQ-2.0 improves macro-F1 from 66.9% to 73.6% (+6.7 on average), with gains of up to +10.8 points.

AI.tldr: PhonoQ-2.0 is a multilingual model that directly predicts phonological feature vectors from self-supervised speech representations, outperforming phoneme-based baselines across seen and unseen languages.
AI.motivation: Phonological features are a compact, linguistically grounded, and cross-lingual way to represent speech, but most systems derive them indirectly from phoneme recognition, which can be error-prone and language-dependent. There is a need for a direct, multilingual feature recognizer that generalizes well across domains and unseen languages.
AI.method: The authors build PhonoQ-2.0, a frame-level phonological feature recognizer on top of self-supervised speech models. For each frame, it predicts a 22-dimensional structured feature vector encoding manner of articulation, vowel quality, place of articulation, and voicing. Instead of mapping from phonemes to features, the model outputs features directly. A manner-conditioned gating mechanism is introduced to enforce phonological coherence: depending on the predicted manner category, it selectively activates only those feature groups that are valid for that manner.
AI.result: Across multiple languages and corpora, PhonoQ-2.0 reaches average macro-F1 scores of 91.3% on in-domain data and 88.9% on out-of-domain data. Relative to a strong CTC-based phoneme recognizer used as a baseline, it yields consistent improvements of about +8.8 macro-F1 in-domain and +8.6 out-of-domain. In evaluation on unseen languages, it raises macro-F1 from 66.9% to 73.6% on average, with maximum gains up to +10.8 points.
AI.conclusion: Direct prediction of structured phonological features from self-supervised speech representations, combined with manner-conditioned gating, yields phonologically coherent and more accurate feature recognition than phoneme-based pipelines. PhonoQ-2.0 generalizes well across domains and provides substantial performance gains, including for unseen languages, highlighting its value as a robust, language-general representation for speech processing tasks.

title: AION: Next-Generation Tasks and Practical Harness for Time Series
comment: Project page and code are available at https://github.com/ztxtech/aion
summary: Time series research is moving beyond fixed forecasting benchmarks toward realistic tasks that combine prediction, contextual reasoning, tool use, and structured decision support. Most benchmarks are built around clean data and short evaluation loops; agents alone may miss temporal constraints, evidence checks, or review before finalizing outputs. We first formalize next-generation time series tasks as three-component tuples consisting of a task file, a workspace, and a validation interface. We then present AION, a time series harness built from six component groups: agents, skills, rules, memory, evaluation, and protocols. In this harness, we use three design principles: temporal grounding, temporal knowledge-grounded reasoning, and reliability mechanisms such as post-experiment analysis and layered review. A Kaggle Store Sales case study shows that the harness produces more detailed process traces, more artifacts, and more review steps than the same base agent operating in OpenCode direct build mode. Taken together, these results argue for a paradigm shift from fixed tasks to realistic ones under real-world constraints.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AION, a new harness for realistic time-series tasks that integrates agents, tools, rules, memory, evaluation, and protocols to better reflect real-world constraints.
AI.motivation: Existing time series benchmarks focus on clean data and simple, fixed forecasting tasks that do not capture realistic workflows involving prediction, contextual reasoning, tool use, and structured decisions. Standard agent setups often ignore temporal constraints, validation, and review, leading to less reliable and less auditable outcomes.
AI.method: The authors formalize next-generation time series tasks as tuples of a task file, workspace, and validation interface, then design AION, a harness composed of agents, skills, rules, memory, evaluation, and protocols. They embed principles of temporal grounding, knowledge-grounded temporal reasoning, and reliability mechanisms like post-experiment analysis and layered review. They compare AION to a baseline agent running in an OpenCode direct build mode on a Kaggle Store Sales task.
AI.result: In the Kaggle Store Sales case study, AION generates richer process traces, more intermediate artifacts, and more review steps than the baseline agent, indicating more structured and auditable workflows under temporal and validation constraints.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that time series research should move away from narrow, fixed forecasting benchmarks towards more realistic task formulations that integrate temporal grounding, reasoning, tool use, and reliability checks, with AION serving as an example framework for such next-generation setups.

title: Toward a Benchmark for Controllable Simulation of Imperfect Students with Large Language Models
summary: Teacher education requires deliberate practice with learners who exhibit identifiable strengths, weaknesses, and partial mastery. Large language models could support such practice by simulating students with known skill components, enabling teachers to rehearse explanations, diagnoses, and instructional responses. For this purpose, however, the central requirement is neither to maximize benchmark accuracy nor to suppress isolated facts, but to control model behavior so that it reflects a specified skill profile. This paper investigates whether prompted language models can be steered to retain some skills while suppressing others. We introduce a benchmark-oriented framework in which an explicit skill vector represents a simulated student, prompt-based control specifies retained and missing competencies, and behavior is evaluated using profile-alignment metrics, retained-versus-forgotten comparisons, and cross-skill calibration analyses. The results show that selective partial mastery can be induced and measured in a structured mathematics setting, although the degree of controllability remains model-dependent. These findings position controllable learner simulation as a distinct research problem at the intersection of teacher education, educational simulation, and language-model control.

AI.tldr: The paper explores controlling large language models to simulate students with specific patterns of partial mastery (some skills present, others missing) for use in teacher education practice.
AI.motivation: Teacher education needs realistic, controllable practice environments where teachers can rehearse diagnosing and responding to students who have nuanced strengths and weaknesses. Existing work on large language models focuses on accuracy or safety, not on precisely shaping behavior to match a target skill profile. The authors are motivated to see if LLMs can be reliably steered to behave like learners with specified partial mastery of mathematical skills.
AI.method: The authors propose a benchmark framework where a simulated student is represented by an explicit skill vector indicating which skills are present or absent. They use prompt-based control techniques to instruct a language model which competencies to retain and which to suppress. They then evaluate the model’s behavior using (1) profile-alignment metrics (how closely outputs match the target skill profile), (2) comparisons of retained vs. forgotten skills (whether the model actually performs well only on the designated skills), and (3) cross-skill calibration analyses (how consistently the model applies the intended skill configuration across tasks). The experiments are conducted in a structured mathematics domain.
AI.result: The experiments demonstrate that it is indeed possible to induce and quantify selective partial mastery in LLMs for mathematics tasks. Models can be made to exhibit correct performance on some skills while systematically failing or omitting others, according to the specified profile. However, the strength and reliability of this controllability vary across different language models; some adhere more faithfully to the desired skill configurations than others.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that simulating learners with controllable partial mastery is feasible with current language models, but the level of control is still limited and model-dependent. It frames “controllable learner simulation” as a new, distinct research direction that combines concerns from teacher education, educational simulation, and LLM control, suggesting future work on more robust steering mechanisms and broader educational domains.

title: Evolutionary Enhanced Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Cooperative Air Combat
summary: As modern air combat evolves toward beyond-visual-range (BVR) multi-aircraft cooperative engagements, autonomous decision-making for unmanned combat aerial vehicles (UCAVs) faces significant challenges due to high-dimensional state spaces, discrete action commands, and strongly adversarial dynamic environments. To overcome the limitations of existing multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) methods in such settings, namely insufficient exploration efficiency, low sample utilization, and poor policy generalization, we propose Adversarial Curriculum and Evolutionary-enhanced Multi-agent Proximal Policy Optimization (ACE-MAPPO), a hybrid learning framework that integrates evolutionary algorithms with MAPPO. Specifically, a genetic soft update mechanism is introduced to enhance population diversity and mitigate convergence to local optima. An evolutionary-augmented prioritized trajectory replay strategy is further employed to improve the utilization of sparse high-value samples. In addition, an adversarial evolutionary curriculum learning mechanism is designed to enable adaptive training with progressively increasing difficulty. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms MAPPO and other baseline algorithms in terms of training stability, convergence speed, and win rate, validating its effectiveness in multi-aircraft cooperative air combat scenarios.

AI.tldr: ACE-MAPPO is a hybrid multi-agent reinforcement learning framework that improves UCAV cooperative air combat performance by combining MAPPO with evolutionary strategies, curriculum learning, and prioritized replay to achieve better stability, convergence, and win rates.
AI.motivation: Modern beyond-visual-range multi-aircraft cooperative air combat presents a highly complex, adversarial decision-making problem for UCAVs, characterized by high-dimensional states, discrete action spaces, and dynamic opponents. Existing MARL methods like MAPPO struggle with exploration efficiency, sample utilization, and policy generalization, limiting their effectiveness in such realistic combat scenarios. The paper aims to overcome these shortcomings so that autonomous UCAVs can reliably handle challenging multi-aircraft engagements.
AI.method: The paper proposes ACE-MAPPO, which augments Multi-agent Proximal Policy Optimization with several evolutionary and curriculum mechanisms. First, a genetic soft update mechanism maintains a diverse population of policies and reduces the risk of converging to local optima. Second, an evolutionary-augmented prioritized trajectory replay strategy focuses learning on sparse yet high-value trajectories, improving sample efficiency. Third, an adversarial evolutionary curriculum learning mechanism automatically adjusts the difficulty of training scenarios, increasing it over time to enhance robustness and generalization in adversarial environments.
AI.result: Through extensive experiments in simulated multi-aircraft cooperative air combat environments, ACE-MAPPO is shown to outperform standard MAPPO and other baselines. The improvements are reported in terms of training stability (less variance and more reliable learning), faster convergence (fewer training steps to reach strong performance), and higher win rates against adversaries in BVR combat scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that integrating evolutionary strategies and adversarial curriculum learning into MAPPO can significantly enhance MARL performance in complex UCAV cooperative air combat tasks. ACE-MAPPO effectively addresses key limitations of existing MARL approaches—exploration, sample utilization, and generalization—and represents a promising direction for autonomous decision-making in realistic, high-dimensional, adversarial multi-agent environments.

title: DVAO: Dynamic Variance-adaptive Advantage Optimization for Multi-reward Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement Learning has become a standard paradigm for aligning Large Language Models with human intent and task requirements. While Group Relative Policy Optimization offers an efficient, value-model-free alternative to Proximal Policy Optimization, adapting it to real-world multi-reward settings remains challenging. Standard scalarization practices, such as Reward Combination and Advantage Combination, suffer from significant drawbacks: Reward Combination frequently generates advantages with excessively large squared magnitudes that lead to training instability, while Advantage Combination relies on static hyperparameters and ignores cross-objective correlations. To address these limitations, we propose Dynamic Variance-adaptive Advantage Optimization (DVAO), which dynamically adjusts combination weights based on the empirical reward variance of each objective within a rollout group, effectively up-weighting objectives with a stronger learning signal while suppressing noisy ones. We mathematically prove that DVAO maintains bounded advantage magnitudes for stable training and introduces a self-adaptive cross-objective regularization mechanism. Extensive experiments on mathematical reasoning and tool-use benchmarks using Qwen3 and Qwen2.5 models demonstrate that DVAO significantly outperforms baseline methods, achieving a superior multi-objective Pareto frontier and robust training stability.

AI.tldr: They propose DVAO, a new way to combine multiple rewards in GRPO-based RL for LLM alignment that is variance-adaptive, keeps advantages bounded, and improves stability and Pareto performance on reasoning and tool-use tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing GRPO-based multi-reward alignment methods mainly use naive scalarization like Reward Combination or Advantage Combination. Reward Combination can create very large advantage magnitudes, causing instability, and Advantage Combination uses fixed weights and ignores correlations across objectives, limiting performance and robustness. There is a need for a principled, stable, and adaptive way to combine multiple objectives in real-world RLHF setups.
AI.method: Introduce Dynamic Variance-adaptive Advantage Optimization (DVAO) within the GRPO framework. For each rollout group and each objective, compute empirical reward variance and use it to adaptively set combination weights over the objectives, effectively prioritizing those with stronger, more reliable learning signals and down-weighting noisy ones. Provide a mathematical analysis proving that the resulting combined advantages remain bounded and that the weighting scheme implicitly implements cross-objective regularization.
AI.result: On mathematical reasoning and tool-use benchmarks using Qwen3 and Qwen2.5 LLMs, the proposed DVAO method yields better performance than baseline scalarization approaches. It achieves a stronger, more favorable multi-objective Pareto frontier and demonstrates more stable training dynamics in practice.
AI.conclusion: DVAO offers a variance-aware, self-adaptive scalarization mechanism for multi-reward GRPO that avoids instability from large advantages and rigidity from static weights. It stabilizes training, respects cross-objective interactions, and empirically improves multi-objective performance for LLM alignment tasks compared to standard methods.

title: RECTOR: Priority-Aware Rule-Based Reranking for Compliance-Aware Autonomous Driving Trajectory Selection
summary:
Autonomous driving stacks must pick one trajectory from a multi-modal candidate set; choosing by model confidence ignores safety, traffic-law, and comfort constraints. We present \textsc{RECTOR} (Rule-Enforced Constrained Trajectory Orchestrator), a post-generation reranking layer that scores candidates against a tiered rulebook (Safety~$\succ$~Legal~$\succ$~Road~$\succ$~Comfort) via differentiable proxies and a scene-conditioned applicability mechanism, then selects with a deterministic $\varepsilon$-lexicographic rule that preserves cross-tier priority by construction -- without retraining the predictor.
  On the Waymo Open Motion Dataset \texttt{validation\_interactive} split (43{,}219 augmented instances, $K{=}6$), under Protocol~B (28-rule proxy catalog, oracle applicability) rule-aware selection cuts Safety+Legal violations from 28.58\% to 20.42\% and Total from 40.32\% to 32.41\% versus confidence-only on the same candidates. A uniform-weight weighted-sum baseline matches binary compliance on this benchmark -- the empirical lift comes from rule-aware ranking, while the lexicographic guarantee is the structural differentiator no weight calibration can replicate. Under adversarial confidence corruption, confidence-only selection fails in 100\% of scenarios while both rule-aware selectors reject the injected mode in $\sim$96\%. All figures are proxy-evaluator results (not a safety certificate), open-loop, 5\,s horizon, U.S.\ rules, validation split.

AI.tldr: They introduce RECTOR, a rule-aware reranking layer for autonomous driving trajectory selection that enforces a priority hierarchy (Safety > Legal > Road > Comfort) using differentiable rule proxies and lexicographic selection, improving rule compliance over confidence-only selection without retraining the predictor.
AI.motivation: Existing autonomous driving systems typically select a trajectory from multiple candidates using model confidence, which can overlook crucial constraints related to safety, traffic laws, road rules, and passenger comfort. There is a need for a selection mechanism that explicitly respects a structured hierarchy of such constraints, can be added post hoc to existing predictors, and is robust under adversarial or corrupted confidence scores.
AI.method: They propose RECTOR, a post-generation reranking module that scores each candidate trajectory according to a tiered rulebook (Safety, then Legal, then Road, then Comfort). The rules are implemented via differentiable proxy evaluators and a scene-conditioned applicability mechanism that determines which rules are relevant for a given scenario. Final selection uses a deterministic ε-lexicographic rule, ensuring that higher-tier rule satisfaction always dominates lower-tier considerations, and this operates without retraining the underlying trajectory predictor.
AI.result: On the Waymo Open Motion Dataset (validation_interactive split) with K=6 candidate trajectories, using a 28-rule proxy catalog and oracle rule applicability (Protocol B), the rule-aware selection with RECTOR reduces combined Safety+Legal violations from 28.58% to 20.42%, and total rule violations from 40.32% to 32.41%, compared with confidence-only selection on the same candidate set. A uniform-weight weighted-sum baseline can match binary compliance on this benchmark, indicating that performance gains arise from rule-aware ranking, but it still lacks the structural lexicographic guarantee. Under adversarially corrupted confidence scores, pure confidence-based selection fails in all tested scenarios, while both rule-aware selectors successfully reject the corrupted mode in about 96% of cases.
AI.conclusion: RECTOR provides a plug-in, rule-structured reranking layer that improves rule compliance of selected trajectories without retraining existing autonomous driving predictors, and offers robustness against confidence corruption. Its lexicographic, tiered prioritization of Safety, Legal, Road, and Comfort constraints yields guarantees that cannot be replicated by simple weighted-sum methods, although the authors emphasize that their results are based on proxy evaluators and do not constitute a formal safety certificate.

title: Beyond Literal Translation: Evaluating Cultural Effectiveness in Social Media UGC
summary: Social media platforms enable large-scale cross-lingual communication, but translating user-generated content (UGC) remains challenging due to its informal style, cultural references, and interaction-based expressions. While recent LLMs have improved translation quality, existing benchmarks and metrics often fail to capture whether translations convey intended meaning and cultural resonance in real-world settings. In this work, we introduce CULTURE-MT, a benchmark for social media translation that focuses on both CULtural Transmission and UGC-specific emotion REsonance. CULTURE-MT consists of 1,002 UGC notes across 14 domains, categorized into four types based on culture-loaded symbols and linguistic style features. We also construct UGC-oriented training data to fine-tune Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-32B as baselines. We propose cultural effectiveness as a new evaluation criterion, focusing on expression accuracy and cultural adaptability. Testing 15 models, including the baselines, we find that traditional metrics fail to capture cultural effectiveness. We also observe that cultural effectiveness on base LLMs correlates with model size. Our work provides a comprehensive evaluation system for UGC translation models and will offer an open evaluation platform to advance research in this area. We release the CULTURE-MT benchmark and provide an online leaderboard where submitted translation results can be evaluated by our trained JUDGER.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CULTURE-MT, a benchmark and evaluation framework for assessing how well machine translation systems handle culturally rich, emotion-laden social media content.
AI.motivation: Existing MT benchmarks and metrics focus mainly on surface-level accuracy and often miss whether translations of social media UGC preserve intended meaning, cultural references, and emotional resonance. With LLMs increasingly used for translation in real-world, cross-lingual social media contexts, there is a need for a benchmark that evaluates cultural transmission and user emotion specifically for informal, culturally loaded content.
AI.method: The authors build CULTURE-MT, a dataset of 1,002 user-generated notes from social media spanning 14 domains. These are categorized into four types depending on culture-loaded symbols and linguistic style features. They also construct UGC-oriented training data and fine-tune Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-32B as baseline MT models. A new evaluation criterion, cultural effectiveness, is proposed, focusing on expression accuracy and cultural adaptability. They test 15 MT models on this benchmark and evaluate them, including via a trained evaluation model called JUDGER, and host an online leaderboard.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation of 15 models shows that traditional MT metrics do not reliably capture cultural effectiveness in social-media UGC translation. Cultural effectiveness scores for base LLMs are observed to correlate positively with model size. The fine-tuned Qwen variants and other systems are evaluated within this new framework using the CULTURE-MT benchmark and the JUDGER-based evaluation pipeline.
AI.conclusion: CULTURE-MT offers a comprehensive, culturally focused benchmark and evaluation system tailored to social media UGC translation. By highlighting the limitations of standard metrics and introducing cultural effectiveness and an automatic JUDGER, the work establishes infrastructure and an open leaderboard to drive future research on culturally aware MT for informal, real-world content.

title: Trust but Verify: Prover-Verifier Deliberation for Selective LLM Prediction
summary: Reliably knowing when a language model is correct is almost as important as being correct. We introduce prover-verifier deliberation (PVD), an inference-time protocol grounded in interactive proof theory, as a mechanism for selective prediction: the protocol produces both an answer and a structured confidence verdict, allowing a system to report high-confidence answers while abstaining on uncertain cases. In each dialogue, a prover defends a candidate answer through checkable sub-claims while a verifier issues targeted challenges and returns \textsc{Accept}, \textsc{Challenge}, or \textsc{Reject}. Because frozen language models are imperfect provers and verifiers operating over a noisy channel, formal soundness and completeness guarantees do not transfer; instead, we characterize the protocol empirically through its coverage-precision behavior. Our main experiment uses Claude Sonnet 4.6 as prover and Claude Haiku 4.5 as verifier on GPQA Diamond. Questions accepted with no answer revision, which we call Accept + No Change (ANC), are reported as the high-confidence subset; we evaluate this subset by its precision and coverage. ANC separates reliable from unreliable answers, yielding a $\sim$30pp HC-Prec gap over the non-ANC complement. Robustness experiments with GPT and Gemini pairings show that high HC-Prec can transfer across model families, while verifier strictness and domain competence largely determine the size of the selection gap. On Humanity's Last Exam, weaker prover-verifier pairings can collapse or invert the ANC signal, illustrating a practical failure mode when the verifier operates outside its effective region. Comparisons with self-consistency, universal self-consistency, multi-agent debate, and Reflexion suggest that prover-verifier deliberation supplies a distinct argument-defensibility signal for selective prediction.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an inference-time prover-verifier deliberation protocol that lets language models output both answers and structured confidence signals, enabling selective prediction with higher precision on high-confidence subsets.
AI.motivation: Language models often cannot reliably indicate when their answers are correct, which is critical for safe deployment. Existing confidence measures are limited, especially in complex reasoning tasks, so a better mechanism is needed to separate reliable from unreliable answers at inference time.
AI.method: They design a prover-verifier deliberation (PVD) protocol inspired by interactive proofs: a prover defends a candidate answer via explicit, checkable sub-claims while a verifier interrogates these claims and issues Accept, Challenge, or Reject. They instantiate this with frozen LMs (e.g., Claude Sonnet 4.6 as prover and Claude Haiku 4.5 as verifier) and empirically study selective prediction behavior on benchmarks such as GPQA Diamond and Humanity's Last Exam, defining Accept + No Change (ANC) as the high-confidence subset and comparing PVD to baselines like self-consistency, multi-agent debate, and Reflexion.
AI.result: On GPQA Diamond, the ANC subset achieves substantially higher high-confidence precision (about a 30 percentage-point gap) compared with non-ANC answers, effectively separating reliable from unreliable predictions. Cross-model experiments with GPT and Gemini show that high HC-precision can transfer between model families, but the gap size depends on verifier strictness and domain competence. On Humanity's Last Exam, weaker or misaligned prover-verifier pairs can cause the ANC signal to degrade or invert, highlighting a concrete failure mode.
AI.conclusion: Prover-verifier deliberation provides a structured, argument-defensibility-based confidence signal for language models, enabling effective selective prediction that outperforms several existing deliberation and uncertainty estimation methods. However, its reliability depends on appropriately capable and well-matched prover and verifier models, especially in difficult domains.

title: When In-Distribution Gains Fail: Evaluating Weak-to-Strong Reward Models under Preference Shift
summary: Weak-to-strong (W2S) generalization is a promising framework for scalable oversight, yet existing evaluations often test students under matched train--test distributions. Therefore, we study W2S preference learning under zero-shot distribution shift and find that strong students trained on weak preference labels can appear successful in-distribution while failing to transfer across preference datasets. We provide evidence for a representational failure mode in which weak-supervised fine-tuning can pull the strong model toward source-domain features instead of maintaining broadly transferable preference representations. To mitigate this, we propose Representation Anchoring (Anchor), a simple yet effective regularizer that constrains excessive drift from the pretrained strong model's representation space during fine-tuning, while still allowing task-relevant adaptation. Across preference domains, datasets, and model families, Anchor consistently improves out-of-distribution transfer while maintaining competitive in-distribution performance. Together, our evaluation protocol, transfer-aware metrics, and method expose hidden brittleness in current W2S reward modeling and provide a practical path toward more robust preference transfer.

AI.tldr: The paper studies weak-to-strong preference learning under distribution shift and proposes a regularization method, Representation Anchoring, to improve out-of-distribution generalization while preserving in-distribution performance.
AI.motivation: Weak-to-strong generalization is promising for scalable oversight, but current evaluations usually test under matched train-test distributions, which can hide failures when models face new preference distributions. The authors observed that strong models trained on weak labels can look good in-distribution yet fail under zero-shot distribution shifts, motivating a systematic study and mitigation of this issue.
AI.method: They empirically analyze weak-to-strong preference learning across multiple preference datasets and domains under zero-shot distribution shift. They identify a representational failure mode where fine-tuning on weak labels drags the strong model toward source-domain-specific features, harming transfer. To address this, they introduce Representation Anchoring, a regularizer that constrains how far the fine-tuned model's internal representations can drift from the pretrained strong model, while still allowing task-relevant adaptation. They also design transfer-aware metrics and an evaluation protocol focused on cross-dataset transfer.
AI.result: Experiments across different preference domains, datasets, and model families show that models fine-tuned with Representation Anchoring achieve better out-of-distribution transfer performance compared to standard weak-supervised fine-tuning, while retaining competitive in-distribution performance. This demonstrates that the anchoring constraint mitigates the identified representational failure mode.
AI.conclusion: Weak-to-strong preference learning can suffer from hidden brittleness due to representational drift toward source-domain features, which undermines cross-distribution generalization. Representation Anchoring offers a simple, practical regularization approach that improves robustness of preference transfer without sacrificing much in-distribution accuracy. The proposed evaluation protocol and metrics highlight the importance of testing under distribution shifts for scalable oversight methods.

title: Reinforcement Learning from Denoising Feedback
summary: Policy loss estimation remains a fundamental and long-standing challenge in reinforcement learning (RL) for diffusion language models (dLLMs). We introduce Reinforcement Learning from Denoising Feedback (RLDF), a novel training paradigm that leverages feedback obtained from rollout and training processes to facilitate accurate and efficient policy loss estimation. To balance the trade-off between computational efficiency and estimation effectiveness, RLDF optimizes the model toward the clipped clean state $\hat{x}_0$ from intermediate noisy states $x_t$, combined with weighted timestep sampling over $t$. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RLDF achieves consistent and substantial improvements in both performance and generalizability across two representative dLLM architectures, LLaDA and Dream, on multiple reasoning benchmarks. Our work lays a principled foundation for scalable reinforcement learning in diffusion language models. We build Drift, a training framework for dLLMs, available at https://github.com/ant-research/Drift.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes RLDF, a new reinforcement learning paradigm tailored for diffusion language models (dLLMs), improving policy loss estimation by training on denoising feedback from intermediate diffusion steps and showing better performance and generalization on reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: In diffusion language models, accurately estimating policy loss for reinforcement learning is difficult and computationally expensive, limiting the scalability and effectiveness of RL on such models. Existing methods do not fully exploit the structure of the diffusion process or feedback available during rollouts and training.
AI.method: The authors introduce Reinforcement Learning from Denoising Feedback (RLDF). Instead of directly optimizing on final outputs, RLDF leverages the feedback arising during the diffusion process, training the model to move noisy intermediate states x_t toward a clipped clean state estimate \hat{x}_0. It uses weighted timestep sampling over diffusion steps t to balance computational efficiency and estimation quality, effectively turning denoising into an RL signal for policy loss estimation. They implement this in a framework named Drift for two dLLM architectures, LLaDA and Dream.
AI.result: Across multiple reasoning benchmarks and on two representative diffusion language model architectures (LLaDA and Dream), RLDF yields consistent and substantial gains in task performance and generalization relative to baselines. Empirical results indicate more accurate and efficient policy loss estimation and improved scalability of RL training for dLLMs.
AI.conclusion: RLDF provides a principled, scalable way to apply reinforcement learning to diffusion language models by using denoising feedback from intermediate diffusion steps to approximate policy loss.
