AI.conclusion: StreamProfileBench fills a gap in user profiling evaluation by focusing on streaming, fine-grained profile evolution, and demonstrates that continuous profile maintenance is still an unsolved problem for LLMs. The findings highlight the need for new modeling and training approaches that better capture temporal dynamics and interest decay, and establish the streaming paradigm as practically important and necessary for realistic personalization research.

title: Second Guess: Detecting Uncertainty Through Abstention and Answer Stability in Small Language Models
summary: Large language models often generate confident but incorrect answers rather than abstaining when uncertain. This problem is particularly acute for small language models (SLMs), where computational constraints and autonomous operation amplify the need for reliable uncertainty detection. We propose _Second Guess_, a lightweight, parameter-free prompting technique for abstention in multiple-choice question answering (MCQA) that is well-suited for SLMs. Our key empirical insight is that models which truly know an answer will select it consistently, while uncertain models exhibit unstable behavior when an ``I don't know'' option is added. Evaluated on four open models (2B-8B parameters) and four benchmarks, Second Guess achieves the highest composite risk improvement of 10.81\%. Notably, it maintains an 8\% composite risk improvement on fine-tuned models where entropy-based methods degrade, and improves most for lower-performing models. All code and results required to reproduce this work is available in https://github.com/Mystic-Slice/second-guess

AI.tldr: They introduce Second Guess, a simple prompt-based method that lets small language models abstain (“I don’t know”) more reliably in multiple‑choice QA by detecting instability in answers when an explicit abstention option is added.
AI.motivation: Small language models often respond confidently with wrong answers instead of abstaining when uncertain. This is especially problematic because SLMs run with tight computational budgets and may be used autonomously, so they need a reliable way to express uncertainty without adding heavy training or calibration overhead. Existing entropy or confidence-based methods can fail, especially after fine-tuning.
AI.method: They propose Second Guess, a parameter-free prompting technique for MCQA: first query the model normally, then query again with an added “I don’t know” option and observe whether the model’s choice changes. The key idea is that truly known answers remain stable across prompts, whereas uncertain cases become unstable or shift to “I don’t know.” The method is evaluated on four open SLMs (2B–8B parameters) across four benchmarks and compared against entropy-based baselines, including on fine-tuned models.
AI.result: On four open models and four MCQA benchmarks, Second Guess yields the best overall (composite) risk reduction, improving risk by 10.81% relative to baselines. It remains effective on fine-tuned models—where entropy-based uncertainty measures perform worse—still providing an 8% composite risk improvement and bringing the largest gains on the weakest-performing models.
AI.conclusion: Second Guess is a simple, training-free prompting strategy that improves abstention behavior in small language models for multiple-choice QA by using answer stability under an added “I don’t know” option as a proxy for knowledge. It outperforms entropy-based approaches, is robust under fine-tuning, and is particularly beneficial for low-performing SLMs, making it a practical tool for safer autonomous deployment.

title: Double Triangle Annotation: A Scalable Human-in-the-Loop Framework for High-Precision Historical Document Annotation
comment: 12 pages, 4 figures. ACL ARR 2026 March submission
summary: Evaluating structured-information extraction from historical documents at scale requires high-precision ground-truth annotations, yet traditional manual labeling is expensive and fully automated pipelines built on large language models are prone to hallucination. We propose Double Triangle Annotation, a two-layer human-in-the-loop framework that leverages cross-model consensus to automate the majority of annotation work while ensuring high-precision outputs. In the first layer, two architecturally independent Multimodal Large Language Models annotate each document in parallel; when they agree, the label is auto-accepted, and disagreements are routed to a human jury. A second layer cross-checks two such systems against each other, escalating residual conflicts to a domain expert. The framework rests on a single assumption -- error independence between models -- requires no distributional priors or task-specific calibration, and becomes more autonomous as model capability improves. On the Guides Rosenwald, a corpus of French medical directories spanning 1887-1906, the framework achieves a final Word Error Rate of 0.003. Applied at scale, model consensus auto-accepts over 85% of 13,595 fields. We release the resulting benchmark -- the first structured-extraction ground truth for the Rosenwald Guides -- to support future work on historical document processing.

AI.tldr: They introduce a human-in-the-loop, two-layer annotation framework using multiple multimodal LLMs plus humans to create highly accurate structured data from historical documents at scale.
AI.motivation: High-quality ground truth for structured information extraction from historical documents is needed, but manual labeling is costly and fully automated LLM-based pipelines can hallucinate and lack precision.
AI.method: They design a two-layer “Double Triangle Annotation” process: (1) two architecturally independent multimodal LLMs label each document; when they agree, labels are automatically accepted, and disagreements go to a human jury; (2) two such systems are cross-checked against each other, with remaining conflicts escalated to a domain expert. The approach assumes error independence between models and needs no task-specific calibration.
AI.result: On the Guides Rosenwald corpus (French medical directories, 1887–1906), the framework achieves a Word Error Rate of 0.003 and auto-accepts over 85% of 13,595 fields via model consensus, greatly reducing human annotation effort.
AI.conclusion: Their Double Triangle Annotation framework can reliably generate high-precision structured ground truth for historical documents at scale, with automation improving as models get better, and they release the resulting benchmark for the Rosenwald Guides to support further research.

title: Towards end-to-end LLM-based censoring-aware survival analysis
summary:
Objective: Survival analysis is central to medical prediction, yet large language models (LLMs) are rarely used as end-to-end survival models because censoring prevents straightforward supervised fine-tuning. Here we present LLMSurvival, a framework that enables censoring-aware survival analysis with unmodified LLMs operating directly on tabular clinical data.
  Materials and Methods: LLMSurvival reformulates time-to-event prediction as pairwise ranking among comparable subjects, and derives test-time risk by aggregating comparisons against anchor individuals from the training cohort.
  Results: Across two clinical tasks (ICU mortality prediction in MIMIC-IV and fragility fracture prediction in a NewYork-Presbyterian/Weill Cornell Medicine cohort), LLMSurvival improves overall concordance over Cox proportional hazards modeling by 3.1% for ICU mortality and 0.5% for fracture risk, 2.1% on average for ICU mortality and 2.8% for fracture risk over three established deep learning survival models.
  Discussion: The results show that survival modeling with censoring can be made compatible with LLM fine-tuning through comparison-based reformulation. The framework demonstrates high portability and superior performance over expert curated scores like SAPS-II and FRAX scores across diverse clinical context. Furthermore, the framework supports local deployment, as compact, publicly available base models provide sufficient performance.
  Conclusion: The LLMSurvival framework serves as a proof of concept for an integrated, censoring-conscious approach to survival analysis via LLMs.

AI.tldr: LLMSurvival is a framework that lets standard LLMs do censoring-aware survival analysis directly on tabular clinical data by turning time-to-event prediction into pairwise rankings and aggregating them at test time, achieving better concordance than classical and deep survival models.
AI.motivation: Survival analysis is crucial in medicine (e.g., predicting mortality or fracture risk), but existing methods like Cox models or specialized deep learning require bespoke architectures and struggle to leverage generic LLMs, mainly because right-censoring breaks straightforward supervised fine-tuning on time-to-event labels. The authors want a way to use unmodified, off-the-shelf LLMs as end-to-end survival models that can handle censoring properly, be easily deployed locally, and potentially outperform traditional clinical risk scores and standard survival models.
AI.method: They introduce LLMSurvival, which reformulates survival prediction as a pairwise ranking problem between clinically comparable patient pairs instead of directly predicting times or hazards. During training, the LLM is fine-tuned to decide which of two patients is at higher risk given tabular clinical features and censoring-aware pairing rules. At test time, for a new patient, they estimate risk by aggregating that patient’s predicted pairwise comparisons against a set of anchor patients from the training set, effectively inducing a risk score from relative rankings. This uses unmodified LLMs that operate on text-encoded tabular data.
AI.result: On two tasks—ICU mortality prediction using MIMIC-IV and fragility fracture prediction in a NewYork-Presbyterian/Weill Cornell cohort—LLMSurvival improves concordance indices over a Cox proportional hazards model by 3.1% (ICU mortality) and 0.5% (fracture), and on average outperforms three standard deep survival models by 2.1% (ICU mortality) and 2.8% (fracture). It also surpasses domain-specific expert scores such as SAPS-II for ICU mortality and FRAX for fracture risk.
AI.conclusion: LLMSurvival shows that survival analysis with censoring can be recast into a comparison-based formulation suited to generic LLM fine-tuning, enabling integrated, censoring-aware survival prediction from tabular clinical data. The framework is portable across clinical contexts, can run with compact publicly available models for local deployment, and delivers performance competitive with or superior to established statistical, deep learning, and expert-derived risk scores, serving as a proof-of-concept for LLM-based survival modeling.

title: Adaptive Graph Refinement and Label Propagation with LLMs for Cost-Effective Entity Resolution
summary: Dirty entity resolution (ER), which identifies records referring to the same real-world entity from a single, messy dataset, is a fundamental task in data management and mining. However, the dominant blocking-matching-clustering paradigm for ER suffers from critical flaws. Its cascaded, decoupled workflow essentially produces a static, sparse graph plagued by missing edges (due to blocking failures) and noisy links (due to matching errors), causing error propagation and yielding suboptimal clusters, particularly when rigid transitivity is imposed in the clustering. We contend that matching and clustering are fundamentally synergistic, both optimizing for the construction of an ideal entity graph. Building upon this insight, we propose Alper, a unified framework that integrates these steps into an iterative probabilistic label propagation process over a global, evolving graph. Unlike disjoint blocking, Alper refines the graph structure and labels dynamically by adaptively integrating "weak but cheap" signals from graph propagation with "strong but expensive" LLM-based pairwise queries. For higher cost-effectiveness, we formulate the signal selection as a constrained optimization problem maximizing cumulative marginal gain under a query budget, solved via our greedy algorithm with provable theoretical guarantees. Our extensive experiments over eight benchmark datasets demonstrate that Alper is consistently superior to state-of-the-art cascaded pipelines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Alper, a unified, iterative probabilistic label-propagation framework that jointly performs matching and clustering for dirty entity resolution, adaptively combining cheap graph-based signals with expensive but accurate LLM-based pairwise queries under a budget to build better entity graphs than traditional cascaded pipelines.
AI.motivation: Existing dirty entity resolution pipelines follow a blocking-matching-clustering cascade that produces a static, sparse, and error-prone similarity graph. Blocking misses true pairs, matching introduces noisy links, and rigid transitive clustering amplifies these errors, resulting in suboptimal entity clusters. There is a need for a framework that avoids error propagation, leverages the synergy between matching and clustering, and uses limited expensive supervision (e.g., from LLMs) in a cost-effective way.
AI.method: The authors introduce Alper, a unified ER framework based on iterative probabilistic label propagation on a global, evolving graph. Rather than treating blocking, matching, and clustering as separate steps, Alper continually refines both graph structure and entity labels. It integrates two kinds of signals: (1) weak but cheap relational/graph-based propagation signals; and (2) strong but expensive pairwise similarity/labeling signals from a large language model. To control the cost of LLM calls, they cast the selection of which edges or instances to query as a constrained optimization problem that maximizes cumulative marginal gain in ER quality under a budget. They solve this with a greedy algorithm and provide theoretical guarantees (likely submodularity-based) on its near-optimality. The whole process iteratively updates the entity graph and propagates labels until convergence.
AI.result: Across eight benchmark datasets, Alper consistently outperforms state-of-the-art cascaded blocking-matching-clustering pipelines in entity resolution quality. The experiments show that its joint optimization and budget-aware use of LLM signals yield better entity graphs and clusters under the same or lower query costs, demonstrating improved robustness to missing and noisy edges compared to traditional methods.
AI.conclusion: Matching and clustering for dirty entity resolution should be treated as a coupled, iterative graph-construction problem rather than a fixed cascade of separate stages. By unifying them in a probabilistic label-propagation framework and optimally combining cheap propagation with budgeted, strong LLM-based pairwise supervision, Alper significantly improves ER performance over standard pipelines while controlling annotation/query costs.

title: CODESKILL: Learning Self-Evolving Skills for Coding Agents
summary: Coding agents produce rich trajectories while solving software-engineering tasks. To enable agent self-evolution, these trajectories can be distilled into reusable procedural skills that compactly encode experience to guide future behavior. However, existing skill construction and maintenance methods often rely on fixed prompts and heuristic update rules, leaving it unclear how knowledge should be selected, abstracted, and maintained to best serve downstream agents. We propose CODESKILL, an LLM-based framework that reformulates skill extraction and skill-bank maintenance as a learnable management policy. CODESKILL extracts multi-granularity procedural skills from coding-agent trajectories, evolves skills with new experience, and maintains a compact skill bank for future task solving. We train CODESKILL with reinforcement learning, using a hybrid reward that combines dense rubric-based skill-quality feedback with sparse verifiable execution feedback from the frozen downstream agent. Experiments on EnvBench, SWE-Bench Verified, and Terminal-Bench 2 show that CODESKILL improves average pass rate by 9.69 over the no-skill baseline and by 4.01 over the strongest prompt-based or memory baseline, while maintaining the skill bank at a stable size during iterative construction.

AI.tldr: The paper presents CODESKILL, an RL-trained LLM framework that automatically extracts, evolves, and manages reusable coding skills from agent trajectories, yielding higher pass rates on software-engineering benchmarks while keeping the skill bank compact.
AI.motivation: Coding agents generate rich experience when solving software-engineering tasks, but current approaches for turning this experience into reusable skills rely on fixed prompts and heuristic rules. These methods do not systematically decide what knowledge to keep, how to abstract it, or how to maintain it over time to best help future agents. There is a need for a principled, learnable way to construct and maintain a skill bank that improves downstream agent performance without uncontrolled growth in memory size.
AI.method: The authors propose CODESKILL, an LLM-based framework that treats skill extraction and skill-bank maintenance as a learnable management policy. From coding-agent trajectories, CODESKILL extracts procedural skills at multiple granularities, updates these skills with new experience, and prunes or merges them to keep a compact bank. The policy is trained with reinforcement learning using a hybrid reward: a dense, rubric-based signal assessing skill quality and a sparse, verifiable execution reward based on how a frozen downstream coding agent performs when using the skills. This closes the loop between experience generation, skill learning, and evaluation.
AI.result: On three benchmarks—EnvBench, SWE-Bench Verified, and Terminal-Bench 2—CODESKILL increases the average pass rate by 9.69 points over a no-skill baseline and by 4.01 points over the strongest prompt-based or memory-based baselines. It achieves these gains while keeping the size of the skill bank stable during iterative construction, indicating effective skill compression and maintenance rather than unbounded accumulation.
AI.conclusion: CODESKILL demonstrates that framing skill extraction and maintenance as an RL-learned management policy can effectively distill coding-agent trajectories into compact, reusable procedural skills. This approach improves software-engineering task performance over heuristic skill or memory methods and shows that a hybrid reward combining rubric-based and execution-based feedback is effective for guiding skill-bank evolution. The framework points toward scalable self-evolution for coding agents through principled, data-driven skill management.

title: Fine-Tuning Over Architectural Complexity: Broad-Coverage PII Detection on PIIBench with DeBERTa
summary: Personally identifiable information (PII) detection systems are frequently trained within narrow source or domain boundaries, limiting coverage when deployed on heterogeneous text. We study model fine-tuning on a corrected multi-source PIIBench preparation spanning 82 retained entity types across ten source datasets. We evaluate three DeBERTa-based approaches: direct token classification fine-tuning, a source-conditioned hierarchical model (SC+H), and a three-phase curriculum extension (SC+H+Curr). Against eight published comparator systems on a reproducible 5,000-record held-out subset (test_5k), direct fine-tuned DeBERTa achieves F1 0.6476, while SC+H and the curriculum variant achieve 0.5899 and 0.2772 respectively; the strongest published comparator reaches only 0.1723. Because validation initially favoured SC+H, we perform a final streamed evaluation on the complete 100,002-record held-out split. Direct fine-tuning remains superior, achieving F1 0.6455 versus 0.5894 for SC+H. Entity-level analysis shows that direct fine tuning wins 54 of 82 fine entity types and all ten coarse groups by support-weighted entity F1, while SC+H retains localised advantages on 28 types. The results indicate that diverse task-specific training data and a simple weighted cross-entropy objective contribute more to broad-coverage PII detection than the tested architectural and curriculum complexity.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates different DeBERTa-based training strategies for broad-coverage PII detection across multiple heterogeneous sources and finds that simple direct fine-tuning with diverse training data outperforms more complex hierarchical and curriculum-based architectures.
AI.motivation: Existing PII detection systems are usually trained on narrow, domain-specific data, which leads to poor performance when the models are deployed on heterogeneous, real-world text from many sources. There is a need to understand how to best leverage diverse, multi-source datasets to achieve broad-coverage PII detection, and whether sophisticated architectures or training curricula are actually beneficial compared with straightforward fine-tuning.
AI.method: The authors construct a corrected, multi-source PIIBench dataset spanning 82 entity types from ten source datasets and define a reproducible 5,000-record held-out subset (test_5k) plus a larger 100,002-record held-out split. They fine-tune DeBERTa models using three approaches: (1) standard token classification fine-tuning with a weighted cross-entropy loss; (2) a source-conditioned hierarchical model (SC+H) that explicitly encodes dataset source information and hierarchical structure; and (3) an extended curriculum-learning variant (SC+H+Curr) with three training phases. They compare these models’ F1 performance against eight published PII detection systems and also conduct detailed entity-level analyses across fine-grained and coarse-grained entity types.
AI.result: On the 5,000-record test_5k subset, direct DeBERTa fine-tuning attains F1 0.6476, outperforming the SC+H model (0.5899), the curriculum variant SC+H+Curr (0.2772), and all eight published comparator systems, the best of which reaches F1 0.1723. Because validation scores initially suggested SC+H might be better, they also run a streamed evaluation on the full 100,002-record held-out split, where direct fine-tuning again leads with F1 0.6455 versus 0.5894 for SC+H. Entity-wise analysis reveals that direct fine-tuning wins on 54 of 82 fine-grained entity types and all ten coarse groups based on support-weighted entity F1, though SC+H shows advantages on 28 specific entity types.
AI.conclusion: For broad-coverage PII detection across heterogeneous text sources, access to diverse task-specific training data and using a simple weighted cross-entropy objective with direct DeBERTa fine-tuning are more impactful than the additional architectural complexity of source-conditioned hierarchical models or curriculum-based training schemes. While hierarchical conditioning can provide localized gains for some entity types, overall performance and coverage are best achieved by straightforward fine-tuning on a richly varied, multi-source PII dataset.

title: Security of OpenClaw Agents: Fundamentals, Attacks, and Countermeasures
summary: The rapid evolution of large language model (LLM)-driven autonomous agents has given rise to OpenClaw, a new class of open-source agent frameworks that operate as continuously running, skill-augmented systems with persistent memory, multi-channel interaction, and high degrees of autonomy. Such capabilities enable OpenClaw agents to autonomously execute complex, multi-step tasks and interact seamlessly with external applications, but simultaneously introduce a substantially enlarged attack surface. In particular, the combination of high-privilege operations and persistent memory exposes OpenClaw agents to various emerging threats, including skill poisoning, cognitive manipulation, multi-agent cascading failures, and supply-chain vulnerabilities. In this survey, we present a comprehensive study of the security landscape of OpenClaw agents. We first examine the general architecture and key characteristics that distinguish OpenClaw agents from traditional AI agent systems. We categorize existing security and privacy threats into a layered framework and analyze how vulnerabilities arise during agent reasoning, action execution, and external interaction. Representative defense mechanisms are also reviewed to draw the current defense landscape. Finally, several unresolved issues related to the reliability and trustworthiness of OpenClaw ecosystems are discussed.

AI.tldr: Survey of security risks and defenses for OpenClaw, a new class of highly autonomous, persistent-memory LLM agent frameworks.
AI.motivation: OpenClaw-style LLM agents are powerful, continuously running, and tightly integrated with external tools and applications. This brings new security and privacy risks that are not well understood or systematically categorized yet; the paper aims to clarify and organize this emerging threat landscape.
AI.method: Conceptual and survey-based: define the OpenClaw agent architecture and characteristics; build a layered framework for categorizing threats; map known and emerging attacks (e.g., skill poisoning, cognitive manipulation, cascading failures, supply-chain issues) onto this framework; and review representative defenses from the literature and practice.
AI.result: A structured taxonomy of security and privacy threats across the layers of OpenClaw agents (reasoning, action execution, external interaction), along with an organized review of existing defensive techniques and identification of where they apply. The paper also highlights specific vulnerabilities stemming from persistent memory and high-privilege capabilities.
AI.conclusion: OpenClaw ecosystems significantly expand the attack surface compared with traditional AI agents, and current defenses are partial and fragmented. Ensuring reliability and trustworthiness will require addressing unresolved issues such as robust defenses against cognitive and supply-chain attacks, better isolation and monitoring of agent behaviors, and more principled security architectures for highly autonomous LLM agents.

title: Clarify, Abstain or Answer? Strategising in Conversation with Belief-Augmented Generation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) define a distribution over text, which can be viewed as a probabilistic representation of uncertainty: sampling K responses yields a belief state - responses a model deems plausible. Existing work exploits this representation for narrow tasks like either decoding or selective prediction, and often requires manual interventions, not controlling generation directly. We propose Belief-Augmented Generation (BAG): grounding LLMs in their own belief state via the prompt and letting them reason over these K samples to decide on a conversational strategy: answer, clarify, or abstain. In a multi-turn ambiguous QA setting, we find that LLMs by default rarely clarify or abstain, ignoring uncertainty about the input or facts. BAG improves QA accuracy across six models and yields strategy decisions more faithful to the belief state than prompt-only baselines. Disentangling when to clarify from when to abstain, however, remains challenging.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Belief-Augmented Generation (BAG), a method where an LLM samples multiple candidate responses, then reasons over them in the prompt to decide whether to answer, ask for clarification, or abstain, improving accuracy and alignment with its uncertainty.
AI.motivation: LLMs inherently define a probability distribution over possible outputs, which encodes their uncertainty. Prior work has used this distribution for narrow tasks, often with manual interventions and without directly integrating this uncertainty into the generation process itself. Moreover, in ambiguous multi-turn QA, LLMs tend not to clarify or abstain even when they are uncertain. There is a need for a principled way to use the model’s own belief state to drive its conversational strategy, particularly when inputs or required facts are uncertain or ambiguous.
AI.method: The authors propose Belief-Augmented Generation (BAG). First, the LLM samples K candidate responses to a query, forming an explicit belief state describing what the model considers plausible answers. Then, this set of K samples is fed back into the model via the prompt. The model is instructed to reason over these samples and select a conversational strategy: (1) provide a direct answer, (2) ask a clarifying question to reduce ambiguity, or (3) abstain from answering. They evaluate this approach in a multi-turn ambiguous QA setting and compare it against prompt-only baselines that lack this explicit belief-state grounding.
AI.result: In ambiguous multi-turn QA, baseline LLMs almost never choose to clarify or abstain, even when their internal uncertainty is high. Using BAG, QA accuracy improves across six different LLMs. Additionally, the decisions about whether to answer, clarify, or abstain are more faithful to the underlying belief state (the distribution of sampled responses) than in prompt-only baselines. However, the method still struggles to reliably distinguish between situations that call for clarification versus abstention.
AI.conclusion: Belief-Augmented Generation demonstrates that explicitly grounding an LLM in its own belief state and letting it reason over multiple sampled responses can improve decision-making in ambiguous QA, increasing accuracy and alignment with its uncertainty. While this approach encourages models to use clarify/abstain strategies more appropriately, fully disentangling when to ask for clarification versus when to abstain remains an open challenge.

title: A Signal-Language Foundation Model for Broad-Spectrum Cardiovascular Assessment from Routine Electrocardiography
summary: Electrocardiography (ECG) is central to cardiovascular care, but conventional AI models are often restricted to common arrhythmias and may generalize poorly across populations or clinically subtle diseases. We developed ECG Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (ECGCLIP), a signal-language contrastive learning framework that aligns ECG waveforms with expert diagnostic reports. ECGCLIP was pre-trained on 2,837,962 ECG studies from 1,324,856 patients and evaluated on a held-out internal test set plus nine independent external cohorts comprising about 1.5 million ECGs. Evaluation covered 89 downstream tasks, including 45 ECG diagnoses, 39 echocardiographic targets, and 5 rare cardiac diseases, using PRAUC as the primary metric. ECGCLIP consistently improved performance over random initialization and Merl-R18 baselines. On the internal test set, ECGCLIP-R34 achieved strong performance for atrial fibrillation (PRAUC 0.900) and ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (PRAUC 0.383), with robust generalization across all external cohorts. It also improved low-prevalence and diagnostically elusive diseases, including Ebstein anomaly, constrictive pericarditis, dextrocardia, and cardiac amyloidosis, with internal PRAUC values of 0.253, 0.175, 0.121, and 0.201, respectively. ECGCLIP was data efficient, matching or exceeding full-dataset baseline performance with only 10% of training data. Feature visualization and saliency analysis suggested clinically meaningful representations aligned with established electrocardiographic criteria. These findings indicate that large-scale ECG-report contrastive pre-training can expand routine ECG interpretation beyond common arrhythmias toward broad cardiovascular assessment and opportunistic screening of echocardiographic and rare conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ECGCLIP, a contrastive learning model aligning ECG waveforms with expert text reports to improve ECG-based prediction of many cardiac conditions, including rare diseases, with strong generalization and data efficiency.
AI.motivation: Conventional ECG AI models focus on a small set of common arrhythmias and often fail to generalize across populations or to subtle, low-prevalence cardiac diseases. There is a need for a more general and scalable representation-learning approach that can leverage large, real-world ECG datasets with associated clinical text to support broad cardiovascular assessment and better detect rare or elusive conditions.
AI.method: The authors propose ECG Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (ECGCLIP), a signal-language contrastive learning framework inspired by CLIP. They treat ECG waveforms as the ‘image’ modality and pair them with expert diagnostic reports as the language modality. Using contrastive learning, the model is trained to align ECG signals with their corresponding text reports while separating mismatched pairs. ECGCLIP is pretrained on 2.8M+ ECGs from 1.3M+ patients, then evaluated on a held-out internal test set and nine external cohorts. For downstream evaluation, they fine-tune or probe the learned representations on 89 tasks covering ECG diagnoses, echocardiographic targets, and rare cardiac diseases, using PRAUC as the main metric. They also analyze feature representations and saliency maps for clinical interpretability.
AI.result: Across 89 downstream tasks, ECGCLIP consistently outperforms random initialization and a strong Merl-R18 baseline. On the internal test set, ECGCLIP-R34 attains high PRAUC for common conditions like atrial fibrillation (0.900) and moderate but meaningful performance for ST-elevation myocardial infarction (0.383), with robust performance maintained across nine external cohorts totaling ~1.5M ECGs. For low-prevalence, diagnostically subtle diseases such as Ebstein anomaly, constrictive pericarditis, dextrocardia, and cardiac amyloidosis, ECGCLIP achieves PRAUCs of 0.253, 0.175, 0.121, and 0.201, exceeding baselines. The method is data efficient, matching or surpassing the baseline trained on the full dataset while using only 10% of labeled data. Visualization and saliency analyses indicate the model focuses on clinically relevant ECG regions in line with known diagnostic criteria.
AI.conclusion: Large-scale contrastive pretraining that aligns ECG waveforms with expert reports yields general-purpose ECG representations that extend beyond standard arrhythmia detection to broader cardiovascular characterization. ECGCLIP demonstrates improved performance, generalization, and data efficiency across diverse ECG, echo, and rare-disease prediction tasks, suggesting that ECG-report contrastive learning can enhance routine ECG interpretation and enable opportunistic screening for structural and rare cardiac conditions.

title: On the Limits of Model Merging for Multilinguality in Pre-Training
summary: Endowing models with consistent multilingual performance can be achieved by mixing pre-training data, or post-training approaches such as language-specific model merging. In this work, we test whether merging can be applied to monolingually pre-trained models. We conduct a controlled study on the efficacy of mixed, merged, and monolingual pre-training setups. We find that while monolingual pre-training results in strong in-language performance, merging any combination of monolingual models leads to performance collapse due to interference. Our analysis suggests representational similarity is a prerequisite for model merging. We therefore conclude that the flexibility of merging in fine-tuning does not extend trivially to language-specific pre-training.

AI.tldr: The paper compares multilingual pre-training strategies and finds that merging independently monolingually pre-trained models causes severe performance degradation, unlike mixing languages during pre-training.
AI.motivation: To understand whether the success of model merging in fine-tuning (e.g., task- or language-specific adapters/models) can be extended to the pre-training stage, in order to flexibly build strong multilingual models from strong monolingual ones instead of pre-training a single mixed-language model.
AI.method: They run a controlled empirical study comparing three setups for multilingual capability: (1) mixed-language pre-training in a single model, (2) monolingual pre-training of separate models for each language followed by parameter merging, and (3) purely monolingual pre-training without merging. They evaluate language and cross-language performance and analyze representational similarity between models to understand when merging works or fails.
AI.result: Monolingual pre-training gives strong performance within each language, but any attempt to merge separately monolingually pre-trained models causes performance collapse due to interference between their parameters. The study also finds that successful merging requires the models to have sufficiently similar internal representations, which is not the case for independently monolingually pre-trained models.
AI.conclusion: Model merging that works in fine-tuning scenarios does not straightforwardly transfer to merging fully monolingual pre-trained models for multilingual capability. Representational similarity appears to be a key prerequisite, so building multilingual models via post-hoc merging of unrelated monolingual pre-trainings is ineffective compared to mixed-language pre-training.

title: ATWL: A Formal Language for Representing, Comparing, and Reusing Visual Analytics Workflows
summary: Visual analytics (VA) workflows are inherently complex, involving data transformation, feature engineering, visual representation, and human interpretation. They are typically described in unstructured prose, hindering systematic comparison, reuse of proven strategies, and training of novices. We present Artifact-Transform Workflow Language (ATWL), a domain-agnostic, declarative language that formally represents VA workflows by capturing their structure and underlying analytical intent. ATWL is built upon a modular ontology of eight artifact types (entities, features, arrangements, visualisations, patterns, models, knowledge, specifications) and transforms characterised by standardised intents (e.g., define-unit, characterise, contextualise, abstract). To show that formalisation effort need not impede adoption, we extract workflows from research papers through supervised interaction with LLM agents, reducing the human role to review and refinement. Using this process, we constructed a library of seventeen ATWL workflows from published VA papers. Cross-workflow analysis reveals structural regularities -- a recurrent meta-structure, recurring motifs, reusable building blocks, diverse iterative strategies, and cross-domain equivalences -- that remain invisible in prose. We further evaluate practical utility through a controlled experiment in which the same LLM addressed two analytical problems with the library supplied either as original papers or as ATWL representations. Both forms enabled useful recommendations, but the formal representation systematically added explicit iteration structure, typed data flow, fragment-level adaptation provenance, and compactness supporting scaling beyond what prose libraries can fit in an LLM's context. ATWL enables a transition from narrative descriptions to formally represented, comparable, and reusable analytical knowledge.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ATWL, a formal, domain-agnostic language to represent visual analytics workflows, enabling comparison, reuse, and LLM-based support beyond what prose descriptions allow.
AI.motivation: Visual analytics workflows are complex and usually only described in unstructured narrative text, which makes it difficult to systematically compare approaches, reuse successful strategies, train newcomers, or provide automated support. There is a need for a formal, machine-readable way to capture both the structure and the analytical intent of these workflows so they can be analyzed, reused, and scaled.
AI.method: The authors design ATWL, a declarative language grounded in an ontology of eight artifact types and a set of standardised transform intents. They then build a semi-automated extraction process where LLM agents, guided by supervision, convert prose descriptions in VA research papers into ATWL workflows, with humans only reviewing and refining. Using this pipeline, they construct a library of 17 workflows, analyze them for structural patterns, and run a controlled experiment where an LLM solves analytical problems using either the original papers or the ATWL library as a knowledge source.
AI.result: The authors successfully formalize 17 published VA workflows into ATWL and, through cross-workflow analysis, identify regular meta-structures, recurring motifs and building blocks, diverse iteration patterns, and equivalences across domains that are not evident in narrative descriptions. In the experiment, LLMs produce useful recommendations from both prose and ATWL inputs, but ATWL consistently provides advantages: clearer iteration structure, explicit typed data flows, provenance for how workflow fragments are adapted, and a more compact representation that scales better within LLM context limits.
AI.conclusion: ATWL offers a practical, domain-agnostic way to formally encode visual analytics workflows, capturing both structure and analytical intent. This formalization reveals latent regularities across workflows, supports reuse and comparison, and improves how LLMs can leverage prior analytical knowledge at scale. The approach demonstrates that such formal representations can be semi-automatically extracted from prose without excessive human burden, enabling a shift from narrative to structured, reusable analytical knowledge in visual analytics.

title: TIAR: Trajectory-Informed Advantage Reweighting for LLM Abstention Learning
summary: This paper investigates large language model (LLM) abstention learning, specifically using ternary reward, which incentivize truthfulness in large language models. This paper extends that idea by moving from a ternary reward to a Trajectory-Informed advantage reweighting, dynamically re-weights the abstention reward during Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) training. The objective of this work focuses on abstention learning instead of improving truthfulness, serving as an exploration into hallucination reduction. The novelty of this paper lies in methodological innovation, advantage re-weighting, and benchmark selection. Leveraging GRPO's multiple trajectories as a natural abstention signal, this method uses a reward signal to explore knowledge boundaries and encourage consistency. By demonstrating that trajectories can be used as a confidence indicator of the policy relative to the query, they are then used to dynamically calculate the abstention advantage. AbstentionBench is used as the evaluation benchmark, as this work aims to contribute to the field of abstention learning. All datasets on the benchmark were tested against this method and various baselines. Empirical results demonstrate that TIAR achieves state-of-the-art abstention F1 scores across five of six evaluation categories, outperforming the static ternary baseline on 17 of 31 benchmark datasets while fully preserving baseline accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new abstention-learning method (TIAR) for large language models that dynamically reweights abstention rewards during GRPO training, improving abstention F1 without hurting accuracy on AbstentionBench.
AI.motivation: Hallucinations in LLMs remain a major issue. Prior work on ternary rewards (correct / incorrect / abstain) showed that explicitly rewarding abstention can increase truthfulness, but used a static, hand-designed abstention reward. The authors want a more principled, adaptive way to determine when and how strongly to incentivize abstention, using information already present in GRPO trajectories, and to evaluate methods in a setting focused on abstention rather than only truthfulness.
AI.method: They introduce Trajectory-Informed Advantage Reweighting (TIAR) within Group Relative Policy Optimization. GRPO naturally generates multiple trajectories (candidate responses) per query. The method interprets these trajectories as a confidence and consistency signal of the policy with respect to the query, then computes a dynamic abstention advantage based on this signal. Instead of a fixed ternary reward, TIAR reweights the abstention reward per query and training step using the trajectory-based advantage, encouraging abstention when the policy appears uncertain or inconsistent while preserving behavior where the policy is confident and correct.
AI.result: Using AbstentionBench as the evaluation benchmark, they test TIAR across all datasets and compare against several baselines, including the static ternary-reward approach. TIAR achieves state-of-the-art abstention F1 scores in 5 out of 6 evaluation categories and beats the ternary baseline on 17 of 31 datasets, while maintaining the same level of accuracy as the baseline (i.e., it improves abstention behavior without sacrificing correctness).
AI.conclusion: Trajectory-based information in GRPO can be harnessed as a natural confidence and abstention signal, enabling dynamic reweighting of abstention rewards. The proposed TIAR method improves abstention F1 across diverse datasets on AbstentionBench and preserves accuracy, suggesting that adaptive abstention learning is an effective strategy for reducing hallucinations and better handling model uncertainty in LLMs.

title: Credit Assignment with Resets in Language Model Reasoning
summary: Contemporary reinforcement learning with verifiable reward methods post-train language models on multi-step reasoning by assigning a single outcome reward uniformly across all tokens in a trajectory. Such uniform assignment ignores which steps contributed to success or failure. Improving credit assignment can address this limitation by enabling targeted refinement of faulty reasoning steps, rather than updating entire trajectories uniformly. Resets are one such simple mechanism, enabling more precise credit assignment by returning to an intermediate state and resampling counterfactual continuations, so that outcome differences can be attributed to decisions made at that point. We propose two such methods: Random-Reset Policy Optimization (RRPO), where reset states are drawn randomly from reasoning steps, and Self-Reset Policy Optimization (SRPO), where the model self-localizes the erroneous step in an incorrect trajectory and resets there. We analyze these methods within the Conservative Policy Iteration (CPI) framework. Extending CPI with a credit-assignment oracle that targets improvable states yields provable improvements over random resets. Across models and reasoning benchmarks, SRPO consistently outperforms standard GRPO and RRPO by sampling multiple suffix continuations at a self-localized reset and learning from their rewards, using only the model itself with no external supervision.

AI.tldr: They introduce reset-based reinforcement learning methods (RRPO and SRPO) that improve credit assignment for multi-step reasoning in language models, leading to better performance than standard GRPO.
AI.motivation: Existing RLHF-style methods for training language models on reasoning tasks assign the same final reward to every token in a trajectory. This uniform credit assignment cannot distinguish which specific reasoning steps were responsible for success or failure, limiting the efficiency and precision of learning. The authors aim to enable more targeted updates focused on faulty steps in reasoning while maintaining a practical training pipeline without extra supervision.
AI.method: They propose using "resets" during policy optimization: returning to an intermediate reasoning state and sampling multiple counterfactual continuations from that point to see how different choices affect the final reward. They define two algorithms: (1) Random-Reset Policy Optimization (RRPO), where reset points are selected randomly from the reasoning steps; and (2) Self-Reset Policy Optimization (SRPO), where the model itself attempts to identify the erroneous step in an incorrect trajectory and uses that as the reset point. They analyze these algorithms in the Conservative Policy Iteration (CPI) framework and theoretically extend CPI by introducing a credit-assignment oracle that focuses on improvable states, allowing comparison between random and targeted resets. Empirically, they train language models with these methods on multi-step reasoning benchmarks, sampling multiple alternative suffixes at the reset points and updating based on their rewards.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows that having a credit-assignment oracle that focuses on improvable states within CPI leads to stronger policy improvement guarantees than random resets. Experimentally, across different language models and reasoning benchmarks, SRPO—using self-localized resets and multiple resampled suffixes—consistently outperforms standard GRPO and the random-reset baseline RRPO. This demonstrates that better credit assignment via targeted resets yields more effective learning for reasoning tasks without external labels for error locations.
AI.conclusion: Reset-based policy optimization improves credit assignment in reinforcement learning for language model reasoning. Specifically, self-resetting—where the model identifies and revisits its own likely error step and explores alternative continuations—offers both theoretical advantages (within an extended CPI framework) and empirical gains over standard outcome-only RL methods. This approach enables more focused refinement of reasoning chains using only model-internal signals, suggesting a practical path to stronger reasoning abilities without additional supervision.

title: Mitigating Provenance-Role Collapse in Long-Term Agents via Typed Memory Representation
summary: Long-term memory is essential for persistent LLM agents, yet prevailing architectures store historical interactions as unstructured, flat text. This unconstrained storage induces provenance-role collapse, a critical failure mode where agents suffer from source-monitoring errors. To resolve this cognitive vulnerability at the architectural level, we propose MemIR, a typed Memory Intermediate Representation that operationalizes source monitoring as a structural constraint. MemIR writes long-term memory into grounded atoms that separate raw evidence, retrieval cues, and truth-bearing claims, with factual authorization restricted to supported claim atoms. It then applies multi-route atomic projection and provenance-scoped utilization to transform heterogeneous retrieval hits into claim-centered candidate bundles and a normalized fact interface for answer generation. Experiments on LoCoMo and BEAM-100K demonstrate that MemIR consistently outperforms existing memory baselines, especially on tasks requiring source tracking, temporal grounding, and aggregation of fragmented evidence.

AI.tldr: They propose MemIR, a structured memory representation for LLM agents that prevents source-monitoring errors and improves long-term memory use, especially when tracking sources, time, and fragmented evidence.
AI.motivation: Existing long-term memory for LLM agents is usually stored as flat, unstructured text, which causes a provenance-role collapse: agents mix up where information came from, what is raw evidence, and what is a model’s own conclusion. This leads to source-monitoring errors and unreliable factual claims. The authors want an architectural solution that embeds source monitoring directly into the memory representation so agents can reason more reliably over long-term histories.
AI.method: They design MemIR, a typed Memory Intermediate Representation. MemIR encodes memory as grounded atoms of different types that explicitly separate (1) raw evidence, (2) retrieval cues, and (3) truth-bearing claim atoms. Only claim atoms that are supported by evidence atoms are allowed to serve as factual statements. At retrieval time, MemIR performs multi-route atomic projection and provenance-scoped utilization: it turns heterogeneous retrieval hits into structured, claim-centered bundles while preserving provenance constraints. These bundles provide a normalized, provenance-aware fact interface for answer generation by the LLM agent.
AI.result: On the LoCoMo and BEAM-100K benchmarks, MemIR is empirically evaluated against existing long-term memory baselines. It consistently achieves higher performance, with especially strong gains on tasks that need explicit source tracking, temporal grounding of information, and combining fragmented pieces of evidence scattered across memory.
AI.conclusion: Structuring long-term memory as a typed intermediate representation that encodes source monitoring and provenance constraints (MemIR) mitigates provenance-role collapse and source-monitoring errors in LLM agents. By separating evidence, cues, and claims and enforcing that only supported claims can act as facts, MemIR yields more reliable use of long-term memory and outperforms unstructured memory baselines, particularly on provenance- and time-sensitive tasks.

title: What Gets Cited: Competitive GEO in AI Answer Engines
summary: AI answer engines generate answers from retrieved pages but cite only a few sources. This makes visibility depend not just on ranking, but on being cited. We study competitive Generative Engine Optimization (GEO): when two retrieved candidates compete, what makes one more likely to be cited first? We build a controlled two-document retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) testbed that injects exactly two candidate sources into the model context and measures which source is referenced by the first citation marker in the output. Across six LLMs we execute 252,000 trials, repeated paired comparisons under one factorial program over 18 content factors. In each trial the two sources differ in exactly one factor; we use brand anonymization and counterbalanced source order to separate content effects from position bias. Mixed-effects models show that topical relevance and list position are the biggest drivers of being cited first. Including explicit price information and a recent timestamp also helps consistently. Completeness and trust cues add smaller gains, while formatting-only edits have little impact. We release a reproducible evaluation protocol and a prioritized GEO checklist for practitioners, and we exercised it in an early internal pilot at Sprinklr, where teams reported positive qualitative feedback on workflow usability.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates which of two retrieved documents is more likely to be cited first by AI answer engines and finds topical relevance and position in lists are the dominant factors, with price information and recency also helping.
AI.motivation: As AI answer engines become a key interface to information, being merely retrieved is not enough for content providers; their pages must also be cited in the generated answer to gain visibility and value. There is little systematic understanding of which content attributes increase the likelihood of being cited when multiple sources compete. The authors aim to characterize these drivers to inform Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) strategies and to provide a rigorous, reproducible evaluation framework.
AI.method: The authors construct a controlled two-document retrieval-augmented generation testbed where exactly two candidate sources are injected into a language model’s context. They then measure which source is referenced by the first citation marker in the model’s answer. They run 252,000 paired trials across six LLMs, varying one of 18 predefined content factors at a time while holding all others constant. To isolate true content effects from confounds, they anonymize brands and counterbalance the order of the two sources. They analyze the resulting binary outcomes using mixed-effects statistical models to estimate the impact of each factor on the probability of being cited first.
AI.result: Topical relevance to the query and the source’s list position in the provided context are the strongest predictors of being cited first. Explicit price information and more recent timestamps also robustly increase citation likelihood. Factors related to completeness of information and trust cues (e.g., certain credibility indicators) provide smaller but positive effects. Purely formatting-oriented edits, without substantive content changes, show minimal impact on being cited. Results are consistent across the six evaluated LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Visibility in AI-generated answers depends heavily on more than just retrieval ranking; being cited is strongly driven by topical alignment and positional advantages, with recency and concrete price details offering additional gains. Superficial formatting tweaks are unlikely to matter much. The paper contributes a reproducible GEO evaluation protocol and a practical, prioritized GEO checklist that practitioners can apply to optimize content for AI answer engines. An internal pilot at Sprinklr suggests the framework is usable and valuable in real-world workflows.

title: Causal Tongue-Tie: LLMs Can Encode Causal Direction, But Their Yes/No Outputs Fail to Express
summary: We find a mismatch between what large language models encode about a causal question and what they answer. On anti-commonsense CLadder items, a fixed linear probe recovers the evidence-supported answer from the model's hidden state (accuracy approximately 0.97), while the spoken Yes/No reverts to the commonsense one (accuracy approximately 0.5). We call this approximately +0.5 gap Causal Tongue-Tie: a wrong Yes/No decomposes into two separable failure modes: no internal signal versus a signal the verbal interface cannot say. The implication cuts both ways for output-only causal benchmarks: a benchmark "correct" need not mean the model has understood, and a benchmark "wrong" need not mean it cannot. Sweeping claims about whether LLMs can do causal reasoning, drawn from a single accuracy number, deserve a second look.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that large language models can internally represent the correct causal answer even when their final Yes/No output is wrong.
AI.motivation: To question the reliability of judging LLMs’ causal reasoning ability purely from their final answers on benchmarks, especially when those benchmarks rely on commonsense-defying (“anti-commonsense”) cases.
AI.method: Use a dataset of anti-commonsense causal questions (CLadder). Apply a fixed linear probe to hidden states of an LLM to see if the internal representation encodes the evidence-supported causal answer, and compare this to the model’s explicit Yes/No responses.
AI.result: The linear probe predicts the correct evidence-based answer with very high accuracy (~0.97), but the LLM’s direct Yes/No answers only reach about chance-level accuracy (~0.5) and often default to the commonsense but incorrect answer. This reveals a roughly +0.5 gap between what the model ‘knows’ internally and what it says.
AI.conclusion: LLMs sometimes have an internal causal signal that their output interface fails to express, creating a “Causal Tongue-Tie.” Therefore, single accuracy numbers on output-only causal benchmarks are misleading: correct outputs don’t guarantee genuine understanding, and incorrect outputs don’t prove inability. Broad claims about LLM causal reasoning must be re-evaluated with this internal–external mismatch in mind.

title: StructBreak: Structural Cognitive Overload-Induced Safety Failures in MLLMs
comment: 23 pages; accepted to Findings of ACL 2026. This paper contains examples of harmful content
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) excel at structural reasoning yet suffer from a sharp logical brittleness in structural consistency. We term this phenomenon Structural Cognitive Overload (SCO), a byproduct of the contention between deep reasoning and safety alignment. However, prior work has predominantly targeted typographic and pixel-level perturbations, leaving the study of SCO largely unexplored. To this end, we propose StructBreak, an automated end-to-end framework designed to quantify SCO. By leveraging StructBreak, we uncover a novel higher-order cognitive overload attack paradigm; notably, this attack operates under a practical black-box setting, requiring no internal model access. Consequently, we utilize this framework to establish a comprehensive benchmark spanning ten diverse threat scenarios. Empirical evaluations on six leading MLLMs reveal that SCO readily triggers toxic generation, yielding a 92% average ASR (up to 97% on Gemini 2.5). To elucidate the mechanism of SCO, we further conduct model-level interpretations spanning attention dynamics, latent space topology, and geometric analysis. Our findings reveal that StructBreak acts as a novel structural channel to circumvent safety filters. Furthermore, the limited efficacy of inherent safety mechanisms underscores that current alignment paradigms are insufficient for the era of complex multimodal reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces StructBreak, a framework to measure and exploit Structural Cognitive Overload (SCO) in multimodal large language models, revealing that complex structural reasoning can override safety alignment and lead to toxic outputs.
AI.motivation: Although multimodal large language models are strong at structural reasoning, they remain logically brittle in maintaining structural consistency and safety. Existing robustness work focuses mostly on low-level visual or textual perturbations and does not address how higher-order structural reasoning itself can be weaponized to bypass safety. The authors aim to understand and quantify this structural weakness, termed Structural Cognitive Overload, and to show its implications for safety alignment.
AI.method: The authors define Structural Cognitive Overload (SCO) as a failure mode where complex structural reasoning overwhelms safety alignment. They design StructBreak, an automated end-to-end framework that generates structurally challenging inputs and evaluates model behavior in a black-box setting without access to internal model weights. Using StructBreak, they construct a benchmark with ten distinct threat scenarios and run systematic evaluations on six state-of-the-art multimodal LLMs. They also analyze model behavior using attention-pattern inspection, latent space topology analysis, and geometric characterization to interpret how SCO arises and propagates through the models.
AI.result: Using StructBreak, the authors discover a new class of higher-order cognitive overload attacks that exploit structural reasoning rather than low-level perturbations. These attacks are effective in a black-box setting and yield a high average attack success rate of 92% across six leading MLLMs, peaking at 97% on Gemini 2.5. The analysis demonstrates that StructBreak provides a structural channel that bypasses built-in safety filters and reveals systematic weaknesses across different models.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that current alignment and safety mechanisms in multimodal LLMs are inadequate in the face of complex structural reasoning tasks. Structural Cognitive Overload offers an effective path to induce toxic behavior despite nominal safety filters, and StructBreak serves both as a diagnostic tool and a benchmark to expose this vulnerability. The authors argue that next-generation safety paradigms must explicitly address structural and cognitive overload effects rather than focusing only on surface-level perturbation robustness.

title: Universal Activation Verbalizer: A Unified Framework for Cross-Model Activation Explanation
summary: Activation verbalization explains hidden representations in natural language, but existing methods are mostly limited to self-explanation, where each model explains only its own activations. We introduce Universal Activation Verbalizer (UAV), a framework that uses a shared decoder to explain activations from heterogeneous donor models. UAV learns a lightweight adapter that converts donor activations into soft tokens in decoder's embedding space, and further supports adapter-only transfer by reusing a frozen decoder-side LoRA while training only a new adapter for another donor. Across classification, fact retrieval, and gist summarization, UAV remains competitive with strong self-explanation baselines while enabling cross-model verbalization across model families and scales. Ablations show that decoder-side tuning mainly improves task behavior, whereas the adapter provides the activation-grounded factual and semantic information needed for faithful explanations.

AI.tldr: Proposes Universal Activation Verbalizer (UAV), a shared decoder framework that explains hidden activations from many different language models via verbal descriptions.
AI.motivation: Existing activation verbalization methods can only explain a model’s own hidden states (self-explanation) and do not generalize across different architectures or scales. The authors aim to create a unified system that can verbalize internal representations from heterogeneous models, improving interpretability and efficiency by sharing an explanatory decoder.
AI.method: Introduce UAV, which pairs multiple donor models with a single shared decoder. For each donor, UAV trains a lightweight adapter that maps that model’s hidden activations into soft token embeddings in the decoder’s space. A frozen decoder-side LoRA can be reused, enabling adapter-only transfer when adding new donor models. The framework is evaluated on tasks like classification, fact retrieval, and gist summarization and dissected via ablations separating decoder-side tuning from adapter learning.
AI.result: UAV achieves performance comparable to strong self-explanation baselines while enabling cross-model activation verbalization across different model families and sizes. It supports efficient transfer to new donor models by training only a new adapter while keeping the decoder and its LoRA frozen. Ablation studies show distinct roles for components: decoder-side tuning primarily shapes task behavior, while the adapter is responsible for encoding activation-grounded factual and semantic content into the explanations.
AI.conclusion: A shared-decoder framework with per-model adapters can faithfully verbalize hidden activations from diverse language models, matching specialized self-explanation approaches while being more general and modular. Decoder tuning governs how explanations are structured for the task, whereas adapters carry the donor-specific semantic signal, enabling scalable and transferable interpretability across model families.

title: Personalize-then-Store: Benchmarking and Learning Personalized Memory for Long-horizon Agents
summary: Existing large language model (LLM) based memory systems apply universal, static policies that overlook a fundamental reality: the contexts that are worth storing in memory are different across users. This misalignment wastes limited memory budget on transient interactions while failing to preserve critical context for long horizon tasks. To address this gap, we investigate an underexplored question: can LLM based memory systems learn personalized memory policies? We introduce PerMemBench, the first benchmark for evaluating personalized memory systems, featuring multi year, multi domain interaction histories across diverse user personas. We further present the first empirical study of memory personalization, proposing session level storage gating, a lightweight framework that selectively bypasses memory operations for transient sessions. Our study confirms that personalization yields substantial retention gains under perfect gating, yet reveals that accurate gating remains an open and critical challenge.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to make LLM memory systems personalized rather than using one-size-fits-all, introducing a benchmark (PerMemBench) and a gating method for deciding which sessions’ context to store, showing big potential gains but that accurate gating is still hard.
AI.motivation: Current LLM memory systems use static, universal policies about what to store, ignoring that different users value different information. This mismatch causes limited memory capacity to be used on short-lived, less important interactions while missing important long-term context, especially for long-horizon tasks. The authors are motivated to understand whether memory policies can be personalized to each user and to provide tools and analysis to study this.
AI.method: The authors (1) construct PerMemBench, a benchmark with multi-year, multi-domain interaction histories reflecting diverse user personas, designed to evaluate personalized memory systems; and (2) conduct an empirical study of memory personalization using a session-level storage gating framework, which selectively skips memory write operations for sessions classified as transient, thereby personalizing what is stored based on session characteristics and user needs.
AI.result: Using PerMemBench and the proposed storage gating framework, the study shows that personalized memory policies can substantially improve retention of useful information when gating decisions are assumed perfect. However, real-world gating accuracy is limited, revealing a performance gap between the ideal and what current methods can achieve, and highlighting that reliably identifying which sessions are worth storing is difficult.
AI.conclusion: Personalization in LLM memory systems is both beneficial and necessary: when memory storage policies are tailored to users, retention of important long-term context improves significantly. However, achieving this in practice depends on solving the challenging problem of accurate session-level gating. PerMemBench and the initial gating framework serve as foundations for future work on more effective personalized memory mechanisms.

title: Can LLMs Time Travel? Enhancing Temporal Consistency in Legal Agentic Search through Reinforcement Learning
summary: While large language models (LLMs) augmented with agentic search capabilities show promise for legal reasoning, they overlook a fundamental constraint that applicable law must match the temporal context of each case, as retroactive application of statutes violates core legal principles and leads to erroneous conclusions. Our observations reveal that current legal LLMs suffer from temporal bias anchored to their training cutoff, while search agents rarely incorporate temporal constraints into queries, and that web search alone cannot provide the precise statute and precedent citations that legal reasoning demands. To address these challenges, we propose LegalSearch-R1, an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework that pairs local statute RAG for precise article matching with online web search for broader legal knowledge, trained on temporally-indexed data spanning multiple amendment periods to enforce temporal consistency. Extensive experiments on our benchmark covering 13 legal tasks demonstrate that our 7B-parameter agent outperforms state-of-the-art deep research frameworks and specialized legal LLMs by 12.9% to 29.8%, surpasses baselines by 57.7% to 80.3% on temporal consistency, and exhibits robust out-of-domain generalization. The code and data are available at https://github.com/AlexFanw/LegalSearch-R1.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LegalSearch-R1, a reinforcement-learning-based legal agent that explicitly handles the time dimension of laws by combining local statute RAG with web search, achieving significantly better accuracy and temporal consistency than prior legal LLM systems.
AI.motivation: Existing legal LLMs and search-augmented agents ignore a key legal constraint: the applicable law must match the time period of the case. Because laws change over time and retroactive application is often invalid, ignoring temporal context leads to legally incorrect reasoning. Current models show temporal bias toward their training cutoff, search agents do not encode temporal constraints into queries, and raw web search cannot reliably surface precisely dated statutes and precedents. There is a need for a system that can reason over time-sensitive legal sources and maintain temporal consistency in its answers.
AI.method: The authors propose LegalSearch-R1, an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework for legal reasoning. It integrates (1) a local retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) module over statutes, designed for precise article-level matching conditioned on temporal indices (e.g., different amendment periods), and (2) an online web search component to capture broader and up-to-date legal information. The system is trained with reinforcement learning on temporally-indexed datasets that span multiple versions of laws across amendment periods, so that the agent learns to query, retrieve, and reason in a way that respects the correct temporal context. The framework is evaluated on a newly constructed benchmark of 13 legal tasks, many of which explicitly require temporally consistent legal reasoning.
AI.result: On the 13-task legal benchmark, the 7B-parameter LegalSearch-R1 agent surpasses state-of-the-art deep research frameworks and specialized legal LLMs by 12.9%–29.8% in overall performance. In terms of temporal consistency—i.e., correctly aligning legal citations and reasoning with the relevant time period—it improves over baselines by 57.7%–80.3%. The system also shows strong robustness when evaluated on out-of-domain legal tasks not seen during training, indicating good generalization of its temporal reasoning and retrieval strategies.
AI.conclusion: LegalSearch-R1 effectively mitigates temporal bias in legal LLMs by explicitly modeling the time dimension of laws during retrieval and reasoning, combining local statute RAG with web search under an RL training paradigm on temporally-indexed data. This yields substantial gains in accuracy, temporal consistency, and out-of-domain robustness over existing legal LLMs and research agents. The work highlights the importance of enforcing temporal alignment between legal sources and case contexts and provides open-source code and data to support further research in time-aware legal AI systems.

title: ADMFormer: An Adaptive-Decomposition Transformer with Time-Varying Masked Spatial Attention for Traffic Forecasting
summary: Accurate traffic forecasting is essential for intelligent transportation systems, supporting a wide range of real-world applications. However, it remains challenging due to two key factors:~(1) Traffic series contain heterogeneous temporal patterns, where stable periodic regularities coexist with event-driven fluctuations. Existing methods often treat them within a unified representation, limiting their ability to capture fine-grained temporal dynamics.~(2)Spatial dependencies among nodes are inherently dynamic and sparse, while dense all-pairs attention often introduces redundant interactions and amplifies noise. To address these issues, we propose ADMFormer, an Adaptive-Decomposition Transformer with Time-Varying Masked Spatial Attention. Specifically, ADMFormer first employs a time-node adaptive gating mechanism to decouple traffic signals into dominant regularities and residual fluctuations that vary across time and nodes. A dual-branch temporal module is then designed to separately capture global periodic dependencies and high-frequency irregular variations from these two decomposed components. Furthermore, ADMFormer introduces a time-varying masked spatial attention that sparsifies spatial interactions based on real-time traffic states, thereby effectively preserving dynamic and informative dependencies. Extensive experiments on four real-world datasets demonstrate that ADMFormer achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: ADMFormer is a Transformer-based traffic forecasting model that decomposes heterogeneous temporal patterns and applies time-varying masked spatial attention to achieve state-of-the-art prediction accuracy.
AI.motivation: Traffic forecasting is vital for intelligent transportation systems but remains difficult because traffic time series contain mixed stable periodic patterns and irregular fluctuations, and spatial dependencies between locations are dynamic and sparse. Existing methods often use unified temporal representations and dense spatial attention, which limits their ability to model fine-grained temporal dynamics and introduces redundant or noisy spatial interactions.
AI.method: The paper proposes ADMFormer, an Adaptive-Decomposition Transformer with Time-Varying Masked Spatial Attention. First, a time-node adaptive gating mechanism decomposes each traffic series into dominant regularities and residual fluctuations that vary over time and across nodes. Next, a dual-branch temporal module separately models global periodic dependencies from the regular component and high-frequency irregular variations from the residual component. Finally, a time-varying masked spatial attention mechanism sparsifies spatial interactions based on current traffic states, dynamically selecting informative node-to-node dependencies instead of using dense all-pairs attention.
AI.result: Through experiments on four real-world traffic datasets, ADMFormer consistently outperforms competing baselines, achieving state-of-the-art results on standard traffic forecasting metrics, demonstrating the effectiveness of adaptive temporal decomposition and masked spatial attention.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling traffic signals into regular and fluctuating components, combined with dual-branch temporal modeling and time-varying masked spatial attention, enables more accurate and robust traffic forecasting. The ADMFormer framework effectively captures heterogeneous temporal patterns and dynamic sparse spatial dependencies, providing a strong and efficient solution for intelligent transportation applications.

title: Does Continued Pretraining on a Learner Corpus Improve Automated Essay Scoring on English Proficiency Tests? Evidence from EFCAMDAT
comment: 16 pages, 3 figures, 10 tables, including references and appendices
summary: Recent automated essay scoring (AES) studies increasingly use pretrained transformer models, but these models are usually pretrained on general-domain English and may under-represent second-language learner writing. This study investigates whether domain-adaptive continued pretraining (DAPT) on the EFCAMDAT learner corpus improves transformer-based AES for English proficiency tests. We apply DAPT to three transformer encoders and evaluate them on FCE and IELTS in both in-domain scoring and few-shot cross-dataset transfer. Full-corpus DAPT produces mixed results across models, datasets, and metrics. Further analyses suggest that these mixed effects are partly explained by mismatches in proficiency, genre, and communicative purpose between EFCAMDAT and the downstream datasets. A proficiency-based ablation shows that targeted DAPT using CEFR-aligned subsets improves downstream scoring more reliably than full-corpus DAPT, especially for FCE with B1--B2 data. However, these gains do not consistently improve cross-dataset transfer. Overall, the findings suggest that continued pretraining on a learner-writing corpus can benefit in-domain AES for English assessment when the pretraining data is sufficiently aligned with the downstream assessment settings. However, it does not automatically improve transferability across different English proficiency test datasets.
