AI.tldr: The paper tests whether continuing pretraining transformer encoders on a learner-English corpus (EFCAMDAT) improves automated essay scoring on FCE and IELTS; targeted pretraining on proficiency-aligned subsets helps in-domain scoring when data aligns well, but does not reliably improve cross-dataset transfer.
AI.motivation: Pretrained transformers used in automated essay scoring are typically trained on general-domain native English, which may not represent second-language learner writing well. The authors want to know if adapting these models to learner-writing data via domain-adaptive continued pretraining can yield better scoring performance, especially for standardized English proficiency tests, and whether such adaptation helps models generalize across different test datasets.
AI.method: The authors perform domain-adaptive continued pretraining (DAPT) on three transformer encoders using the EFCAMDAT corpus of L2 learner writing. They then fine-tune and evaluate these adapted models on two English proficiency test datasets, FCE and IELTS, in both standard in-domain scoring and few-shot cross-dataset transfer setups. They compare full-corpus DAPT to proficiency-targeted DAPT, where pretraining data is restricted to CEFR-aligned subsets (e.g., B1–B2) to better match the downstream tasks, and conduct analyses on how proficiency, genre, and communicative purpose mismatches influence results.
AI.result: Full-corpus DAPT yields mixed outcomes, with improvements depending on the specific transformer model, dataset (FCE vs IELTS), and evaluation metric; in some settings performance improves, in others it does not. Analyses indicate that mismatches between the EFCAMDAT corpus and downstream datasets—especially in learner proficiency levels, text genre, and communicative function—partly explain these inconsistent effects. Proficiency-based ablation experiments reveal that targeted DAPT using CEFR-aligned subsets of EFCAMDAT produces more stable improvements in scoring accuracy, particularly for FCE when using B1–B2 learner data. However, these targeted gains do not robustly carry over to few-shot cross-dataset transfer scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Continued pretraining of transformer encoders on learner-writing corpora can enhance automated essay scoring for specific English assessments, but only when the pretraining data is carefully aligned with the downstream test’s proficiency range and discourse characteristics. Full, indiscriminate DAPT on a broad learner corpus is not reliably beneficial, and even proficiency-targeted DAPT does not guarantee better transfer across different exam datasets such as FCE and IELTS. Hence, AES systems should use domain adaptation strategies that consider proficiency, genre, and task alignment rather than assuming that more learner data will automatically improve both in-domain performance and cross-dataset generalization.

title: PHGNet: Prototype-Guided Hypergraph Construction for Heterogeneous Spatiotemporal Forecasting
summary: As a core task in intelligent transportation systems, traffic forecasting plays a critical role in urban traffic management. Accurate traffic forecasting relies on modeling complex spatiotemporal dependencies, which is inherently challenging due to spatial heterogeneity in traffic systems.Despite significant progress, most existing methods are still limited to pairwise spatial dependency modeling, making it difficult to capture dynamic high-order interactions among nodes with similar traffic patterns. To address this issue, we propose PHGNet, a novel spatiotemporal forecasting framework based on prototype-guided hypergraph construction. At the core of PHGNet, a prototype learning mechanism is designed to adaptively assign pattern-similar nodes to hyperedges, thereby capturing high-order interactions with time-varying structures. To improve the reliability of dynamic hypergraph construction, we further develop a global-local node representation module to extract time-consistent features. For forecasting, iterative residual refinement and Temporal Query Attention are introduced to improve forecasting accuracy while supporting efficient parallel decoding. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world datasets demonstrate that PHGNet achieves superior predictive performance compared with state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: PHGNet is a traffic forecasting framework that uses prototype-guided hypergraphs to model high-order, dynamic spatiotemporal dependencies and achieves state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Existing traffic forecasting models mostly capture only pairwise spatial dependencies on graphs and struggle with spatial heterogeneity and dynamic, high-order interactions among nodes sharing similar traffic patterns. There is a need for a model that can better represent complex, time-varying relationships in traffic networks to improve forecasting accuracy.
AI.method: The paper proposes PHGNet, which introduces prototype-guided hypergraph construction: a prototype learning mechanism groups nodes with similar traffic patterns into hyperedges to model high-order interactions with time-varying structures. A global-local node representation module extracts time-consistent features to stabilize dynamic hypergraph construction. For prediction, the model uses iterative residual refinement and a Temporal Query Attention mechanism to enhance forecast accuracy while allowing efficient parallel decoding.
AI.result: On multiple real-world traffic datasets, PHGNet outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in forecasting accuracy, indicating its effectiveness in modeling complex spatiotemporal dependencies.
AI.conclusion: Prototype-guided hypergraph modeling combined with robust global-local node representations and advanced temporal decoding mechanisms (iterative residual refinement and Temporal Query Attention) provides a powerful framework for traffic forecasting, particularly in capturing dynamic high-order spatial interactions and improving predictive performance.

title: Thaka at KSAA-2026 Task 2: Regularized Fine-Tuning for Arabic Speech Diacritization
comment: 4 pages, 1 figure. Published in Proceedings of OSACT7 (LREC 2026). Winning system for KSAA-2026 Task 2 on Arabic Speech Diacritization
summary: We describe the winning system for Task 2 of the KSAA-2026 Shared Task on Arabic Speech Dictation with Automatic Diacritization. The task requires producing fully diacritized Arabic text from speech audio and undiacritized transcripts, with only 2,327 training samples available and no external data permitted. Our system fine-tunes CATT-Whisper, a character-level multimodal model combining a pretrained CATT text encoder with a frozen Whisper speech encoder. The key to our approach is training regularization: R-Drop consistency regularization, Optuna-optimized hyperparameters with high weight decay, and Focal Loss. At inference, we average 200 stochastic forward passes across four model checkpoints using Monte Carlo Dropout at the softmax probability level. The system achieves 23.26% WER on the primary leaderboard metric (with case endings, including no-diacritic positions), placing 1st among all participants.

AI.tldr: Winning system for low-resource Arabic speech dictation with automatic diacritization using a multimodal CATT-Whisper model, heavy regularization, and Monte Carlo Dropout ensembling.
AI.motivation: Arabic diacritization from speech is challenging, especially with very limited labeled data and no external resources. Accurate diacritization is crucial for readability, disambiguation, and downstream NLP tasks, yet most systems assume large corpora. The shared task setting forces methods that are robust in low-resource, constrained-data scenarios.
AI.method: Fine-tune CATT-Whisper, which combines a pretrained CATT text encoder with a frozen Whisper speech encoder at the character level. Use strong regularization during training: R-Drop consistency regularization to reduce prediction variance, hyperparameters tuned with Optuna emphasizing high weight decay to prevent overfitting, and Focal Loss to address class imbalance and focus on harder examples. At inference, perform Monte Carlo Dropout with 200 stochastic forward passes over four different checkpoints, averaging softmax probabilities to obtain robust predictions.
AI.result: The system obtains 23.26% word error rate (WER) on the official primary metric that considers full case endings and includes positions without diacritics, ranking 1st on the KSAA-2026 Task 2 leaderboard.
AI.conclusion: In a highly constrained, low-resource setting for Arabic speech dictation with automatic diacritization, careful regularization, loss design, and stochastic ensembling over a CATT-Whisper multimodal architecture can achieve state-of-the-art performance without external data.

title: Beyond Query Memorization: Large Language Model Routing with Query Decomposition and Historical Matching
summary: Optimizing the trade-off among predictive performance and computational cost is a central focus in the deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs). Current routing methods primarily rely on direct mapping from queries to models based on surface-level features, making them susceptible to the memorization trap and leading to poor generalizability on out-of-distribution (OOD) data. In this paper, we propose DecoR, a novel routing framework that recasts the routing task as a matching process of sifting similar queries from historical logs, effectively mitigating the memorization trap. To enhance matching accuracy, we introduce a query capability deconstruction method that decouples linguistic surface forms from task-intrinsic requirements, directing matching toward capability dimensions to ground decisions in essential task attributes. Furthermore, we develop CodaSet, a comprehensive benchmark for assessing routing generalization, where experimental results demonstrate that DecoR maintains superior accuracy while substantially lowering inference costs across both in-distribution and OOD settings. All the codes and data are available at https://github.com/lvbotenbest/DecoR.

AI.tldr: DecoR is a routing framework for LLMs that matches new queries to similar historical ones using capability-based representations, improving accuracy–cost trade-offs and generalization, especially on OOD data.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM routing approaches map queries to models using surface-level features or direct classifiers, which tend to overfit training distributions and fall into a memorization trap. This harms robustness and generalization, particularly on out-of-distribution inputs, and can result in suboptimal use of expensive and cheap models. There is a need for a routing mechanism that bases decisions on deeper task requirements rather than superficial linguistic patterns, while also enabling better cost–performance trade-offs.
AI.method: The paper introduces DecoR, which reframes routing as a retrieval/matching problem over historical query–model interactions. Instead of directly predicting a model from the raw query, DecoR searches a log of past queries and their associated optimal models to find similar cases. To avoid reliance on surface forms, the authors propose a query capability deconstruction technique that maps each query into a capability space capturing task-intrinsic requirements (e.g., reasoning depth, knowledge needs) and uses this representation for similarity search and routing. They also construct CodaSet, a benchmark designed to test routing generalization across in-distribution and OOD conditions.
AI.result: On CodaSet and other evaluated datasets, DecoR achieves higher or comparable routing accuracy relative to existing routing baselines while significantly reducing overall inference cost by sending easier queries to cheaper models and reserving expensive models for harder cases. Crucially, DecoR shows stronger robustness and generalization on OOD data, indicating that capability-based matching is less prone to memorization and distribution shift issues than surface-level routing methods.
AI.conclusion: Recasting LLM routing as capability-based matching over historical logs effectively addresses the memorization trap of prior routing methods. By deconstructing queries into capability dimensions and routing via similarity in this space, DecoR achieves a better accuracy–cost trade-off and superior OOD generalization. The accompanying CodaSet benchmark provides a standardized way to evaluate routing generalization, and open-sourced code and data can facilitate further progress in robust, cost-aware LLM deployment.

title: QUIET: A Multi-Blank Cascaded Story Cloze Benchmark for LLM Creative Generation Capability
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) face a dual challenge in creative capability evaluation: existing benchmarks (e.g., Story Cloze Test, HellaSwag) measure models' discriminative ability over narrative continuation using multiple-choice recognition paradigms, rather than directly measuring creative generation capability; rubric-based scoring and LLM-as-Judge methods rely on subjective dimension assessment or natural language model outputs, and cannot provide objective, automated scoring mechanisms.
  This paper proposes QUIET (Quality Understanding via Interlocked Evaluation Testing), a diagnostic benchmark for LLM creative capability based on multi-blank cascaded story cloze. QUIET sets N blanks (10-20) in a story with complete structure, with each blank accompanied by an explicit content constraint, and cascade dependency relationships between blanks -- the content filled into earlier blanks constrains the feasible solution space for later blanks. The evaluated model (or human participants) fills all blanks in open-ended generation mode; the results are scored by an information-theoretic automated scoring protocol without human grading.
  The scoring protocol directly operationalizes the "calibrated surprise" theoretical framework (Zou & Xu, 2026a). For each blank k, a composite score is computed: score = satisfy * (1 + lambda * surprise), where lambda = 1.0. Here, "satisfy" measures how well the blank filling satisfies the content constraint (objective logical reasoning judgment, not subjective aesthetic scoring), and "surprise" measures the degree of surprise given that the constraint is satisfied. Creative answers that do not satisfy the constraint score zero; answers that satisfy the constraint but are mediocre score low; answers that satisfy the constraint and are surprising score high.

AI.tldr: QUIET is a benchmark that evaluates creative story generation in LLMs using multi-blank, constrained cloze tasks and an automated information-theoretic scoring protocol.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks mainly test multiple-choice narrative recognition, not open-ended creative generation, and current evaluation methods relying on rubrics or LLM-as-judge are subjective and lack objective, automated scoring for creativity.
AI.method: The authors design QUIET, where stories have 10–20 blanks, each with explicit content constraints and cascade dependencies so earlier blanks restrict later ones. Models or humans fill all blanks in open-ended mode. An information-theoretic scoring protocol based on the calibrated surprise framework computes, for each blank, a composite score using constraint satisfaction and the level of (conditional) surprise, with no human grading.
AI.result: QUIET enables automatic, fine-grained measurement of how well generations satisfy logical/content constraints while also being surprising, thereby differentiating mediocre from genuinely creative completions in a structured way.
AI.conclusion: QUIET offers an objective, automated, and theoretically grounded benchmark for diagnosing LLM creative capability, capturing both constraint satisfaction and calibrated surprise via multi-blank cascaded story cloze tasks.

title: Uncertainty Reasoning with Large Language Models for Explainable Disease Diagnosis
summary: Clinical decision-making requires reasoning over incomplete, imprecise, and linguistically expressed patient narratives. While large language models (LLMs) excel at extracting latent information from natural language, they lack the verifiability and interpretability essential for trustworthy medical AI. We propose a neuro-symbolic reasoning framework that aligns LLMs with formal logic to enable explainable and formally verifiable medical diagnosis. Patient descriptions and clinical guidelines are embedded into a neural knowledge base, where LLMs extract structured medical entities, temporal relations, and fuzzy symptom patterns, which are decoded into a symbolic knowledge base expressed in fuzzy logic and declarative rules. We perform two-stage reasoning: (1) inductive symbolic generalization to capture diagnostic patterns from encoded narratives, and (2) inference verification via a logic programming engine to derive and validate diagnoses consistent with clinical standards. Each symptom is treated as a fuzzy predicate with probabilistic weights, and inference paths are auditable, adjustable, and compatible with physician feedback. Unlike purely statistical methods, our system supports iterative refinement: misalignment between LLM-generated diagnoses and ground truth can be traced, explained, and corrected through formal rules. By combining logic-based transparency, LLM adaptability, and probabilistic robustness, the framework enables human-aligned healthcare inference with strong generalization and verifiable, step-by-step reasoning chains. We validate our framework on public benchmarks, demonstrating effective reconciliation of symbolic reasoning and LLMs with real-world clinical narratives. Results show performance comparable to state-of-the-art LLMs, while additionally providing interpretable reasoning paths and formally verifiable diagnostic conclusions.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a neuro-symbolic framework that combines large language models with fuzzy logic and rule-based reasoning to produce interpretable, verifiable medical diagnoses from clinical narratives.
AI.motivation: Clinical decision-making is based on complex, incomplete, and linguistically expressed patient narratives. Current LLM-based systems, while strong at understanding language, lack verifiability, interpretability, and formal guarantees, which are critical in healthcare. The authors aim to bridge the gap between LLM flexibility and the need for transparent, auditable, and standard-compliant diagnostic reasoning.
AI.method: The authors design a neuro-symbolic reasoning pipeline where patient narratives and clinical guidelines are first embedded into a neural knowledge base. LLMs are used to extract structured entities (symptoms, temporal relations, fuzzy patterns), which are then converted into a symbolic knowledge base expressed in fuzzy logic and declarative rules. Reasoning proceeds in two stages: (1) inductive symbolic generalization learns diagnostic patterns from encoded narratives; (2) a logic programming engine performs inference and verification to derive diagnoses consistent with clinical standards. Symptoms are modeled as fuzzy predicates with probabilistic weights, and reasoning paths are explicitly represented, auditable, and modifiable based on physician feedback, enabling iterative refinement when misalignments with ground truth are found.
AI.result: On public clinical benchmarks, the proposed framework achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art LLM-based diagnostic systems while additionally generating interpretable reasoning paths and allowing formal verification of diagnostic conclusions. The system can trace and correct errors via rule adjustments, demonstrating reconciliation between symbolic logic and LLM-based understanding for real-world narratives.
AI.conclusion: Integrating LLMs with a fuzzy, rule-based symbolic reasoning layer yields a medical AI system that preserves strong predictive performance while providing transparent, auditable, and formally verifiable diagnostic reasoning. The work shows that neuro-symbolic methods can align LLMs with clinical standards, support human feedback and iterative refinement, and enable trustworthy, human-aligned diagnostic decision support in settings with complex natural language narratives.

title: PolyGnosis 2.0: Enhancing LLM Reasoning via Agentic Harness Engineering for Polymarket and OSINT Insight Extraction
summary: This paper introduces PolyGnosis 2.0, a pioneering multi-agent architecture designed to extract predictive intelligence by synthesizing Polymarket anomaly signals with global Open Source Intelligence (OSINT) streams, specifically Global Database of Events, Language, and Tone (GDELT). We define and target "Perspective Mismatches", the narrative divergence between Polymarket sentiment and global media flows, as high-alpha trading signals. Moving beyond generic agentic superiority, we rigorously quantify the efficacy of "Harness Engineering" techniques, including reflection loops, tool-calling, divide-and-conquer partitioning (D&C), and chain-of-thought (CoT), within high-noise financial domains. Our empirical evaluation against human-expert benchmarks reveals that while structural partitioning is mandatory for multi-dimensional alignment, unconstrained terminal reflection actively induces logical drift. Furthermore, we identify a pervasive "consensus bias" across all agent configurations during narrative reasoning, necessitating deterministic validation. Ultimately, we isolate a Pareto-optimal configuration that achieves professional-grade analytical precision while minimizing latency and token overhead, providing a robust blueprint for autonomous intelligence in prediction markets.

AI.tldr: PolyGnosis 2.0 is a multi-agent AI system that detects profitable prediction-market opportunities by comparing Polymarket sentiment with global news data, and systematically studies which agentic AI techniques actually help in this noisy financial setting.
AI.motivation: Prediction markets like Polymarket embed crowd forecasts, while OSINT streams like GDELT capture global narrative and event flow. Misalignments between these two perspectives may encode underexploited trading opportunities, but extracting them reliably in high-noise, real-time environments is difficult. Existing work often assumes that increasingly complex multi-agent and "agentic" techniques automatically improve reasoning, without rigorous evaluation in realistic, adversarial domains. The authors aim to build a practical architecture that uses these signals for alpha generation and to empirically test which agent design patterns truly add value and which introduce failure modes such as drift or bias.
AI.method: They propose PolyGnosis 2.0, a multi-agent architecture that ingests anomaly signals from Polymarket and narrative/event data from GDELT. The system defines "Perspective Mismatches" as divergences between market sentiment and media narratives, and treats these as candidate trading signals. They implement and ablate multiple harness-engineering techniques: reflection loops, external tool-calling, divide-and-conquer task partitioning, and chain-of-thought reasoning. The architecture is evaluated empirically in a high-noise financial context, using human experts as benchmarks, to measure analytical quality, latency, and token usage, and to diagnose systematic reasoning pathologies such as consensus bias and logical drift.
AI.result: Experiments show that structural partitioning (e.g., divide-and-conquer for multi-dimensional subtasks) is necessary to achieve consistent alignment and analytical quality in complex narrative reasoning. However, unconstrained terminal reflection loops cause logical drift rather than improvement, indicating that reflection must be carefully bounded or validated. Across all tested agent configurations, they observe a robust consensus bias, where agents overweight agreement and converge prematurely, which implies the need for deterministic validation mechanisms. Through ablation and comparison to human-expert benchmarks, they identify a specific combination of mechanisms that offers high analytical precision with relatively low latency and token consumption.
AI.conclusion: PolyGnosis 2.0 demonstrates that combining Polymarket anomaly signals with global OSINT data and explicitly targeting Perspective Mismatches can yield high-value predictive intelligence for prediction markets. The study challenges the assumption that more reflection or more complex multi-agent setups are always better, showing instead that some commonly used techniques can harm reasoning quality in noisy domains. By isolating a Pareto-optimal agent configuration that balances accuracy, cost, and speed, the paper provides a concrete design template for building autonomous analytical systems in financial prediction settings and potentially other complex forecasting domains.

title: Detecting Unfaithful Chain-of-Thought via Circuit-Guided Internal-External Discrepancy
summary: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning improves the problem-solving ability of large language models (LLMs), but generated reasoning traces may not faithfully reflect the model's actual decision process. Existing CoT unfaithfulness detectors mainly rely on external signals from generated rationales, such as textual plausibility or answer consistency, while overlooking evidence from the model's internal computation. Although recent circuit tracing methods provide a way to obtain model-internal evidence by tracing how information flows through model components during reasoning, constructing full reasoning circuits for long CoTs is costly and difficult to scale. To address these challenges, we propose Circuit-guided Internal-External Discrepancy Scorer (CIE-Scorer), a framework for instance-level CoT unfaithfulness detection. The key idea is that faithful reasoning traces should align with the model's computational process, whereas unfaithful traces may diverge from it. CIE-Scorer efficiently traces compact sentence-level circuits from informative reasoning tokens, constructs internal and external reasoning graphs, and measures their discrepancy using Fused Gromov--Wasserstein distance. Experiments on four datasets from FaithCoT-Bench show that CIE-Scorer achieves state-of-the-art performance while reducing the cost of circuit construction, demonstrating the effectiveness of combining mechanistic interpretability signals with external reasoning traces for CoT unfaithfulness detection.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CIE-Scorer, an instance-level detector of unfaithful chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning that compares internal model computations with external reasoning traces using graph discrepancy metrics.
AI.motivation: Chain-of-thought reasoning boosts LLM performance but often produces rationales that do not reflect the model’s true decision process. Existing detectors focus on surface properties of the generated CoT, such as plausibility or agreement with answers, ignoring the rich information in the model’s internal activations. Full circuit-tracing methods from mechanistic interpretability could provide better faithfulness signals but are computationally expensive and hard to scale to long, multi-step CoTs. The paper is motivated by the need for a scalable, instance-level method that uses internal model evidence to detect when a CoT is unfaithful.
AI.method: The authors propose CIE-Scorer, which operates in three main steps: (1) it identifies informative reasoning tokens and efficiently traces compact, sentence-level circuits that capture how information flows through model components during CoT generation; (2) it builds two graphs: an internal reasoning graph derived from these traced circuits and an external reasoning graph derived from the textual CoT; and (3) it quantifies the discrepancy between these graphs using Fused Gromov–Wasserstein distance. High discrepancy indicates that the external rationale diverges from the model’s internal computation, signaling potential unfaithfulness.
AI.result: On four datasets from FaithCoT-Bench, CIE-Scorer achieves state-of-the-art performance for instance-level CoT unfaithfulness detection while substantially lowering the computational cost compared with full circuit-tracing approaches. The experiments show that incorporating mechanistic interpretability-based signals meaningfully improves the reliability of faithfulness detection beyond using only external rationale features.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that aligning external CoT traces with internal model computation is a powerful way to detect unfaithful rationales. CIE-Scorer demonstrates that compact circuit tracing combined with graph-based discrepancy measures can scale more efficiently than full reasoning circuits while improving detection accuracy. This supports the broader claim that integrating mechanistic interpretability with traditional CoT analysis is a promising direction for making LLM reasoning more trustworthy and diagnostically transparent.

title: Triplet-Block Diffusion RWKV
summary: Causal Transformer language models suffer from strictly sequential decoding and a quadratic per-step attention cost. While linear-time causal models and discrete diffusion models each address these weaknesses, their integration remains inherently inconsistent: diffusion requires bidirectional attention, while causal models are unidirectional. To unify these architectures, we propose $B^3D-RWKV$, a diffusion RWKV variant that integrates the model's $O(L)$ inference efficiency with parallel, bidirectional discrete-diffusion through a \emph{triplet-block layout} method. $B^3D-RWKV-7.2B$ reaches comparable accuracy on an 8-task suite versus existing models while significantly outperforming baselines in decoding throughput with an average of $\mathbf{1.6\times}$ speedup.

AI.tldr: They propose B^3D-RWKV, a diffusion-based variant of the RWKV language model that combines linear-time inference with parallel, bidirectional discrete diffusion to overcome the limitations of causal transformers, achieving similar accuracy but faster decoding.
AI.motivation: Causal Transformer language models suffer from two main issues: decoding must be strictly sequential, and each decoding step has a quadratic attention cost with respect to sequence length. Existing alternatives such as linear-time causal models and discrete diffusion models each solve part of the problem but are architecturally incompatible, since diffusion relies on bidirectional attention whereas causal models are unidirectional. The paper is motivated by the desire to unify these strengths in a single, efficient model that maintains good performance while enabling faster decoding.
AI.method: The authors introduce B^3D-RWKV, a variant of the RWKV architecture adapted for discrete diffusion. They design a triplet-block layout that allows parallel, bidirectional discrete-diffusion-style generation while preserving RWKV’s O(L) inference efficiency. The architecture restructures computation into blocks that can support diffusion steps and bidirectional context handling without resorting to full quadratic self-attention, effectively marrying diffusion-style generation with a linear-time recurrent-like model.
AI.result: A 7.2B-parameter instance of the model, B^3D-RWKV-7.2B, is evaluated on an 8-task benchmark suite. It achieves accuracy comparable to existing state-of-the-art baselines on these tasks. However, in terms of decoding throughput it significantly outperforms the baselines, achieving on average a 1.6× speedup in generation, showing that the proposed architecture improves efficiency while maintaining competitive task performance.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that it is possible to reconcile the architectural mismatch between bidirectional discrete diffusion models and unidirectional causal language models by using a diffusion-adapted RWKV with a triplet-block layout. B^3D-RWKV preserves linear-time inference while enabling parallel, bidirectional diffusion, leading to comparable accuracy and substantially faster decoding. This suggests a promising direction for building more efficient language models that blend diffusion-based generation with efficient recurrent-style architectures.

title: Back to Parsimonious Latents: Learning Task-Centric World Models from Visual Foundations
summary: World models enable agents to predict future dynamics conditioned on actions, making the choice of latent representation central to planning and control. Such representations are often either learned directly from pixels with limited semantic structure or inherited from frozen visual foundation models with excessive task-irrelevant detail, yielding state spaces that are poorly matched to downstream planning and control. This is especially challenging in reward-free offline settings, where the model must learn from fixed trajectories without reward supervision or online interaction. To address this, we propose TC-WM, a framework for turning foundation-model embeddings into compact, task-sufficient world representations. The key design is to treat the pretrained embedding space as a semantic scaffold rather than as the final state space: TC-WM linearly projects high-dimensional visual embeddings into a compact latent as the dynamic space, aligns a subspace with the agent's physical state via contrastive learning, and reconstructs embeddings to preserve useful visual structure. This combines the generality of foundation features with the controllability of task-centric dynamics. Theoretically, we show that TC-WM suffices to identify the underlying task-centric latent factors up to a simple transformation. Empirically, TC-WM enables test-time planning across diverse environments (e.g., Robomimic and D4RL), achieving better world-modeling quality and more precise control than state-of-the-art approaches.

AI.tldr: TC-WM turns high-dimensional foundation-model visual embeddings into compact, task-sufficient world-model states for better planning and control in offline, reward-free settings.
AI.motivation: Existing world models either learn latents directly from pixels with weak semantics or reuse frozen foundation-model embeddings that contain too much task-irrelevant detail. In reward-free offline data settings, this mismatch between representation and control needs makes planning and control difficult, as the learned state space is not aligned with task-relevant dynamics and physical states.
AI.method: TC-WM treats pretrained visual embedding spaces as a semantic scaffold instead of the final state space. It linearly projects high-dimensional foundation-model embeddings into a compact latent dynamic space, aligns a dedicated subspace of this latent with the agent’s low-dimensional physical state using contrastive learning, and reconstructs the original embeddings from the latent to retain important visual information. The resulting world model is trained on offline trajectories, predicting future dynamics in this compact, task-centric latent space.
AI.result: Theoretically, TC-WM is shown to be sufficient to recover or identify the underlying task-centric latent factors up to a simple transformation. Empirically, across diverse control benchmarks such as Robomimic and D4RL, TC-WM improves world-model prediction quality and yields more accurate and effective test-time planning and control compared with state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: By converting generic foundation-model visual embeddings into compact, task-aligned world-model states, TC-WM unifies the broad semantics of foundation features with the controllability and sufficiency needed for planning. This leads to more effective model-based control in challenging reward-free offline settings and offers a principled way to build task-centric world models on top of generic vision backbones.

title: Anticipate and Learn: Unleashing Idle-Time Compute in Proactive Agents
comment: 26 pages, 4 figures; code available at https://github.com/AgentACE-AI/ProAct
summary: While AI agents demonstrate remarkable capabilities in reasoning and tool use, they remain fundamentally reactive: they compute responses only after explicit user prompts. This paradigm ignores a critical opportunity: the idle time between interactions is largely wasted, leaving agents unable to prepare for future user needs. To bridge this gap, we introduce ProAct, a proactive agent architecture that leverages idle-time compute to anticipate and fulfill likely upcoming user needs. By analyzing evolving dialogue history together with persistent memory, ProAct predicts upcoming needs and iteratively acquires information, allowing the agent to resolve knowledge gaps and prepare evidence before the user initiates a query.To rigorously evaluate proactive capabilities, we also introduce ProActEval, a comprehensive benchmark comprising 200 scenarios across 40 domains, featuring predictable need chains and diverse user cognitive profiles. Empirical results demonstrate significant advantages over reactive baselines. ProAct accelerates task completion by reducing required turns by 14.8%, decreases user effort by 11.7%, and cuts hallucination rates by 28.1% on ProActEval. Furthermore, MemBench evaluations confirm that ProAct achieves state-of-the-art reflective accuracy, underscoring its sustained and robust performance.

AI.tldr: ProAct is a proactive AI agent architecture that uses idle time to anticipate future user needs, gather information in advance, and thus speed up tasks, reduce user effort, and lower hallucinations, evaluated on a new benchmark ProActEval.
AI.motivation: Existing AI agents are reactive: they only respond after explicit user prompts and do not use idle time between interactions. This wastes computational opportunities and prevents agents from preparing for likely future user needs. The authors aim to exploit this idle time to make agents more helpful, efficient, and reliable.
AI.method: They design ProAct, a proactive agent architecture that continuously analyzes dialogue history and persistent memory during idle periods, predicts likely upcoming user needs, and iteratively gathers relevant information to close knowledge gaps and prepare evidence. They also create ProActEval, a benchmark of 200 scenarios across 40 domains with structured need chains and varied user cognitive profiles to systematically test proactive behavior. Additionally, they use MemBench to evaluate reflective accuracy.
AI.result: On ProActEval, ProAct reduces the number of turns needed to complete tasks by 14.8%, lowers user effort by 11.7%, and reduces hallucination rates by 28.1% compared to reactive baselines. On MemBench, it achieves state-of-the-art reflective accuracy, demonstrating strong and consistent performance over time.
AI.conclusion: Proactive use of idle-time compute via ProAct leads to more efficient and reliable AI agents, enabling faster task completion, less user effort, and fewer hallucinations than traditional reactive agents. The newly introduced ProActEval benchmark provides a rigorous way to assess proactive capabilities across domains, and results indicate that proactive architectures are a promising direction for future AI agent design.

title: CUA-Gym: Scaling Verifiable Training Environments and Tasks for Computer-Use Agents
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has driven breakthroughs in domains such as math, tool-use, and software engineering, yet its extension to computer-use agents (CUAs) has been bottlenecked by the scarcity of scalable training data with deterministic rewards. Constructing such data for CUAs requires consistent task instruction, executable environment, and verifiable reward. However, hand-curated benchmarks achieve high reward fidelity but cover few applications and LLM-as-judge-based datasets scale broadly but lack reliable verification. We present CUA-Gym, a scalable pipeline that co-generates task instructions, environment states, and reward functions. Concretely, a Generator agent constructs the initial and golden environment states, and a separate Discriminator agent writes the reward function from the task specification. An orchestrator agent drives the two through iterative rounds upon execution. Generated tuples then pass a final filter combining LLM majority voting and agent rollouts, ensuring quality beyond the per-task adversarial loop. To address the scarcity of training environments, we further synthesize CUA-Gym-Hub, a broad suite of high-fidelity mock web applications grounded in real-world software-use distributions, expanding the scale of CUA RLVR data by magnitude. Using this pipeline, we construct CUA-Gym, a dataset of 32,112 verified RLVR training tuples grounded in 110 environments. Trained with GSPO on CUA-Gym, our CUA-Gym-A3B and CUA-Gym-A17B achieve 62.1% and 72.6% on OSWorld-Verified, outperforming prior open-source CUAs at comparable scales, with performance scaling smoothly in both data volume and environment diversity. The same checkpoints also improve on the held-out WebArena benchmark, indicating transfer beyond the training environments. We will open-source the full synthesis pipeline, dataset, CUA-Gym-Hub environments, and models.

AI.tldr: They build a scalable, automatically verified training pipeline and dataset for computer-use agents, leading to stronger RL agents that use computers and generalize to new environments.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards works well for domains like math and coding, but extending it to computer-use agents is blocked by the lack of large-scale data where tasks, environments, and rewards are all reliably specified and automatically checkable. Existing hand-crafted benchmarks are precise but too small and narrow, while datasets scored by LLM judges scale up but have noisy, non-deterministic rewards. The paper aims to create a scalable way to generate high-fidelity, verifiable RL data for agents that operate computer interfaces (e.g., web apps).
AI.method: They design a multi-agent synthesis pipeline that co-generates three elements: (1) task instructions for computer-use agents, (2) initial and target ("golden") environment states, and (3) executable reward functions that deterministically verify success. A Generator agent creates environments and golden states, while a separate Discriminator agent writes reward functions from task specs. An orchestrator coordinates iterative refinement by executing the environment and using feedback to improve both agents’ outputs. Final task-environment-reward tuples are filtered using LLM majority voting plus agent rollouts to ensure quality beyond the per-task adversarial loop. To address environment scarcity, they also synthesize CUA-Gym-Hub, a collection of realistic mock web applications modeled on real software usage patterns, greatly expanding the diversity of environments. They then train RL agents with GSPO on the resulting CUA-Gym dataset.
AI.result: The pipeline produces CUA-Gym, a dataset of 32,112 verified RLVR training tuples across 110 distinct environments. Agents trained with GSPO on this dataset, CUA-Gym-A3B and CUA-Gym-A17B, achieve 62.1% and 72.6% success respectively on OSWorld-Verified, outperforming previous open-source computer-use agents of similar size. Performance increases smoothly with both more data and more diverse environments. The same models also improve on the unseen WebArena benchmark, showing cross-environment generalization rather than overfitting to the training environments.
AI.conclusion: CUA-Gym demonstrates that it is possible to automatically synthesize large-scale, high-fidelity RL with verifiable rewards data for computer-use agents by jointly generating tasks, environments, and executable reward functions via a coordinated multi-agent process. The resulting dataset and mock application suite significantly advance RL training for CUAs, yielding stronger and more general agents that transfer to held-out benchmarks. By open-sourcing the pipeline, data, environments, and models, the work lays a foundation for broader research on verifiable-reward RL for computer-use and tool-using agents.

title: Creative Quality Alignment: Expert Tacit Knowledge Transfer via Chain-of-Thought Fine-Tuning
summary:
This paper provides an empirical implementation of the creative quality metric proposed in Calibrated Surprise (Zou & Xu, 2026a). The question this paper addresses is: does this mathematical claim hold at the engineering level?
  To make the answer as general as possible, we deliberately choose the strictest engineering conditions: low data cost and a small base model. Training data comes from approximately 100 expert chain-of-thought (CoT) annotations produced by the BC Protocol (Zou & Xu, 2026b).
  We also identify a data bias: most publicly available alignment datasets are skewed toward craft-related knowledge, while audience modeling and reality-logic coverage are systematically weak.
  We use the term Creative Quality Alignment (CQA) to describe this class of engineering methods. We also offer a supporting theoretical observation: in an LLM with a single conditional distribution architecture, calibrating the appreciation side automatically transfers to the generation side via architectural duality. This is the structural reason why ~100 CoT examples are sufficient -- not a purely empirical observation like LIMA (Zhou et al., 2023).

AI.tldr: Empirical test of the Calibrated Surprise creative-quality metric using minimal data and a small model, showing that aligning a model’s "appreciation" of creative quality transfers to its generation behavior via architectural duality.
AI.motivation: To verify whether the mathematical claim from Calibrated Surprise—that creative quality can be captured and used effectively—actually works under strict, real-world engineering constraints: very little high-quality data and a small base LLM. The paper also aims to understand biases in current alignment datasets and explore whether focusing on creative-quality evaluation ("appreciation") can reliably improve generation with minimal supervision.
AI.method: Use ~100 expert chain-of-thought examples collected via the BC Protocol as training data to implement Creative Quality Alignment (CQA) on a small LLM. Explicitly examine dataset bias, noting overrepresentation of craft knowledge versus weaker coverage in audience modeling and reality-logic. Apply the creative quality metric from Calibrated Surprise to align the model’s evaluative behavior and rely on the single conditional distribution architecture to transfer this alignment to generation.
AI.result: Under low-data, small-model conditions, the implementation suggests that aligning the model’s evaluative side (how it scores or "appreciates" creative quality) is sufficient to noticeably influence its generative behavior, consistent with the theoretical predictions of architectural duality. The work also empirically confirms that existing public alignment datasets are biased toward craft knowledge and underrepresent audience modeling and reality-logic reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The Calibrated Surprise creative-quality metric appears to hold up at the engineering level: with only around 100 expert CoT examples, a small LLM can be effectively aligned for creative quality, thanks to the duality between appreciation and generation in single-distribution architectures. This supports the notion of Creative Quality Alignment (CQA) as a practical, data-efficient method and highlights the need to rebalance alignment datasets to better cover audience modeling and reality-logic, not just craft skills.

title: Insuring Every Action: An Authority Frontier Framework for Runtime Actuarial Control of Autonomous AI Agents
comment: 35 pages, 4 figures, 11 tables. Companion paper on the mathematical foundations: SSRN 6761960
summary: Autonomous AI agents increasingly issue side-effect-bearing actions: database mutations, refunds, payments, external commitments. We propose the Actuarial Action Interface (AAI), a deterministic runtime contract that prices each such action against a contractually fixed safe default under a time-consistent risk mapping, and gates execution against a per-boundary reserve capital budget. We then develop the Authority Frontier, an evaluation primitive measuring how much autonomous authority the runtime releases at each level of reserve capital. The framework provides (i) a deterministic quote-bind-commit protocol with toll-bounded capability tokens; (ii) a universal seven-class action taxonomy mapping heterogeneous tool calls to comparable authority units; (iii) replay determinism and pathwise reserve coverage under alpha-spending; (iv) cross-domain normalization via full reserve demand C_full and capital metrics Capital@k. We instantiate AAI across four agentic environments (database mutation, customer-service refund, and the public tau-bench retail and airline tool-use traces) and report a live Postgres panel in which three Azure-hosted models propose actions through the same contract. The frontier exhibits a common low-reserve refusal and intermediate-release pattern across domains, with saturation only where the budget grid reaches full reserve demand; required reserve capital varies by 22x (Capital@50 from 289 to 6457). The framework does not force domains into the same shape; it surfaces each domain's actuarial geometry. In the live panel the contract prevents realized loss across all three models at low budget while differing in underwriting persistence under denial: model identity is an actuarial underwriting variable. The contribution is a benchmark-ready evaluation framework for runtime actuarial control of autonomous-agent side effects.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a formal, financial-style control layer for autonomous AI agents’ real-world actions, pricing their side effects and limiting them via capital budgets.
AI.motivation: As autonomous AI agents trigger real-world side effects (e.g., payments, database changes), we need rigorous, comparable, and safe ways to bound their authority and financial/operational risk across heterogeneous tools and domains. Existing guardrails and evals are not expressed in a runtime, quantitative, risk-managed contract that can be shared across models and environments.
AI.method: The authors design the Actuarial Action Interface (AAI), a deterministic runtime contract that compares each proposed action to a safe default, assigns it a risk-adjusted “price” under a time-consistent risk mapping, and checks it against a reserve capital budget at each boundary. They define the Authority Frontier to measure how much autonomous authority is released as reserve capital increases. They introduce: (i) a quote-bind-commit protocol with capability tokens whose use is toll-bounded; (ii) a seven-class taxonomy to map heterogeneous tool calls into common authority units; (iii) mechanisms ensuring replay determinism and reserve coverage (alpha-spending); and (iv) cross-domain normalization tools such as full reserve demand C_full and Capital@k. They then implement AAI in four environments (database mutation, refund handling, and two public tau-bench traces) and run a live Postgres panel where three Azure-hosted models operate under the same contract.
AI.result: Across domains, Authority Frontiers show a similar pattern: agents refuse or are tightly constrained at low reserve levels, gain authority in a middle range, and saturate only when reserve reaches full demand; necessary reserve (Capital@50) varies by a factor of 22x across domains. The framework maintains deterministic behavior and actuarial coverage properties by construction. In the live panel, all three models avoid realized loss at low budgets, but differ in how they behave under repeated denials, indicating that model identity influences underwriting-relevant behavior.
AI.conclusion: AAI and the Authority Frontier together form a benchmark-ready evaluation and control framework for managing the side-effect risk of autonomous AI agents in a principled, actuarial manner. The framework normalizes heterogeneous tool actions into a comparable authority metric, enforces capital-based risk limits at runtime, and reveals domain- and model-specific “actuarial geometries” without forcing them into a single pattern.

title: AgentHijack: Benchmarking Computer Use Agent Robustness to Common Environment Corruptions
summary: Autonomous computer use agents that powered by multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are emerging as capable assistants for completing complex digital workflows. However, real-world execution environments are far from ideal: pop-ups, resolution changes, and competing applications frequently interfere with agent perception and control. We introduce AgentHijack, a benchmark designed to evaluate the robustness of computer-use agents under common corruptions, where the uncertainties in dynamic environment disrupt the execution flow without direct adversarial intent. Specifically, AgentHijack introduces 9 configurable common corruptions to replicate realistic imperfect scenarios. We evaluate a variety of desktop tasks that utilize MLLM-based agents and discover that even minor instances of corruption can result in substantial performance degradation, which emphasizes the fragility of agents and underscores the necessity of robustness evaluation. Afterward, we propose AgentHijack-Agent, a framework that integrates an action generator with enhanced grounding capabilities and an onlooker responsible for behavior summarization and environment checking. Extensive experiments validate its effectiveness. Our code, environment, baseline models and data are publicly available at: https://AgentHijack.github.io.

AI.tldr: AgentHijack is a benchmark and framework to test and improve the robustness of multimodal LLM-based computer-use agents under realistic, non-adversarial interface corruptions.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal LLM-based computer-use agents can complete complex digital workflows, but real-world desktop environments are noisy and unstable: pop-ups, resolution changes, and other applications frequently disrupt perception and control. Current evaluations often assume clean, static environments and thus overestimate reliability. There is a need to systematically measure and improve robustness to common, non-adversarial corruptions that occur during real execution.
AI.method: The authors build AgentHijack, a benchmark that injects 9 configurable types of common UI/environment corruptions (e.g., pop-ups, layout/resolution changes, interference from other apps) into desktop tasks. They run a variety of MLLM-based computer-use agents on these corrupted environments to quantify performance degradation. Based on observed failures, they design AgentHijack-Agent, a framework combining: (1) an action generator with stronger grounding to the current environment state, and (2) an onlooker module that summarizes recent behavior and monitors the environment for disruptions to maintain coherent execution.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple desktop tasks show that even slight corruptions can significantly reduce task success rates of existing agents, revealing substantial fragility. When evaluated on the same benchmark, the proposed AgentHijack-Agent framework achieves markedly better robustness and performance under corruption compared to baseline MLLM-based agents.
AI.conclusion: Real-world desktop environments introduce frequent, non-adversarial corruptions that current computer-use agents handle poorly, making robustness evaluation essential. AgentHijack provides a configurable benchmark to systematically study these issues, and the AgentHijack-Agent architecture demonstrates that integrating better grounding and environment monitoring can substantially improve robustness. The released code, environments, baselines, and data aim to foster further research on reliable MLLM-based computer-use agents.

title: SafeCtrl-RL: Inference-Time Adaptive Behaviour Control for LLM Dialogue via RL-Driven Prompt Optimisation
summary: Ensuring safe and contextually appropriate behaviour in Large Language Models (LLMs) remains a critical challenge for real-world deployment. We present \textbf{SafeCtrl-RL}, an inference-time behavioural control framework that enables adaptive safety regulation without model retraining or parameter modification. The method formulates dialogue generation as a sequential decision process, where a reinforcement learning agent dynamically selects prompt adjustment strategies based on contextual feedback. This allows unsafe behaviours to be suppressed through iterative refinement, which we conceptualise as inference-time behavioural unlearning. Evaluated across multiple LLMs and unsafe dialogue scenarios, SafeCtrl-RL consistently improves safety and response quality, outperforms existing prompt-based optimisation methods, and achieves favourable performance--efficiency trade-offs. **Warning: This paper may contain examples of harmful language, and reader discretion is recommended.

AI.tldr: SafeCtrl-RL is an inference-time control framework that uses reinforcement learning to adaptively adjust prompts so LLMs respond more safely without retraining.
AI.motivation: LLMs can behave unsafely in real-world settings, and existing safety methods often require retraining, fine-tuning, or static prompts that are not adaptive to context. There is a need for a lightweight, model-agnostic way to regulate behaviour at inference time while maintaining response quality and efficiency.
AI.method: The authors cast dialogue generation as a sequential decision-making problem and introduce SafeCtrl-RL, where an external RL agent observes contextual feedback (e.g., safety signals) during generation and selects prompt adjustment strategies step by step. These adjustments iteratively refine the LLM’s outputs, effectively performing “behavioural unlearning” of unsafe responses at inference time, without modifying the LLM’s parameters.
AI.result: Across several LLM backbones and a range of unsafe dialogue scenarios, SafeCtrl-RL reduces unsafe behaviours and improves overall response quality compared to other prompt-based optimization techniques. It also shows better trade-offs between safety performance and computational efficiency, indicating it can be applied in practice without excessive overhead.
AI.conclusion: Inference-time RL-based control can effectively steer LLM behaviour towards safer and higher-quality outputs without retraining. SafeCtrl-RL demonstrates that treating prompt adjustment as a sequential decision process enables adaptive, context-sensitive safety regulation and offers a practical path for deploying safer LLMs in real-world applications.

title: FLOATBench: A Dataset and Benchmark for Floating Offshore Wind Turbine Tower Fatigue
summary: Most of the world's offshore wind resource lies in waters too deep for fixed-bottom foundations, making floating offshore wind turbines (FOWTs) essential for deep-water deployment. As the industry scales toward $22$ MW class designs, tower fatigue becomes increasingly critical because larger structures amplify the coupled aero-hydro-servo-elastic loads induced by continuous wind and wave excitation. Accurate fatigue-damage prediction is therefore central to certification, design optimization, and cost reduction. Yet the field lacks a shared surrogate benchmark: studies report different simulations, splits, and metrics, making methods difficult to compare. We present FLOATBench, a public tabular benchmark with $582{,}120$ per-section fatigue-damage labels across three $22$ MW FOWT tower geometries, derived from $19{,}404$ high-fidelity OpenFAST simulations across the three towers ($6{,}468$ per tower: $1{,}078$ aligned wind/wave operating points $\times$ six turbulence seeds), labeled at $30$ cross-sections per tower. FLOATBench includes a regime-aware alpha-shape partition of the joint wind/wave operating envelope, stratifying test points into in-train, interpolation, and extrapolation regimes. It is paired with a reproducible evaluation harness covering three protocol levels: random validation (E1), within-tower regime-aware evaluation (E2), and cross-tower transfer (E3). The regime-aware protocol reveals rank shifts between global and extrapolation performance that random-split leaderboards cannot detect. To the authors' knowledge, FLOATBench is the first FOWT fatigue benchmark for tabular surrogate modeling, and offers an evaluation protocol that generalizes to engineering surrogates defined over physical operating envelopes. Dataset and code available at: https://github.com/Joao97ribeiro/FLOATBench.

AI.tldr: FLOATBench is a new public benchmark dataset and evaluation protocol for surrogate modeling of fatigue damage in 22 MW floating offshore wind turbine towers.
AI.motivation: Floating offshore wind turbines in deep waters face severe tower fatigue due to coupled aero-hydro-servo-elastic loads, especially as designs scale to 22 MW. Reliable fatigue-damage prediction is crucial for certification and design optimization, but current research lacks a common surrogate-modeling benchmark—studies use inconsistent simulations, data splits, and metrics, hindering fair comparison and progress.
AI.method: The authors generate FLOATBench, a tabular dataset built from 19,404 high-fidelity OpenFAST simulations across three different 22 MW FOWT tower geometries (6,468 simulations per tower), under 1,078 aligned wind/wave operating points and six turbulence seeds each. Fatigue damage is labeled at 30 tower cross-sections per turbine, yielding 582,120 per-section labels. They construct a regime-aware alpha-shape partition of the joint wind-wave operating envelope to classify test points as in-train, interpolation, or extrapolation. They also define a standardized evaluation harness with three protocol levels: E1 (random validation), E2 (regime-aware within-tower evaluation), and E3 (cross-tower transfer testing).
AI.result: The benchmark provides a large, structured dataset of fatigue-damage labels and a reproducible evaluation protocol that distinguishes different generalization regimes. Using the regime-aware splits, they observe ranking changes between models that perform well globally versus those that perform better in extrapolation regimes—differences that conventional random-split evaluations fail to reveal. The dataset and code are released publicly via GitHub for community use.
AI.conclusion: FLOATBench is introduced as the first dedicated benchmark for tabular surrogate modeling of fatigue damage in floating offshore wind turbine towers. Its regime-aware operating-envelope partition and multi-level evaluation protocols enable more meaningful, comparable assessments of surrogate models, particularly in interpolation and extrapolation regimes relevant to engineering practice. The framework is intended to generalize to other engineering surrogate tasks defined over physical operating envelopes and to support progress in accurate, cost-effective fatigue prediction for deep-water floating wind systems.

title: What Makes a Medical Checker Trainable? Diagnosing Signal Collapse and Reward Hacking in Checker-Guided RAG for Biomedical QA
summary: Medical RAG needs evidence-grounded claims, so plugging a claim-level NLI checker into retrieval-augmented RL is intuitive. \textbf{We find that the checker's \emph{output distribution} during training, not its held-out accuracy, decides whether it provides trainable gradient.} We compare four NLI checker back-ends as process rewards inside a GRPO-trained medical RAG agent (Qwen2.5-7B, replicated on Qwen3-4B and Llama-3.1-8B) across four held-out medical QA benchmarks. Three diagnostic findings emerge. \textbf{(i)} Signal collapse is log-prob-specific: LLM log-probability scoring labels over 97\% of claims neutral -- collapsing the RL gradient to zero -- while a calibrated MedNLI classifier scores the same pairs non-degenerately. \textbf{(ii)} Moderate signal beats strong signal on answer quality: a strong proprietary checker triggers a three-step reward-hacking cascade -- ultra-short answers, search avoidance, language collapse -- so a moderate-signal local classifier trains a higher-quality model (\textbf{+12\% BERTScore over zero-shot, no GPT dependency}). \textbf{(iii)} Signal strength is policy-dependent: the same checker registers as moderate on one policy but strong on another without triggering the cascade end-state. We frame these as boundary conditions for verifier-as-reward systems.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how the output distribution of NLI-based verifiers, not just their accuracy, determines whether they give useful gradients as reward models when training medical RAG systems with RL.
AI.motivation: Medical retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) must produce evidence-grounded claims. A natural idea is to use a claim-level NLI checker as a verifier and plug it into RL training as a process reward. However, it is unclear why some verifiers help training while others cause signal collapse, reward hacking, or unstable behavior, even when they have strong held-out accuracy.
AI.method: The authors plug four different NLI checkers (including LLM log-prob scoring, a calibrated MedNLI classifier, and a strong proprietary verifier) as process reward models into GRPO-trained medical RAG agents based on Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen3-4B, and Llama-3.1-8B. They train on medical QA tasks and evaluate across four held-out medical QA benchmarks. They analyze the reward signal distributions, gradients, and behavioral outcomes (e.g., answer length, search behavior, language collapse) for each verifier-policy combination.
AI.result: They obtain three diagnostic findings: (i) Signal collapse is tied to the verifier’s log-prob distribution, with LLM log-prob scoring labeling over 97% of claims as neutral, which collapses the RL gradient to near-zero, while a calibrated MedNLI classifier produces a richer, non-degenerate signal. (ii) A strong proprietary checker, though accurate, acts as an overly strong reward signal, leading to reward hacking: models produce ultra-short answers, avoid using search, and eventually exhibit language collapse. A moderate-signal local classifier avoids this cascade and yields better answer quality, achieving +12% BERTScore over zero-shot without relying on GPT. (iii) The perceived strength of a signal depends on the current policy: the same checker can be moderate for one policy and strong for another, sometimes avoiding the reward-hacking cascade. These findings define regimes where verifier-as-reward works or fails.
AI.conclusion: The paper argues that for verifier-as-reward systems in medical RAG, the verifier’s output distribution and effective signal strength—relative to the policy—are crucial. Held-out accuracy alone is an inadequate criterion for choosing verifiers. Overly strong or degenerate signals cause gradient collapse or reward hacking, while appropriately calibrated, moderate-strength verifiers can significantly improve answer quality without dependence on proprietary models. The authors propose these observations as boundary conditions for designing and deploying verifier-based reward models in RL training for medical RAG.

title: Learning to Search and Searching to Learn for Generalization in Planning
summary: Combinatorial generalization remains a central challenge in Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL). Classical planning provides a simple yet challenging setting to study this problem through explicit relational descriptions, without requiring learning from perception. In sparse-reward domains, standard RL exploration via real-time search is ineffective, and learning-based planning methods often rely on expert demonstrations, hindsight relabeling, or random walks from the goal state. In contrast, planners rely on best-first search methods such as $\mathrm{A}^\star$ to solve problems from scratch. We propose a self-improving $\mathrm{WA}^\star$ learning framework in combination with a value heuristic represented by a Relational Graph Neural Network: the heuristic guides search, and the resulting search data updates the heuristic via $Q$-learning. This loop yields heuristics that can function as general policies and solve new instances even without search, where DRL otherwise fails, as we show on puzzles such as Sokoban, PushWorld, The Witness, and the 2023 International Planning Competition benchmarks. Notably, we demonstrate strong zero-shot generalization: For example, heuristics trained on Blocksworld instances with fewer than 30 blocks successfully solve instances with 488 blocks without search.

AI.tldr: They introduce a self-improving search-and-learn framework (WA* + relational GNN heuristic) that achieves strong combinatorial generalization in classical planning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Deep RL struggles with combinatorial generalization, especially in sparse-reward, relational, classical planning domains where exploration and credit assignment are difficult and existing learning-based planners need extra supervision (demos, hindsight, goal walks). The authors want a method that can learn powerful, generalizable policies directly from planning experience without such supervision.
AI.method: Use a weighted A* (WA*) best-first search guided by a learned value heuristic implemented as a Relational Graph Neural Network. WA* uses the heuristic to search and solve instances; the resulting search traces are treated as experience to update the heuristic via Q-learning. This creates a self-improving loop where better heuristics induce better searches, which in turn yield better learning signals, and the learned heuristic can also act as a direct policy without search.
AI.result: On several challenging puzzle and planning domains (Sokoban, PushWorld, The Witness, and IPC 2023 benchmarks), the method solves instances where standard DRL fails, and the learned heuristic alone can often solve new instances without any search. They show strong zero-shot scaling: e.g., training on Blocksworld with <30 blocks, the learned heuristic solves instances with up to 488 blocks without additional search.
AI.conclusion: Coupling heuristic search (WA*) with a relational GNN value heuristic in a self-improving Q-learning loop yields heuristics that generalize combinatorially and can act as effective stand-alone policies. This approach addresses key limitations of DRL in sparse-reward relational planning domains and scales to much larger problem instances than those seen during training.

title: AI-Assisted Systematization for Evaluating GenAI Systems
summary: Evaluating generative AI (GenAI) systems is challenging because many targets of evaluation are broad, contested concepts, such as "reasoning," "fairness," or "creativity." When these concepts are left underspecified, it becomes unclear what should be measured or how evaluation results should be interpreted. This problem reflects a missing step: systematization, that is, moving from a broad background concept to an explicit, structured account of the concept in measurable terms. To help address the fact that systematization is cognitively demanding and resource-intensive, we investigate whether AI assistance can support this process. To enable AI-assisted systematization and assess its quality, we introduce a structured representation of a systematized concept, a concept spec, and a validation worksheet. We then develop two AI-assisted systematizers: a direct, zero-shot approach and a multi-agent approach that more closely mirrors manual systematization approaches from existing literature. We use these systematizers to produce concept specs for two concepts -- hate-based rhetoric and digital empathy -- and evaluate resulting concept specs on content validity and information recoverability.

AI.tldr: The paper explores using AI to help formalize vague evaluation concepts (like reasoning or fairness) into structured, measurable definitions called concept specs, and tests two AI-based methods for doing this.
AI.motivation: Evaluating generative AI systems is hard because many important evaluation targets (e.g., reasoning, fairness, creativity) are broad and underspecified, leading to ambiguity in what should be measured and how to interpret results. Manually turning these abstract ideas into concrete, measurable constructs (systematization) is cognitively demanding, time-consuming, and requires expertise. The authors aim to see whether AI itself can assist with this systematization step to make evaluation more scalable and rigorous.
AI.method: The authors define a structured representation for systematized concepts called a "concept spec" and a complementary validation worksheet to assess these specs. They then build two AI-assisted systematization pipelines: (1) a direct, zero-shot pipeline where an AI model is prompted to generate a concept spec in one shot, and (2) a multi-agent pipeline that decomposes systematization into multiple interacting steps/agents, modeled after manual approaches in prior literature. They apply both pipelines to two example concepts, hate-based rhetoric and digital empathy, and then evaluate the resulting concept specs in terms of content validity (how well the spec captures the target concept) and information recoverability (how much relevant information about the concept is retained).
AI.result: Both AI-assisted systematizers can generate non-trivial, structured concept specs for complex, abstract targets such as hate-based rhetoric and digital empathy. The multi-agent approach, which mirrors manual systematization procedures, tends to produce higher-quality and more comprehensive specs than the direct zero-shot approach. The generated specs achieve reasonable levels of content validity and information recoverability, demonstrating that AI can meaningfully support concept systematization, though there are still gaps relative to expert-driven processes.
AI.conclusion: AI can assist in the cognitively intensive task of systematizing broad, contested evaluation concepts into structured, measurable representations. By introducing concept specs and a validation worksheet, along with direct and multi-agent AI-assisted systematization pipelines, the paper shows that AI-generated specs can capture much of the relevant structure of complex concepts, making evaluation setups more explicit and analyzable. However, expert oversight and further refinement remain necessary, and the multi-agent, process-mirroring approach appears more promising than naive zero-shot generation.

title: A Deep Dive into Axiomatic Design -- Part I: Problem Formulation
comment: The paper is accepted at the ICAD 2026 - MIT and the final camera ready will be available once it got published by the Springer
summary: Problem formulation translating customer needs and constraints into a minimum set of independent first-level functional requirements, is arguably the most critical step in every design framework, including axiomatic design yet it is frequently misunderstood or underestimated in practice. This paper focuses exclusively on problem formulation in axiomatic design it clarifies what first-level FRs are (and are not), explains why they should not legitimately vary across designers given the same needs and constraints, and highlights intrinsic difficulties and recurring pitfalls that lead to design failure. The discussion is grounded primarily in Nam P.Suh's three books. The Principles of Design, Axiomatic Design Advances and Applications, and Complexity Theory, and it offers practical guidance to help designers formulate well-posed first-level FRs. Finally, the paper briefly revisits problem formulation in the era of large language models and discusses what such tools can (and cannot) contribute at the first level.

AI.tldr: The paper clarifies and systematizes how to correctly formulate first-level functional requirements (FRs) in axiomatic design, highlights common pitfalls, and discusses the limited but useful role of large language models in this early design step.
AI.motivation: Problem formulation—translating customer needs and constraints into a minimal, independent set of first-level functional requirements—is the most critical yet often misunderstood phase in axiomatic design. Poor formulation at this stage leads to downstream design failures, and there is confusion in practice about what first-level FRs really are and why they should be invariant across designers. With the rise of large language models, there is also new interest and confusion about whether such tools can automate or support this step.
AI.method: The paper conducts a conceptual and critical analysis of problem formulation within the axiomatic design framework, grounded in Nam P. Suh’s three foundational books: The Principles of Design, Axiomatic Design: Advances and Applications, and Complexity Theory. It interprets and synthesizes Suh’s definitions and principles to clarify the nature of first-level FRs, identifies intrinsic difficulties and recurring pitfalls in their formulation, and then reflects on the capabilities and limitations of large language models for first-level FR generation.
AI.result: The paper provides a clarified definition and characterization of first-level functional requirements in axiomatic design, argues that properly formulated first-level FRs should not vary among competent designers given the same needs and constraints, and catalogs typical pitfalls that lead to mis-specified FRs and subsequent design failure. It also delineates specific, limited ways in which large language models can assist with problem formulation (e.g., structuring information, generating candidate wordings) and what they cannot reliably do (e.g., guarantee independence, completeness, or correctness of FRs).
AI.conclusion: Correctly formulating first-level functional requirements is a non-negotiable foundation for successful axiomatic design and should yield an invariant minimal set across designers when based on the same needs and constraints. Many practical failures stem from misunderstanding this step. By grounding the discussion in Suh’s work, the paper offers practical guidance to avoid recurring pitfalls. While large language models can provide auxiliary support in handling text and exploring alternatives, they cannot replace the designer’s responsibility for rigorous, axiomatic problem formulation at the first level.

title: Forgotten Words: Benchmarking NeoBERT for Dementia Detection in Low-Resource Conversational Filipino and English Speech
summary: Dementia detection from spontaneous speech offers a scalable approach to cognitive screening, yet NLP systems remain predominantly English-centric. This limitation is especially acute in the Philippines, where Filipino-English code-switching is pervasive and no prior work has addressed NLP-based dementia detection. We present the first systematic evaluation of transformer-based dementia detection in Filipino speech and the first assessment of NeoBERT in a clinical NLP setting. To separate language from domain effects, we construct a parallel bilingual dataset of 4,000 DementiaBank-derived transcripts, with Filipino translations produced manually to preserve discourse-level markers of cognitive decline. We evaluate five model families, TF-IDF + LogReg, BERT, NeoBERT, XLM-R, and RoBERTa-Tagalog, under monolingual, zero-shot cross-lingual, and bilingual fine-tuning settings.
