Existing benchmarks similarly provide only partial user state and therefore fail to capture performance in such a broad, always-on setting. To address this gap, we introduce Claw-Anything, a benchmark that expands agent context along three dimensions: long-horizon activity histories, interdependent backend services, and integrated GUI and CLI interaction across multiple devices. To instantiate this setting, we simulate months of user activity through multi-round event injection, producing complex world states and realistic noise, including irrelevant events and conflicting signals. Agents must reason over rich contextual environments while remaining robust to such noise. This expanded scope also enables the evaluation of proactive assistance, requiring agents to anticipate user needs and deliver timely recommendations. Experiments show that GPT-5.5 achieves only 34.5% pass@1, substantially below prior benchmarks, underscoring a gap between current agent capabilities and the demands of always-on personal assistance. Alongside the benchmark, we release an automated data-generation pipeline that yields 2,000 training environments and improves the base model by 23.7%, demonstrating its utility of scalable data infrastructure.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Claw-Anything, a benchmark and data-generation pipeline for evaluating and training always-on LLM agents that operate over rich, noisy, long-horizon user contexts across devices and services.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agents and their benchmarks operate on narrow, short-term, and clean slices of a user’s digital world, which fails to reflect the complexity of real always-on personal assistants that must integrate long histories, multiple tools, and noisy, conflicting information. A new benchmark is needed to stress-test and improve agents in this realistic setting.
AI.method: They design Claw-Anything, a benchmark that expands agent context along three axes: (1) long-horizon user activity histories spanning months, (2) multiple interdependent backend services, and (3) integrated GUI and CLI interactions across devices. They simulate months of user activity via multi-round event injection to create complex, noisy world states with irrelevant and conflicting signals. They also build an automated data-generation pipeline that can produce 2,000 training environments for agents.
AI.result: When evaluated on Claw-Anything, GPT-5.5 attains only 34.5% pass@1, which is much lower than on prior, simpler benchmarks. Training with data from their automated pipeline improves the base model’s performance by 23.7%, indicating that the new data and benchmark can significantly boost agent capabilities in this domain.
AI.conclusion: Claw-Anything exposes a substantial gap between current LLM agents and the requirements of robust, always-on personal assistants operating over rich, noisy, long-horizon user contexts. The benchmark and scalable data-generation pipeline provide a practical testbed and training resource to develop more capable, proactive, and context-aware LLM agents.

title: From Model Scaling to System Scaling: Scaling the Harness in Agentic AI
summary: This paper studies the next major bottleneck in agentic AI as system scaling, not only model scaling: the design of auditable, persistent, modular, and verifiable architectures around foundation models. We refer to this shift as scaling the harness: treating the structured execution layer around a foundation model as a first-class object of design, evaluation, and optimization. Although recent large language models enable agents to use tools, retrieve information, maintain memory, and execute long-horizon workflows, evaluation remains largely model-centric, often reducing agents to final-task success while treating memory, retrieval, tool use, orchestration, verification, and governance as secondary implementation details. This framing is increasingly inadequate because agent performance emerges from the interaction among the foundation model, memory substrate, context constructor, skill-routing layer, orchestration loop, and verification-and-governance layer. Together, these components form the agent harness, which translates model capability into long-horizon agent behavior. We study scaling the harness through three core bottlenecks: context governance, trustworthy memory, and dynamic skill routing, together with the orchestration and governance mechanisms that coordinate and constrain them. We further outline a research agenda for harness-level benchmarks that go beyond one-shot task success to measure trajectory quality, memory hygiene, context efficiency, communication fidelity, verification cost, and safe evolution over time. To make the discussion concrete, we develop CheetahClaws: https://github.com/SafeRL-Lab/cheetahclaws, a Python-native reference harness, and compare it with Claude Code and OpenClaw. Our main claim is that future progress in agentic AI will depend as much on system design as on stronger foundation models.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that the main bottleneck for future agentic AI is not just bigger models but better system architectures (“harnesses”) around them, and proposes concepts, bottlenecks, benchmarks, and a reference implementation (CheetahClaws) to study this.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation and design of AI agents focus mostly on the foundation model itself and on final task success, underplaying key systems aspects like memory, retrieval, orchestration, verification, and governance. As agents get more complex and operate over longer horizons, their performance emerges from the interaction of many components around the model, so treating these as secondary implementation details is no longer sufficient.
AI.method: The authors conceptualize the ‘agent harness’ as the structured execution layer surrounding a foundation model, decomposed into components such as memory substrate, context constructor, skill-routing, orchestration loop, and verification-and-governance layer. They analyze three core bottlenecks—context governance, trustworthy memory, and dynamic skill routing—and discuss orchestration and governance mechanisms that coordinate these. They also propose a research agenda for new harness-level benchmarks that measure properties like trajectory quality and memory hygiene, and they develop a Python-native reference harness, CheetahClaws, to concretize and compare these ideas against existing systems like Claude Code and OpenClaw.
AI.result: Conceptual results include a taxonomy and definition of the ‘agent harness’ and its key bottlenecks, along with proposed dimensions for future benchmarks that move beyond one-shot task success to richer, system-level metrics. Practically, they provide CheetahClaws, a reference implementation of such a harness, and an initial comparative analysis versus other harnesses (Claude Code, OpenClaw).
AI.conclusion: Progress in agentic AI will increasingly hinge on system-level design—the architecture and governance of the agent harness—at least as much as on scaling foundation models themselves. To advance the field, researchers must directly design, evaluate, and optimize these harnesses, using new benchmarks and reference systems that explicitly target context governance, memory trustworthiness, skill routing, and long-horizon, safely governed behavior.

title: Tokenizer Fertility and Zero-Shot Performance of Foundation Models on Ukrainian Legal Text: A Comparative Study
comment: 25 pages, 13 tables, 5 figures; v2 adds cross-temporal generalization experiment and classical baseline
summary: Tokenizer fertility varies 1.6x across foundation models on Ukrainian legal text, yet this cost-critical dimension is absent from model selection practice. We benchmark seven models from five providers on 273 validated court decisions from Ukraine's state registry (EDRSR), measuring tokenizer fertility and zero-shot performance on three tasks. Four findings emerge. (1) Qwen 3 models consume 60% more tokens than Llama-family models on identical input, making tokenizer analysis a prerequisite for cost-efficient deployment. (2) NVIDIA Nemotron Super 3 (120B) achieves the highest composite score (83.1), outperforming Mistral Large 3 (5.6x more total parameters) at one-third the API cost model scale is a poor proxy for domain performance. (3) Few-shot prompting degrades performance by up to 26 percentage points; stratified and prompt-sensitivity ablations confirm this is intrinsic to Ukrainian-language demonstrations, not an artifact of example selection. (4) A cross-temporal generalization experiment reveals that classifiers trained on pre-war court ecisions (2008-2013) lose 27.9 percentage points when applied to full-scale invasion era decisions (2022-2026), with a pronounced forward-backward asymmetry: newer models transfer backward (+14.6 pp above forward transfer), but older models fail catastrophically on wartime legal language. For practitioners: tokenizer analysis should precede model selection, and zero-shot is a more reliable default than few-shot for morphologically rich languages. To support reproducibility and address the absence of Ukrainian from legal NLP benchmarks, we release a public dataset of 14,452 court decisions spanning 2008-2026, annotated with seven outcome labels across three temporal epochs that capture the impact of armed conflict on judicial proceedings.

AI.tldr: The paper benchmarks several large language models on Ukrainian legal text and finds large differences in tokenization cost, domain performance, few-shot behavior, and temporal generalization, releasing a new Ukrainian legal dataset.
AI.motivation: Model selection often focuses on accuracy and parameter count, ignoring tokenizer fertility and cost, especially for under-resourced languages like Ukrainian in specialized domains such as law. There is also a lack of Ukrainian legal NLP benchmarks and understanding of how models generalize across major socio-legal shifts such as war.
AI.method: The authors benchmark seven foundation models from five providers on 273 validated Ukrainian court decisions from the state registry (EDRSR). They measure tokenizer fertility (tokens per character/word) and zero-shot performance on three legal tasks, and compare it to few-shot prompting. They perform prompt-sensitivity and stratified ablations to test whether few-shot degradation is intrinsic to Ukrainian demonstrations. They also design a cross-temporal generalization experiment: training classifiers on pre-war decisions (2008-2013) and testing on wartime era decisions (2022-2026), and vice versa, to study temporal transfer asymmetries. Finally, they compile and release a larger dataset of 14,452 labeled court decisions across three temporal epochs.
AI.result: (1) Tokenizer fertility differs by about 1.6x across models, with Qwen 3 using roughly 60% more tokens than Llama-family models on the same Ukrainian legal inputs, implying higher costs. (2) NVIDIA Nemotron Super 3 (120B) achieves the best composite score (83.1), outperforming the much larger Mistral Large 3 while being cheaper, indicating model scale is a poor proxy for in-domain legal performance. (3) Few-shot prompting on Ukrainian legal tasks degrades performance by up to 26 percentage points relative to zero-shot, and analyses show this is due to the nature of Ukrainian-language demonstrations rather than bad example choice. (4) Models trained on pre-war decisions suffer a 27.9-point drop when applied to wartime-era cases, with newer models transferring better backward than forward, while older models perform very poorly on wartime legal language.
AI.conclusion: Practitioners working with Ukrainian legal text should analyze tokenizer fertility before choosing a model, as it strongly affects deployment cost, and should treat zero-shot prompting as a safer default than few-shot for morphologically rich languages like Ukrainian. Model parameter count is not a reliable stand-in for domain performance. Temporal distribution shifts, such as those caused by armed conflict, significantly impair cross-period generalization, and models trained only on pre-war data can fail on wartime-era legal language. The authors address resource gaps by releasing a new Ukrainian legal dataset with 14,452 court decisions and labeled outcomes across temporal epochs.
